topics.title: Fluid Statics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET413 - MECHANICS OF FLUID FLOW > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Fluid Statics # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Fluid Statics **Fluid Statics (Hydrostatics)** is the branch of fluid mechanics that deals with fluids at rest — i.e., fluids in which every element is in static equilibrium with no relative motion between adjacent layers. Since the fluid is at rest, there is no shear stress within the fluid, and only normal stresses (pressure) act on any submerged surface. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** > *Fluid Statics is the study of fluids in equilibrium, where the analysis is governed by the balance of normal forces (pressure) and body forces (gravity), with shear stress being identically zero.* Mathematically, fluid statics is governed by the equilibrium condition: $$\vec{\nabla} p = \rho \vec{g}$$ where $p$ is the pressure, $\rho$ is the fluid density, and $\vec{g}$ is the gravitational acceleration vector. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Stacked-Books Model":** > Imagine a stack of books resting on a table. Each book in the stack bears the weight of all the books above it plus its own weight. The lowest book experiences the highest compressive "pressure" (force per unit area), and the topmost book experiences the least. Now replace the books with a vertical column of water inside a transparent tube. The water at the bottom is "squeezed" by the weight of all the water above it, so the pressure is maximum at the bottom. This is the essence of hydrostatics — **pressure in a static fluid increases linearly with depth** because the fluid at the bottom has to support the weight of all the fluid above it. ## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities - **Pressure ($p$):** Normal force per unit area, measured in **Pa (N/m²)**, **bar**, **psi**, or **m of fluid column**. - **Specific Weight ($\gamma$):** Weight per unit volume, $\gamma = \rho g$, units **N/m³**. - **Density ($\rho$):** Mass per unit volume, units **kg/m³**. - **Specific Gravity ($S$):** Dimensionless ratio of fluid density to water density at **4°C** ($\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$). - **Depth ($h$):** Vertical distance from the free surface, units **m**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Reference Conditions (KTU Convention):** > - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ > - Density of water: $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ > - Specific weight of water: $\gamma_w = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$ > - Atmospheric pressure: $p_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} \approx 10.33 \text{ m of water}$ --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Pressure at a Point — Pascal's Law > [!NOTE] > **Pascal's Law (1651):** *A change in pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted undiminished to every portion of the fluid and to the walls of the containing vessel.* ### Consequences: 1. Pressure at a point in a static fluid is **scalar** (no directional dependence). 2. Pressure acts **perpendicularly** to any surface in contact with the fluid. 3. Pressure is the **same at all points in a horizontal plane** within a connected, static, single-fluid body. ## 2.2 Hydrostatic Pressure Equation For a static fluid of constant density $\rho$, the pressure variation with depth $h$ below the free surface is: $$p = p_0 + \rho g h = p_0 + \gamma h$$ where $p_0$ is the pressure at the free surface. ### Pressure Head and the Three Pressure Scales | Pressure Scale | Reference Point | Formula | |---|---|---| | **Absolute Pressure** | Perfect vacuum ($p = 0$) | $p_{abs} = p_{atm} + p_{gauge}$ | | **Gauge Pressure** | Local atmospheric pressure | $p_{gauge} = p_{abs} - p_{atm}$ | | **Vacuum Pressure** | When $p_{abs} < p_{atm}$ | $p_{vac} = p_{atm} - p_{abs}$ | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is your **exam-day cheat sheet** for Fluid Statics. Memorize all entries. | # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Hydrostatic pressure | $p = p_0 + \rho g h$ | Pa | | 2 | Specific weight | $\gamma = \rho g$ | N/m³ | | 3 | Pressure head | $h = p / \gamma$ | m of fluid | | 4 | Absolute pressure | $p_{abs} = p_{atm} + p_{gauge}$ | Pa | | 5 | Gauge pressure | $p_{gauge} = \gamma h$ | Pa | | 6 | Simple U-tube manometer | $p_A - p_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$ | Pa | | 7 | Differential manometer | $\Delta p = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$ | Pa | | 8 | Inclined manometer | $\Delta p = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g L \sin\theta$ | Pa | | 9 | Total pressure (vertical, top edge at surface) | $P = \gamma h_c A$ | N | | 10 | Depth of centroid | $h_c = \bar{h}$ (depth of centroid) | m | | 11 | Depth of center of pressure | $h_p = \dfrac{I_{xc} \sin^2\theta}{A \bar{h}} + \bar{h}$ | m | | 12 | Center of pressure for vertical rectangular surface from surface | $h_p = \dfrac{2h}{3}$ (for surface from surface to depth $h$) | m | | 13 | Force on curved surface (horizontal component) | $F_H = \gamma h_c A_v$ | N | | 14 | Force on curved surface (vertical component) | $F_V = \gamma \mathcal{V}$ (weight of fluid above/imaginary) | N | | 15 | Resultant force on curved surface | $F_R = \sqrt{F_H^2 + F_V^2}$ | N | | 16 | Buoyant force | $F_B = \gamma_f \mathcal{V}_{displaced}$ | N | | 17 | Metacentric height | $GM = \dfrac{I - \mathcal{V} h}{\mathcal{V}}$ | m | **Symbol legend:** $h_c$ = depth of centroid, $A$ = area, $I_{xc}$ = second moment of area about centroidal axis, $\theta$ = angle of inclination with horizontal, $\mathcal{V}$ = volume, $I$ = moment of inertia of waterline area, $h$ = distance between centroid of float and metacenter. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Dam design:** Computing horizontal thrust on the upstream face. - **Storage tanks:** Determining wall thickness for hydrostatic loading. - **Submarine design:** Buoyancy and pressure hull analysis. - **Hydraulic presses and brakes:** Pascal's law applications. - **Manometers:** Pressure measurement in pipelines and process industries. - **Shipbuilding:** Stability analysis using metacentric height. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Hydrostatic Pressure Equation Consider a static fluid of constant density $\rho$ in a container. Take an elementary fluid element of cross-sectional area $dA$ and height $dz$, with the $z$-axis pointing upward. **Free Body Diagram of the element:** The forces acting on the element in the vertical direction are: - Pressure force from below (pushing up): $p \, dA$ - Pressure force from above (pushing down): $(p + dp) \, dA$ - Weight of the element (pulling down): $dW = \rho g \, dA \, dz$ **Equilibrium condition (sum of forces = 0):** $$\sum F_z = 0$$ $$p \, dA - (p + dp) \, dA - \rho g \, dA \, dz = 0$$ Expanding and simplifying: $$p \, dA - p \, dA - dp \, dA - \rho g \, dA \, dz = 0$$ $$- dp \, dA - \rho g \, dA \, dz = 0$$ Dividing throughout by $dA$: $$- dp - \rho g \, dz = 0$$ $$\frac{dp}{dz} = -\rho g$$ Since the fluid is incompressible ($\rho$ = constant), we can integrate directly: $$\int_{p_0}^{p} dp = -\int_{h_0}^{h} \rho g \, dz$$ $$p - p_0 = -\rho g (h - h_0)$$ Taking the free surface as the reference ($p = p_0$ at $z = 0$, depth measured downward as $h = -z$): $$p = p_0 + \rho g h$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical interpretation:** The negative sign in $\frac{dp}{dz} = -\rho g$ indicates that pressure **decreases** in the upward direction and **increases** with depth — exactly what we expect intuitively. ## 3.2 Derivation: Total Pressure on a Vertically Submerged Surface Consider a vertical plane surface submerged in a static fluid, with its top edge at the free surface and bottom edge at depth $H$. **Step 1: Pressure at a depth $h$.** From the hydrostatic equation, the pressure at depth $h$ below the free surface is: $$p = \rho g h = \gamma h$$ **Step 2: Force on an elemental strip.** Consider a horizontal strip of width $b(h)$ at depth $h$, with thickness $dh$. The area of this strip is: $$dA = b(h) \, dh$$ The force on this strip (perpendicular to the surface) is: $$dF = p \, dA = \gamma h \, b(h) \, dh$$ **Step 3: Total force by integration.** $$F = \int_0^H \gamma h \, b(h) \, dh = \gamma \int_0^H h \, b(h) \, dh$$ **Step 4: Recognize the geometric meaning.** The integral $\int_0^H h \, b(h) \, dh$ is the **first moment of area** about the free surface, which equals $A \cdot \bar{h}$, where $A$ is the total area and $\bar{h}$ is the depth of the centroid. Therefore: $$F = \gamma \, \bar{h} \, A = \gamma h_c A$$ ## 3.3 Derivation: Depth of Center of Pressure (Vertical Surface) The center of pressure is the point where the resultant hydrostatic force acts. Its depth $h_p$ is found by taking moments about the free surface. **Step 1: Moment of elemental force about free surface.** The elemental force $dF = \gamma h \, b(h) \, dh$ acts at depth $h$, so its moment about the free surface is: $$dM = h \cdot dF = \gamma h^2 \, b(h) \, dh$$ **Step 2: Total moment.** $$M = \int_0^H \gamma h^2 \, b(h) \, dh = \gamma \int_0^H h^2 \, b(h) \, dh$$ **Step 3: Recognize the geometric meaning.** The integral $\int_0^H h^2 \, b(h) \, dh$ is the **second moment of area** about the free surface, denoted $I_{x,fs}$. **Step 4: Equate moments to find $h_p$.** By definition of center of pressure: $$F \cdot h_p = M$$ $$\gamma \bar{h} A \cdot h_p = \gamma \, I_{x,fs}$$ $$h_p = \frac{I_{x,fs}}{A \bar{h}}$$ **Step 5: Use the parallel axis theorem to express in terms of centroidal moment.** By the parallel axis theorem: $$I_{x,fs} = I_{xc} + A \bar{h}^2$$ Substituting: $$h_p = \frac{I_{xc} + A \bar{h}^2}{A \bar{h}} = \frac{I_{xc}}{A \bar{h}} + \bar{h}$$ **Step 6: Generalization to inclined surface.** For an inclined surface making angle $\theta$ with the horizontal, with the centroid at vertical depth $\bar{h}$: $$h_p = \frac{I_{xc} \sin^2\theta}{A \bar{h}} + \bar{h}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key observation:** The center of pressure is **always below the centroid** of the submerged surface (for surfaces fully below the free surface). The deeper the centroid, the closer $h_p$ and $\bar{h}$ become. ## 3.4 Derivation: Buoyancy (Archimedes' Principle) Consider a body of arbitrary shape fully submerged in a static fluid of density $\rho_f$. **Step 1: Consider an elemental column of the body.** An elementary horizontal slab of the body at depth $h$ has area $dA$ and thickness $dh$. The vertical forces on this slab are: - Pressure force from below (upward): $p_b \, dA = (p_0 + \gamma_f h) \, dA$ - Pressure force from above (downward): $p_t \, dA = (p_0 + \gamma_f (h + dh)) \, dA$ **Step 2: Net upward force on the slab.** $$dF_B = p_b \, dA - p_t \, dA = \gamma_f \, dh \, dA = \gamma_f \, d\mathcal{V}$$ where $d\mathcal{V} = dA \cdot dh$ is the elemental volume of the slab. **Step 3: Total buoyant force.** $$F_B = \int_{\mathcal{V}} \gamma_f \, d\mathcal{V} = \gamma_f \mathcal{V}$$ > [!NOTE] > **This is Archimedes' Principle:** The buoyant force on a submerged body equals the **weight of the fluid displaced** by the body. It acts vertically upward through the centroid of the displaced volume (center of buoyancy). --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Pressure Variation in a Static Fluid ```mermaid graph TD A[Free Surface
Pressure p0] --> B[Depth h1
Pressure p0 + gamma h1] B --> C[Depth h2
Pressure p0 + gamma h2] C --> D[Depth h3 Maximum
Pressure p0 + gamma h3] E[Atmospheric Pressure patm] --> F[Absolute vs Gauge] F --> G[p abs equals patm plus pgauge] F --> H[p gauge equals pabs minus patm] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a U-Tube Manometer ```mermaid graph LR A[Pipe A carrying fluid at Pressure pA] --> B[Left Limb of U Tube] D[Pipe B carrying fluid at Pressure pB] --> E[Right Limb of U Tube] B --> F[Manometer fluid denser than process fluid] E --> F F --> G[Level difference h between manometer fluid columns] G --> H[Compute pressure difference: pA minus pB equals rho m minus rho f times g times h] H --> I[Output pressure drop in pipeline] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Forces on a Curved Submerged Surface ```mermaid graph TD A[Curved Surface submerged in fluid] --> B[Resolve forces into two components] B --> C[Horizontal Force FH equals gamma hc A vertical projection] B --> D[Vertical Force FV equals weight of fluid above or imaginary fluid] C --> E[Resultant FR equals sqrt FH squared plus FV squared] D --> E E --> F[Angle alpha equals arctan FV divided by FH] F --> G[Apply to dam gates, lock walls, and pipe bends] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Stability of a Floating Body ```mermaid graph TD A[Floating Body] --> B[Weight W acts at Center of Gravity G] A --> C[Buoyancy FB acts at Center of Buoyancy B] A --> D[Tilted slightly, Buoyancy shifts to new B prime] D --> E[Vertical through B prime intersects original vertical at M Metacenter] E --> F[GM equals MB minus GB] F --> G{Is GM positive?} G -->|Yes| H[Stable Equilibrium] G -->|No| I[Unstable Equilibrium] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — 3 Mark Questions ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **State Pascal's law and mention any two of its engineering applications.** **Model Answer:** Pascal's Law states that *the pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible static fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished in all directions throughout the fluid, and acts perpendicular to the surfaces with which it is in contact.* **Engineering Applications:** 1. **Hydraulic Press / Hydraulic Jack:** A small force applied on a small-area piston produces a large force on a large-area piston (force multiplication). 2. **Hydraulic Brakes in Automobiles:** The brake pedal force is transmitted via brake fluid to brake shoes with equalized pressure at all wheels. 3. **Hydraulic Lift:** Used in vehicle service stations to lift cars using incompressible oil as the working fluid. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Distinguish between gauge pressure and absolute pressure with a suitable sketch.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Gauge Pressure | Absolute Pressure | |---|---|---| | Reference | Local atmospheric pressure | Perfect vacuum | | Zero level | $p_{atm}$ | $0$ Pa | | Formula | $p_g = p_{abs} - p_{atm}$ | $p_{abs} = p_{atm} + p_g$ | | Vacuum case | Negative value | Always positive | | Barometer reading | Zero at sea level | $101.325$ kPa at sea level | **Sketch description:** Both pressures are measured from different datum lines on the same vertical scale. Atmospheric pressure $p_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa is the dividing line; gauge pressure is measured below this line, absolute pressure is the sum of both. --- ## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set A] **(a)** Derive the hydrostatic pressure equation $p = p_0 + \rho g h$ for a static incompressible fluid. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A vertical rectangular gate 1.5 m wide and 2.0 m high is installed in a dam with its top edge 3.0 m below the water surface. Find (i) the total hydrostatic force on the gate, and (ii) the depth of the center of pressure. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution to Question A: **Part (a) — Derivation of Hydrostatic Pressure Equation:** Consider a small fluid element of cross-sectional area $dA$ and vertical thickness $dz$ in a static fluid. The forces acting on the element are: - Upward pressure force: $p \, dA$ (acting on the bottom face) - Downward pressure force: $(p + dp) \, dA$ (acting on the top face) - Downward body force (weight): $dW = \rho g \, dA \, dz$ Applying the equilibrium equation $\sum F_z = 0$: $$p \, dA - (p + dp) \, dA - \rho g \, dA \, dz = 0$$ Simplifying: $$-dp \, dA - \rho g \, dA \, dz = 0$$ Dividing by $dA$: $$\frac{dp}{dz} = -\rho g$$ For an incompressible fluid ($\rho$ = constant), integrating from the free surface (where $p = p_0$ at $z = 0$) to a depth $h$ (where $z = -h$): $$\int_{p_0}^{p} dp = \int_0^{-h} (-\rho g) \, dz$$ $$p - p_0 = \rho g h$$ $$\boxed{p = p_0 + \rho g h}$$ **Part (b) — Force on Vertical Rectangular Gate:** **Given:** - Width $b = 1.5$ m - Height $H = 2.0$ m - Top edge depth below water surface: $h_1 = 3.0$ m - Bottom edge depth: $h_2 = 3.0 + 2.0 = 5.0$ m **Step 1: Compute area and centroid depth.** $$A = b \times H = 1.5 \times 2.0 = 3.0 \text{ m}^2$$ $$\bar{h} = \frac{h_1 + h_2}{2} = \frac{3.0 + 5.0}{2} = 4.0 \text{ m}$$ **Step 2: Compute total hydrostatic force.** $$F = \gamma \, \bar{h} \, A = 9810 \times 4.0 \times 3.0$$ $$\boxed{F = 117{,}720 \text{ N} \approx 117.72 \text{ kN}}$$ **Step 3: Compute depth of center of pressure.** For a rectangle, $I_{xc} = \dfrac{b H^3}{12}$: $$I_{xc} = \frac{1.5 \times 2.0^3}{12} = \frac{1.5 \times 8}{12} = 1.0 \text{ m}^4$$ $$h_p = \frac{I_{xc}}{A \bar{h}} + \bar{h} = \frac{1.0}{3.0 \times 4.0} + 4.0$$ $$h_p = \frac{1.0}{12.0} + 4.0 = 0.0833 + 4.0$$ $$\boxed{h_p = 4.0833 \text{ m below the free surface}}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set B] **(a)** Explain the working principle of a U-tube differential manometer with a neat sketch. Derive the expression for the pressure difference. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A pipe carrying oil (specific gravity 0.85) is connected to a water U-tube manometer. The mercury level in the manometer shows a difference of 20 cm. The center of the pipe is 15 cm below the free oil surface in a tank. Find the pressure at the center of the pipe. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution to Question B: **Part (a) — U-Tube Differential Manometer:** A U-tube differential manometer consists of a U-shaped tube containing a heavier manometric fluid (commonly mercury, SG = 13.6) connected between two points A and B in a pipe carrying a lighter process fluid. The difference in heights of the manometric fluid in the two limbs gives the differential pressure. **Derivation:** Let: - $p_A$ = pressure at point A, $p_B$ = pressure at point B - $\rho_f$ = density of process fluid, $\rho_m$ = density of manometric fluid - $h$ = difference in mercury levels Starting from A and equating pressures at the lower mercury level (level 1-1): $$p_A + \rho_f g (h_1) = p_B + \rho_f g (h_2) + \rho_m g h$$ where $h_1 - h_2 = h$. Rearranging: $$p_A - p_B = \rho_m g h - \rho_f g h$$ $$\boxed{p_A - p_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h}$$ **Part (b) — Pressure in the Oil Pipe:** **Given:** - SG of oil = 0.85, so $\rho_{oil} = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - SG of mercury = 13.6, so $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - Mercury level difference $h = 20 \text{ cm} = 0.20 \text{ m}$ - Center of pipe is 15 cm below free oil surface, so $z = 0.15 \text{ m}$ **Setup:** The pipe is connected via the oil-filled limb to the manometer. The free oil surface is exposed to atmosphere, so $p_{surface} = p_{atm}$ (gauge = 0). **Apply manometer equation from oil surface to pipe center:** Starting at the free oil surface and going down to the pipe center, then across the manometer: $$p_{pipe} + \rho_{oil} g (0) - \rho_{Hg} g h + \rho_{oil} g (0.15 + h) = p_{atm}$$ Wait, let me set up the geometry carefully. The pipe center is 15 cm below the free oil surface. The manometer limb connects to the pipe. The mercury in the manometer has a 20 cm difference. **Standard approach (gauge pressures, $p_{atm} = 0$):** $$p_{pipe} = \rho_{Hg} g h - \rho_{oil} g (h - 0.15)$$ where the oil column from pipe center to top of mercury on the pipe side has height $(h - 0.15)$ if $h > 0.15$. $$p_{pipe} = (13600)(9.81)(0.20) - (850)(9.81)(0.20 - 0.15)$$ $$p_{pipe} = 26683.2 - 416.925$$ $$\boxed{p_{pipe} \approx 26{,}266.3 \text{ Pa (gauge)} = 26.27 \text{ kPa (gauge)}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Sign convention errors:** Students often forget the sign of $(h - 0.15)$ in the oil column subtraction. Always draw the manometer and trace the path from one point to the other, adding when going down and subtracting when going up. > 2. **Confusing gauge vs absolute:** If the question says "find the pressure" without specifying, give **gauge pressure**. If it says "absolute", add $p_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa. > 3. **Unit mismatch:** Always convert cm to m before computation. $h$ must be in meters. > 4. **Forgetting the parallel axis theorem:** When computing center of pressure for surfaces NOT starting at the free surface, students often use the wrong formula. Use $h_p = \dfrac{I_{xc}}{A \bar{h}} + \bar{h}$, where $\bar{h}$ is the depth of the centroid (NOT the depth of the top edge). > 5. **Specific weight vs density:** Use $\gamma = \rho g$ consistently; mixing $\rho$ and $\gamma$ in the same equation is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for Fluid Statics (Module 1):** - **Pressure:** Scalar quantity, SI unit Pa = N/m², and $1 \text{ atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$. - **Hydrostatic Law:** $p = p_0 + \rho g h$ — pressure increases linearly with depth. - **Three Pressure Scales:** Absolute, Gauge, Vacuum. Always identify which is asked. - **Pascal's Law:** Pressure transmitted undiminished — basis of hydraulic systems. - **Manometers:** - Piezometer: measures gauge pressure directly in a vertical tube. - U-tube manometer: $\Delta p = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$. - Inclined manometer: $\Delta p = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g L \sin\theta$ — used for small pressure differences. - **Total Hydrostatic Force on Plane Surface:** $F = \gamma h_c A$ — acts at the **center of pressure**. - **Center of Pressure Depth:** $h_p = \dfrac{I_{xc} \sin^2\theta}{A \bar{h}} + \bar{h}$ — always below centroid. - **Special Case — Vertical Rectangular Surface (top edge at surface):** $F = \dfrac{\gamma b H^2}{2}$ and $h_p = \dfrac{2H}{3}$. - **Curved Surfaces:** Resolve into $F_H$ (horizontal, on vertical projection) and $F_V$ (vertical, weight of fluid column). - **Buoyancy:** $F_B = \gamma_f \mathcal{V}_{displaced}$ — Archimedes' principle, acts upward through center of buoyancy. - **Floatation:** Body floats when weight = buoyant force, i.e., $\mathcal{V}_{submerged} / \mathcal{V}_{total} = \rho_{body} / \rho_{fluid}$. - **Stability:** Body is stable if **metacentric height $GM > 0$**, i.e., metacenter $M$ is **above** center of gravity $G$. - **Constants to memorize:** $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³, $\rho_{Hg} = 13600$ kg/m³, $\rho_{air} \approx 1.225$ kg/m³, SG of mercury = 13.6.

topics.title: Cartesian Tensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET413 - MECHANICS OF FLUID FLOW
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cartesian Tensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Cartesian Tensor?

A **Cartesian Tensor** is a mathematical object whose components transform in a specific, well-defined manner when the underlying Cartesian coordinate system is rotated or translated. Tensors provide a coordinate-independent language to express physical laws — a fact that makes them indispensable in continuum mechanics, fluid dynamics, and stress analysis.

In the most elementary sense, physical quantities are classified by their transformation behaviour:

| Quantity | Rank / Order | Transformation Law | Examples |
|:--------:|:------------:|:------------------:|:--------:|
| **Scalar** | **0** (Zero-order tensor) | Invariant under rotation | Pressure, Temperature, Density |
| **Vector** | **1** (First-order tensor) | $\bar{v}_i = a_{ij} v_j$ | Velocity, Force, Acceleration |
| **Tensor** | **2** or higher | $\bar{T}_{ij} = a_{ip} a_{jq} T_{pq}$ | Stress, Strain, Inertia |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must understand tensor notation, the summation convention, the Kronecker delta, the alternating (Levi-Civita) symbol, and how physical quantities like stress and strain rate are represented as second-order tensors.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are describing the **strength of wind** in a room.

- A **thermometer** reading is a *scalar* — it gives one number (say, 30 °C) regardless of where you stand.
- A **flag's flutter direction** is a *vector* — it needs both a magnitude (how strong) and a direction (which way it blows). If you rotate yourself 90°, the direction you report changes.
- A **piece of stretched rubber sheet** is a *tensor object* — you cannot describe it by a single arrow. It has different stiffnesses along different directions, and these stretch properties change in a coupled way when you rotate your viewpoint.

> [!NOTE]
> A tensor is essentially a **"machine for multilinear mapping"** — it takes one or more vectors as input and produces a scalar. The way its components shuffle under coordinate changes follows rigid, unambiguous algebraic rules.

## 1.3 Index Notation and the Summation Convention

**Einstein Summation Convention (ESC):** When an index appears twice in a term, summation over that index is implied. The range of summation is the dimensionality of space (usually 3).

$$v_i v_i = v_1 v_1 + v_2 v_2 + v_3 v_3 = v_1^2 + v_2^2 + v_3^2$$

- **Free index**: appears once in a term → indicates the component.
- **Dummy (repeated) index**: appears twice → summed over.
- An index cannot appear more than twice in the same term.

**Position of indices** matters in tensor algebra:
- $T_{ij}$ → covariant components (lower index)
- $T^{ij}$ → contravariant components (upper index)
- $T_i^{\;j}$ → mixed components

In a strict **Cartesian** system, there is no distinction between covariant and contravariant — they are identical. Hence $T_{ij} = T^{ij} = T_i^{\;j}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Kronecker Delta ($\delta_{ij}$)

The Kronecker delta is the fundamental second-order isotropic tensor:

$$\delta_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } i = j \\ 0 & \text{if } i \neq j \end{cases}$$

**Key Properties:**

1. **Index Substitution Property:** $\delta_{ij} A_j = A_i$

   $$\delta_{ij} A_j = \delta_{i1}A_1 + \delta_{i2}A_2 + \delta_{i3}A_3 = A_i$$

2. **Trace Property:** $\delta_{ii} = 3$ (in 3-D space)

3. **Symmetry:** $\delta_{ij} = \delta_{ji}$

4. **Invariance under rotation:** $\bar{\delta}_{ij} = \delta_{ij}$

5. **Higher-order relations:**
   - $\delta_{ij}\delta_{jk} = \delta_{ik}$ (chain substitution)
   - $\delta_{ij}\delta_{ij} = \delta_{ii} = 3$

## 2.2 The Levi-Civita Symbol (Alternating Symbol $e_{ijk}$)

A third-order pseudo-tensor defined as:

$$e_{ijk} = \begin{cases} +1 & \text{if } (i,j,k) \text{ is an even permutation of } (1,2,3) \\ -1 & \text{if } (i,j,k) \text{ is an odd permutation of } (1,2,3) \\ 0 & \text{if any two indices are equal} \end{cases}$$

**Even permutations:** (1,2,3), (2,3,1), (3,1,2) → $+1$
**Odd permutations:** (1,3,2), (3,2,1), (2,1,3) → $-1$

**Key Properties:**

1. **Cyclic Permutation:** $e_{ijk} = e_{jki} = e_{kij}$ (cyclic, even)
2. **Anticlockwise Swap:** $e_{ijk} = -e_{jik} = -e_{ikj}$ (transposition)
3. **Cross Product:** $(A \times B)_i = e_{ijk} A_j B_k$
4. **Curl in Index Form:** $(\nabla \times \vec{V})_i = e_{ijk} \dfrac{\partial V_k}{\partial x_j}$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **core toolkit** for solving any KTU Module 1 problem on Cartesian tensors. Memorize these and their application patterns.

| **Operation / Identity** | **Formula** | **Application** |
|:------------------------|:-----------:|:----------------|
| Summation convention | Repeated index = sum | Simplifies dot/cross products |
| Kronecker delta definition | $\delta_{ij} = 1$ if $i=j$, else $0$ | Index replacement, isotropic tensors |
| Substitution rule | $\delta_{ij}A_j = A_i$ | Eliminates dummy indices |
| Trace identity | $\delta_{ii} = n$ (n=3) | Scalar extraction from tensors |
| Levi-Civita even | $e_{123} = e_{231} = e_{312} = +1$ | Cross products, curl operator |
| Levi-Civita odd | $e_{132} = e_{321} = e_{213} = -1$ | Determinants, area vectors |
| Cross product | $(A \times B)_i = e_{ijk} A_j B_k$ | Angular velocity, vorticity |
| Curl | $(\nabla \times \vec{V})_i = e_{ijk} \dfrac{\partial V_k}{\partial x_j}$ | Vorticity in fluid flow |
| Divergence of vector | $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = \dfrac{\partial V_i}{\partial x_i}$ | Continuity equation |
| Vector transformation | $\bar{v}_i = a_{ij} v_j$ | Rotation of basis |
| Tensor transformation | $\bar{T}_{ij} = a_{ip} a_{jq} T_{pq}$ | 2nd-rank tensor rotation |
| Orthogonality of $a_{ij}$ | $a_{ip} a_{jp} = \delta_{ij}$ | Inverse transformation |
| $e-\delta$ identity | $e_{ijk} e_{lmk} = \delta_{il}\delta_{jm} - \delta_{im}\delta_{jl}$ | Simplifying products of $e$ |
| Permutation identity | $e_{ijk} e_{ijk} = 6$ | Determinant identity |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cartesian tensors are not abstract gymnastics — they directly underpin:

- **Stress Tensor in Fluids:** The viscous stress $\tau_{ij}$ relates to velocity gradients via $\tau_{ij} = \mu\left(\dfrac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} + \dfrac{\partial u_j}{\partial x_i}\right) - \dfrac{2}{3}\mu(\nabla \cdot \vec{V})\delta_{ij}$.
- **Strain-Rate Tensor:** $e_{ij} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} + \dfrac{\partial u_j}{\partial x_i}\right)$.
- **Vorticity:** $\omega_i = e_{ijk}\dfrac{\partial u_k}{\partial x_j}$ — central to rotational flow analysis.
- **Navier-Stokes Equation:** Incompressible form $\rho\left(\dfrac{\partial u_i}{\partial t} + u_j\dfrac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j}\right) = -\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x_i} + \mu\dfrac{\partial^2 u_i}{\partial x_j \partial x_j} + \rho f_i$.

> [!NOTE]
> The index form of the Navier-Stokes equation (3 scalar equations packed into 1 tensor equation) is **exactly the kind of elegant compression** that KTU examiners reward with full marks in Part B answers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Orthogonality of Direction Cosines

Let $a_{ij}$ denote the direction cosine between the new $x_i'$ axis and the old $x_j$ axis. That is, $a_{ij} = \cos\theta_{ij} = \hat{e}_i' \cdot \hat{e}_j$.

**Step 1:** The transformation of a position vector is:
$$\bar{x}_i = x_j \cos\theta_{ij} = a_{ij} x_j$$

**Step 2:** Since rotation preserves length:
$$x_i x_i = \bar{x}_i \bar{x}_i$$

**Step 3:** Substituting $\bar{x}_i = a_{ij} x_j$ on the right side:
$$x_i x_i = (a_{ij} x_j)(a_{ik} x_k)$$

**Step 4:** Relabel dummy indices on the RHS so the first factor uses $j$ and second uses $k$:
$$\bar{x}_i \bar{x}_i = a_{ij} a_{ik} x_j x_k$$

**Step 5:** For this to equal $x_j x_j$ (LHS), we require:
$$a_{ij} a_{ik} = \delta_{jk}$$

**Conclusion:** $a_{ij} a_{ik} = \delta_{jk}$ is the **orthogonality condition** of the rotation tensor.

## 3.2 Derivation: Transformation of a Second-Order Tensor

A second-order tensor $T_{ij}$ transforms as a bilinear object.

**Step 1:** A vector transforms as $\bar{v}_i = a_{im} v_m$.

**Step 2:** Consider a tensor defined by the dyadic product of two vectors: $T_{ij} = u_i v_j$.

**Step 3:** Transform each factor:
$$\bar{u}_i = a_{im} u_m, \quad \bar{v}_j = a_{jn} v_n$$

**Step 4:** Compute the transformed dyadic:
$$\bar{T}_{ij} = \bar{u}_i \bar{v}_j = (a_{im} u_m)(a_{jn} v_n) = a_{im} a_{jn} u_m v_n$$

**Step 5:** Recognise that $u_m v_n = T_{mn}$:
$$\bar{T}_{ij} = a_{im} a_{jn} T_{mn}$$

**Conclusion:** A second-order tensor transforms via **two rotation factors** (one per index).

## 3.3 Derivation: The $e-\delta$ Contraction Identity

We wish to prove:
$$e_{ijk} e_{lmk} = \delta_{il}\delta_{jm} - \delta_{im}\delta_{jl}$$

**Step 1:** Both sides are antisymmetric in $(i,j)$ and in $(l,m)$, and symmetric under simultaneous swap $(i \leftrightarrow j,\; l \leftrightarrow m)$.

**Step 2:** Set $i = j$. The LHS becomes $e_{iik} = 0$. The RHS becomes $\delta_{il}\delta_{im} - \delta_{im}\delta_{il} = 0$. ✓

**Step 3:** Set $l = m$. The LHS becomes $e_{ijk} e_{ljk} = e_{ijk} e_{ljk}$. Since $e_{ljk} = e_{jkl}$ (cyclic), and $e_{ijk} e_{jkl} = 2\delta_{il}$ (this is a known sub-identity), the LHS becomes $2\delta_{il}$. The RHS becomes $\delta_{il}\delta_{jj} - \delta_{ij}\delta_{jl}$. Using $\delta_{jj} = 3$ and $\delta_{ij}\delta_{jl} = \delta_{il}$: RHS $= 3\delta_{il} - \delta_{il} = 2\delta_{il}$. ✓

**Step 4:** The most general scalar built from $e_{ijk}$ and two free index pairs $(i,j)$ and $(l,m)$ with the right symmetry is $\alpha \delta_{il}\delta_{jm} + \beta \delta_{im}\delta_{jl}$. Antisymmetry in $i,j$ requires $\alpha = -\beta$, giving $\alpha(\delta_{il}\delta_{jm} - \delta_{im}\delta_{jl})$.

**Step 5:** Choose a single case to fix $\alpha$: $i=1, j=2, l=1, m=2$.

LHS: $e_{12k} e_{12k} = e_{123} e_{123} = (1)(1) = 1$.

RHS: $\alpha(\delta_{11}\delta_{22} - \delta_{12}\delta_{21}) = \alpha(1 \cdot 1 - 0) = \alpha$.

Therefore $\alpha = 1$, and the identity is proven.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Simplifying a Triple Cross Product

**Problem:** Express $\vec{A} \times (\vec{B} \times \vec{C})$ in index form and simplify.

**Step 1:** Write the inner cross product first:
$$(B \times C)_m = e_{mnp} B_n C_p$$

**Step 2:** Write the full cross product:
$$[A \times (B \times C)]_i = e_{ijk} A_j (B \times C)_k = e_{ijk} A_j e_{kpq} B_p C_q$$

**Step 3:** Note the repeated $k$ allows rearrangement:
$$[A \times (B \times C)]_i = e_{ijk} e_{kpq} A_j B_p C_q$$

**Step 4:** Use the cyclic property $e_{kpq} = e_{pqk}$ to apply the $e-\delta$ identity:
$$e_{ijk} e_{pqk} = \delta_{ip}\delta_{jq} - \delta_{iq}\delta_{jp}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute:
$$[A \times (B \times C)]_i = (\delta_{ip}\delta_{jq} - \delta_{iq}\delta_{jp}) A_j B_p C_q$$

**Step 6:** Apply index substitution ($\delta_{ip} B_p = B_i$, $\delta_{jq} A_j = A_q$, etc.):
$$[A \times (B \times C)]_i = B_i A_q C_q - C_i A_j B_j$$

**Step 7:** Recognise $A_q C_q = \vec{A} \cdot \vec{C}$ and $A_j B_j = \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}$:
$$\vec{A} \times (\vec{B} \times \vec{C}) = \vec{B}(\vec{A} \cdot \vec{C}) - \vec{C}(\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B})$$

This is the famous **BAC-CAB rule** — derived purely from tensor identities.

## 3.5 Worked Example: Strain-Rate Tensor in a 2-D Flow

**Given:** Velocity field $u_1 = x_2$, $u_2 = -x_1$, $u_3 = 0$.

**Step 1:** Strain rate tensor definition:
$$e_{ij} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} + \frac{\partial u_j}{\partial x_i}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Compute the velocity gradient matrix:
$$\frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** Symmetrise:
$$e_{ij} = \frac{1}{2}\begin{pmatrix} 0+0 & 1+(-1) & 0 \\ 1+(-1) & 0+0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** The strain rate tensor is zero. This is **pure rigid-body rotation** (vorticity $\omega_3 = -1 \neq 0$, but no deformation). Tensor calculus cleanly distinguishes rotation from deformation — a result unattainable with vector calculus alone.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchy of Tensor Objects

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Quantity] --> B[Scalar Rank 0]
    A --> C[Vector Rank 1]
    A --> D[Tensor Rank 2]
    A --> E[Tensor Rank N]
    B --> B1[Pressure, Temperature, Density]
    B --> B2["Transform: invariant"]
    C --> C1[Velocity, Force, Acceleration]
    C --> C2["Transform: bar v_i = a_ij v_j"]
    D --> D1[Stress, Strain Rate, Inertia]
    D --> D2["Transform: bar T_ij = a_ip a_jq T_pq"]
    D --> D3[Symmetric vs Skew-symmetric]
    D3 --> D31[Strain Rate Symmetric]
    D3 --> D32[Vorticity Skew-symmetric]
    E --> E1[Third and Higher Rank]
    E --> E2["Used in elasticity, piezoelectricity"]
```

    GRAD --> DECOMP[Symmetric plus Skew Decomposition]

    DECOMP --> S2[Vorticity Tensor omega_ij Skew]

    B --> B2[Curl curl V subscript i equals e_ijk partial V_k over partial x_j]

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define a Cartesian tensor of rank two. State the transformation law for a second-order tensor under rotation of axes.**

**Model Answer:**

A Cartesian tensor of rank two is a physical or mathematical entity that, in a given 3-D Cartesian coordinate system, is represented by nine components $T_{ij}$ ($i,j = 1,2,3$), and which transforms under a rotation of axes according to the law:

$$\bar{T}_{ij} = a_{ip}\, a_{jq}\, T_{pq}$$

where $a_{ij} = \cos\theta_{ij}$ is the direction cosine between the new $x_i'$ axis and the old $x_j$ axis. Examples in fluid mechanics are the stress tensor $\tau_{ij}$ and the strain-rate tensor $e_{ij}$. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Explain the significance of the Kronecker delta $\delta_{ij}$ as an isotropic tensor.**

**Model Answer:**

The Kronecker delta $\delta_{ij}$ is a second-order tensor whose components are $1$ when $i = j$ and $0$ when $i \neq j$. Its significance as an isotropic tensor is that its components remain unchanged under any rotation of axes, i.e., $\bar{\delta}_{ij} = \delta_{ij}$. This makes it the fundamental tool for **index substitution** ($\delta_{ij}A_j = A_i$), for constructing isotropic tensors, and for performing the **trace operation** ($\delta_{ii} = 3$ in 3-D). It is the unique second-order isotropic tensor. **(3 marks)**

---

## **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** State and explain the Einstein summation convention. Using it, write down the index form of:
   (i) the dot product of two vectors $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$
   (ii) the curl of a vector field $\vec{V}$
   (iii) the Laplacian of a scalar field $\phi$.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define the Levi-Civita symbol $e_{ijk}$. Prove the identity $e_{ijk} e_{lmk} = \delta_{il}\delta_{jm} - \delta_{im}\delta_{jl}$.  **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Einstein Summation Convention:** When an index appears twice in a term, it is understood to be summed over its range (here $1$ to $3$). For example:
$$A_i B_i = \sum_{i=1}^{3} A_i B_i = A_1 B_1 + A_2 B_2 + A_3 B_3$$

An index that appears only once is a **free index** (indicates component); one that appears twice is a **dummy index** (summed). No index may appear more than twice in any term.

**(i) Dot product:**
$$\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B} = A_i B_i$$

**(ii) Curl:** Using $e_{ijk}$ for the alternating symbol:
$$(\nabla \times \vec{V})_i = e_{ijk} \frac{\partial V_k}{\partial x_j}$$

**(iii) Laplacian of a scalar:**
$$\nabla^2 \phi = \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x_i \partial x_i} = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x_i}\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x_i}\; \text{(summed)}$$

Or more directly, $\nabla^2 \phi = \dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x_i \partial x_i}$. **(7 marks — 2 for ESC definition, 1.5 each for the three sub-parts)**

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Definition:** The Levi-Civita symbol $e_{ijk}$ is defined as:
$$e_{ijk} = \begin{cases} +1 & \text{for cyclic permutations of } (1,2,3) \\ -1 & \text{for anti-cyclic permutations of } (1,2,3) \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Proof of the identity:**

*Step 1 — Symmetry analysis.* The LHS is antisymmetric in $(i,j)$ (swapping $i \leftrightarrow j$ changes sign of $e_{ijk}$ but leaves $e_{lmk}$ unchanged). It is also antisymmetric in $(l,m)$. It vanishes whenever $i = j$ or $l = m$.

*Step 2 — Most general form.* A tensor with two index pairs having this symmetry must be of the form:
$$\alpha(\delta_{il}\delta_{jm} - \delta_{im}\delta_{jl})$$

*Step 3 — Fix $\alpha$.* Set $i=1, j=2, l=1, m=2$:
- LHS: $e_{12k}e_{12k} = e_{123}e_{123} = 1$
- RHS: $\alpha(\delta_{11}\delta_{22} - \delta_{12}\delta_{21}) = \alpha(1)(1) = \alpha$

Therefore $\alpha = 1$ and the identity is:
$$\boxed{e_{ijk} e_{lmk} = \delta_{il}\delta_{jm} - \delta_{im}\delta_{jl}}$$

**(7 marks — 1 for definition, 3 for symmetry analysis, 3 for fixing $\alpha$)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Derive the transformation law for a second-order Cartesian tensor under a rotation of axes. Hence show that the Kronecker delta is an isotropic tensor.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a velocity field $u_1 = 3x_1 + x_2$, $u_2 = x_1 + 2x_2$, $u_3 = 0$, determine:
   (i) the strain-rate tensor $e_{ij}$
   (ii) the vorticity vector $\vec{\omega}$
   (iii) the rate of dilatation $\nabla \cdot \vec{u}$  **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

Consider a second-order tensor defined by a dyadic product of two vectors: $T_{ij} = u_i v_j$.

**Step 1 — Vector transformation:** Under a rotation, a vector transforms as:
$$\bar{u}_i = a_{im} u_m, \quad \bar{v}_j = a_{jn} v_n$$

**Step 2 — Dyadic transformation:**
$$\bar{T}_{ij} = \bar{u}_i \bar{v}_j = (a_{im} u_m)(a_{jn} v_n) = a_{im} a_{jn} (u_m v_n)$$

**Step 3 — Identification:** $u_m v_n = T_{mn}$, hence:
$$\bar{T}_{ij} = a_{im} a_{jn} T_{mn}$$

This is the transformation law for a second-order Cartesian tensor. The generalisation to any rank-$N$ tensor is by induction. **(4 marks)**

**Step 4 — Kronecker delta is isotropic:** A tensor $S_{ij}$ is isotropic if $\bar{S}_{ij} = S_{ij}$ for all rotations. Test:
$$\bar{\delta}_{ij} = a_{im} a_{jn} \delta_{mn} = a_{im} a_{jm} = \delta_{ij}$$

The last equality is the orthogonality condition. Hence $\bar{\delta}_{ij} = \delta_{ij}$ — isotropic. **(3 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Velocity gradient matrix:**
$$\frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

**(i) Strain-rate tensor:**
$$e_{ij} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} + \frac{\partial u_j}{\partial x_i}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\begin{pmatrix} 6 & 2 & 0 \\ 2 & 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

**(ii) Vorticity vector:** First compute the antisymmetric part:
$$\Omega_{ij} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} - \frac{\partial u_j}{\partial x_i}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix} = 0$$

The flow is **irrotational** — vorticity vector is $\vec{\omega} = (0, 0, 0)$.

*(Verification using $e_{ijk}$: $\omega_k = e_{kij}\dfrac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j}$. Only non-zero component potentially: $\omega_3 = e_{312}(\partial u_1/\partial x_2 - \partial u_2/\partial x_1) = (1)(1 - 1) = 0$.)*

**(iii) Rate of dilatation:**
$$\nabla \cdot \vec{u} = \frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_i} = 3 + 2 + 0 = 5 \;\; \text{s}^{-1}$$

**(7 marks — 3 for (i), 2 for (ii), 2 for (iii))**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Index overflow:** Writing an index three times in one term (e.g., $A_i B_i C_i$) — the examiner deducts **2 marks immediately** for violating the summation convention.
> 2. **Confusing covariant/contravariant:** In Cartesian systems, lower and upper indices are interchangeable. Mixing them up loses **1 mark** in transformations.
> 3. **Forgetting symmetry of $e_{ijk}$:** Students often forget to specify which permutations are $+1$ vs $-1$. Always state both cases explicitly.
> 4. **Missing the chain rule on $e_{ijk}e_{lmk}$:** The dummy index being summed is $k$ (the *third* in the first, *third* in the second). Many students mistakenly sum over the wrong position.
> 5. **Strain-rate sign errors:** The off-diagonal components are $e_{12} = e_{21} = \tfrac{1}{2}(\partial u_1/\partial x_2 + \partial u_2/\partial x_1)$, **not** just one term. Half-marks if you forget the symmetrisation.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Cartesian Tensors (Module 1)**

- **Rank classification:** Rank 0 = scalar, Rank 1 = vector, Rank 2 = tensor, Rank $N$ general.
- **Einstein Summation Convention:** Repeated index = sum; one free index = component.
- **Kronecker delta** $\delta_{ij}$: identity matrix, isotropic, $\delta_{ij}A_j = A_i$, $\delta_{ii} = 3$.
- **Levi-Civita symbol** $e_{ijk}$: $+1$ for even, $-1$ for odd, $0$ for repeated; antisymmetric.
- **Cross product in index form:** $(A \times B)_i = e_{ijk} A_j B_k$.
- **Curl in index form:** $(\nabla \times \vec{V})_i = e_{ijk} \dfrac{\partial V_k}{\partial x_j}$.
- **Divergence in index form:** $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = \partial V_i / \partial x_i$.
- **Vector transformation:** $\bar{v}_i = a_{ij} v_j$.
- **Tensor transformation:** $\bar{T}_{ij} = a_{ip} a_{jq} T_{pq}$.
- **Orthogonality:** $a_{ip} a_{jp} = \delta_{ij}$.
- **Identity to memorise:** $e_{ijk} e_{lmk} = \delta_{il}\delta_{jm} - \delta_{im}\delta_{jl}$.
- **Trace property:** $e_{ijk} e_{ijk} = 6$ and $\delta_{ii} = 3$.
- **Strain-rate tensor:** $e_{ij} = \tfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} + \dfrac{\partial u_j}{\partial x_i}\right)$ — symmetric.
- **Vorticity tensor:** $\Omega_{ij} = \tfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} - \dfrac{\partial u_j}{\partial x_i}\right)$ — skew-symmetric.
- **Dilatation:** $\nabla \cdot \vec{u} = e_{ii} = \partial u_i / \partial x_i$.
- **Cartesian = no distinction** between covariant and contravariant components.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When a question asks "show that tensor $T_{ij}$ is isotropic," always substitute the transformation law and reduce using the orthogonality condition. This is a guaranteed 3-mark sub-question in most KTU papers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fluid Kinematics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET413 - MECHANICS OF FLUID FLOW
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fluid Kinematics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fluid Kinematics** is the branch of fluid mechanics that studies the motion of fluids (liquids and gases) **without considering the forces causing the motion**. It deals purely with the description of fluid motion through geometric and kinematic parameters such as **velocity**, **acceleration**, **streamlines**, **pathlines**, and **streaklines**.

In simpler KTU board terminology, fluid kinematics answers the question: *"How is the fluid moving?"* — and explicitly **does NOT** answer *"Why is the fluid moving?"* (the latter belongs to **Fluid Dynamics / Kinetics**).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Highway Camera

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine installing a CCTV camera on the side of a National Highway.
> - **Kinematics** (Fluid Kinematics): The camera records the *trajectories* of cars, their *velocities*, and *accelerations*. It does not know or care about engine torque, friction from the road, or aerodynamic drag.
> - **Dynamics** (Fluid Kinetics): A mechanical engineer would analyze the *engine force*, *brake force*, and *air resistance* acting on each car.
>
> Fluid Kinematics is the **"CCTV recording"** of fluid flow — it describes what the fluid is doing, not what is making it do so.

## 1.3 Eulerian vs. Lagrangian Descriptions

There are two fundamentally different ways to describe fluid motion in kinematics:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Lagrangian Approach:** Tracks a *specific fluid particle* as it moves through space and time, much like following a single marked car on the highway. The variables are functions of the *initial position* of the particle and time $t$.
> - **Eulerian Approach (Preferred in KTU):** Observes fluid motion at *fixed points in space* through which fluid particles pass, like the CCTV camera. The variables (velocity, pressure) are functions of *spatial coordinates* $(x, y, z)$ and time $t$.
>
> **Engineering Preference:** Almost all KTU problems and real-world CFD (Computational Fluid Dynamics) codes use the **Eulerian** description because it is mathematically simpler to handle.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard gravity** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (used when converting pressure to head).
- **Density of water** $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (reference value for incompressible flow).
- **Dynamic viscosity of water** $\mu_{water} = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ at 20°C.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Fluid Flow

The KTU syllabus demands mastery of the following flow classifications. They are **not mutually exclusive** — a single flow can be both *steady* and *laminar* and *incompressible*.

### 2.1.1 Steady vs. Unsteady Flow

- **Steady Flow:** Fluid properties at any fixed point do **not** change with time.
$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial t}\bigg|_{fixed\ point} = 0$$
- **Unsteady Flow:** Fluid properties at a fixed point *do* change with time. Examples: a **dam break**, **pulsating blood flow**, **oscillating water in a U-tube**.

### 2.1.2 Uniform vs. Non-Uniform Flow

- **Uniform Flow:** Velocity is *constant in magnitude and direction* along the flow path (i.e., $\frac{\partial V}{\partial s} = 0$).
- **Non-Uniform Flow:** Velocity *changes* along the flow path. Example: flow through a **converging nozzle**.

### 2.1.3 Laminar vs. Turbulent Flow

- **Laminar Flow:** Fluid particles move in *smooth, parallel layers* with no mixing between layers. Reynolds number $Re < 2000$ in pipe flow.
- **Turbulent Flow:** Fluid particles move in *irregular, chaotic paths* with significant mixing. $Re > 4000$ in pipe flow.

### 2.1.4 Compressible vs. Incompressible Flow

- **Incompressible:** Density $\rho =$ constant. Valid for liquids and low-speed gases (Mach number $M < 0.3$).
- **Compressible:** $\rho$ varies. Important in high-speed aerodynamics, gas pipelines.

### 2.1.5 Rotational vs. Irrotational Flow

- **Rotational Flow:** Fluid elements *rotate* about their own axes as they move. Characterized by non-zero vorticity $\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V} \neq 0$.
- **Irrotational Flow:** Fluid elements translate *without* rotation. $\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$.

## 2.2 The Three Fundamental Flow Visualization Lines

| Line Type | Definition | Governing Equation / Property |
|---|---|---|
| **Streamline** | A curve whose tangent at every point is parallel to the *instantaneous velocity vector* at that point. | $\dfrac{dx}{u} = \dfrac{dy}{v} = \dfrac{dz}{w}$ |
| **Pathline** | The actual *trajectory* traced by a single fluid particle over time. | Lagrangian concept |
| **Streakline** | The locus of all particles that have *previously passed* through a given point. | Visualization by dye injection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Fact:** For **steady flow**, *streamlines*, *pathlines*, and *streaklines* are **identical**. This is a guaranteed 2- or 3-mark question.

## 2.3 Velocity Field, Acceleration Field & Material Derivative

The **Eulerian velocity field** is:
$$\vec{V}(x, y, z, t) = u\,\hat{i} + v\,\hat{j} + w\,\hat{k}$$

The **acceleration** is the time rate of change of velocity **following a fluid particle** (NOT at a fixed point). The chain rule for the Eulerian description gives the **material (substantial) derivative**:

$$\vec{a} = \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + ( \vec{V} \cdot \nabla )\vec{V}$$

Expanded in 3D Cartesian coordinates:

$$a_x = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z}$$

$$a_y = \frac{\partial v}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial v}{\partial z}$$

$$a_z = \frac{\partial w}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial w}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial w}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial w}{\partial z}$$

- **Local acceleration** $\partial \vec{V}/\partial t$: rate of change at a fixed point (zero for steady flow).
- **Convective acceleration** $(\vec{V}\cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$: change due to particle's motion to a new location (zero for uniform flow).

## 2.4 Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass)

The integral form, valid for any control volume, is:
$$\frac{d}{dt}\int_{CV} \rho\, d\forall + \int_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n})\, dA = 0$$

For an **incompressible flow** ($\rho =$ constant), the differential form simplifies to the **divergence-free condition**:
$$\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0$$

## 2.5 Stream Function (2D Incompressible Flow)

For 2D incompressible flow, a scalar function $\psi(x, y)$ exists such that:
$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \qquad v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

This automatically satisfies the 2D continuity equation. Lines of constant $\psi$ are **streamlines**. The **volumetric flow rate** between two streamlines $\psi_1$ and $\psi_2$ is:
$$Q = \psi_2 - \psi_1$$

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Units / Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Streamline equation | $dx/u = dy/v = dz/w$ | Geometric locus |
| 2 | Material derivative | $\dfrac{D}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial t} + u\dfrac{\partial}{\partial x} + v\dfrac{\partial}{\partial y} + w\dfrac{\partial}{\partial z}$ | $\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 3 | Local acceleration | $\partial \vec{V} / \partial t$ | $\text{m/s}^2$ |
| 4 | Convective acceleration | $(\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V}$ | $\text{m/s}^2$ |
| 5 | Continuity (general) | $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla\cdot(\rho\vec{V}) = 0$ | $\text{kg/(m}^3\cdot\text{s)}$ |
| 6 | Continuity (incompressible) | $\nabla\cdot\vec{V} = 0$ | $\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 7 | Continuity (1D pipe) | $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 8 | Vorticity | $\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V}$ | $\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 9 | Stream function (2D) | $u = \partial\psi/\partial y,\ v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| 10 | Velocity potential (2D, irrotational) | $u = \partial\phi/\partial x,\ v = \partial\phi/\partial y$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| 11 | Relation between $\phi$ and $\psi$ | $\nabla^2\phi = \nabla^2\psi = 0$ (Laplace equation) | Harmonic functions |
| 12 | Reynolds number | $Re = \rho V D / \mu$ | Dimensionless |
| 13 | Volumetric flow rate (1D) | $Q = A V$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 14 | Mass flow rate | $\dot{m} = \rho A V$ | $\text{kg/s}$ |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Aerospace Engineering:** Streamlines around an aircraft wing are visualized and computed to maximize lift-to-drag ratio.
> - **Civil / Hydraulic Engineering:** Continuity equation is used to design **water supply networks**, **spillways**, and **weirs** of dams.
> - **Chemical Engineering:** Stream functions and velocity potentials are used to analyze flow in **packed-bed reactors** and **heat exchangers**.
> - **Biomedical Engineering:** Blood flow (a non-Newtonian fluid) is analyzed using kinematic equations to detect **aneurysms** and **stenosis** in arteries.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Material (Substantial) Derivative

We begin with the Eulerian velocity field, which gives velocity as a function of space and time:
$$\vec{V} = \vec{V}(x, y, z, t)$$

For a *specific fluid particle* that is moving, its spatial coordinates also change with time:
$$x = x(t), \quad y = y(t), \quad z = z(t)$$

We wish to find the time rate of change of velocity **following the particle**, i.e., the *total* derivative $D\vec{V}/Dt$. By the multivariate chain rule of calculus:

$$\frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial x}\frac{dx}{dt} + \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial y}\frac{dy}{dt} + \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial z}\frac{dz}{dt}$$

We know that $dx/dt = u$, $dy/dt = v$, and $dz/dt = w$ — these are the *velocity components* of the particle itself. Substituting:

$$\frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial z}$$

This is often written compactly using the *del* operator $\nabla$ and the dot product:

$$\boxed{\frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}}$$

**Interpretation:**
- $\partial \vec{V}/\partial t$ → **local** (unsteadiness) acceleration.
- $(\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V}$ → **convective** (spatial) acceleration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important derivation** in KTU Module 1. Marks are awarded for distinguishing *local* and *convective* terms in the final expression.

## 3.2 Derivation: Continuity Equation in Differential Form (Incompressible Flow)

**Step 1 — Control Volume:** Consider an infinitesimal rectangular fluid element of dimensions $dx, dy, dz$ centered at point $(x, y, z)$.

**Step 2 — Mass Flux in x-direction:**
Mass entering the *left* face (at $x - dx/2$) per unit time:
$$\dot{m}_{in, x} = \rho u \cdot (dy\,dz)$$

Mass leaving the *right* face (at $x + dx/2$) per unit time:
$$\dot{m}_{out, x} = \left[ \rho u + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x}\,dx \right] (dy\,dz)$$

**Step 3 — Net Mass Accumulation in x-direction:**
$$\Delta \dot{m}_x = \dot{m}_{in, x} - \dot{m}_{out, x} = -\frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x}\,dx\,dy\,dz$$

**Step 4 — Repeat for y- and z-directions:**
$$\Delta \dot{m}_y = -\frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y}\,dx\,dy\,dz$$
$$\Delta \dot{m}_z = -\frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z}\,dx\,dy\,dz$$

**Step 5 — Conservation of Mass:** The total net mass flux into the control volume equals the rate of increase of mass within:
$$\Delta \dot{m}_x + \Delta \dot{m}_y + \Delta \dot{m}_z = \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}\,dx\,dy\,dz$$

**Step 6 — Substitute and Divide** by $dx\,dy\,dz$:
$$-\frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}$$

**Step 7 — General Form (Rearranged):**
$$\boxed{\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0}$$

**Step 8 — Incompressible Flow:** Since $\rho =$ constant, $\partial \rho/\partial t = 0$ and $\rho$ factors out:
$$\boxed{\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad \nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Continuity Equation in 1D Pipe Flow

For a single pipe of varying cross-section, consider a control volume bounded by sections 1 and 2.

**Mass flow rate at section 1:** $\dot{m}_1 = \rho_1 A_1 V_1$

**Mass flow rate at section 2:** $\dot{m}_2 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$

For steady flow, mass entering = mass leaving, so:
$$\rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$$

For incompressible flow ($\rho_1 = \rho_2 = \rho$):
$$\boxed{A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q = \text{constant}}$$

This proves the intuitive fact that **fluid velocity increases as the cross-section decreases** (e.g., water speeds up through a narrowed nozzle).

## 3.4 Derivation: Stream Function Relationship for 2D Incompressible Flow

Starting from the 2D continuity equation:
$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$$

We postulate the existence of a stream function $\psi(x, y)$ such that the velocity components are *exact partial derivatives* of $\psi$:
$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \qquad v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

Substituting into the continuity equation:
$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(-\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}\right) = \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x \partial y} - \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y \partial x} = 0$$

The two mixed partial derivatives are equal (Clairaut's theorem) for any smooth function. Therefore, the substitution is **always valid** for any 2D incompressible flow. The total differential of $\psi$ along a streamline is:

$$d\psi = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}dx + \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}dy = -v\,dx + u\,dy$$

But by definition of a streamline, $dy/dx = v/u$, which gives $u\,dy - v\,dx = 0$, hence $d\psi = 0$. This proves that **streamlines are curves of constant $\psi$**, a beautiful and important result.

## 3.5 Acceleration Along a Streamline

For a fluid particle moving along a streamline, the tangential direction is $s$. The component of acceleration along $s$ is:

$$a_s = \frac{\partial V}{\partial t} + V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s} = \frac{\partial V}{\partial t} + \frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial (V^2)}{\partial s}$$

The first term is the local acceleration. The second term can be written using the chain rule:
$$V\frac{\partial V}{\partial s} = \frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial (V^2)}{\partial s}$$

This form is *extremely* useful when deriving **Euler's and Bernoulli's equations** in the next module.
