# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | Module 1, Q1]**
> **Q1. Distinguish between Lagrangian and Eulerian descriptions of fluid flow. Which one is preferred in fluid mechanics and why?**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> - **Lagrangian description** studies fluid motion by tracking *individual fluid particles* and recording how their positions and velocities change with time. The independent variables are the initial coordinates $(a, b, c)$ of the particle and time $t$. It is analogous to following a single car on a highway.
> - **Eulerian description** studies fluid motion by observing fluid properties at *fixed points in space* through which fluid particles flow. The independent variables are the spatial coordinates $(x, y, z)$ and time $t$. It is analogous to a CCTV camera mounted on a highway overpass.
> - **Preference:** The **Eulerian** approach is preferred in fluid mechanics because:
>   1. It is mathematically simpler to handle, especially for deriving differential equations.
>   2. Most practical engineering instruments (pitot tubes, anemometers, pressure gauges) measure fluid properties at fixed points, which directly matches the Eulerian framework.
>   3. Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) codes are inherently Eulerian.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | Module 1, Q2]**
> **Q2. With the help of neat sketches, differentiate between streamlines, streaklines, and pathlines. State their relationship for steady flow.**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> - **Streamline:** A curve whose tangent at any point is parallel to the *instantaneous velocity vector* at that point. Equation: $dx/u = dy/v = dz/w$. It represents the *direction of motion* of multiple particles at a single instant.
> - **Pathline:** The *trajectory* (the actual path) traced by a *single fluid particle* over a period of time. It is fundamentally a *Lagrangian* concept.
> - **Streakline:** The locus of *all particles* that have previously passed through a given fixed point in space. It is generated experimentally by continuously injecting dye at a fixed location.
> - **For Steady Flow:** Because the velocity at any point does not change with time, a fluid particle's velocity at a given location remains the same. Therefore, **streamlines, pathlines, and streaklines coincide exactly**. This is a guaranteed KTU result.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | Module 1, Q3]**
>
> ### **Question A (14 Marks)**
>
> **(a)** Derive the general continuity equation in differential form for a 3D, unsteady, compressible fluid flow. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** A 2D velocity field is given by $\vec{V} = (3x + 2y)\hat{i} + (2x - 3y)\hat{j}\ \text{m/s}$. Determine:
>   (i) Whether the flow is physically possible (verify continuity).
>   (ii) Whether the flow is rotational or irrotational (compute the z-component of vorticity).
>   (iii) The stream function $\psi(x, y)$. (7 Marks)
>
> ---
>
> **Model Solution:**
>
> **(a) Derivation of the General Continuity Equation:**
> Consider an infinitesimal rectangular fluid element of dimensions $dx, dy, dz$ centered at point $(x, y, z)$.
>
> **Step 1:** Mass flow entering the left face (at $x - dx/2$) per unit time:
> $$\dot{m}_{x,in} = \rho u \cdot dy\, dz$$
>
> **Step 2:** Mass flow leaving the right face (at $x + dx/2$) per unit time, expanded using Taylor series:
> $$\dot{m}_{x,out} = \left[\rho u + \frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x}dx\right] dy\, dz$$
>
> **Step 3:** Net mass outflow in x-direction:
> $$\Delta \dot{m}_x = \frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x}\, dx\, dy\, dz$$
>
> **Step 4:** By symmetry, the net mass outflows in y and z directions are:
> $$\Delta \dot{m}_y = \frac{\partial(\rho v)}{\partial y}\, dx\, dy\, dz$$
> $$\Delta \dot{m}_z = \frac{\partial(\rho w)}{\partial z}\, dx\, dy\, dz$$
>
> **Step 5:** Total net rate of mass efflux from the control volume:
> $$\sum \Delta \dot{m} = \left[\frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial(\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial(\rho w)}{\partial z}\right] dx\, dy\, dz$$
>
> **Step 6:** This net efflux must equal the rate of decrease of mass inside the CV (conservation of mass):
> $$\left[\frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial(\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial(\rho w)}{\partial z}\right] dx\, dy\, dz = -\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}\, dx\, dy\, dz$$
>
> **Step 7:** Dividing by $dx\,dy\,dz$ and rearranging:
> $$\boxed{\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial(\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial(\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0}$$
>
> In vector notation: $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$.
>
> **(b) Verification of the 2D Velocity Field:**
> Given: $u = 3x + 2y$ and $v = 2x - 3y$ (in m/s).
>
> **(i) Continuity check** (incompressible, 2D):
> $$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial(3x + 2y)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial(2x - 3y)}{\partial y}$$
> $$= 3 + (-3) = 0$$
>
> Since the divergence is **zero**, the flow *satisfies* the incompressible continuity equation. The flow is **physically possible**.
>
> **(ii) Rotational or irrotational?**
> The z-component of vorticity is:
> $$\omega_z = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial(2x - 3y)}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial(3x + 2y)}{\partial y}$$
> $$= 2 - 2 = 0$$
>
> Since $\omega_z = 0$, the flow is **irrotational**.
>
> **(iii) Stream function:**
> We use: $u = \dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}$ and $v = -\dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$.
>
> From $u$:
> $$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = 3x + 2y \quad \Rightarrow \quad \psi = 3xy + y^2 + f(x)$$
>
> Differentiating with respect to $x$ and applying the second condition:
> $$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = 3y + f'(x) = -v = -(2x - 3y) = -2x + 3y$$
>
> Therefore: $f'(x) = -2x$, which integrates to $f(x) = -x^2 + C$.
>
> $$\boxed{\psi(x, y) = -x^2 + 3xy + y^2 + C\ \ \text{m}^2/\text{s}}$$

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | Module 1, Q3 — Alternative]**
>
> ### **Question B (14 Marks)**
>
> **(a)** Starting from the Eulerian velocity field, derive the expression for the **material (substantial) derivative** of velocity. Clearly identify and explain the physical meaning of each term. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** The velocity field for a 2D incompressible flow is given by $\psi(x, y) = 3x^2 y - y^3$ m²/s.
>   (i) Determine the velocity components $u$ and $v$.
>   (ii) Show that the flow is irrotational.
>   (iii) Calculate the volumetric flow rate $Q$ across the streamline $\psi = 0$ and the streamline $\psi = 6$ m²/s. (7 Marks)
>
> ---
>
> **Model Solution:**
>
> **(a) Derivation of the Material Derivative:**
> In the Eulerian framework, the velocity field is:
> $$\vec{V} = \vec{V}(x, y, z, t)$$
> where the spatial coordinates of a fluid particle are themselves functions of time: $x = x(t)$, $y = y(t)$, $z = z(t)$.
>
> The total time rate of change of velocity *following the particle* is the **material derivative** $D\vec{V}/Dt$. Applying the chain rule for a function of multiple variables:
> $$\frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial x}\frac{dx}{dt} + \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial y}\frac{dy}{dt} + \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial z}\frac{dz}{dt}$$
>
> Recognizing that $dx/dt = u$, $dy/dt = v$, $dz/dt = w$ (the velocity components of the particle), we substitute:
> $$\boxed{\frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V}\cdot \nabla)\vec{V}}$$
>
> **Physical meaning of the two terms:**
> 1. **Local acceleration** $\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t}$: This is the rate of change of velocity at a *fixed point in space*. It is non-zero *only for unsteady flows*. Example: oscillating water level in a U-tube.
> 2. **Convective acceleration** $(\vec{V}\cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$: This is the acceleration experienced by a particle *purely because it moves* to a new location where the velocity is different. It is non-zero *only for non-uniform flows*. Example: fluid speeding up through a converging nozzle.
>
> **(b) Velocity Components from Stream Function:**
> Given: $\psi(x, y) = 3x^2 y - y^3$.
>
> **(i) Velocity components:**
> $$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = 3x^2 - 3y^2$$
> $$v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = -6xy$$
>
> **(ii) Check irrotationality:**
> $$\omega_z = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = -6y - (-6y) = -6y + 6y = 0$$
>
> Hence the flow is **irrotational** everywhere.
>
> **(iii) Volumetric flow rate between streamlines:**
> For 2D incompressible flow, the volumetric flow rate per unit depth between two streamlines is:
> $$Q = \psi_2 - \psi_1$$
>
> Substituting $\psi_1 = 0$ and $\psi_2 = 6$ m²/s:
> $$Q = 6 - 0 = 6\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}\ \text{per unit depth}$$
>
> In SI units of flow rate (per unit depth, since this is 2D), $Q = 6$ m²/s. (If total depth is given, multiply by depth.)

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Easy Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing local and convective acceleration:** The biggest trap in Material Derivative questions. Always explicitly state: "Local = zero for steady; Convective = zero for uniform."
> 2. **Forgetting the negative sign in $v = -\partial \psi / \partial x$:** Examiners *love* to deduct 1 mark here. Memorize: $u = +\partial \psi/\partial y$ and $v = -\partial \psi/\partial x$.
> 3. **Mixing up the continuity equation forms:** Writing the *incompressible* form $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ for a *compressible* problem. Always check the problem statement for the word "incompressible" or any density variation.
> 4. **Forgetting the Clairaut symmetry** in stream function derivation: The equality $\partial^2 \psi/\partial x \partial y = \partial^2 \psi/\partial y \partial x$ is *the reason* stream functions work for 2D incompressible flow. Examiners will specifically look for this reasoning.
> 5. **Streamline equation sign errors:** Forgetting the $+$ sign in $u\,dy = v\,dx$ and getting the wrong sign of the slope. Always derive from $dy/dx = v/u$ first.
> 6. **Missing units in final answer:** Velocity in m/s, flow rate in m³/s, stream function in m²/s, vorticity in s⁻¹. Examiners *do* deduct for missing units.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fluid Kinematics (Module 1)**
>
> ✅ **Core philosophy:** Kinematics = *how* fluid moves. Dynamics = *why* fluid moves.
>
> ✅ **Two descriptions:** Lagrangian (particle-tracking) vs. Eulerian (field-based). Engineering prefers **Eulerian**.
>
> ✅ **6 flow classifications:** Steady/Unsteady, Uniform/Non-uniform, Laminar/Turbulent/Transitional, Rotational/Irrotational, Compressible/Incompressible, 1D/2D/3D. They are **independent** axes.
>
> ✅ **3 visualization lines:** Streamline ($dx/u = dy/v = dz/w$), Pathline (single particle trail), Streakline (dye locus). **All three coincide for steady flow.**
>
> ✅ **Material derivative formula (MUST MEMORIZE):**
> $$\frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V}$$
>
> ✅ **Local acceleration = 0 for steady flow.**
> ✅ **Convective acceleration = 0 for uniform flow.**
>
> ✅ **Continuity equation:**
> - General 3D: $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$
> - Incompressible: $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$
> - 1D pipe: $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ (assuming $\rho =$ constant)
>
> ✅ **Stream function relations (2D incompressible):**
> $$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \quad v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}, \quad Q = \psi_2 - \psi_1$$
>
> ✅ **Irrotational condition:** $\omega_z = \partial v/\partial x - \partial u/\partial y = 0$
>
> ✅ **Reynolds number transitions:** $Re < 2000 \to$ Laminar, $2000 \le Re \le 4000 \to$ Transitional, $Re > 4000 \to$ Turbulent.
>
> ✅ **Sign convention for $v$ in stream function** is the *single most-failed* derivation step — practice it.
>
> ✅ **Continuity MUST be satisfied** before a velocity field is declared physically possible. Always check first.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Description of fluid motion - Types of motion of fluid elements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET413 - MECHANICS OF FLUID FLOW
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Description of fluid motion - Types of motion of fluid elements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Description of Fluid Motion

The **description of fluid motion** refers to the systematic mathematical framework used to track and characterize how a fluid (liquid or gas) moves through space and time. In continuum mechanics, a fluid is treated as a continuous medium composed of infinitesimal **fluid particles** (or material points), each carrying properties such as velocity, pressure, density, and temperature.

There are two classical approaches to describe fluid motion:

| Approach | Focus | Tracking Entity |
|----------|-------|-----------------|
| **Lagrangian Description** | History of individual particles | Follows a *specific* fluid particle |
| **Eulerian Description** | Field at fixed locations in space | Observes what happens at a *fixed point* |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Eulerian approach is overwhelmingly preferred in engineering practice because it leads to tractable partial differential equations (Navier–Stokes, Euler, Continuity). The Lagrangian approach is dominant in **Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD)** with particle-tracking methods and in solid mechanics.

## 1.2 The Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are standing on a bridge over a flowing river:

- **Lagrangian (Tag-Along Method):** You drop a floating leaf into the river and follow it with binoculars, recording its speed, direction, and acceleration as it travels downstream. You are tracking a *specific parcel of water*.
- **Eulerian (Traffic-Camera Method):** You fix a camera at one spot on the bridge and record the velocity of every water particle that passes *through that spot* at different times. You are recording a *velocity field* over space and time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** In real engineering problems (pipe flow, open channel flow, aerodynamics), we usually place sensors at fixed locations (pressure taps, Pitot tubes, hot-wire anemometers). The data we obtain is therefore inherently **Eulerian**, even though physically we often *think* in Lagrangian terms. Bridging this gap is precisely what the **Material Derivative** (Section 2) does.

## 1.3 Types of Fluid Motion — Broad Classification

A fluid element can exhibit different categories of motion depending on the imposed boundary conditions, geometry, and forcing. The principal classifications are:

1. **Steady vs. Unsteady Flow**
2. **Uniform vs. Non-Uniform Flow**
3. **Laminar vs. Turbulent Flow**
4. **Compressible vs. Incompressible Flow**
5. **Rotational vs. Irrotational Flow**
6. **One-, Two-, Three-Dimensional Flow**
7. **Viscous vs. Inviscid (Ideal) Flow**

## 1.4 Motion of a Fluid Element

When a small fluid element (a tiny blob of fluid of infinitesimal size) moves through a flow field, it generally undergoes three fundamental kinematic motions simultaneously:

1. **Translation** — pure movement of the centroid from one point to another.
2. **Rotation (Rigid Body Rotation)** — spinning of the element about its own centroid.
3. **Deformation** — change of *shape* and *volume* of the element.
   - **Linear Deformation (Stretching):** along principal axes.
   - **Angular Deformation (Shearing):** change of angle between originally perpendicular edges.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why It Matters:** The rotation of a fluid element defines whether a flow is **rotational** (vorticity ≠ 0) or **irrotational** (vorticity = 0). This single property decides whether a **velocity potential exists** and simplifies the entire Navier–Stokes equation in many practical cases (e.g., flow around airfoils).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Lagrangian Description (Particle Tracking)

We label each fluid particle at time $t = 0$ by its initial position vector $\vec{a} = (a_1, a_2, a_3)$. The position of that *same* particle at any later time $t$ is:

$$\vec{r} = \vec{r}(\vec{a}, t)$$

In Cartesian components:
$$x = x(a_1, a_2, a_3, t), \quad y = y(a_1, a_2, a_3, t), \quad z = z(a_1, a_2, a_3, t)$$

The **velocity** and **acceleration** of the labelled particle are then obtained by *partial* differentiation with respect to $t$ (holding $\vec{a}$ fixed):

$$\vec{V}_L = \left(\frac{\partial \vec{r}}{\partial t}\right)_{\vec{a}}, \qquad \vec{a}_L = \left(\frac{\partial^2 \vec{r}}{\partial t^2}\right)_{\vec{a}}$$

## 2.2 Eulerian Description (Field Description)

Here, the kinematic quantities are written as **fields** — functions of position $\vec{r}$ and time $t$:

$$\vec{V} = \vec{V}(\vec{r}, t), \qquad P = P(\vec{r}, t), \qquad \rho = \rho(\vec{r}, t)$$

The independent variables are the spatial coordinates $(x, y, z)$ and time $t$. The velocity field is the foundation for the entire Navier–Stokes framework.

## 2.3 The Material (Substantial) Derivative

The bridge between Lagrangian and Eulerian viewpoints. It gives the **time rate of change of any property** as experienced by a particular fluid particle moving through an Eulerian field.

For any scalar property $\phi(\vec{r}, t)$:
$$\frac{D\phi}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\phi$$

For the velocity vector (acceleration of a fluid particle):
$$\boxed{\;\vec{a} = \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \underbrace{\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t}}_{\text{Local (unsteady)}} + \underbrace{(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}}_{\text{Convective}}\;}$$

### Physical Interpretation of Each Term

| Term | Name | Physical Meaning |
|------|------|------------------|
| $\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t}$ | Local Acceleration | Change of velocity *at a fixed point* with time |
| $(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$ | Convective Acceleration | Change of velocity *because the particle moves* into a new region |

## 2.4 Streamlines, Pathlines, and Streaklines

These three fundamental curves form the *geometric backbone* of fluid-flow visualisation.

### 2.4.1 Streamline
A line that is **everywhere tangent to the instantaneous velocity vector**. It represents the *snapshot* of the flow at one instant.

Mathematically, in 2D:
$$\frac{dx}{u} = \frac{dy}{v} = \frac{dz}{w}$$

### 2.4.2 Pathline
The actual **trajectory** of a specific fluid particle. It is the *Lagrangian* identity of the particle over time.

### 2.4.3 Streakline
The locus of **all particles that have previously passed through a given fixed point** (think of dye continuously injected at one point in a flow).

### 2.4.4 Key Identity
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For *Steady Flow*:** Streamlines, Pathlines, and Streaklines **coincide**. For *Unsteady Flow*, they are generally three distinct curves.

## 2.5 Classification of Fluid Motion — Detailed

### 2.5.1 Steady vs. Unsteady Flow
$$\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Steady}$$

### 2.5.2 Uniform vs. Non-Uniform Flow
$$\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial s} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Uniform (along a streamline)}$$

### 2.5.3 Laminar vs. Turbulent Flow
Defined by the **Reynolds Number**:
$$Re = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \frac{V L}{\nu}$$

- $Re < 2000$ in pipe flow → **Laminar** (smooth, layered)
- $Re > 4000$ in pipe flow → **Turbulent** (chaotic, mixing)

### 2.5.4 Compressible vs. Incompressible Flow
$$\frac{D\rho}{Dt} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Incompressible}$$

### 2.5.5 Rotational vs. Irrotational Flow
Defined by the **Vorticity Vector**:
$$\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V}$$

If $\vec{\omega} = \vec{0}$ everywhere → **Irrotational flow** (a velocity potential $\phi$ exists with $\vec{V} = \nabla\phi$).

## 2.6 Motion of a Fluid Element — Mathematical Decomposition

Consider an infinitesimal rectangular fluid element of dimensions $dx, dy, dz$. After a small time $dt$, the element may:

### (i) Translate
The centroid simply moves from $(x, y, z)$ to $(x + V_x dt, \; y + V_y dt, \; z + V_z dt)$.

### (ii) Rotate (Angular Velocity of the Element)
The angular velocity about the $z$-axis is the average of angular velocities of two perpendicular edges:
$$\omega_z = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial V_y}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial V_x}{\partial y}\right)$$

Generalising, the **vorticity vector** is twice the angular velocity:
$$\boxed{\;\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V} = 2\vec{\Omega}_{element}\;}$$

Components:
$$\omega_x = \frac{\partial V_z}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial V_y}{\partial z}, \quad \omega_y = \frac{\partial V_x}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial V_z}{\partial x}, \quad \omega_z = \frac{\partial V_y}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial V_x}{\partial y}$$

### (iii) Linear Deformation (Volumetric Strain Rate)
$$\epsilon_{xx} = \frac{\partial V_x}{\partial x}, \quad \epsilon_{yy} = \frac{\partial V_y}{\partial y}, \quad \epsilon_{zz} = \frac{\partial V_z}{\partial z}$$

The **volumetric dilatation rate** (rate of change of volume per unit volume) is the divergence of velocity:
$$\frac{1}{V}\frac{DV}{Dt} = \nabla \cdot \vec{V}$$

### (iv) Angular Deformation (Shear Strain Rate)
$$\epsilon_{xy} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial V_y}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial V_x}{\partial y}\right), \quad \text{(similarly for } yz, zx\text{)}$$

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Condition / Use |
|---|---------|-------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Material Derivative (Scalar) | $\dfrac{D\phi}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + (\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\phi$ | Rate following a particle |
| 2 | Material Derivative (Vector) | $\dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V}$ | Particle acceleration |
| 3 | Streamline Equation | $\dfrac{dx}{u} = \dfrac{dy}{v} = \dfrac{dz}{w}$ | Instantaneous flow direction |
| 4 | Vorticity | $\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V}$ | Rotation indicator |
| 5 | Irrotationality | $\nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$ | Velocity potential exists |
| 6 | Velocity Potential | $\vec{V} = \nabla\phi$ | Exists iff flow is irrotational |
| 7 | Dilatation Rate | $\nabla \cdot \vec{V}$ | Volumetric expansion |
| 8 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \dfrac{V L}{\nu}$ | Laminar/Turbulent |
| 9 | Mach Number | $Ma = \dfrac{V}{c}$ | Compressible indicator |
| 10 | Steady Flow | $\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} = 0$ | Time-invariant field |
| 11 | Uniform Flow | $\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial s} = 0$ along stream | Spatial invariance |
| 12 | Incompressibility | $\dfrac{D\rho}{Dt} = 0$ | Constant density |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The classification framework above is the *gateway* to solving real problems. For example, in the design of an aircraft wing, engineers assume **steady, irrotational, incompressible, inviscid flow** at high $Re$, enabling the use of potential-flow theory and giving a tractable analytical solution.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Material Derivative

**Aim:** Obtain the rate of change of a scalar property $\phi$ following a fluid particle, given that $\phi = \phi(\vec{r}, t)$.

**Step 1 — Eulerian Representation**
A fluid particle at time $t$ occupies position $\vec{r}(t) = (x(t), y(t), z(t))$. The scalar property as measured by an Eulerian observer at that point and time is:
$$\phi = \phi(x, y, z, t)$$

**Step 2 — Chain Rule for Total Time Derivative**
Since $\phi$ depends on $t$ both directly and through the moving coordinates $(x(t), y(t), z(t))$:
$$\frac{d\phi}{dt} = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\frac{dx}{dt} + \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}\frac{dy}{dt} + \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z}\frac{dz}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Particle Velocities**
By definition, $\dfrac{dx}{dt} = V_x,\; \dfrac{dy}{dt} = V_y,\; \dfrac{dz}{dt} = V_z$. Hence:
$$\frac{d\phi}{dt} = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + V_x\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} + V_y\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} + V_z\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z}$$

**Step 4 — Compact Vector Form**
Recognising the gradient operator $\nabla = \hat{i}\partial/\partial x + \hat{j}\partial/\partial y + \hat{k}\partial/\partial z$, we obtain:
$$\frac{D\phi}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + (\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\phi$$

**Step 5 — Vector Form for Acceleration**
Apply to $\phi = \vec{V}$ component-wise:
$$\vec{a} = \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V}$$

This is the celebrated expression of fluid-particle acceleration. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Vorticity from Element Rotation

**Aim:** Show that the $z$-component of vorticity is twice the angular velocity of a fluid element about the $z$-axis.

**Step 1 — Initial Configuration**
Consider a 2D square fluid element of side $dx, dy$ centred at $(x_0, y_0)$.

**Step 2 — Deformation After Time $dt$**
- Point A $(x_0, y_0)$ moves to A' with velocity $(V_x, V_y)$.
- Point B $(x_0 + dx, y_0)$ moves to B' with velocity $\left(V_x + \dfrac{\partial V_x}{\partial x}dx,\; V_y + \dfrac{\partial V_y}{\partial x}dx\right)$.
- Point D $(x_0, y_0 + dy)$ moves to D' with velocity $\left(V_x + \dfrac{\partial V_x}{\partial y}dy,\; V_y + \dfrac{\partial V_y}{\partial y}dy\right)$.

**Step 3 — Compute Angles of Rotation**

The edge AB rotates by an angle:
$$\alpha = \frac{\text{transverse displacement of B}}{\text{length of AB}} = \frac{\left(\dfrac{\partial V_y}{\partial x}\right)dx \cdot dt}{dx} = \frac{\partial V_y}{\partial x}dt$$

The edge AD rotates by an angle:
$$\beta = \frac{\text{transverse displacement of D}}{\text{length of AD}} = \frac{\left(\dfrac{\partial V_x}{\partial y}\right)dy \cdot dt}{dy} = \frac{\partial V_x}{\partial y}dt$$

**Step 4 — Net Rotation of the Element**
Since AB and AD rotate in *opposite senses*, the angular velocity about the $z$-axis is the average:
$$\Omega_z = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial V_y}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial V_x}{\partial y}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Vorticity Relation**
By definition, $\omega_z = \dfrac{\partial V_y}{\partial x} - \dfrac{\partial V_x}{\partial y}$, hence:
$$\omega_z = 2\,\Omega_z \quad\Rightarrow\quad \vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V} = 2\vec{\Omega}_{element} \quad\blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 Convective Acceleration in Cartesian Components

**Aim:** Expand $(\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V}$ in full to make its physical meaning clear.

**Step 1 — Operator Expansion**
$$(\vec{V}\cdot\nabla) = V_x\frac{\partial}{\partial x} + V_y\frac{\partial}{\partial y} + V_z\frac{\partial}{\partial z}$$

**Step 2 — Apply to Each Velocity Component**

For the $x$-component:
$$(\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)V_x = V_x\frac{\partial V_x}{\partial x} + V_y\frac{\partial V_x}{\partial y} + V_z\frac{\partial V_x}{\partial z}$$

Similarly for $y$ and $z$. Therefore the convective acceleration vector is:
$$\vec{a}_{conv} = \begin{bmatrix} V_x\dfrac{\partial V_x}{\partial x} + V_y\dfrac{\partial V_x}{\partial y} + V_z\dfrac{\partial V_x}{\partial z} \\[4pt] V_x\dfrac{\partial V_y}{\partial x} + V_y\dfrac{\partial V_y}{\partial y} + V_z\dfrac{\partial V_y}{\partial z} \\[4pt] V_x\dfrac{\partial V_z}{\partial x} + V_y\dfrac{\partial V_z}{\partial y} + V_z\dfrac{\partial V_z}{\partial z} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Vector Identity Useful in Inviscid Flow**
Using the identity $(\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V} = \nabla\!\left(\dfrac{V^2}{2}\right) - \vec{V}\times(\nabla\times\vec{V})$ we can write the **Euler equation** in the elegant Bernoulli-friendly form used throughout Module 2. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Material Derivative in a Converging Nozzle

**Problem:** A 2D converging nozzle has the velocity field:
$$\vec{V} = (u, v) = \left(\dfrac{U_0\,x}{L},\; -\dfrac{U_0\,y}{L}\right)$$

Find the $x$-component of acceleration of a fluid particle using the material derivative.

**Step 1 — Local (Unsteady) Term**
Since the field has no explicit $t$-dependence:
$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Convective Term**
$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = \frac{U_0}{L}, \qquad \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 0$$

Hence:
$$(\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)u = u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \left(\frac{U_0 x}{L}\right)\left(\frac{U_0}{L}\right) + 0 = \frac{U_0^{\,2}\,x}{L^2}$$

**Step 3 — Material Acceleration in $x$**
$$a_x = \frac{Du}{Dt} = 0 + \frac{U_0^{\,2}\,x}{L^2} = \frac{U_0^{\,2}\,x}{L^2}$$

**Step 4 — Verification of Incompressibility**
$$\nabla\cdot\vec{V} = \frac{U_0}{L} - \frac{U_0}{L} = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

The flow is incompressible. The element accelerates as it approaches the exit because it enters regions of higher velocity. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decoupling Convective vs Local Acceleration

| Situation | $\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t}$ | $(\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\vec{V}$ | Which dominates? |
|-----------|-------------------|------------------------------|------------------|
| Steady flow around a wing | $=0$ | $\neq 0$ | Convective only |
| Flow after sudden valve closure | $\neq 0$ | Can be small | Local only |
| Pulsatile arterial blood flow | $\neq 0$ | $\neq 0$ | Both comparable |
| Ocean current at fixed location | $=0$ | $=0$ | Trivial (uniform steady) |
| Vortex shedding in cylinder wake | $\neq 0$ | $\neq 0$ | Both — fully unsteady |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Lagrangian vs Eulerian Frame — Conceptual Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FLUID MOTION DESCRIPTION] --> B[Lagrangian Approach]
    A --> C[Eulerian Approach]
    B --> B1[Label each particle at t = 0]
    B1 --> B2[Track position r at t as a function of initial label a]
    B2 --> B3[Compute velocity by partial derivative wrt t at constant a]
    B3 --> B4[Use case: Particle tracking CFD, Solid Mechanics analogy]
    C --> C1[Define field V at each spatial point x]
    C1 --> C2[Record property as function of x and t]
    C2 --> C3[Compute acceleration using Material Derivative]
    C3 --> C4[Use case: Navier Stokes equations, Pipe flow, Aerodynamics]
    B2 -.Bridge.-> C2
    C3 -.Bridge.-> B3
```

## 4.2 Streamline, Pathline, Streakline Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SteadyFlow[STEADY FLOW]
        S1[Streamline]
        S2[Pathline]
        S3[Streakline]
        S1 --> S4[All three curves COINCIDE]
        S2 --> S4
        S3 --> S4
    end
    subgraph UnsteadyFlow[UNSTEADY FLOW]
        U1[Streamline: instantaneous tangent snapshot]
        U2[Pathline: actual particle trajectory]
        U3[Streakline: locus of all particles through fixed point]
        U1 --> U5[Three DISTINCT curves in general]
        U2 --> U5
        U3 --> U5
    end
```

## 4.3 Kinematics of a Fluid Element — Deformation Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E[FLUID ELEMENT MOTION] --> T[1. TRANSLATION]
    E --> R[2. ROTATION]
    E --> D[3. DEFORMATION]
    T --> T1[Centroid moves from r to r plus V dt]
    R --> R1[Angular velocity Omega equals half of curl V]
    R1 --> R2[Vorticity omega equals curl V equals 2 Omega]
    D --> D1[Linear Stretch along axes]
    D --> D2[Angular Shear between edges]
    D1 --> D1a[Strain rate tensor: partial V_i / partial x_j]
    D2 --> D2a[Shear strain rate: 1/2 of dV_i/dx_j plus dV_j/dx_i]
    R2 --> K[Vorticity zero IRROTATIONAL FLOW]
    R2 --> L[Vorticity nonzero ROTATIONAL FLOW]
```

## 4.4 Material Derivative — Logical Breakdown

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[MATERIAL DERIVATIVE D/dt] --> M1[LOCAL TERM]
    M --> M2[CONVECTIVE TERM]
    M1 --> M1A[partial phi by partial t]
    M1A --> M1B[Change at FIXED point due to time]
    M2 --> M2A[V dot grad phi]
    M2A --> M2B[Change because particle MOVES to new region]
    M1B --> M3[Sum gives TOTAL rate following the particle]
    M2B --> M3
```

## 4.5 Classification Taxonomy — Fluid Motion Types

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    F[FLUID MOTION TYPES] --> F1[Steady vs Unsteady]
    F --> F2[Uniform vs Non Uniform]
    F --> F3[Laminar vs Turbulent]
    F --> F4[Compressible vs Incompressible]
    F --> F5[Rotational vs Irrotational]
    F --> F6[1D 2D 3D Flow]
    F --> F7[Viscous vs Inviscid]
    F1 --> F1A[Criteria: partial V by partial t]
    F3 --> F3A[Criteria: Reynolds number Re]
    F4 --> F4A[Criteria: Mach number Ma or D rho by Dt]
    F5 --> F5A[Criteria: curl V equals zero or nonzero]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagrams above provide the structural relationships between concepts without requiring physical drawings. Each box represents a *concept node* and arrows depict the *dependency / derivation flow*, as recommended for analytical fluid mechanics courses.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Differentiate between Lagrangian and Eulerian descriptions of fluid motion. *(Commonly Asked)* `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**
The **Lagrangian description** tracks individual fluid particles by labelling them with their initial position $\vec{a}$ and following them through space and time; the position vector is $\vec{r} = \vec{r}(\vec{a}, t)$. It is analogous to recording the journey of a particular water parcel.

The **Eulerian description** observes fluid properties as fields at fixed spatial locations, e.g., $\vec{V} = \vec{V}(x, y, z, t)$. It is analogous to recording data from a fixed sensor in the flow.

Key distinction: Lagrangian uses the *initial label* $\vec{a}$ as the independent variable; Eulerian uses the *current position* $\vec{r}$. In engineering, the Eulerian approach dominates because the governing equations (continuity, Navier–Stokes) become tractable partial differential equations in space–time.

### Q2. Define vorticity. State the condition for a flow to be irrotational. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
**Vorticity** is defined as the curl of the velocity field:
$$\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V}$$

It is a vector quantity that measures twice the local angular velocity of a fluid element. A flow is **irrotational** if the vorticity vanishes everywhere in the flow domain:
$$\nabla \times \vec{V} = \vec{0}$$

In such cases, a scalar **velocity potential** $\phi$ exists such that $\vec{V} = \nabla\phi$, which drastically simplifies analysis of inviscid, incompressible flows.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Internal Choice Provision: KTU 2024 Scheme ESE mandates that Part B questions for Module 1 offer an internal choice. Two fully independent alternatives are presented below.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the material derivative of a scalar property $\phi(x, y, z, t)$ following a fluid particle. Explain the physical significance of each term with a suitable example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the velocity field $\vec{V} = (2x)\hat{i} + (3y)\hat{j} + (5tz)\hat{k}$ m/s, determine: **(i)** whether the flow is steady or unsteady, **(ii)** whether the flow is rotational or irrotational, **(iii)** the acceleration of a fluid particle at the point $(1, 1, 1)$ m at $t = 1$ s. **(7 Marks)**

#### Solution to Q A (a):

Starting from the Eulerian form $\phi = \phi(x(t), y(t), z(t), t)$, applying the chain rule:

$$\frac{D\phi}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\frac{dx}{dt} + \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}\frac{dy}{dt} + \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z}\frac{dz}{dt}$$

Substituting particle velocities $dx/dt = u$, $dy/dt = v$, $dz/dt = w$:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{D\phi}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + (\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\phi\;}$$

**Significance:**
- **Local term** $\partial\phi/\partial t$: time rate of change at a fixed location — e.g., pulsatile arterial flow.
- **Convective term** $(\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)\phi$: rate of change as the particle moves through spatial gradients — e.g., acceleration of water entering a narrowing pipe (the flow is steady, $\partial V/\partial t = 0$, but acceleration is nonzero due solely to convection).

#### Solution to Q A (b):

**Step 1 — Steady or Unsteady?**
Since $\vec{V}$ depends explicitly on $t$ (term $5tz$), $\partial \vec{V}/\partial t \neq 0$. **Flow is unsteady.**

**Step 2 — Rotational or Irrotational?**

Compute vorticity components:
$$\omega_x = \frac{\partial V_z}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial V_y}{\partial z} = 0 - 0 = 0$$
$$\omega_y = \frac{\partial V_x}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial V_z}{\partial x} = 0 - 0 = 0$$
$$\omega_z = \frac{\partial V_y}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial V_x}{\partial y} = 0 - 0 = 0$$

Since $\vec{\omega} = \vec{0}$ everywhere, the flow is **irrotational**.

**Step 3 — Acceleration at $(1, 1, 1)$ m and $t = 1$ s:**

Local term:
$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial t} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial w}{\partial t} = 5z = 5 \times 1 = 5$$

Convective term:
$$(\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)u = u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = (2x)(2) = 4x = 4$$
$$(\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)v = v\frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = (3y)(3) = 9y = 9$$
$$(\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)w = u\frac{\partial w}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial w}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0 + 0 + (5tz)(5t) = 25t^2 z = 25$$

Total acceleration:
$$a_x = 0 + 4 = 4 \text{ m/s}^2$$
$$a_y = 0 + 9 = 9 \text{ m/s}^2$$
$$a_z = 5 + 25 = 30 \text{ m/s}^2$$

$$\boxed{\;\vec{a} = 4\hat{i} + 9\hat{j} + 30\hat{k} \text{ m/s}^2\;}$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain the three fundamental modes of motion of a fluid element — translation, rotation, and deformation — with neat mathematical expressions. Define linear and angular strain rates. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Classify fluid motion in detail with appropriate criteria. For a velocity field $\vec{V} = (3y)\hat{i} + (-2x)\hat{j}$ m/s, identify whether the flow is (i) steady/unsteady, (ii) uniform/non-uniform, (iii) rotational/irrotational, and (iv) compute the streamline equation passing through the point $(1, 2)$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Solution to Q B (a):

**(i) Translation:** Movement of the centroid from position $(x, y, z)$ to $(x + V_x dt, y + V_y dt, z + V_z dt)$. Mathematically, the velocity of translation is simply $\vec{V}$.

**(ii) Rotation:** The angular velocity of the element about the $z$-axis is:
$$\Omega_z = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial V_y}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial V_x}{\partial y}\right)$$

Generalising to all axes:
$$\vec{\Omega} = \frac{1}{2}\nabla \times \vec{V} = \frac{1}{2}\vec{\omega}$$

**(iii) Deformation:**
- *Linear (normal) strain rates:* $\epsilon_{xx} = \dfrac{\partial V_x}{\partial x}$, $\epsilon_{yy} = \dfrac{\partial V_y}{\partial y}$, $\epsilon_{zz} = \dfrac{\partial V_z}{\partial z}$
- *Angular (shear) strain rates:* $\epsilon_{xy} = \dfrac{1}{2}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial V_y}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial V_x}{\partial y}\right)$, and similarly for $yz$, $zx$.

**Volumetric dilatation rate:**
$$\frac{1}{\delta V}\frac{D(\delta V)}{Dt} = \nabla \cdot \vec{V}$$

#### Solution to Q B (b):

**(i) Steady/Unsteady:** No explicit $t$ in $\vec{V}$ → **Steady**.

**(ii) Uniform/Non-uniform:** $V_x = 3y$ depends on $y$, $V_y = -2x$ depends on $x$ → **Non-uniform**.

**(iii) Rotational/Irrotational:**
$$\omega_z = \frac{\partial V_y}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial V_x}{\partial y} = (-2) - (3) = -5 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

$\vec{\omega} \neq 0$ → **Rotational**.

**(iv) Streamline through (1, 2):**
$$\frac{dx}{V_x} = \frac{dy}{V_y} \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{dx}{3y} = \frac{dy}{-2x}$$

Cross-multiplying:
$$-2x\,dx = 3y\,dy \;\Rightarrow\; -x^2 = \frac{3}{2}y^2 + C$$

$$\boxed{\;x^2 + \frac{3}{2}y^2 = \text{constant}\;}$$

Applying $(1, 2)$: $1 + 6 = 7$, so streamline: $x^2 + 1.5\,y^2 = 7$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Lagrangian with Eulerian variables** — In Lagrangian, derivative with respect to time holds $\vec{a}$ constant; in Eulerian, it holds $\vec{r}$ constant. Mixing them up invalidates the material derivative derivation entirely.
> 2. **Forgetting the convective term in steady flow** — Many students wrongly conclude acceleration is zero for steady flow. **Steady only kills the local term; the convective term remains.**
> 3. **Sign errors in vorticity components** — The cross-product convention is strict: $\omega_x = \partial V_z / \partial y - \partial V_y / \partial z$. Reversing the order is a frequent, costly mistake.
> 4. **Streamline equation setup** — Always use $\dfrac{dx}{u} = \dfrac{dy}{v} = \dfrac{dz}{w}$. Trying to integrate $dy/dx$ directly without ensuring $V_x \neq 0$ is dangerous.
> 5. **Distinguishing rotation of element from circulation** — Rotation refers to a *local* property at a point (vorticity); circulation is a *line integral* over a closed curve. Marks are deducted if these are confused.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- Two descriptions of motion exist: **Lagrangian (particle-tracking)** and **Eulerian (field-based)**; the latter is the engineering standard.
- The **Material Derivative** $D/Dt = \partial/\partial t + (\vec{V}\cdot\nabla)$ is the bridge between the two.
- For **steady flow**, streamlines, pathlines, and streaklines coincide; otherwise they are distinct.
- **Vorticity** $\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V}$ is twice the angular velocity of a fluid element; $\vec{\omega} = \vec{0}$ defines **irrotational flow** and allows use of velocity potential $\vec{V} = \nabla\phi$.
- Fluid element motions: **translation** + **rotation** + **deformation** (linear + angular).
- **Linear strain rate** along $x$-axis: $\partial V_x/\partial x$; **angular strain rate**: $\tfrac{1}{2}(\partial V_i/\partial x_j + \partial V_j/\partial x_i)$.
- **Volumetric dilatation rate** = $\nabla \cdot \vec{V}$.
- Classification criteria: steady $\Leftrightarrow \partial \vec{V}/\partial t = 0$; uniform $\Leftrightarrow \partial \vec{V}/\partial s = 0$; incompressible $\Leftrightarrow D\rho/Dt = 0$; irrotational $\Leftrightarrow \nabla \times \vec{V} = \vec{0}$.
- **Reynolds number** $Re = \rho V L / \mu$ governs laminar vs. turbulent transition.
- **Mach number** $Ma = V/c$ governs compressibility effects.
- Always separate **local** and **convective** acceleration terms in problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vorticity and circulation - Concept of rotational and irrotational flows. Equation of motion of forced and free vortex flow. Stream function and its relation with velocity field
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET413 - MECHANICS OF FLUID FLOW
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Vorticity and circulation - Concept of rotational and irrotational flows. Equation of motion of forced and free vortex flow. Stream function and its relation with velocity field

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Vorticity (ω)
**Vorticity** is a vector quantity that measures the **local rotation** of a fluid element about its own axis. It is mathematically defined as **one-half of the curl of the velocity vector field**, or equivalently, the **curl of the velocity vector**.

$$\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V} = 2\vec{\Omega}$$

where:
- $\vec{\omega}$ = Vorticity vector (rad/s)
- $\nabla \times$ = Curl operator
- $\vec{V}$ = Velocity vector (m/s)
- $\vec{\Omega}$ = Angular velocity of fluid element (rad/s)

For a **2-D incompressible flow** in the x-y plane, vorticity has only one component along the z-axis:

$$\omega_z = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Meaning of Vorticity:** It quantifies the *spin* of an infinitesimally small fluid particle about its own centroid. A non-zero vorticity means the fluid particle is *spinning* (not just translating).

**Intuitive Analogy — Vorticity as a "Mini Whirlpool":**
Imagine throwing a tiny leaf into a river. If the leaf **spins around its own center** as it floats, the flow has vorticity. If the leaf simply **drifts straight** (or follows a curve without spinning on itself), the flow is **irrotational**. Vorticity is the "self-rotation" of a fluid element — not its movement along a streamline.

---

### 1.2 Circulation (Γ)
**Circulation** is a **scalar (or integral) quantity** that measures the **net rotation of a finite fluid region** along a closed curve. It is the line integral of the velocity vector along a closed path.

$$\Gamma = \oint_C \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{s}$$

where:
- $\Gamma$ = Circulation (m²/s)
- $C$ = Closed contour (curve)
- $d\vec{s}$ = Infinitesimal vector tangent to the curve

By **Stokes' Theorem**, circulation is directly related to vorticity:

$$\Gamma = \oint_C \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{s} = \int_A (\nabla \times \vec{V}) \cdot d\vec{A} = \int_A \vec{\omega} \cdot d\vec{A}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Circulation = Total Vorticity** enclosed by the closed loop. If the loop shrinks to a point, the ratio $\Gamma / A \rightarrow \omega_z$, confirming that vorticity is the *circulation per unit area*.

**Intuitive Analogy — Circulation as a "Hula Hoop":**
Take a hula hoop and push it into a spinning tornado. If the air is rotating around the hoop's axis, the line integral of velocity around the hoop gives you **circulation** — the strength of the swirl enclosing that area. Vorticity is what you'd get if you shrank the hoop to an infinitesimal ring.

---

### 1.3 Rotational vs. Irrotational Flow

| Property | Rotational Flow | Irrotational Flow |
|---|---|---|
| Vorticity (ω) | $\omega \neq 0$ | $\omega = 0$ everywhere |
| Circulation (Γ) | May be non-zero around closed path | Zero around any closed path |
| Fluid particles | **Rotate** about their own axis | Do **NOT rotate** about their own axis |
| Velocity potential (φ) | Does **not** exist | **Exists** |
| Stream function (ψ) | Exists (incompressible flow) | Exists |
| Examples | Forced vortex, flow in rotating tank, Couette flow | Free vortex, flow past aerofoil (far field), ideal flow around a bend | 

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KEY KTU DISTINCTION:** A fluid can move in a curved path (circular streamline) and still be *irrotational*! Example: a **free vortex** has circular streamlines, but the fluid particles do not spin on themselves — they only translate along the curve. Hence free vortex is **irrotational** despite having curved streamlines.

**Intuitive Analogy — Curved Track vs. Spinning Top:**
Think of a train moving on a circular railway track. The train follows a **curved path** (just like a curved streamline) but the wheels of the train do **not** spin on their own axis — they just translate. This is **irrotational** motion. In contrast, the wheels of a car on a **rotating turntable** *do* spin on their own axis — that is **rotational** motion.

---

### 1.4 Vortex Flow — Definition
A **vortex** is a flow pattern in which fluid particles move in concentric circular paths about a common axis (the **vortex core**). The two principal types are:

- **Free Vortex (Irrotational):** $v \cdot r = \text{constant}$ — fluid moves with tangential velocity inversely proportional to radius. No external torque needed.
- **Forced Vortex (Rotational):** $v = \omega r$ — fluid rotates like a solid body with constant angular velocity. Requires external torque.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Vorticity in Cartesian Coordinates (Full Vector Form)

For a 3-D velocity field $\vec{V} = u\hat{i} + v\hat{j} + w\hat{k}$:

$$\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x} & \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y} & \dfrac{\partial}{\partial z} \\ u & v & w \end{vmatrix}$$

Expanding:

$$\omega_x = \frac{\partial w}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial v}{\partial z}, \quad \omega_y = \frac{\partial u}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial w}{\partial x}, \quad \omega_z = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}$$

For **2-D flow** ($w = 0$, no variation in $z$): $\omega_x = 0, \omega_y = 0$, and $\omega_z$ alone survives.

---

### 2.2 Vorticity in Polar (Cylindrical) Coordinates

For axisymmetric / polar flow with $\vec{V} = v_r \hat{e}_r + v_\theta \hat{e}_\theta$:

$$\omega_z = \frac{1}{r}\left[\frac{\partial(r v_\theta)}{\partial r} - \frac{\partial v_r}{\partial \theta}\right]$$

This is the **most tested form** in KTU numerical problems on free and forced vortices.

---

### 2.3 Free Vortex — Detailed Physical Behaviour

In a **free vortex**, the angular momentum of each fluid particle about the centre is **conserved** (no external torque is applied).

**Derivation of Free Vortex Velocity Distribution:**

Consider a small fluid element at radius $r$ moving with tangential velocity $v_\theta$. The moment of momentum per unit mass about the axis is $r \cdot v_\theta$. In absence of external torque, this is conserved:

$$r \cdot v_\theta = C \quad \text{(a constant)}$$

$$\boxed{v_\theta = \frac{C}{r}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world examples of free vortex:** Water flowing out of a bathtub drain, a tornado, a hurricane, flow through a centrifugal pump impeller (in the region outside the impeller), water flowing around a bend in a river.

---

### 2.4 Forced Vortex — Detailed Physical Behaviour

In a **forced vortex**, the fluid rotates like a **rigid body** under an applied external torque. Every fluid particle has the **same angular velocity** $\omega$ about the axis.

$$v_\theta = \omega \cdot r$$

The vorticity is **uniform and constant** throughout the fluid:

$$\omega_z = 2\omega$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world examples of forced vortex:** Water in a rotating cylindrical tank, tea leaves gathering at the centre when stirred in a cup, flow inside the impeller of a centrifugal pump (driven by the impeller blades), a spinning top.

---

### 2.5 Stream Function (ψ) — Definition

A **stream function** $\psi(x, y)$ is a scalar function whose partial derivatives give the velocity components. For a **2-D incompressible flow**:

$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \qquad v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The stream function **always exists** for a 2-D incompressible flow — *whether rotational or irrotational*. Its existence does **not** require irrotationality. It only requires incompressibility (continuity must be automatically satisfied).

The **continuity equation** is automatically satisfied because:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x \partial y} - \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x \partial y} = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

---

### 2.6 Relationship Between Stream Function and Vorticity

Starting from the definition of vorticity $\omega_z$:

$$\omega_z = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}$$

Substituting $u = \partial\psi/\partial y$ and $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$:

$$\omega_z = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(-\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}\right) - \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}\right) = -\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x^2} - \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y^2}$$

$$\boxed{\nabla^2 \psi = -\omega_z}$$

**Special Case — Irrotational Flow:** When $\omega_z = 0$:

$$\boxed{\nabla^2 \psi = 0 \quad \text{(Laplace Equation)}}$$

The stream function for irrotational flow satisfies **Laplace's equation**, an elliptic PDE whose solutions are harmonic functions.

---

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Conditions / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Vorticity (2-D Cartesian) | $\omega_z = \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y}$ | rad/s |
| 2 | Vorticity (Polar) | $\omega_z = \dfrac{1}{r}\left[\dfrac{\partial(r v_\theta)}{\partial r} - \dfrac{\partial v_r}{\partial \theta}\right]$ | rad/s |
| 3 | Circulation | $\Gamma = \oint_C \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{s}$ | m²/s |
| 4 | Stokes' Theorem | $\Gamma = \int_A \omega_z \, dA$ | m²/s |
| 5 | Free Vortex Velocity | $v_\theta = C/r$ | Irrotational, m/s |
| 6 | Free Vortex Constant | $C = v_\theta \cdot r$ | m²/s |
| 7 | Forced Vortex Velocity | $v_\theta = \omega r$ | Rotational, m/s |
| 8 | Stream Function (Cartesian) | $u = \partial\psi/\partial y$, $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$ | m²/s |
| 9 | Stream Function (Polar) | $v_r = \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial\psi}{\partial\theta}$, $v_\theta = -\dfrac{\partial\psi}{\partial r}$ | m²/s |
| 10 | Poisson Eq. for ψ | $\nabla^2 \psi = -\omega_z$ | Incompressible flow |
| 11 | Laplace Eq. for ψ | $\nabla^2 \psi = 0$ | Incompressible, irrotational |
| 12 | Rate of flow (between streamlines) | $q = \psi_2 - \psi_1$ | m³/s per unit depth |
| 13 | Irrotationality condition | $\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x} = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y}$ | $\omega_z = 0$ |
| 14 | Bernoulli (free vortex, on free surface) | $P/\rho g + C^2/(2 g r^2) + z = \text{const}$ | Free surface profile |
| 15 | Bernoulli (forced vortex, on free surface) | $z = (\omega^2 r^2)/(2g) + \text{const}$ | Paraboloid surface |

---

### 2.8 Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KTU emphasises this topic:** These concepts form the analytical backbone of:
> - **Aerodynamics** — design of wings, lift generation (Kutta-Joukowski theorem: $L = \rho V \Gamma$)
> - **Turbomachinery** — design of pumps, turbines, blowers (slip, circulation losses)
> - **Meteorology** — tracking cyclones, hurricanes (free vortex wind fields)
> - **Mixing tanks** — understanding solid-body rotation for chemical mixing
> - **Ship hydrodynamics** — wake analysis and propeller-induced vorticity

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Continuity Equation in Polar Coordinates

The general continuity equation for an incompressible fluid in 3-D is:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$$

In **polar coordinates** $(r, \theta)$ for an axisymmetric 2-D flow (no $z$-variation), the divergence operator is:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial(r v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \theta} = 0$$

Multiplying through by $r$:

$$\frac{\partial(r v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \theta} = 0$$

This is the **continuity equation in polar coordinates** for incompressible 2-D flow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU often asks this as a 7-mark derivation. Show the divergence operator expansion step-by-step, do not skip it.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Stream Function in Polar Coordinates

Starting from the 2-D continuity equation in polar form:

$$\frac{\partial(r v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial \theta} = 0$$

For a function $\psi(r, \theta)$, we can always write the following *ansatz* (a trial form that is guaranteed to satisfy the equation):

$$r v_r = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial \theta}, \qquad v_\theta = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial r}$$

**Verification — plug back into continuity:**

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial \theta}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\left(-\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial r}\right) = \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial r \partial \theta} - \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial \theta \partial r} = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

By the equality of mixed partials (Clairaut's theorem), the continuity equation is automatically satisfied. Therefore, the polar stream function relations are:

$$\boxed{v_r = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial \theta}, \qquad v_\theta = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial r}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Stream Function for a Free Vortex

For a **free vortex**:
- $v_r = 0$ (purely tangential motion)
- $v_\theta = C/r$

From the polar stream function relations:

$$v_r = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial \theta} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial \theta} = 0$$

This means $\psi$ is a function of $r$ alone: $\psi = f(r)$.

Also:

$$v_\theta = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial r} = \frac{C}{r}$$

Integrating with respect to $r$:

$$\frac{d\psi}{dr} = -\frac{C}{r} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \psi = -C \ln(r) + K$$

where $K$ is an arbitrary constant. The free vortex stream function is therefore:

$$\boxed{\psi = -C \ln(r) + K}$$

Since $\psi$ depends only on $r$ (not on $\theta$), the **streamlines are circles** centred at the origin.

**Irrotationality check:** Using $\omega_z = \nabla^2 \psi$ in polar form:

$$\omega_z = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial r}\right) + \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial \theta^2}$$

Substituting $\psi = -C \ln r$:

$$\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial r} = -\frac{C}{r}, \quad r\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial r} = -C, \quad \frac{\partial}{\partial r}(-C) = 0$$

$$\frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial \theta^2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_z = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

So the free vortex is **irrotational**, confirming our earlier statement.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Stream Function for a Forced Vortex

For a **forced vortex**:
- $v_r = 0$
- $v_\theta = \omega r$

Using $v_r = 0$:

$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial \theta} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \psi = f(r) \text{ only}$$

Using $v_\theta = -\partial\psi/\partial r$:

$$-\frac{d\psi}{dr} = \omega r \quad \Rightarrow \quad d\psi = -\omega r \, dr$$

Integrating:

$$\psi = -\frac{\omega r^2}{2} + K$$

$$\boxed{\psi = -\frac{\omega r^2}{2} + K}$$

**Vorticity check (forced vortex is rotational):**

$$\omega_z = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r \cdot \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial r}\right) = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r \cdot (-\omega r)\right) = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}(-\omega r^2) = \frac{1}{r}(-2\omega r) = -2\omega$$

So vorticity is **constant and non-zero** ($\omega_z = -2\omega$, the sign depends on rotation direction), confirming the forced vortex is **rotational**.

---

### 3.5 Derivation: Pressure Distribution in a Forced Vortex (Free Surface Shape)

Apply **Bernoulli's equation** along a horizontal streamline (or to the free surface) in a rotating frame. For a fluid rotating as a solid body, the pressure gradient must provide the centripetal acceleration. Using Euler's equation in radial direction:

$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial r} = \rho \cdot \frac{v_\theta^2}{r} = \rho \omega^2 r$$

Integrating with respect to $r$:

$$P = \frac{\rho \omega^2 r^2}{2} + f(z)$$

At the **free surface**, the pressure equals atmospheric pressure $P_{atm}$, and the surface elevation is $z(r)$. The free surface satisfies:

$$P_{atm} = \frac{\rho \omega^2 r^2}{2} + \rho g z(r) + C_0$$

Solving for $z(r)$ (taking $z = 0$ at $r = 0$):

$$\boxed{z(r) = \frac{\omega^2 r^2}{2g} + \text{const}}$$

The free surface is a **paraboloid of revolution**. (This is why a stirred cup of tea has a curved surface — coffee or tea leaves migrate to the centre where the surface is lowest.)

---

### 3.6 Derivation: Pressure Distribution in a Free Vortex (Free Surface Shape)

For a free vortex, applying Bernoulli between two points on the free surface (where $P = P_{atm}$ at both):

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_{\theta 1}^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_{\theta 2}^2}{2g} + z_2$$

Since $P_1 = P_2 = P_{atm}$ on the free surface:

$$z_1 - z_2 = \frac{v_{\theta 2}^2 - v_{\theta 1}^2}{2g} = \frac{1}{2g}\left(\frac{C^2}{r_2^2} - \frac{C^2}{r_1^2}\right)$$

Hence the free surface of a free vortex is **NOT a horizontal plane** — it is a **hyperbolic surface** that drops sharply near the core.

$$\boxed{z = -\frac{C^2}{2g r^2} + \text{const}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU exam trick:** In a free vortex, the fluid surface is depressed at the centre (vortex core) and rises outward (asymptotic to horizontal far away). In a forced vortex, the fluid surface rises *outward* (paraboloid). Many students confuse these — *remember: free vortex → hyperbolic depression at centre; forced vortex → parabolic elevation outward*.

---

### 3.7 Derivation: Stream Function & Velocity Field — Worked Example

**Problem:** Given $\psi = 3x^2 - 3y^2$, determine the velocity field, the vorticity, and classify the flow.

**Step 1 — Compute velocity components:**

$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(3x^2 - 3y^2) = -6y$$

$$v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial x}(3x^2 - 3y^2) = -6x$$

**Step 2 — Compute vorticity:**

$$\omega_z = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(-6x) - \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(-6y) = -6 - (-6) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Classification:** Since $\omega_z = 0$, the flow is **irrotational**.

**Step 4 — Verification of incompressibility:**

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(-6y) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(-6x) = 0 + 0 = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Streamline equation** ($\psi = $ const): $3x^2 - 3y^2 = c$, i.e., $x^2 - y^2 = c/3$ — these are **rectangular hyperbolas**.
