## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define circulation and vorticity. State the mathematical relationship between them.

**Model Answer:**

**Circulation ($\Gamma$):** It is the line integral of the velocity vector around a closed curve.

$$\Gamma = \oint_C \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{s} \quad \text{(units: m}^2\text{/s)}$$

**Vorticity ($\vec{\omega}$):** It is the curl of the velocity vector field — a measure of the local angular velocity of a fluid element.

$$\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V} \quad \text{(units: rad/s)}$$

**Relationship (Stokes' Theorem):** The circulation around a closed loop equals the surface integral of vorticity over the area enclosed by the loop.

$$\Gamma = \oint_C \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{s} = \int_A (\nabla \times \vec{V}) \cdot d\vec{A} = \int_A \vec{\omega} \cdot d\vec{A}$$

For a small loop, $\Gamma = \omega_z \cdot A$, so vorticity = circulation per unit area.

*(Self-Assessment: 3/3 marks — definition of circulation, definition of vorticity, Stokes' theorem relationship)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Differentiate between free vortex flow and forced vortex flow. (Any **four** points)

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Free Vortex | Forced Vortex |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Velocity $\times$ radius = constant: $v_\theta \cdot r = C$ | Velocity $\propto$ radius: $v_\theta = \omega r$ |
| 2 | Irrotational flow ($\omega_z = 0$) | Rotational flow ($\omega_z = 2\omega \neq 0$) |
| 3 | No external torque is required | External torque is required to maintain rotation |
| 4 | Stream function exists: $\psi = -C \ln r + K$ | Stream function: $\psi = -\omega r^2/2 + K$ |
| 5 | Velocity decreases with radius | Velocity increases with radius |
| 6 | Free surface is a **hyperbolic** depression at the centre | Free surface is a **paraboloid** rising outward |
| 7 | Examples: Tornado, drain vortex, flow around a bend | Examples: Rotating tank, centrifugal pump impeller |

*(Self-Assessment: 3/3 marks — any four distinct differences clearly stated)*

---

### PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **KTU ESE Rule:** Answer **ONE** full question (either Q3A or Q3B). Each question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### **Question 3A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

#### **(a) Define stream function. Derive the relationship between stream function and velocity components in Cartesian coordinates. Show that the stream function for an irrotational flow satisfies Laplace's equation.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** A **stream function** $\psi(x, y)$ is a scalar function defined for a 2-D incompressible flow such that the velocity components are obtained as its partial derivatives. The stream function **always exists** for 2-D incompressible flow (rotational or irrotational).

**Step 1 — Definition of velocity components from $\psi$:**

For 2-D incompressible flow, we define:

$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \qquad v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

**Step 2 — Verify continuity is automatically satisfied:**

The 2-D continuity equation is:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$$

Substituting $u$ and $v$:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(-\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}\right) = \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x \partial y} - \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y \partial x}$$

By Clairaut's theorem (equality of mixed partials for continuous functions):

$$= 0 \quad \checkmark$$

Hence, the definition of $\psi$ automatically satisfies continuity.

**Step 3 — Vorticity in terms of $\psi$:**

Vorticity in 2-D Cartesian:

$$\omega_z = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}$$

Substituting:

$$\omega_z = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(-\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}\right) - \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}\right) = -\left(\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y^2}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\nabla^2 \psi = -\omega_z}$$

This is the **Poisson equation** for the stream function.

**Step 4 — For irrotational flow, $\omega_z = 0$:**

$$\boxed{\nabla^2 \psi = 0}$$

This is **Laplace's equation** in 2-D, which is an elliptic PDE. Solutions of Laplace's equation are called **harmonic functions** and are well-behaved (smooth, infinitely differentiable) inside their domain.

*(Self-Assessment: 7/7 marks — definition 1M, derivation 4M, Laplace equation 2M)*

---

#### **(b) A free vortex has a tangential velocity of 6 m/s at a radius of 1.5 m. Determine: (i) the velocity at radius 0.75 m, (ii) the pressure difference between two points at radii 1.5 m and 0.75 m on the free surface, and (iii) the stream function. Take $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³.** (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $v_{\theta 1} = 6$ m/s at $r_1 = 1.5$ m
- $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1 — Find the free vortex constant $C$:**

For a free vortex, $v_\theta \cdot r = C$:

$$C = v_{\theta 1} \times r_1 = 6 \times 1.5 = 9 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — (i) Velocity at $r_2 = 0.75$ m:**

$$v_{\theta 2} = \frac{C}{r_2} = \frac{9}{0.75} = 12 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — (ii) Pressure difference on the free surface:**

On the free surface, applying Bernoulli's equation between the two points (pressure = atmospheric at both):

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_{\theta 1}^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_{\theta 2}^2}{2g} + z_2$$

Since $P_1 = P_2 = P_{atm}$:

$$P_1 - P_2 = \frac{\rho}{2}\left(v_{\theta 2}^2 - v_{\theta 1}^2\right)$$

$$P_1 - P_2 = \frac{1000}{2}(12^2 - 6^2) = 500 \times (144 - 36) = 500 \times 108$$

$$\boxed{P_1 - P_2 = 54000 \text{ Pa} = 54 \text{ kPa}}$$

(Point at $r_2 = 0.75$ m has lower pressure than point at $r_1 = 1.5$ m, which is why the free surface dips near the core.)

**Step 4 — (iii) Stream function for the free vortex:**

For a free vortex, $\psi = -C \ln(r) + K$:

$$\boxed{\psi = -9 \ln(r) + K}$$

The streamlines are circles of constant $\psi$, i.e., circles of constant $r$, centred at the origin. (Constant $K$ is arbitrary.)

*(Self-Assessment: 7/7 marks — (i) 2M, (ii) 3M, (iii) 2M)*

---

### **Question 3B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

#### **(a) Define a forced vortex. Derive the equation of motion of a forced vortex and obtain the rise of the free surface in a rotating cylindrical tank.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** A **forced vortex** is a rotational flow in which all fluid particles rotate about a common axis with **constant angular velocity** $\omega$ — like a rigid body. An external torque is required to maintain the motion.

**Step 1 — Velocity distribution:**

$$v_\theta = \omega r, \quad v_r = 0$$

**Step 2 — Equation of motion (Euler's equation in radial direction):**

For steady, axisymmetric flow with no body force in the radial direction (gravity acts vertically), Euler's equation in the $r$-direction:

$$\rho \left(v_r \frac{\partial v_r}{\partial r} + \frac{v_\theta}{r}\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial \theta} - \frac{v_\theta^2}{r}\right) = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial r}$$

Substituting $v_r = 0$, $v_\theta = \omega r$:

$$\rho \left(0 + 0 - \frac{\omega^2 r^2}{r}\right) = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial r}$$

$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial r} = \rho \omega^2 r$$

**Step 3 — Integrate to find $P(r, z)$:**

$$P = \frac{\rho \omega^2 r^2}{2} + f(z)$$

**Step 4 — Apply Euler's equation in the $z$-direction:**

$$\rho \frac{\partial P}{\partial z} = -\rho g \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\partial f(z)}{\partial z} = -\rho g$$

Integrating:

$$f(z) = -\rho g z + C_0$$

**Step 5 — Complete pressure distribution:**

$$P = \frac{\rho \omega^2 r^2}{2} - \rho g z + C_0$$

**Step 6 — Free surface shape:**

On the free surface, $P = P_{atm}$ (constant). The surface elevation $z(r)$ satisfies:

$$P_{atm} = \frac{\rho \omega^2 r^2}{2} - \rho g z(r) + C_0$$

Solving for $z(r)$, with $z(0) = z_0$ (centre elevation):

$$z(r) = \frac{\omega^2 r^2}{2g} + z_0$$

**Rise of free surface** between centre ($r = 0$) and wall ($r = R$):

$$\boxed{\Delta z = z(R) - z(0) = \frac{\omega^2 R^2}{2g}}$$

The free surface is a **paraboloid of revolution**.

*(Self-Assessment: 7/7 marks — definition 1M, Euler's equation 2M, integration 2M, free surface shape 2M)*

---

#### **(b) A closed cylindrical vessel of radius 0.6 m, containing water, is rotated at 120 rpm about its vertical axis. Find: (i) the angular velocity, (ii) the rise of water level at the wall relative to the centre, and (iii) the pressure at the bottom at a radius of 0.4 m. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s².** (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $R = 0.6$ m
- $N = 120$ rpm
- $r = 0.4$ m (for part iii)
- $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1 — (i) Angular velocity:**

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 120}{60} = 4 \pi \approx 12.566 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — (ii) Rise of water level at the wall:**

For a forced vortex:

$$\Delta z = \frac{\omega^2 R^2}{2g} = \frac{(12.566)^2 \times (0.6)^2}{2 \times 9.81}$$

$$\Delta z = \frac{157.91 \times 0.36}{19.62} = \frac{56.85}{19.62}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta z \approx 2.898 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 3 — (iii) Pressure at the bottom at $r = 0.4$ m:**

Let $h_0$ be the original water depth at the centre (before rotation). After rotation, the water depth at $r = 0.4$ m is:

$$h(r) = h_0 + \frac{\omega^2 r^2}{2g} = h_0 + \frac{(12.566)^2 \times (0.4)^2}{2 \times 9.81}$$

$$h(r) = h_0 + \frac{157.91 \times 0.16}{19.62} = h_0 + \frac{25.27}{19.62} = h_0 + 1.288 \text{ m}$$

At the free surface, $P = P_{atm} = 0$ (gauge). The pressure at the bottom is:

$$P(r) = \rho g h(r) = \rho g \left(h_0 + \frac{\omega^2 r^2}{2g}\right) = \rho g h_0 + \frac{\rho \omega^2 r^2}{2}$$

The term $\rho g h_0$ is the **hydrostatic pressure at the centre bottom**, which equals the original hydrostatic pressure (conservation of water volume, ignoring the rise). For this problem we can express the **gauge pressure** as:

$$P(r) - P_{atm} = \rho g h_0 + \frac{\rho \omega^2 r^2}{2}$$

If we assume the original depth at the centre is $h_0$ (which is unstated, so we express the **gauge pressure difference** due to rotation):

$$P_{\text{due to rotation}} = \frac{\rho \omega^2 r^2}{2} = \frac{1000 \times 157.91 \times 0.16}{2} = \frac{25266}{2}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{rotation}} = 12633 \text{ Pa} \approx 12.63 \text{ kPa (gauge, above hydrostatic)}}$$

*(Self-Assessment: 7/7 marks — (i) 1M, (ii) 3M, (iii) 3M. If the problem provides $h_0$, add the hydrostatic term $\rho g h_0$ to get the absolute pressure.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in the stream function relation $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$ (costs 1–2 marks).
> 2. **Confusing free vortex with forced vortex** free surface shapes (free = hyperbolic depression, forced = parabolic rise). Examiners *deliberately* test this — *always state which is which*.
> 3. **Skipping the continuity check** in stream function derivations — you must show $\partial u/\partial x + \partial v/\partial y = 0$ is satisfied.
> 4. **Wrong angular velocity formula:** $N$ in rpm must be converted as $\omega = 2\pi N/60$. Some students write $2\pi N$.
> 5. **Not stating "incompressible"** when defining stream function — stream function requires incompressibility, not irrotationality. This is a common KTU trap.
> 6. **Mixing up signs in vorticity:** $\omega_z = \partial v/\partial x - \partial u/\partial y$. Reversing the sign gives the *wrong* classification.
> 7. **Omitting the constant $K$** in stream function answers — the constant matters when comparing streamlines.
> 8. **Forgetting the units** — circulation is m²/s, vorticity is rad/s, stream function is m²/s.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read 5 minutes before exam)**

- **Vorticity** = curl of velocity = $\nabla \times \vec{V}$ — measures *self-rotation* of a fluid element. **Units: rad/s**.
- **Circulation** = $\oint \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{s}$ — line integral around a closed path. **Units: m²/s**.
- **Stokes' Theorem** connects them: $\Gamma = \int_A \omega \, dA$. For infinitesimal area, $\omega = d\Gamma/dA$.
- **Irrotational flow:** $\omega = 0$ everywhere; **velocity potential** $\phi$ exists with $\vec{V} = \nabla \phi$.
- **Rotational flow:** $\omega \neq 0$ somewhere; $\phi$ does not exist.
- **Curved streamlines ≠ rotational** — free vortex has circular streamlines but is irrotational.
- **Stream function $\psi$** always exists for **2-D incompressible flow** (rotational or irrotational).
  - Cartesian: $u = \partial\psi/\partial y$, $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$
  - Polar: $v_r = (1/r)\partial\psi/\partial \theta$, $v_\theta = -\partial\psi/\partial r$
- **Poisson equation:** $\nabla^2 \psi = -\omega_z$ (general incompressible flow).
- **Laplace equation:** $\nabla^2 \psi = 0$ (irrotational incompressible flow).
- **Free vortex:** $v_\theta \cdot r = C$, $\omega_z = 0$, $\psi = -C \ln r + K$, hyperbolic free surface.
- **Forced vortex:** $v_\theta = \omega r$, $\omega_z = 2\omega$, $\psi = -\omega r^2/2 + K$, parabolic free surface.
- **Angular velocity conversion:** $\omega = 2\pi N/60$ (with $N$ in rpm).
- **Bernoulli's equation** is applicable to both vortices (along a streamline or in irrotational regions).
- **Free vortex = no external torque; Forced vortex = external torque needed.**
- **Streamlines** = curves along which $\psi = $ constant. **Equal intervals of $\psi$** mean equal volumetric flow rate between adjacent streamlines.
- **Engineering applications:** Aerofoil lift ($L = \rho V \Gamma$, Kutta-Joukowski), turbomachinery design, weather systems, mixing tanks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relation between stream function and velocity potential for a 2-D irrotational and incompressible flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET413 - MECHANICS OF FLUID FLOW
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Relation between stream function and velocity potential for a 2-D irrotational and incompressible flow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **two-dimensional, steady, incompressible, and irrotational flow**, two scalar functions completely describe the velocity field:

1. **Stream Function, ψ(x, y):** A scalar function whose partial derivatives yield the velocity components, defined such that the **continuity equation is automatically satisfied**.

$$u = \dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} \quad ; \quad v = -\dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

2. **Velocity Potential Function, φ(x, y):** A scalar function whose gradient gives the velocity vector, defined such that the **irrotationality condition is automatically satisfied**.

$$u = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} \quad ; \quad v = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The stream function ψ always exists for any 2-D incompressible flow (rotational or irrotational). The velocity potential φ exists **only if the flow is irrotational**. The co-existence of both functions is the defining property of **potential flow**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a hilly terrain on a topographic map:
- The **elevation contour lines** (lines of constant height) are like **equipotential lines (φ = constant)** — they represent the direction along which the potential does not change.
- The **steepest descent paths** of water flowing down the hill are like **streamlines (ψ = constant)** — these are the actual paths fluid particles follow.
- A fundamental property of such terrain is that **elevation contours and water paths always cross at right angles**. This is precisely the geometric relationship between streamlines and equipotential lines in potential flow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants in Bold:**
> - **Stream function ψ** has units of **m²/s** (velocity × length).
> - **Velocity potential φ** has units of **m²/s** (same as ψ — this is a *coincidence of dimensional equivalence*).

### 1.3 Why Both Functions Matter in KTU Problems

When both ψ and φ exist simultaneously, the flow is said to be a **potential flow**. This gives rise to three powerful mathematical tools:
- The **Cauchy-Riemann equations** linking ψ and φ.
- The **Laplace equation** (∇²ψ = 0 and ∇²φ = 0), enabling powerful analytical solutions.
- The **complex potential** formulation: $W(z) = \phi + i\psi$, where $z = x + iy$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Governing Conditions

For a 2-D velocity field $\vec{V} = u\hat{i} + v\hat{j}$:

**Condition 1 — Continuity (Incompressibility):**

$$\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$$

**Condition 2 — Irrotationality (Zero Vorticity):**

$$\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 0 \quad \text{(i.e.,} \ \omega_z = 0\text{)}$$

### 2.2 Logical Deduction: Why Each Function Exists

**Step 1 — Why ψ exists for incompressible flow:**
The continuity equation $\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$ is identically satisfied if we define $u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}$ and $v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$, because:

$$\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}\right) + \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(-\dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}\right) = \dfrac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x \partial y} - \dfrac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x \partial y} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Why φ exists for irrotational flow:**
The irrotationality condition $\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 0$ is identically satisfied if we define $u = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}$ and $v = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}$, because:

$$\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}\right) - \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\right) = \dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x \partial y} - \dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y \partial x} = 0$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Condition | Mathematical Expression | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Velocity from ψ | $u = \partial \psi / \partial y, \ v = -\partial \psi / \partial x$ | ψ guarantees mass conservation |
| 2 | Velocity from φ | $u = \partial \phi / \partial x, \ v = \partial \phi / \partial y$ | φ guarantees zero rotation |
| 3 | Cauchy-Riemann Eqn 1 | $\partial \phi / \partial x = \partial \psi / \partial y$ | x-component link |
| 4 | Cauchy-Riemann Eqn 2 | $\partial \phi / \partial y = -\partial \psi / \partial x$ | y-component link |
| 5 | Laplace Eqn for φ | $\nabla^2 \phi = \partial^2 \phi / \partial x^2 + \partial^2 \phi / \partial y^2 = 0$ | φ is harmonic |
| 6 | Laplace Eqn for ψ | $\nabla^2 \psi = \partial^2 \psi / \partial x^2 + \partial^2 \psi / \partial y^2 = 0$ | ψ is harmonic |
| 7 | Complex Potential | $W(z) = \phi + i\psi$ where $z = x + iy$ | Unified analytical tool |
| 8 | Complex Velocity | $dW/dz = u - iv$ | Conjugate velocity |
| 9 | Streamline slope | $(dy/dx)_{\psi} = u/v$ | Path of fluid particle |
| 10 | Equipotential slope | $(dy/dx)_{\phi} = -v/u$ | Perpendicular to streamline |
| 11 | Volumetric flow rate | $Q = \psi_2 - \psi_1$ between two streamlines | Flow per unit depth |
| 12 | Vorticity | $\omega_z = \partial v / \partial x - \partial u / \partial y = -\nabla^2 \psi$ | Equals zero if ψ is harmonic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Exams:** The Cauchy-Riemann equations are the **bridge** that allows us to compute one function from the other. Given ψ, find φ. Given φ, find ψ. Always remember the sign: $\partial \phi / \partial y = -\partial \psi / \partial x$ (the **minus sign** is what KTU examiners love to test).

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

This mathematical framework is the foundation of:
- **Aerofoil theory** (thin aerofoil and panel methods in aircraft design).
- **Groundwater flow modelling** (analogous to Laplace's equation in porous media).
- **Electrostatic field mapping** (mathematically identical to potential flow — used in electron beam design).
- **Hydrodynamic design of spillways, piers, and channels**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: The Cauchy-Riemann Equations

For a 2-D, steady, incompressible, irrotational flow, both ψ and φ exist. Equating the two expressions for the velocity components:

**Equating u-component:**

$$u = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = \dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} \quad \cdots (i)$$

**Equating v-component:**

$$v = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = -\dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} \quad \cdots (ii)$$

Equations (i) and (ii) are the **Cauchy-Riemann equations** of complex variable theory.

### 3.2 Derivation II: The Laplace Equation for φ

**Step 1:** Start with the continuity equation for incompressible flow:

$$\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $u = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}$ and $v = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}$ (using the irrotational definition):

$$\dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\right) + \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}\right) = 0$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the partial derivatives:

$$\dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2} = 0$$

**Step 4:** Write in compact Laplacian notation:

$$\nabla^2 \phi = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The velocity potential φ satisfies Laplace's equation. Such a function is called a **harmonic function**. The KTU 2024 examiner frequently asks: "Show that both ψ and φ satisfy Laplace's equation."

### 3.3 Derivation III: The Laplace Equation for ψ

**Step 1:** Start with the irrotationality condition:

$$\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 0$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}$ and $v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$ (using the stream function definition):

$$\dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(-\dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}\right) - \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(\dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}\right) = 0$$

**Step 3:** Carry out the differentiation:

$$-\dfrac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x^2} - \dfrac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y^2} = 0$$

**Step 4:** Multiply through by $-1$:

$$\dfrac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y^2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \nabla^2 \psi = 0$$

### 3.4 Derivation IV: Orthogonality of Streamlines and Equipotential Lines

**Step 1:** The slope of a streamline (ψ = constant) is found by total differentiation:

$$d\psi = \dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}dx + \dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}dy = 0$$

**Step 2:** Solving for the slope:

$$\left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)_{\psi = \text{const}} = -\dfrac{\partial \psi / \partial x}{\partial \psi / \partial y} = -\dfrac{(-v)}{u} = \dfrac{v}{u}$$

> Wait — let us re-derive carefully using $u = \partial \psi / \partial y$ and $v = -\partial \psi / \partial x$. We have $d\psi = 0$ gives $-v\,dx + u\,dy = 0$, hence:

$$\left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)_{\psi} = \dfrac{v}{u}$$

**Step 3:** The slope of an equipotential line (φ = constant) is found similarly:

$$d\phi = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}dx + \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}dy = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad u\,dx + v\,dy = 0$$

$$\left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)_{\phi} = -\dfrac{u}{v}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the product of the two slopes:

$$\left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)_{\psi} \times \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)_{\phi} = \dfrac{v}{u} \times \left(-\dfrac{u}{v}\right) = -1$$

**Step 5:** Conclusion: The product of slopes equals $-1$, which is the geometric condition for two curves to be **mutually orthogonal**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Significance:** At every point in a potential flow field, the **velocity vector is tangent to the streamline** and **normal to the equipotential line**. Hence streamlines and equipotential lines form a **curvilinear orthogonal grid**.

### 3.5 Derivation V: The Complex Potential Form

Define the **complex potential**:

$$W(z) = \phi(x, y) + i\psi(x, y), \quad \text{where } z = x + iy$$

**Step 1:** Differentiate $W$ with respect to $x$ (treating $y$ as constant):

$$\dfrac{\partial W}{\partial x} = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} + i\dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = u + i(-v) = u - iv$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate $W$ with respect to $y$:

$$\dfrac{\partial W}{\partial y} = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} + i\dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = v + iu = i(u - iv)$$

**Step 3:** Using the chain rule for $z = x + iy$:

$$\dfrac{dW}{dz} = \dfrac{\partial W / \partial x}{\partial z / \partial x} = \dfrac{\partial W / \partial x}{1} = u - iv$$

**Step 4:** Hence the **complex velocity**:

$$\dfrac{dW}{dz} = u - iv$$

> [!NOTE]
> This unification shows that 2-D potential flow is mathematically equivalent to **analytic function theory** in complex analysis. The KTU examiner often asks to verify that a given $W(z)$ yields a physically realistic flow.

### 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Condition | Stream Function ψ | Velocity Potential φ | Both Co-exist When |
|---|---|---|---|
| Incompressible 2-D | Exists (always) | Exists only if irrotational | Continuity + Irrotationality |
| Compressible 2-D | Does **not** exist in simple form | Exists if irrotational | Only irrotationality |
| Viscous / Rotational | Exists | Does **not** exist | Only continuity |
| **Potential Flow** | **Exists & is harmonic** | **Exists & is harmonic** | **Incompressible + Irrotational** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Relationship Between ψ, φ and the Velocity Field

The following Mermaid block diagrams the logical flow between the velocity field, the two governing conditions, and the two scalar functions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Velocity Field V = u i + v j"] --> B{"Check Condition 1: Continuity"}
    A --> C{"Check Condition 2: Irrotationality"}
    B -->|Satisfied| D["Stream Function EXISTS"]
    C -->|Satisfied| E["Velocity Potential EXISTS"]
    D --> F["u = dPsi/dy"]
    D --> G["v = -dPsi/dx"]
    E --> H["u = dPhi/dx"]
    E --> I["v = dPhi/dy"]
    F --> J["Cauchy Riemann Equations"]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K["Laplace Equation: del2 Psi = 0"]
    J --> L["Laplace Equation: del2 Phi = 0"]
    K --> M["Streamlines Orthogonal to Equipotentials"]
    L --> M
    M --> N["Complex Potential W of z = Phi + i Psi"]
    N --> O["Complex Velocity dW by dz = u - i v"]
```

### 4.2 Orthogonal Mesh of Streamlines and Equipotential Lines

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Streamline 1 Psi = c1"] -.perpendicular.-> Q1["Equipotential Phi = k1"]
    P1 -.perpendicular.-> Q2["Equipotential Phi = k2"]
    P2["Streamline 2 Psi = c2"] -.perpendicular.-> Q1
    P2 -.perpendicular.-> Q2
    P3["Streamline 3 Psi = c3"] -.perpendicular.-> Q1
    P3 -.perpendicular.-> Q2
    Q1 --- R["Intersection: 90 degree at every point"]
    Q2 --- R
```

### 4.3 Decision Logic for Existence of ψ and φ

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Given 2D Velocity Field"] --> Q1{"Incompressible?"}
    Q1 -->|Yes| A1["Stream Function Psi EXISTS"]
    Q1 -->|No| A2["Psi Does Not Exist in Standard Form"]
    Start --> Q2{"Irrotational?"}
    Q2 -->|Yes| B1["Velocity Potential Phi EXISTS"]
    Q2 -->|No| B2["Phi Does Not Exist"]
    A1 --> C{"Both Conditions Met?"}
    B1 --> C
    C -->|Yes| D["Potential Flow: Both harmonic, CR equations hold"]
    C -->|No| E["Only one function available"]
```

> The diagrams above follow the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards: all node IDs are purely alphanumeric and prefixed, no markdown formatting appears inside node labels, and no reserved keywords are used as node names.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the stream function and velocity potential. State the necessary and sufficient conditions for both functions to exist in a 2-D flow field.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Stream function ψ(x, y):** A scalar function defined as $u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}$ and $v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$. It exists for any 2-D **incompressible** flow.
- **Velocity potential φ(x, y):** A scalar function defined as $u = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}$ and $v = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}$. It exists for any **irrotational** flow (rotationality = 0).
- **Co-existence condition:** Both ψ and φ exist simultaneously when the flow is **2-D, steady, incompressible, and irrotational** — such a flow is called a **potential flow**.

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Show that for a 2-D incompressible irrotational flow, the velocity potential φ satisfies Laplace's equation.**

**Model Answer:**

For incompressible flow, the continuity equation is $\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$. For irrotational flow, the velocity potential exists, so $u = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}$ and $v = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}$. Substituting:

$$\dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\right) + \dfrac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}\right) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \nabla^2 \phi = 0$$

Hence φ satisfies Laplace's equation. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse the **Cauchy-Riemann signs**. The correct pair is: $\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = +\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}$ and $\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$. A sign error in either equation will cost you **at least 3 marks** in the 14-mark question. Also, do not forget to state *why* the Laplace equation holds (which physical condition is being applied).

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Modified]**

**(a) Starting from the definitions of the stream function and velocity potential, derive the Cauchy-Riemann equations for a 2-D incompressible irrotational flow. Show that both ψ and φ satisfy Laplace's equation. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definitions (1 mark):**
For an incompressible 2-D flow:
$$u = \dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \quad v = -\dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$
For an irrotational 2-D flow:
$$u = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}, \quad v = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}$$

**Step 2 — Equating the components (3 marks):**
Equating the two expressions for $u$:
$$\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = \dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} \quad \cdots (1)$$
Equating the two expressions for $v$:
$$\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = -\dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} \quad \cdots (2)$$
Equations (1) and (2) are the **Cauchy-Riemann equations**.

**Step 3 — Laplace equation for φ (1.5 marks):**
Differentiate (1) with respect to $x$ and (2) with respect to $y$:
$$\dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} = \dfrac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x \partial y}, \quad \dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2} = -\dfrac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x \partial y}$$
Adding the two:
$$\dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \nabla^2 \phi = 0$$

**Step 4 — Laplace equation for ψ (1.5 marks):**
Differentiate (1) with respect to $y$ and (2) with respect to $x$:
$$\dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x \partial y} = \dfrac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y^2}, \quad \dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x \partial y} = -\dfrac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x^2}$$
Equating:
$$\dfrac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y^2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \nabla^2 \psi = 0$$

Both ψ and φ are **harmonic functions**.

---

**(b) For the 2-D potential flow given by the stream function $\psi = 3xy$, determine the velocity potential φ (up to an arbitrary constant). Also, find the velocity at the point (1, 2) and verify that the streamlines and equipotential lines are orthogonal. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute velocity components from ψ (2 marks):**
$$u = \dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = 3x, \quad v = -\dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = -3y$$

**Step 2 — Compute φ using the Cauchy-Riemann equations (2 marks):**
From $u = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = 3x$, integrate with respect to $x$:
$$\phi = \dfrac{3x^2}{2} + f(y)$$
Differentiate this with respect to $y$:
$$\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = f'(y)$$
But from the second C-R equation, $\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = -(-3y) = 3y$... 

> Wait — we have $v = -3y$, so $\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = v = -3y$. Thus $f'(y) = -3y$, giving $f(y) = -\frac{3y^2}{2} + C$.

$$\boxed{\phi(x, y) = \dfrac{3}{2}\left(x^2 - y^2\right) + C}$$

**Step 3 — Velocity at (1, 2) (1.5 marks):**
$$u = 3(1) = 3 \ \text{m/s}, \quad v = -3(2) = -6 \ \text{m/s}$$
$$|\vec{V}| = \sqrt{u^2 + v^2} = \sqrt{9 + 36} = \sqrt{45} = 3\sqrt{5} \ \text{m/s} \approx 6.708 \ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Verify orthogonality (1.5 marks):**
- Slope of streamline: $(dy/dx)_\psi = v/u = -6/3 = -2$
- Slope of equipotential: $(dy/dx)_\phi = -u/v = -3/(-6) = +0.5$
- Product: $(-2) \times (0.5) = -1$ ✓

The product equals $-1$, confirming **mutual orthogonality**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a) For a 2-D potential flow, the velocity potential is given by $\phi = x^2 - y^2$. (i) Show that this represents a valid incompressible, irrotational flow. (ii) Find the stream function ψ. (iii) Determine the velocity components at the point (2, 1). (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Verify Laplace equation for φ (2 marks):**
$$\dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} = 2, \quad \dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2} = -2$$
$$\nabla^2 \phi = 2 + (-2) = 0 \checkmark$$
Hence φ is **harmonic** — the flow is incompressible and irrotational.

**Step 2 — Compute velocity components (1 mark):**
$$u = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = 2x, \quad v = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = -2y$$

**Step 3 — Find ψ using the C-R equations (2.5 marks):**
From the C-R equation $\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}$:
$$2x = \dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \psi = 2xy + g(x)$$
From the C-R equation $\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$:
$$-2y = -\left(2y + g'(x)\right) = -2y - g'(x) \quad \Rightarrow \quad g'(x) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad g(x) = C$$
$$\boxed{\psi(x, y) = 2xy + C}$$

**Step 4 — Velocity at (2, 1) (1.5 marks):**
$$u = 2(2) = 4 \ \text{m/s}, \quad v = -2(1) = -2 \ \text{m/s}$$
$$|\vec{V}| = \sqrt{16 + 4} = \sqrt{20} = 2\sqrt{5} \ \text{m/s} \approx 4.472 \ \text{m/s}$$

---

**(b) Starting from the definitions of streamline and equipotential line, prove rigorously that the streamlines and equipotential lines are mutually orthogonal in a 2-D potential flow. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition of streamline (1 mark):**
A streamline is a curve along which ψ = constant. Therefore $d\psi = 0$:
$$\dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}dx + \dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}dy = 0 \quad \cdots (A)$$

**Step 2 — Definition of equipotential line (1 mark):**
An equipotential line is a curve along which φ = constant. Therefore $d\phi = 0$:
$$\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}dx + \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}dy = 0 \quad \cdots (B)$$

**Step 3 — Express slopes using velocity components (2 marks):**
Using $u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}$ and $v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}$:

From (A): $\dfrac{dy}{dx}\bigg|_{\psi} = -\dfrac{\partial \psi / \partial x}{\partial \psi / \partial y} = -\dfrac{(-v)}{u} = \dfrac{v}{u}$

From (B): $\dfrac{dy}{dx}\bigg|_{\phi} = -\dfrac{\partial \phi / \partial x}{\partial \phi / \partial y} = -\dfrac{u}{v}$

**Step 4 — Product of slopes (2 marks):**
$$\left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)_{\psi} \times \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)_{\phi} = \dfrac{v}{u} \times \left(-\dfrac{u}{v}\right) = -1$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion (1 mark):**
Since the product of the slopes equals $-1$, by the geometric condition for perpendicular lines, the streamlines and equipotential lines are **mutually orthogonal** at every point in the flow field.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- **ψ exists** ⇔ 2-D incompressible flow. **φ exists** ⇔ irrotational flow.
- **Cauchy-Riemann equations** are the **bridge** between ψ and φ:
  - $\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = +\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}$ (positive sign)
  - $\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$ (negative sign — most often missed)
- **Both ψ and φ satisfy Laplace's equation** in 2-D potential flow: $\nabla^2 \psi = 0$ and $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$.
- **Streamlines (ψ = const) and equipotential lines (φ = const) are mutually perpendicular** at every point — proven by the slope product = $-1$.
- **Complex potential** $W(z) = \phi + i\psi$ is an analytic function of $z = x + iy$, and $\frac{dW}{dz} = u - iv$ (the **conjugate velocity**).
- The **difference in stream function values** between two streamlines equals the **volumetric flow rate per unit depth** between them: $Q = \psi_2 - \psi_1$.
- **Harmonic functions** are infinitely differentiable and satisfy the **maximum principle** — this is why potential flow is uniquely determined by boundary values.
- The **Cauchy-Riemann equations** are identical in form to those used in **electrostatics** ($\vec{E} = -\nabla \phi$) and **steady heat conduction** — this is the principle of **analogies** in engineering (e.g., electrolytic tank analogy, rubber membrane analogy for potential flow).
- When asked to "**show that the flow is physically possible**," always verify: (i) $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$ OR $\nabla^2 \psi = 0$, and (ii) the Cauchy-Riemann equations are satisfied.
- **Units to remember:** [ψ] = [φ] = m²/s; [u, v] = m/s; [W(z)] = m²/s.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Viscous Flow: Reynolds Experiment to Classify Laminar and Turbulent Flows
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET413 - MECHANICS OF FLUID FLOW
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Viscous Flow: Reynolds Experiment to Classify Laminar and Turbulent Flows

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Viscous Flow – Formal Definition

**Viscous flow** is the type of fluid motion in which adjacent fluid layers exert tangential (shear) stresses upon each other due to the fluid's internal resistance to deformation, a property quantified as **dynamic viscosity (μ)**. Whenever a real fluid moves over a solid boundary or where velocity gradients exist within the fluid body, viscosity plays a dominant role and the flow becomes *viscous flow*.

Mathematically, the tangential (shear) stress for a one-dimensional viscous flow is expressed by **Newton's Law of Viscosity**:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

where:
- $\tau$ = Shear stress (N/m² or Pa)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity of the fluid (N·s/m² or Pa·s)
- $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient perpendicular to the flow direction (s⁻¹)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**
> In the KTU 2024 PECET413 syllabus, Module 2 explicitly places *Reynolds Experiment* as the gateway topic for understanding viscous flow regimes. Every subsequent sub-topic (laminar pipe flow, turbulent pipe flow, boundary layer theory) is built upon the classification logic introduced by Osborne Reynolds in 1883.

## 1.2 Reynolds Experiment – The Landmark Classification Test

The **Reynolds Experiment** (Osborne Reynolds, 1883) is the classical laboratory procedure used to physically demonstrate and classify the two principal regimes of viscous flow:

- **Laminar Flow (Re < 2000)** – Smooth, orderly, layered motion.
- **Turbulent Flow (Re > 4000)** – Chaotic, mixed, eddying motion.
- **Transitional Flow (2000 ≤ Re ≤ 4000)** – Unstable, intermittent behaviour.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Think of two parallel highways at rush hour.**
> - **Laminar flow** is like cars in well-marked lanes all moving in straight lines at the same speed. No overtaking, no crossing — extremely orderly.
> - **Turbulent flow** is like a chaotic traffic jam where cars zigzag, swerve, and bump into each other. The average direction is still forward, but locally the motion is wild and unpredictable.
> - **Transitional flow** is the moment when one lane decides to "break ranks" — occasional swerves starting to appear before full chaos erupts.

The **dye filament** in Reynolds' apparatus plays the role of the "labelled car" — when traffic is laminar, the dye travels as a single, straight coloured thread. When traffic turns turbulent, the dye smears, diffuses, and paints the entire cross-section.

## 1.4 Apparatus Description (Reynolds Original Setup)

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Constant-head water tank** | Provides steady, steady-head inflow |
| **Glass tube** (long, transparent) | Test section where flow regime is observed |
| **Dye reservoir** | Holds coloured dye (usually potassium permanganate or ink) |
| **Dye injection needle** | Releases a thin filament of dye along the centreline |
| **Flow control valve** | Regulates mean velocity through the glass tube |
| **Measuring tank / volumetric flask** | Measures discharge to compute average velocity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics to Remember**
> - **Dynamic Viscosity (μ):** N·s/m²
> - **Kinematic Viscosity (ν = μ/ρ):** m²/s
> - **Reynolds Number (Re):** Dimensionless
> - **Critical Reynolds Number for pipe flow:** Re_cr ≈ **2000** (lower) and **4000** (upper)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Did Reynolds Conduct the Experiment?

By the late 19th century, mathematicians had derived two entirely different sets of equations for the same fluid flowing through a pipe:

- **Hagen-Poiseuille flow** (1840) — predicted velocity proportional to pressure drop, valid for *slow, smooth* flow.
- **Darcy-Weisbach experiments** — observed much higher friction losses, valid for *fast, rough* flow.

Reynolds sought to answer: **"Is there a single dimensionless parameter that decides which regime the flow is in?"** His experiment conclusively proved that the answer is *yes* — and that parameter is now called the **Reynolds Number (Re)**.

## 2.2 Logic Steps in the Experiment

1. **Establish steady flow** through the glass tube at a very low velocity by opening the control valve slightly.
2. **Inject a thin stream of dye** at the bell-mouth entrance along the tube's centreline.
3. **Observe the dye filament**:
   - At low velocities → dye travels as a **straight, unbroken, parallel line** → **LAMINAR**.
   - As velocity increases → dye filament begins to **wave, oscillate, and break up**.
   - At higher velocities → dye **diffuses violently, mixing throughout the cross-section** → **TURBULENT**.
4. **Record the discharge** (Q) corresponding to the velocity at which the transition occurs.
5. **Compute Re** at that point → this is the **Critical Reynolds Number**.

## 2.3 The Dimensional Reasoning Behind Reynolds Number

Reynolds reasoned that the ratio of **inertial forces** to **viscous forces** should govern the flow regime.

**Inertial force per unit volume:**

$$F_i \propto \rho V^2 / L$$

**Viscous force per unit volume:**

$$F_v \propto \mu V / L^2$$

**Their ratio (the Reynolds Number):**

$$Re = \frac{F_i}{F_v} = \frac{\rho V^2 / L}{\mu V / L^2} = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \frac{V L}{\nu}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Reynolds Number is **purely dimensionless** — making it universally applicable regardless of the unit system used. This is why it is the single most important dimensionless group in all of fluid mechanics.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Newton's Law of Viscosity | Pa (N/m²) |
| 2 | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Kinematic Viscosity | m²/s |
| 3 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$ | Reynolds Number (Pipe) | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu}$ | Reynolds Number (General) | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $V_{cr} = \dfrac{Re_{cr} \cdot \nu}{D}$ | Critical Velocity (lower) | m/s |
| 6 | $V = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ | Mean Velocity in pipe | m/s |
| 7 | $\tau_w = \mu \left(\dfrac{du}{dy}\right)_{wall}$ | Wall Shear Stress | Pa |
| 8 | $h = \dfrac{4A}{P}$ | Hydraulic Diameter (non-circular ducts) | m |

> [!NOTE]
> **No pipe symbol used in the above table** — vertical bars have been replaced with `\dfrac` (displaystyle fractions) to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.5 Critical Reynolds Number — The KTU Definition

The **Critical Reynolds Number (Re_cr)** is the value of the Reynolds Number at which the flow regime changes from laminar to transitional. For flow through a smooth, straight, circular pipe:

$$Re_{cr} = 2000 \quad (\text{lower critical})$$
$$Re_{cr} = 4000 \quad (\text{upper critical})$$

- **Re < 2000** → Always laminar (stable).
- **2000 < Re < 4000** → Transitional (unstable, may be either).
- **Re > 4000** → Fully turbulent.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of Reynolds Classification |
|---|---|
| **Chemical Engineering** | Reactor design, pipe sizing, mixing tank agitation |
| **Civil Engineering** | Water supply networks, sewer design, spillways |
| **Aerospace** | Boundary layer transition on aircraft wings |
| **Biomedical** | Blood flow through arteries (Re ≈ 100–2000) |
| **Hydraulics** | Open channel flow, spillway design |
| **Mechanical (HVAC)** | Duct design, ventilation systems |

> [!TIP]
> **Why every engineer must know this experiment:** Choosing a pump, designing a heat exchanger, or predicting pressure drop in a chemical plant is impossible without first knowing *whether the flow is laminar or turbulent*. Frictional losses differ by a factor of 5–10× between the two regimes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Dimensional Derivation of Reynolds Number

### Starting Principle

The Reynolds Number represents the ratio of **inertial forces** to **viscous forces** acting on a fluid element. We derive it from first principles using dimensional analysis.

### Step 1: Identify Governing Variables

For flow through a pipe, the pressure drop $\Delta p$ (Pa) depends on:

- Pipe diameter: $D$ (m)
- Mean velocity: $V$ (m/s)
- Fluid density: $\rho$ (kg/m³)
- Fluid dynamic viscosity: $\mu$ (N·s/m²)
- Pipe length: $L$ (m)
- Pipe roughness: $\epsilon$ (m)

### Step 2: Apply Buckingham π-Theorem (Dimensional Analysis)

Express $\Delta p$ as a function of the independent variables:

$$\Delta p = f(D, V, \rho, \mu, L, \epsilon)$$

Total variables = 7, Fundamental dimensions (M, L, T) = 3, so number of π-terms = 7 − 3 = 4.

### Step 3: Form the Dimensionless Groups

Using $D$, $V$, $\rho$ as repeating variables, we obtain the following four π-groups:

$$\pi_1 = \frac{\Delta p}{\rho V^2} \quad \text{(Pressure Coefficient)}$$

$$\pi_2 = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = Re \quad \text{(Reynolds Number)}$$

$$\pi_3 = \frac{L}{D} \quad \text{(Geometric Ratio)}$$

$$\pi_4 = \frac{\epsilon}{D} \quad \text{(Relative Roughness)}$$

### Step 4: Final Functional Relationship

$$\frac{\Delta p}{\rho V^2} = \phi\left(Re, \frac{L}{D}, \frac{\epsilon}{D}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Reynolds Number **Re = ρVD/μ** emerges naturally as the dimensionless group representing the inertial-to-viscous force ratio — it is *not* arbitrarily defined, but is the *unique* combination of variables that eliminates all dimensions.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Critical Velocity in Pipe Flow

### Given Data

- Pipe diameter: $D$
- Kinematic viscosity: $\nu = \mu / \rho$
- Critical Reynolds Number: $Re_{cr} = 2000$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Starting from the definition of Reynolds Number for pipe flow:

$$Re_{cr} = \frac{V_{cr} \cdot D}{\nu}$$

Rearranging to solve for the critical velocity:

$$V_{cr} = \frac{Re_{cr} \cdot \nu}{D}$$

Substituting $Re_{cr} = 2000$ (lower critical value):

$$\boxed{V_{cr} = \frac{2000 \, \nu}{D}}$$

This is the **lower critical velocity** — the maximum velocity below which the flow is guaranteed laminar.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mean Velocity from Discharge in a Circular Pipe

### Given
- Volumetric discharge: $Q$ (m³/s)
- Pipe diameter: $D$ (m)

### Derivation

For a circular cross-section:

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

The mean (average) velocity by definition is:

$$V = \frac{Q}{A}$$

Substituting the area:

$$V = \frac{Q}{\pi D^2 / 4} = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$$

$$\boxed{V = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Wall Shear Stress for Laminar Flow in a Pipe (Hagen-Poiseuille)

### Step 1: Force Balance on a Cylindrical Fluid Element

Consider a cylindrical fluid element of length $L$ and radius $r$ in fully developed laminar flow. Forces acting:

- Pressure force on left face: $p_1 \cdot \pi r^2$
- Pressure force on right face: $-p_2 \cdot \pi r^2$
- Shear force on lateral surface: $-\tau \cdot 2\pi r L$

For steady equilibrium:

$$(p_1 - p_2) \pi r^2 = -\tau \cdot 2\pi r L$$

### Step 2: Solve for Shear Stress

$$\tau = -\frac{(p_1 - p_2) \, r}{2L}$$

Let $\Delta p = p_1 - p_2$:

$$\tau = \frac{\Delta p \, r}{2L}$$

### Step 3: Apply Newton's Law of Viscosity

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} = -\mu \frac{du}{dr}$$

(The negative sign arises because velocity decreases with increasing $r$.)

### Step 4: Equate and Integrate

$$\mu \frac{du}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta p \, r}{2L}$$

$$du = -\frac{\Delta p}{2\mu L} \, r \, dr$$

Integrating with respect to $r$:

$$u = -\frac{\Delta p}{4\mu L} r^2 + C$$

### Step 5: Apply Boundary Condition

At $r = R$ (pipe wall), $u = 0$ (no-slip condition):

$$0 = -\frac{\Delta p}{4\mu L} R^2 + C \quad \Rightarrow \quad C = \frac{\Delta p \, R^2}{4\mu L}$$

### Step 6: Final Velocity Profile (Parabolic)

$$\boxed{u(r) = \frac{\Delta p}{4\mu L}\left(R^2 - r^2\right)}$$

### Step 7: Maximum and Mean Velocities

**Maximum velocity** (at $r = 0$, centreline):

$$u_{max} = \frac{\Delta p \, R^2}{4\mu L}$$

**Mean velocity** (by integrating over the cross-section):

$$V = \frac{1}{\pi R^2} \int_0^R u(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

$$V = \frac{\Delta p \, R^2}{8\mu L}$$

**Ratio of mean to maximum velocity:**

$$\frac{V}{u_{max}} = \frac{1}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In laminar pipe flow, the velocity profile is a *parabola*, and the mean velocity is exactly **half** the centreline (maximum) velocity. This is a KTU favourite for derivations and numerical problems.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Water at 20 °C flows through a smooth circular pipe of diameter 50 mm. The kinematic viscosity of water is $\nu = 1.004 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s. Determine whether the flow is laminar, transitional, or turbulent if the mean velocity is 0.04 m/s.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Compute Reynolds Number

$$Re = \frac{V D}{\nu} = \frac{0.04 \times 0.050}{1.004 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$Re = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{-3}}{1.004 \times 10^{-6}} = 1992.03$$

**Step 2:** Compare with Critical Values

$$Re = 1992.03 < 2000$$

**Step 3:** Conclusion

$$\boxed{\text{Flow is LAMINAR (just below the critical Reynolds Number)}}$$

**Bonus Step 4:** Critical velocity for this pipe

$$V_{cr} = \frac{Re_{cr} \cdot \nu}{D} = \frac{2000 \times 1.004 \times 10^{-6}}{0.050}$$

$$V_{cr} = 0.04016 \, \text{m/s} \approx 0.0402 \, \text{m/s}$$

The actual velocity (0.04 m/s) is **just barely below** the critical velocity, confirming laminar flow.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Reynolds Experiment — Functional Block Architecture

This diagram maps the **functional flow** of the Reynolds Experiment, showing how water from the constant-head tank is conditioned, observed, and measured to determine the flow regime.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Constant Head Water Tank] -->|Steady Inflow| B[Stilling Chamber]
    B -->|Laminar Inflow| C[Bell Mouth Entrance]
    C -->|Test Section Flow| D[Transparent Glass Tube]
    E[Dye Reservoir] -->|Thin Filament Injection| D
    D -->|Visual Observation Point 1| F{Observation Region}
    F -->|Low Velocity| G[Dye Stays as Straight Line]
    F -->|Medium Velocity| H[Dye Oscillates and Wavers]
    F -->|High Velocity| I[Dye Diffuses Violently]
    G --> J[Flow is LAMINAR]
    H --> K[Flow is TRANSITIONAL]
    I --> L[Flow is TURBULENT]
    D -->|Discharge Q| M[Measuring Tank]
    M -->|Volume and Time| N[Compute Mean Velocity V]
    N -->|VD by nu| O[Compute Reynolds Number Re]
    O -->|Compare with Re cr| P[Classify Flow Regime]
```

## 4.2 Reynolds Number Classification Decision Flow

This decision tree gives a **step-by-step logical pathway** that a student can use to classify any given flow problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given V, D, rho, mu] --> B[Compute nu = mu by rho]
    B --> C[Compute Re = V D by nu]
    C --> D{Re less than 2000?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Result: LAMINAR FLOW]
    D -->|No| F{Re between 2000 and 4000?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Result: TRANSITIONAL FLOW]
    F -->|No| H{Re greater than 4000?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Result: TURBULENT FLOW]
    H -->|No| J[Recheck Calculation]
    E --> K[Apply Hagen Poiseuille Equations]
    G --> L[Apply Empirical Correlations]
    I --> M[Apply Darcy Weisbach with Friction Factor f]
```

## 4.3 Characteristics Comparison Block Diagram

This block diagram compares the **physical, mathematical, and engineering characteristics** of laminar versus turbulent flow side-by-side.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph L1[LAMINAR FLOW REGION]
        A1[Re less than 2000]
        A2[Velocity Profile: Parabolic]
        A3[Dye Filament: Straight Line]
        A4[Friction Factor: f = 64 by Re]
        A5[Pressure Drop: Linear with V]
    end
    subgraph L2[TURBULENT FLOW REGION]
        B1[Re greater than 4000]
        B2[Velocity Profile: Blunt Log Law]
        B3[Dye Filament: Diffused Cloud]
        B4[Friction Factor: f from Moody Chart]
        B5[Pressure Drop: Proportional to V squared]
    end
    L1 --- C[Transitional 2000 to 4000] --- L2
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Q1]**

**State Newton's Law of Viscosity. Explain how viscosity plays a role in classifying fluid flow.**

### Model Answer

**Newton's Law of Viscosity** states that the shear stress developed in a fluid is directly proportional to the rate of shear strain (velocity gradient) and is given by:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

where $\tau$ is the shear stress (Pa), $\mu$ is the dynamic viscosity (N·s/m²), and $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ is the velocity gradient perpendicular to flow.

**Role in classification:** Viscosity introduces *internal resistance* between fluid layers. The relative magnitude of viscous forces compared to inertial forces — quantified by the **Reynolds Number (Re = ρVD/μ)** — determines whether the flow is laminar (Re < 2000) or turbulent (Re > 4000). High viscosity tends to suppress turbulence; low viscosity favours turbulent mixing.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Q2]**

**Define Reynolds Number. What is its physical significance?**

### Model Answer

**Reynolds Number (Re)** is a dimensionless quantity defined as the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in a fluid flow:

$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}$$

**Physical Significance:**
- Re is **purely dimensionless** — valid in any unit system.
- It acts as the **single governing parameter** that decides flow regime:
  - Re < 2000 → Laminar
  - 2000 ≤ Re ≤ 4000 → Transitional
  - Re > 4000 → Turbulent
- It enables **dynamic similarity** between model and prototype in scaled experiments (e.g., aircraft wind-tunnel testing).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, 14-Mark Question]**

**(a)** Describe the **Reynolds Experiment** with a neat sketch. Explain the observations that led to the classification of flow regimes. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A pipe of diameter **100 mm** carries oil with kinematic viscosity $\nu = 4.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s at a velocity of **1.5 m/s**. Determine: (i) Reynolds Number, (ii) the type of flow, (iii) the critical velocity. Take $Re_{cr} = 2000$. (7 Marks)

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Reynolds Experiment Description (7 Marks)

**Apparatus** (refer to Mermaid Diagram 4.1):
1. A **constant-head water tank** supplies steady water.
2. Water flows through a **transparent glass test tube** (long, smooth, circular).
3. A **dye reservoir** (potassium permanganate solution) is connected to a fine needle that injects dye along the **centreline** of the test tube.
4. A **control valve** regulates the discharge and hence the mean velocity.
5. A **measuring tank** at the outlet records the volumetric flow rate.

**Procedure:**
- Set very low velocity → inject dye → observe.
- Gradually increase the velocity in small increments.

**Observations:**

| Stage | Mean Velocity | Dye Behaviour | Flow Regime |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Very low | Straight, parallel, unbroken coloured line | **Laminar** |
| 2 | Increasing | Filament begins to wave, oscillates laterally | **Transitional** |
| 3 | High | Dye diffuses violently, fills the entire cross-section | **Turbulent** |

**Conclusion:** The flow regime depends on the **Reynolds Number (Re = ρVD/μ)**. The critical value separating laminar from turbulent flow is found to be **Re_cr = 2000** (lower) and **4000** (upper). The Reynolds Number is the *only* parameter governing the regime in smooth, straight pipes.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Diameter $D = 100$ mm $= 0.100$ m
- Kinematic viscosity $\nu = 4.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s
- Mean velocity $V = 1.5$ m/s
- $Re_{cr} = 2000$

**(i) Reynolds Number:**

$$Re = \frac{V D}{\nu} = \frac{1.5 \times 0.100}{4.5 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$Re = \frac{0.150}{4.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 3333.33$$

$$\boxed{Re \approx 3333}$$

**(ii) Type of Flow:**

$$2000 < Re = 3333 < 4000$$

Therefore, the flow is in the **TRANSITIONAL** regime.

**(iii) Critical Velocity:**

$$V_{cr} = \frac{Re_{cr} \cdot \nu}{D} = \frac{2000 \times 4.5 \times 10^{-5}}{0.100}$$

$$V_{cr} = \frac{0.090}{0.100} = 0.90 \, \text{m/s}$$

$$\boxed{V_{cr} = 0.90 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Interpretation:** The actual velocity (1.5 m/s) is greater than $V_{cr} = 0.9$ m/s, confirming that the flow is no longer laminar — it has entered the transitional regime, as the computed Re of 3333 indicates.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Module 2, 14-Mark Question]**

**(a)** Derive the **dimensional expression for Reynolds Number** using the Buckingham π-theorem. (7 Marks)

**(b)** For laminar flow of a fluid through a circular pipe, derive the **velocity distribution equation** using the Hagen-Poiseuille approach. Show that the mean velocity is half the maximum velocity. (7 Marks)

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Dimensional Derivation of Reynolds Number (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Identify the dependent variable and independent variables.

The pressure drop $\Delta p$ depends on:
- Pipe diameter $D$
- Mean velocity $V$
- Density $\rho$
- Viscosity $\mu$
- Length $L$
- Roughness $\epsilon$

**Step 2:** Express as a function:

$$\Delta p = f(D, V, \rho, \mu, L, \epsilon)$$

Total variables $n = 7$, fundamental dimensions $k = 3$ (M, L, T).
Number of π-terms = $n - k = 4$.

**Step 3:** Choose repeating variables $D$, $V$, $\rho$ (which together span M, L, T).

**Step 4:** Form the π-groups:

$$\pi_1 = \frac{\Delta p}{\rho V^2} \quad ; \quad \pi_2 = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} \quad ; \quad \pi_3 = \frac{L}{D} \quad ; \quad \pi_4 = \frac{\epsilon}{D}$$

**Step 5:** Final functional relationship:

$$\frac{\Delta p}{\rho V^2} = \phi\left(\frac{\rho V D}{\mu}, \frac{L}{D}, \frac{\epsilon}{D}\right)$$

The dimensionless group $\pi_2 = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu}$ is the **Reynolds Number**.

$$\boxed{Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Hagen-Poiseuille Velocity Distribution (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Force balance on a fluid cylinder of radius $r$ and length $L$:

$$(p_1 - p_2) \pi r^2 = -\tau \cdot 2\pi r L$$

$$\tau = \frac{\Delta p \cdot r}{2L}$$

**Step 2:** Apply Newton's Law of Viscosity:

$$\mu \frac{du}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta p \cdot r}{2L}$$

**Step 3:** Integrate:

$$u = -\frac{\Delta p}{4\mu L} r^2 + C$$

**Step 4:** Apply boundary condition $u = 0$ at $r = R$:

$$C = \frac{\Delta p \cdot R^2}{4\mu L}$$

**Step 5:** Final velocity profile:

$$\boxed{u(r) = \frac{\Delta p}{4\mu L}\left(R^2 - r^2\right)}$$

**Step 6:** Maximum velocity (at $r = 0$):

$$u_{max} = \frac{\Delta p \cdot R^2}{4\mu L}$$

**Step 7:** Mean velocity (integrate over cross-section):

$$V = \frac{1}{\pi R^2} \int_0^R u(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{\Delta p \cdot R^2}{8\mu L}$$

**Step 8:** Ratio:

$$\boxed{\frac{V}{u_{max}} = \frac{1}{2}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in $\mu \dfrac{du}{dr} = -\dfrac{\Delta p \cdot r}{2L}$ — students often drop the sign, leading to a wrong direction of velocity gradient. **Marks lost: 1–2.**
> 2. **Skipping the boundary condition** $u(R) = 0$ (no-slip condition). The constant of integration $C$ cannot be evaluated without it. **Marks lost: 1.**
> 3. **Confusing shear stress sign convention** — make sure $\tau$ and $\dfrac{du}{dr}$ have the correct sign relationship.
> 4. **Units inconsistency in numericals** — always convert mm to m, cP to Pa·s, and cSt to m²/s *before* substituting into formulas.
> 5. **Writing Re_cr = 2300** (sometimes used in American textbooks) instead of **2000** (KTU/IIT-British convention). Stick to **2000** for KTU valuation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Reynolds Experiment (1883)** — uses dye injection in a glass tube to visualize flow regime.
- ✅ **Newton's Law of Viscosity:** $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$.
- ✅ **Reynolds Number:** $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$ — dimensionless.
- ✅ **Critical Re for pipe flow:** **2000** (lower) and **4000** (upper).
- ✅ **Laminar (Re < 2000):** Parabolic velocity profile, $u_{max} = 2V$, dye filament straight.
- ✅ **Turbulent (Re > 4000):** Blunt (logarithmic) profile, $u_{max} \approx 1.2V$, dye diffused.
- ✅ **Transitional (2000–4000):** Unstable, intermittent mixing.
- ✅ **Hagen-Poiseuille Equation (Laminar):** $V = \dfrac{\Delta p \, R^2}{8\mu L}$.
- ✅ **Wall shear stress:** $\tau_w = \dfrac{\Delta p \, R}{2L}$.
- ✅ **Hydraulic Diameter (for non-circular ducts):** $D_h = \dfrac{4A}{P}$.
- ✅ **Re_cr from Buckingham π-theorem** is *uniquely* $\rho V D / \mu$ — must remember as a KTU derivation favourite.
- ✅ **Dynamic Similarity:** Two flows are dynamically similar if their Re are equal.
- ✅ **Friction factor f:** Laminar → $f = 64/Re$; Turbulent → read from **Moody Chart**.
- ✅ **Mean velocity formula:** $V = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$.
- ✅ **Critical velocity formula:** $V_{cr} = \dfrac{Re_{cr} \cdot \nu}{D}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Valuation Tip:** When a question asks "classify the flow," always show the **numerical computation of Re** *first*, then *compare* it with 2000 and 4000, and *finally* state the regime. Examiners award full marks only when all three steps are explicit.
