topics.title: Significance of Reynolds Number
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET413 - MECHANICS OF FLUID FLOW
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Significance of Reynolds Number

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Reynolds Number (Re)** is a fundamental **dimensionless quantity** in fluid mechanics that represents the ratio of **inertial forces** to **viscous forces** within a fluid flow. It serves as the primary criterion for predicting the nature of flow — whether it will be **laminar, transitional, or turbulent**.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$Re = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \frac{V L}{\nu}$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = Density of the fluid (kg/m³)
- $V$ = Mean velocity of flow (m/s)
- $L$ = Characteristic length (m) — typically diameter for pipe flow
- $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity of the fluid (N·s/m² or Pa·s)
- $\nu$ = Kinematic viscosity of the fluid (m²/s)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Reynolds Number is **purely dimensionless** — it has **no units**. This property is what makes it universally applicable across different fluids, scales, and geometries in engineering analysis.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The River vs. Honey Analogy

Imagine pouring water (low viscosity) versus honey (high viscosity) through the same pipe at the same speed:
- **Water** flows smoothly in parallel layers → **Inertia dominates** → High Re
- **Honey** flows sluggishly, layers stick together → **Viscosity dominates** → Low Re

Think of Reynolds Number as a **"tug-of-war" score** between two forces:
- **Inertial forces** want the flow to keep moving forward and create disturbances (turbulence).
- **Viscous forces** want to keep the flow calm, smooth, and orderly (laminar).

> [!NOTE]
> **If Re is HIGH** → Inertia wins → Flow becomes **turbulent** (chaotic, mixing)
> **If Re is LOW** → Viscosity wins → Flow remains **laminar** (smooth, layered)

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Critical Reynolds Number for pipe flow (Re_cr) = 2000** (Lower limit of transition)
- **Upper transition limit ≈ 4000**
- Below 2000: **Laminar flow**
- 2000–4000: **Transitional flow**
- Above 4000: **Turbulent flow**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These thresholds are valid **only for flow in circular pipes** with the characteristic length taken as the **pipe diameter (D)**. For other geometries (flat plates, open channels, etc.), the critical value differs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Interpretation — The "Why" Behind Reynolds Number

The Reynolds Number is fundamentally a **ratio of forces** at the microscopic level of fluid motion:

$$Re = \frac{\text{Inertial Forces}}{\text{Viscous Forces}} = \frac{F_i}{F_v}$$

This can be derived by examining the Navier–Stokes equations of motion and identifying the dominant terms:

- **Inertial force per unit volume** ≈ $\rho V \cdot \frac{V}{L} = \frac{\rho V^2}{L}$
- **Viscous force per unit volume** ≈ $\mu \cdot \frac{V}{L^2}$

Taking the ratio:

$$\frac{F_i}{F_v} = \frac{\rho V^2 / L}{\mu V / L^2} = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = Re$$

### 2.2 Significance in Engineering Practice

The Reynolds Number holds immense engineering significance in the following areas:

**1. Flow Regime Prediction**
- Engineers use Re to determine whether a pipeline will have smooth (laminar) or chaotic (turbulent) flow before construction.
- This directly impacts the **frictional head loss** calculations.

**2. Friction Factor Correlation**
- The **Moody Chart** and **Darcy-Weisbach equation** both use Re as the primary input to determine the friction factor $f$.
- $h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$

**3. Model Analysis and Similitude**
- Dynamic similarity between a model and prototype is achieved when their Reynolds Numbers are equal: **Re_model = Re_prototype**
- This allows engineers to test aircraft, ships, and pipelines using small-scale models.

**4. Selection of Hydraulic Machinery**
- Pumps, turbines, and valves are designed for specific Re ranges.
- Flow meters (orifice, venturi) have calibration curves dependent on Re.

**5. Boundary Layer Analysis**
- On flat plates and aerodynamic surfaces, Re determines whether the boundary layer is laminar or turbulent, affecting **drag and heat transfer**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Expression** | **Application** | **Units** |
|-------------|----------------|-----------------|-----------|
| Reynolds Number (General) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu}$ | All fluid flows | Dimensionless |
| Reynolds Number (Kinematic form) | $Re = \dfrac{V L}{\nu}$ | When $\nu$ is known | Dimensionless |
| Reynolds Number for Pipe Flow | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu}$ | Flow in circular pipes | Dimensionless |
| Reynolds Number for Open Channel | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V R_h}{\mu}$ | Flow in open channels ($R_h$ = hydraulic radius) | Dimensionless |
| Reynolds Number for Flat Plate | $Re_L = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu}$ | Boundary layer over flat plate | Dimensionless |
| Reynolds Number for Sphere/Cylinder | $Re_D = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu}$ | Flow past submerged bodies | Dimensionless |
| Kinematic Viscosity relation | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Convert between forms | m²/s |
| Critical Re (Pipe - Laminar limit) | $Re_{cr} = 2000$ | Lower bound of transition | Dimensionless |
| Critical Re (Pipe - Turbulent limit) | $Re_{cr} = 4000$ | Upper bound of transition | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> For **closed conduits (pipes)**, use **diameter (D)** as the characteristic length.
> For **open channels**, use **hydraulic radius ($R_h$)**, where $R_h = \dfrac{A}{P}$ (Area / Wetted Perimeter).
> For **flat plates**, use **length from leading edge (L)**.

### 2.4 Flow Classification Logic (Step-by-Step Reasoning)

1. **Step 1:** Identify the geometry (pipe, channel, plate, submerged body).
2. **Step 2:** Determine the appropriate characteristic length (D, $R_h$, L).
3. **Step 3:** Compute $Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu}$.
4. **Step 4:** Compare with the **critical Re** for that geometry.
5. **Step 5:** Classify as laminar / transitional / turbulent.
6. **Step 6:** Use the appropriate empirical formula (Hagen-Poiseuille for laminar, Darcy-Weisbach + Moody chart for turbulent).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Reynolds Number as Ratio of Forces

**Starting Point:** The general Navier–Stokes equation for an incompressible fluid:

$$\rho \left( \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + \vec{V} \cdot \nabla \vec{V} \right) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}$$

**Step 1:** Identify the inertial term on the left-hand side.
The convective acceleration term is:

$$\rho \left( \vec{V} \cdot \nabla \vec{V} \right) \sim \rho \cdot V \cdot \frac{V}{L} = \frac{\rho V^2}{L}$$

This represents the **inertial force per unit volume** — the fluid's resistance to changes in motion.

**Step 2:** Identify the viscous term on the right-hand side.
The viscous diffusion term is:

$$\mu \nabla^2 \vec{V} \sim \mu \cdot \frac{V}{L^2} = \frac{\mu V}{L^2}$$

This represents the **viscous force per unit volume** — internal friction resisting motion.

**Step 3:** Form the dimensionless ratio (force per unit volume basis):

$$\frac{\text{Inertial force/volume}}{\text{Viscous force/volume}} = \frac{\rho V^2 / L}{\mu V / L^2}$$

**Step 4:** Simplify the expression algebraically:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\rho V^2 / L}{\mu V / L^2} &= \frac{\rho V^2}{L} \times \frac{L^2}{\mu V} \\
&= \frac{\rho V^2 \cdot L^2}{L \cdot \mu V} \\
&= \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = Re
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Final result — the Reynolds Number:

$$\boxed{Re = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \frac{V L}{\nu}}$$

### 3.2 Proof of Dimensionless Nature

To verify that Re is dimensionless, we perform dimensional analysis using the **MLT system** (Mass, Length, Time):

| **Variable** | **Symbol** | **Dimensions** |
|--------------|------------|----------------|
| Density | $\rho$ | $M L^{-3}$ |
| Velocity | $V$ | $L T^{-1}$ |
| Length | $L$ | $L$ |
| Dynamic Viscosity | $\mu$ | $M L^{-1} T^{-1}$ |

**Step 1:** Substitute dimensions into Re:

$$[Re] = \frac{[\rho] [V] [L]}{[\mu]}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the dimensional symbols:

$$\begin{aligned}
[Re] &= \frac{(M L^{-3}) \times (L T^{-1}) \times (L)}{(M L^{-1} T^{-1})} \\
&= \frac{M L^{-3} \cdot L T^{-1} \cdot L}{M L^{-1} T^{-1}} \\
&= \frac{M L^{-1} T^{-1}}{M L^{-1} T^{-1}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** All terms cancel, leaving:

$$[Re] = M^0 L^0 T^0 = 1 \text{ (dimensionless)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The dimensionless nature of Re is the **foundation of dynamic similitude**. It allows engineers to use small laboratory models (with different fluids) to predict the behavior of full-scale prototypes (e.g., submarines, aircraft, pipelines).

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Identifying Flow Regime

**Problem:** Water ($\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.001 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s) flows through a pipe of diameter 50 mm at a velocity of 2 m/s. Determine the flow regime.

**Step 1:** List the given data:
- $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³
- $\mu = 1.001 \times 10^{-3}$ N·s/m²
- $D = 50$ mm $= 0.05$ m
- $V = 2$ m/s

**Step 2:** Apply the Reynolds Number formula:

$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the values:

$$\begin{aligned}
Re &= \frac{1000 \times 2 \times 0.05}{1.001 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= \frac{100}{1.001 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= 99900.1 \approx 99900
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Compare with critical values:
- Since $Re = 99900 \gg 4000$, the flow is **fully turbulent**.

**Step 5:** Conclusion:
> The flow in the pipe is **turbulent**, and the engineer should use the **Darcy-Weisbach equation with the Moody Chart** for accurate head loss calculations.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Reynolds Number — Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the logical flow for determining and applying the Reynolds Number in engineering practice:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Flow Geometry] --> B[Select Characteristic Length]
    B --> B1[Pipe: D]
    B --> B2[Open Channel: Rh]
    B --> B3[Flat Plate: L]
    B --> B4[Submerged Body: D]
    B1 --> C[Compute Re = rhoVL/mu]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    C --> D{Compare with Critical Re}
    D -->|Re less than 2000| E[LAMINAR FLOW]
    D -->|Re between 2000 and 4000| F[TRANSITIONAL FLOW]
    D -->|Re greater than 4000| G[TURBULENT FLOW]
    E --> H1[Use Hagen-Poiseuille Equation]
    F --> H2[Empirical Correlations]
    G --> H3[Use Darcy-Weisbach with Moody Chart]
    H1 --> I[Engineering Decision]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
```

### 4.2 Force Balance Block Diagram

The following block diagram illustrates the physical meaning of Reynolds Number as a competition between two fundamental forces:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FLUID FLOW SYSTEM] --> B{Inertial Forces}
    A --> C{Viscous Forces}
    B -->|Dominate: High Re| D[TURBULENT FLOW]
    C -->|Dominate: Low Re| E[LAMINAR FLOW]
    D --> F[Chaotic, Mixing, High Energy Loss]
    E --> G[Smooth, Layered, Predictable]
    F --> H[Engineering Applications]
    G --> H
    H --> H1[Pipeline Design]
    H --> H2[Pump Selection]
    H --> H3[Aerodynamics]
    H --> H4[Heat Exchangers]
    H --> H5[Model Testing]
```

### 4.3 Critical Reynolds Number Reference Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reynolds Number Re] --> B[Laminar Region]
    A --> C[Transitional Region]
    A --> D[Turbulent Region]
    B --> B1[Re less than 2000]
    B --> B2[Smooth Parallel Layers]
    B --> B3[Viscous Forces Dominate]
    C --> C1[2000 less than Re less than 4000]
    C --> C2[Unstable, Intermittent]
    C --> C3[Flow Fluctuations Begin]
    D --> D1[Re greater than 4000]
    D --> D2[Chaotic Eddies and Swirls]
    D --> D3[Inertial Forces Dominate]
```

> [!NOTE]
> These schematic diagrams provide a **functional architecture view** of how the Reynolds Number governs flow classification and downstream engineering decisions. They are designed to be KTU board-exam friendly for quick visual recall.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Reynolds Number. What is its physical significance?**

**Model Answer:**
The Reynolds Number is a dimensionless quantity defined as the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in a fluid flow.

$$Re = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \frac{V L}{\nu}$$

**Physical Significance:**
- It predicts whether the flow will be **laminar, transitional, or turbulent**.
- It indicates the **relative dominance** of inertia versus viscosity.
- It enables **dynamic similitude** in model-to-prototype scaling.
- For pipe flow, if $Re < 2000$ → laminar; $Re > 4000$ → turbulent.

**Marks Distribution (KTU Pattern):**
- Definition: 1 mark
- Formula: 1 mark
- Significance (any 2 points): 1 mark

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the values of critical Reynolds Number for flow in a pipe and classify the flow regimes.**

**Model Answer:**
For flow in a circular pipe (characteristic length = diameter):

| **Re Range** | **Flow Regime** |
|--------------|-----------------|
| $Re < 2000$ | Laminar |
| $2000 \le Re \le 4000$ | Transitional |
| $Re > 4000$ | Turbulent |

- **Lower critical Re = 2000**
- **Upper critical Re = 4000**

**Marks Distribution (KTU Pattern):**
- Three regimes with values: 2 marks
- Statement of critical limits: 1 mark

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students often write Reynolds Number formula without specifying the **characteristic length** (D vs L vs $R_h$). Always clarify which length is used.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Many students forget that critical Re = 2000 applies **only to pipe flow**. For open channels, it is ~500; for flat plates, it is ~$5 \times 10^5$.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting units conversion (e.g., mm to m, cP to Pa·s). Examiner deducts 1 mark for unit errors.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Writing "Re is unitless" without showing dimensional proof loses marks. Always prove dimensionless nature using [M, L, T] analysis.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**Q.A (a)** Derive the expression for Reynolds Number as a ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces. Show that it is dimensionless. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Consider the Navier–Stokes equation for incompressible flow:

$$\rho \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}$$

**Step 2:** Estimate the order of magnitude of inertial term:

$$\rho \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} \sim \rho \cdot \frac{V}{t} \sim \rho \cdot \frac{V^2}{L}$$

**Step 3:** Estimate the order of magnitude of viscous term:

$$\mu \nabla^2 \vec{V} \sim \mu \cdot \frac{V}{L^2}$$

**Step 4:** Form the ratio:

$$\begin{aligned}
Re &= \frac{\text{Inertial force/volume}}{\text{Viscous force/volume}} \\
&= \frac{\rho V^2 / L}{\mu V / L^2} \\
&= \frac{\rho V L}{\mu}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Dimensional check:

$$[Re] = \frac{[M L^{-3}][L T^{-1}][L]}{[M L^{-1} T^{-1}]} = \frac{M L^{-1} T^{-1}}{M L^{-1} T^{-1}} = 1$$

Hence, Re is **dimensionless**. ✓

**Marks Distribution:**
- Navier-Stokes reference: 1 mark
- Inertial force estimation: 2 marks
- Viscous force estimation: 2 marks
- Ratio and simplification: 1 mark
- Dimensional proof: 1 mark

---

**Q.A (b)** An oil of density 850 kg/m³ and dynamic viscosity $0.08$ N·s/m² flows through a pipe of diameter 15 cm at a rate of 0.025 m³/s. Determine: (i) the Reynolds Number, and (ii) the type of flow. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Given data:
- $\rho = 850$ kg/m³
- $\mu = 0.08$ N·s/m²
- $D = 15$ cm $= 0.15$ m
- $Q = 0.025$ m³/s

**Step 2:** Calculate the cross-sectional area:

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.15)^2}{4} = 0.01767 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the mean velocity:

$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.025}{0.01767} = 1.414 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate Reynolds Number:

$$\begin{aligned}
Re &= \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} \\
&= \frac{850 \times 1.414 \times 0.15}{0.08} \\
&= \frac{180.05}{0.08} \\
&= 2250.6 \approx 2251
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Classify the flow:
- Since $2000 < Re = 2251 < 4000$, the flow is in the **transitional regime**.

**Marks Distribution:**
- Data extraction: 1 mark
- Area calculation: 1 mark
- Velocity calculation: 1 mark
- Re calculation: 2 marks
- Flow classification: 1 mark
- Final conclusion with units: 1 mark

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2, Alternative]**

**Q.B (a)** Explain the significance of Reynolds Number in fluid mechanics with reference to: (i) flow regime prediction, (ii) dynamic similarity in model studies, and (iii) selection of friction factor. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Flow Regime Prediction (2.5 Marks):**
The Reynolds Number is the **primary criterion** for classifying flow. Based on the value of Re:
- $Re < 2000$: **Laminar** — flow in smooth parallel layers, predictable velocity profile (parabolic in pipes).
- $2000 \le Re \le 4000$: **Transitional** — flow is unstable and unpredictable.
- $Re > 4000$: **Turbulent** — chaotic motion with eddies, high mixing and energy loss.

This classification directly determines which empirical equations to use for head loss calculations.

**(ii) Dynamic Similarity in Model Studies (2.5 Marks):**
For two geometrically similar flow systems to be **dynamically similar**, their Reynolds Numbers must be equal:

$$Re_{model} = Re_{prototype}$$

This is the **primary similarity law** for flows where viscous effects dominate. It allows:
- Testing aircraft in wind tunnels using scaled models.
- Testing ship hulls in towing tanks.
- Hydraulic model studies of dams, spillways, and rivers.
- Using convenient fluids (air, water) in the lab to simulate prototype conditions (oil, high-speed gases).

**(iii) Selection of Friction Factor (2 Marks):**
The friction factor $f$ in the Darcy-Weisbach equation is **dependent on Re** (and relative roughness for turbulent flow):

$$h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

- For **laminar flow**: $f = \dfrac{64}{Re}$ (theoretical from Hagen-Poiseuille).
- For **turbulent flow**: $f$ is read from the **Moody Chart** at the given Re and relative roughness $\epsilon/D$.

Thus, Re is the **key input parameter** to determine frictional losses in pipe design.

---

**Q.B (b)** Water at 20°C flows through a 100 mm diameter pipe at an average velocity of 0.5 m/s. For water at 20°C: $\rho = 998$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s. Determine: (i) the Reynolds Number, (ii) the type of flow, and (iii) the friction factor using $f = 64/Re$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Given data:
- $\rho = 998$ kg/m³
- $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ N·s/m²
- $D = 100$ mm $= 0.1$ m
- $V = 0.5$ m/s

**Step 2:** Calculate Reynolds Number:

$$\begin{aligned}
Re &= \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} \\
&= \frac{998 \times 0.5 \times 0.1}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= \frac{49.9}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= 49800.4 \approx 49800
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Classify the flow:
- Since $Re = 49800 \gg 4000$, the flow is **fully turbulent**.

**Step 4:** Calculate the friction factor (laminar formula given in question):

$$f = \frac{64}{Re} = \frac{64}{49800} = 1.285 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 5:** Comment: Although the laminar formula $f = 64/Re$ was used as requested, in actual turbulent flow the friction factor would be obtained from the **Moody Chart** for the correct Re and pipe roughness.

**Marks Distribution:**
- Data and unit conversion: 1 mark
- Re calculation: 2 marks
- Flow classification: 1 mark
- Friction factor formula: 1 mark
- Friction factor calculation: 1 mark
- Final conclusion: 1 mark

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Significance of Reynolds Number**

- **Definition:** $Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \dfrac{V L}{\nu}$ — **dimensionless**.
- **Physical meaning:** Ratio of **inertial forces** to **viscous forces**.
- **Characteristic length depends on geometry:**
  - Pipe flow → Diameter (D)
  - Open channel → Hydraulic radius ($R_h = A/P$)
  - Flat plate → Length from leading edge (L)
  - Submerged body → Diameter (D)
- **Critical values for circular pipes:**
  - $Re < 2000$ → **Laminar**
  - $2000 \le Re \le 4000$ → **Transitional**
  - $Re > 4000$ → **Turbulent**
- **Critical values for other geometries (commonly tested):**
  - Open channel: $Re \approx 500$ (laminar-turbulent transition)
  - Flat plate: $Re_L \approx 5 \times 10^5$ (boundary layer transition)
  - Sphere: $Re \approx 0.1$ to $2 \times 10^5$ (drag crisis at high Re)
- **Engineering utilities:**
  1. Flow regime prediction
  2. Friction factor determination (Moody Chart)
  3. Dynamic similitude ($Re_m = Re_p$)
  4. Pipe sizing and pump selection
  5. Boundary layer and drag analysis
- **Common KTU board exam traps:**
  - Always mention the **characteristic length** when defining Re.
  - Always **state units conversion** (mm → m, cP → Pa·s).
  - Always **prove dimensionless nature** when derivation is asked.
  - Critical Re = 2000 is **only for pipe flow**; don't generalize.
- **Memory trick:** *"Re = Inertia / Viscosity = ρVL/μ. Low Re = sticky flow; High Re = chaotic flow."*
- **Friction factor relation (laminar):** $f = \dfrac{64}{Re}$
- **Moody Chart input:** Re and relative roughness $\epsilon/D$ → output: $f$ (turbulent).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Critical Reynolds Number
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET413 - MECHANICS OF FLUID FLOW
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Critical Reynolds Number

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Critical Reynolds Number** (often denoted as **Re_c** or **Re_cr**) is defined as the *specific value of the Reynolds Number at which the flow regime in a conduit undergoes a fundamental transition* — from **laminar flow** (smooth, orderly, layered streamlines) to **turbulent flow** (chaotic, random, eddying motion), or vice versa.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> *"The Critical Reynolds Number is the Reynolds Number at which the flow pattern changes from laminar to turbulent flow in a pipe or conduit."*

In the context of flow through a **circular pipe** (the most commonly tested case in KTU exams), two boundary values are recognized:

- **Lower Critical Reynolds Number (Re_c,lower)** = **2000** — Below this value, flow is *always* laminar.
- **Upper Critical Reynolds Number (Re_c,upper)** = **4000** — Above this value, flow is *always* turbulent.

Between these two limits lies a **transition zone** (2000 < Re < 4000) where the flow may be either laminar or turbulent depending on initial disturbances, entrance conditions, surface roughness, and external vibrations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a calm, lazy river flowing slowly through a smooth channel…**
>
> If you watch the leaves floating on the water, they move in **smooth, parallel paths** — never crossing each other, never swirling. This is **laminar flow**.
>
> Now imagine increasing the velocity of the river (e.g., during a flood or after releasing a dam). At some point, the water begins to **churn, swirl, and form eddies**. The leaves now tumble chaotically. The boundary at which this **calm order breaks into disorder** is the **Critical Reynolds Number**.
>
> Physically, the Reynolds Number represents the **ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces** in the fluid. When inertia (the tendency of fluid to keep moving and form eddies) overwhelms viscosity (the "stickiness" or damping action of the fluid), turbulence is born.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Key Metrics

- **Standard Value for Pipe Flow**: Re_c ≈ **2000** (lower) to **4000** (upper)
- **Geometry Matters**: For flow between parallel plates, Re_c ≈ **1000** (based on gap width)
- **For Flow Over a Flat Plate**: Re_c ≈ **5 × 10⁵** (based on distance from leading edge)
- **For Flow Around a Sphere**: Re_c ≈ **1** (very low due to strong wake formation)

### 1.4 Why This Number Matters in Engineering

| Engineering Field | Significance |
|-------------------|--------------|
| **Chemical Process Piping** | Determines whether to use Darcy's law (laminar) or empirical friction factor charts (turbulent) for pressure drop calculations. |
| **Aerospace (Wings, Bodies)** | Predicts whether boundary layer remains attached (laminar) or separates (turbulent) — critical for drag estimation. |
| **Hydraulic Engineering** | Sediment transport, pipe design for water supply, and open channel flow all depend on regime identification. |
| **Biomedical (Blood Flow)** | Distinguishes between normal arterial flow and pathological turbulence near stenoses. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The Critical Reynolds Number is *not* a universal constant — it is a **dimensionless transition value** that is **highly dependent on geometry, surface roughness, and flow disturbance levels**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Reynolds Number — Foundation Concept

The Reynolds Number is the **governing dimensionless parameter** of viscous flow analysis. It is defined as:

$$Re = \frac{\rho \cdot V \cdot D}{\mu} = \frac{V \cdot D}{\nu}$$

where:
- $\rho$ = density of the fluid (kg/m³)
- $V$ = mean velocity of flow (m/s)
- $D$ = characteristic length (pipe diameter for internal flow) (m)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of the fluid (N·s/m² or Pa·s)
- $\nu = \mu / \rho$ = kinematic viscosity (m²/s)

**Physical Interpretation**: $Re$ is the ratio of **inertial forces** to **viscous forces**:

$$Re = \frac{F_{inertial}}{F_{viscous}} = \frac{\rho V^2 D^2}{\mu V D / D^2 \cdot D^2} = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu}$$

- **Low Re** → Viscous forces dominate → Flow is **laminar** (smooth, predictable).
- **High Re** → Inertial forces dominate → Flow is **turbulent** (chaotic, eddying).

### 2.2 Origin of the Critical Reynolds Number — Osborne Reynolds' Experiment (1883)

Professor **Osborne Reynolds** of Manchester, England, conducted a classical experiment that established the transition criterion:

1. A horizontal glass tube was immersed in a tank of water.
2. A **dye injector** introduced a thin filament of colored water at the entrance of the tube.
3. By varying the **flow rate** (and hence the velocity $V$), Reynolds observed three distinct behaviors:

   - **At low velocities**: The dye filament remained a **sharp, straight line** along the tube axis → **Laminar Flow**.
   - **At a critical velocity**: The dye filament began to **oscillate and break up** somewhere in the middle of the tube → **Transition Flow**.
   - **At high velocities**: The dye filament **immediately dispersed** across the entire cross-section, mixing chaotically → **Turbulent Flow**.

4. By measuring the velocity at the point of transition, Reynolds calculated the **dimensionless ratio** $Re_c$ and found it to be approximately **2000** (lower) to **4000** (upper) for circular pipes.

### 2.3 Factors Influencing the Critical Reynolds Number

| Factor | Effect on Re_c |
|--------|----------------|
| **Entrance/Entry Length** | A short, sharp-edged entrance promotes turbulence (lowers effective Re_c). A well-rounded, long entrance stabilizes laminar flow. |
| **Surface Roughness** | Rougher walls trip the boundary layer earlier, lowering Re_c. |
| **External Vibrations** | Mechanical disturbances can trigger transition at lower Re values. |
| **Fluid Type** | The numerical value of Re_c is the same for all Newtonian fluids in the same geometry (since Re is dimensionless). |
| **Geometry** | Pipes, parallel plates, annuli, and flat plates each have their own characteristic Re_c. |
| **Pressure Gradient** | Adverse pressure gradients (dp/dx > 0) promote transition. |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready formula reference** for problems on Critical Reynolds Number.

| S.No. | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|:-----:|---------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu}$ | General Reynolds Number for pipe flow | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $Re = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$ | Reynolds Number using kinematic viscosity | $\nu$ in m²/s |
| 3 | $Re_c = \dfrac{\rho V_c D}{\mu} = 2000$ | Lower Critical Reynolds Number (pipe flow) | $V_c$ = critical velocity |
| 4 | $Re_c = 4000$ | Upper Critical Reynolds Number (pipe flow) | Engineering design criterion |
| 5 | $V_c = \dfrac{Re_c \cdot \mu}{\rho \cdot D} = \dfrac{Re_c \cdot \nu}{D}$ | Critical velocity at transition | m/s |
| 6 | $Re_c = \dfrac{V_c \cdot D}{\nu}$ | Solved for critical velocity | Alternative form |
| 7 | $f = \dfrac{64}{Re}$ | Friction factor (laminar, Darcy) | Valid for Re < 2000 |
| 8 | $Re_{x,c} = \dfrac{\rho V x_c}{\mu} = 5 \times 10^5$ | Critical Re for flat plate (x = distance from leading edge) | For boundary layer transition |
| 9 | $Re_c = 1000$ | Critical Re for flow between parallel plates | Based on gap width |
| 10 | $Re_{D_h} = \dfrac{\rho V D_h}{\mu}$ | Reynolds Number using hydraulic diameter $D_h$ | For non-circular ducts |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility — The Three Regimes

The Critical Reynolds Number divides pipe flow into three operational regimes:

1. **Laminar Zone** ($Re < 2000$):
   - Head loss is **proportional to velocity** ($h_f \propto V$).
   - Friction factor is $f = 64/Re$.
   - Velocity profile is **parabolic** (Poiseuille flow).

2. **Transition Zone** ($2000 \le Re \le 4000$):
   - Flow is **unstable** and unpredictable.
   - Design should **avoid** this zone in practical systems.

3. **Turbulent Zone** ($Re > 4000$):
   - Head loss is **proportional to velocity squared** ($h_f \propto V^2$).
   - Friction factor is given by empirical correlations (Moody chart, Colebrook equation, Blasius formula: $f = 0.316/Re^{0.25}$ for $4000 < Re < 10^5$).
   - Velocity profile is much **flatter** (logarithmic or power-law).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked *"Classify the flow"*, always state the **regime**, the **Re value**, and the **reference criterion** (e.g., *"The flow is turbulent since Re = 8500 > 4000"*).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Expression for Critical Velocity

**Given:** A fluid of density $\rho$ and dynamic viscosity $\mu$ flows through a pipe of diameter $D$. The flow transitions from laminar to turbulent at a critical Reynolds number $Re_c$.

**Step 1 — Start with the General Reynolds Number Formula**

The Reynolds number is defined as:

$$Re = \frac{\rho \cdot V \cdot D}{\mu}$$

This is the dimensionless ratio that characterizes the flow regime.

**Step 2 — Substitute the Critical Condition**

At the point of transition, the velocity equals the **critical velocity** $V_c$ and the Reynolds number equals the **critical Reynolds number** $Re_c$:

$$Re_c = \frac{\rho \cdot V_c \cdot D}{\mu}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the Critical Velocity**

Rearranging the equation to isolate $V_c$:

$$V_c = \frac{Re_c \cdot \mu}{\rho \cdot D}$$

**Step 4 — Express in Terms of Kinematic Viscosity**

Since $\nu = \mu / \rho$, we can substitute:

$$V_c = \frac{Re_c \cdot \nu}{D}$$

This is the **final, simplified form** for the critical velocity.

**Step 5 — Critical Discharge (Volumetric Flow Rate)**

The corresponding critical volumetric flow rate is:

$$Q_c = A \cdot V_c = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \frac{Re_c \cdot \nu}{D} = \frac{\pi D \cdot Re_c \cdot \nu}{4}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This expression is frequently used in KTU problems where the **maximum flow rate for laminar flow** must be determined.

### 3.2 Derivation: Critical Reynolds Number in Terms of Darcy Friction Factor

**Step 1 — Darcy-Weisbach Equation for Head Loss**

$$h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

**Step 2 — Hagen-Poiseuille Equation for Laminar Flow**

For laminar flow in a horizontal pipe, the head loss is also given by:

$$h_f = \frac{32 \mu V L}{\rho g D^2}$$

**Step 3 — Equate the Two Expressions (Both Valid for Laminar Flow)**

$$f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g} = \frac{32 \mu V L}{\rho g D^2}$$

**Step 4 — Cancel Common Terms**

Cancel $L$ and $g$ from both sides:

$$f \cdot \frac{V^2}{2D} = \frac{32 \mu V}{\rho D^2}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the Friction Factor**

$$f = \frac{64 \mu}{\rho V D} = \frac{64}{Re}$$

This **proves** that the friction factor in laminar flow is inversely proportional to the Reynolds number.

**Step 6 — At the Critical Condition**

$$f_c = \frac{64}{Re_c}$$

For $Re_c = 2000$:

$$f_c = \frac{64}{2000} = 0.032$$

For $Re_c = 4000$:

$$f_c = \frac{64}{4000} = 0.016$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Model KTU Problem)

**Problem Statement:**

> *Water at 20°C flows through a smooth horizontal pipe of diameter 50 mm. The kinematic viscosity of water is $1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s. Determine the velocity at which the flow becomes turbulent. Also, calculate the maximum volumetric flow rate for laminar flow. Take the lower critical Reynolds number as 2000.*

**Given Data:**
- $D = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
- $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- $Re_c = 2000$ (lower critical value)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate the Critical Velocity**

Using the derived formula:

$$V_c = \frac{Re_c \cdot \nu}{D}$$

Substituting values:

$$V_c = \frac{2000 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-6}}{0.05}$$

$$V_c = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{-3}}{0.05} = 0.04 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the Maximum Laminar Volumetric Flow Rate**

The cross-sectional area of the pipe:

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.05)^2}{4} = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

The critical discharge:

$$Q_c = A \cdot V_c = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.04$$

$$Q_c = 7.854 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 78.54 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Verification using the alternate formula:**

$$Q_c = \frac{\pi D \cdot Re_c \cdot \nu}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.05 \times 2000 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-6}}{4}$$

$$Q_c = \frac{\pi \times 10^{-4}}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \quad \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The flow becomes turbulent when the velocity exceeds **0.04 m/s**, and the maximum laminar flow rate is approximately **78.54 cm³/s**.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Flow Regime Classification

| Re Range | Flow Regime | Velocity Profile | Head Loss Law | Friction Factor | KTU Problem Type |
|----------|-------------|-------------------|---------------|-----------------|-------------------|
| $Re < 2000$ | **Laminar** | Parabolic | $h_f \propto V$ | $f = 64/Re$ | "Classify" type questions |
| $2000 \le Re \le 4000$ | **Transition** | Unstable | Empirical | Uncertain | "Predict" type questions |
| $Re > 4000$ | **Turbulent** | Flat / Logarithmic | $h_f \propto V^{1.75 \text{ to } 2.0}$ | Moody chart / Blasius | "Calculate head loss" type |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Osborne Reynolds' Experimental Setup (Block Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the functional architecture of Osborne Reynolds' classical dye-injection experiment used to identify the Critical Reynolds Number.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Water Reservoir Tank] --> B[Flow Control Valve]
    B --> C[Glass Test Pipe - Horizontal]
    D[Dye Reservoir with Colored Fluid] --> E[Dye Injection Needle]
    E --> C
    C --> F[Flow Discharge Outlet]
    G[Velocity Measurement] --> C
    H[Temperature Sensor] --> C
    I[Visual Observation Window] --> C

    subgraph Observation_Zone ["Observation Zone Inside Pipe"]
        C1["Case 1: LOW VELOCITY - Dye Filament Sharp and Straight = LAMINAR FLOW"]
        C2["Case 2: CRITICAL VELOCITY - Dye Filament Oscillates and Wavers = TRANSITION"]
        C3["Case 3: HIGH VELOCITY - Dye Filament Disperses Chaotically = TURBULENT FLOW"]
    end
    C -.-> Observation_Zone

    J[Calculate Re = rho V D / mu] --> K{Compare with Re_c = 2000}
    K -->|Re less than 2000| L[Classify as LAMINAR]
    K -->|Re between 2000 and 4000| M[Classify as TRANSITION]
    K -->|Re greater than 4000| N[Classify as TURBULENT]
```

### 4.2 Flow Regime Decision Flowchart

The following block diagram presents the algorithmic decision process used in KTU exam problems to classify a flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: Given V, D, rho, mu]) --> ComputeR["Compute Reynolds Number: Re = rho V D / mu"]
    ComputeR --> Decision1{Re less than 2000?}
    Decision1 -->|Yes| Lam["LAMINAR FLOW - Use f = 64 over Re - Velocity Profile is Parabolic"]
    Decision1 -->|No| Decision2{Re between 2000 and 4000?}
    Decision2 -->|Yes| Trans["TRANSITION ZONE - Avoid in Design - Use Empirical Correlations"]
    Decision2 -->|No| Decision3{Re greater than 4000?}
    Decision3 -->|Yes| Turb["TURBULENT FLOW - Use Moody Chart or Blasius Equation - Velocity Profile is Flat"]
    Decision3 -->|No| Error["Invalid Range - Check Calculations"]

    Lam --> End([End: Flow Regime Identified])
    Trans --> End
    Turb --> End
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Mapping of Reynolds Number and Flow Behavior

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph LowRe ["LOW REYNOLDS NUMBER REGION: Re less than 2000"]
        L1["Dominant Force: VISCOSITY"]
        L2["Behavior: Smooth, Layered Streamlines"]
        L3["Energy Loss: LOW - Proportional to V"]
        L4["Mixing: MINIMAL"]
    end

    subgraph CritRe ["CRITICAL REYNOLDS NUMBER: Re = 2000 to 4000"]
        C1["Forces: NEARLY BALANCED"]
        C2["Behavior: UNSTABLE - May Laminar or Turbulent"]
        C3["Energy Loss: TRANSITIONAL"]
        C4["Mixing: BEGINNING TO OCCUR"]
    end

    subgraph HighRe ["HIGH REYNOLDS NUMBER REGION: Re greater than 4000"]
        H1["Dominant Force: INERTIA"]
        H2["Behavior: Chaotic, Eddying, Random"]
        H3["Energy Loss: HIGH - Proportional to V squared"]
        H4["Mixing: INTENSE"]
    end

    LowRe -->|At Re = 2000| CritRe
    CritRe -->|At Re = 4000| HighRe
```

### 4.4 Summary of Critical Reynolds Numbers Across Geometries (Block Reference)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G[Geometry Type] --> P1["Circular Pipe: Re_c = 2000 (lower) to 4000 (upper)"]
    G --> P2["Parallel Plates: Re_c = 1000 (based on gap)"]
    G --> P3["Flat Plate: Re_x_c = 5 times 10 to power 5"]
    G --> P4["Concentric Annulus: Re_c = 2000 (based on hydraulic diameter)"]
    G --> P5["Sphere: Re_c approximately 1"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagrams above represent the **functional flow of logic** for the Critical Reynolds Number concept. They are designed to be exam-friendly mental maps rather than literal physical drawings.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Define the term "Critical Reynolds Number" for flow in a pipe. What is its standard value?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Critical Reynolds Number** is the dimensionless value of the Reynolds Number at which the flow regime transitions from **laminar to turbulent flow** (or vice versa) inside a pipe.

For flow through a **circular pipe**, two standard values are recognized:

- **Lower Critical Reynolds Number** = **2000** — Below this value, the flow is *guaranteed* to be laminar.
- **Upper Critical Reynolds Number** = **4000** — Above this value, the flow is *guaranteed* to be turbulent.

The Reynolds Number is defined as $Re = \rho V D / \mu$, where $\rho$ is the fluid density, $V$ is the mean velocity, $D$ is the pipe diameter, and $\mu$ is the dynamic viscosity. The critical value $Re_c = 2000$ is used as the **design criterion** to ensure that pipe systems operate in the laminar regime.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**State Osborne Reynolds' experiment and explain how the Critical Reynolds Number was determined.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Osborne Reynolds' Experiment (1883):** A classical visualization experiment was conducted using a horizontal glass tube connected to a water reservoir. A thin filament of **colored dye** was injected at the entrance of the tube using a needle, and the flow rate was gradually increased by adjusting a control valve.

**Observations at Three Velocity Ranges:**

1. **At low velocity:** The dye filament remained a **sharp, distinct, straight line** along the entire length of the tube — indicating **laminar flow**.
2. **At a critical velocity:** The dye filament began to **waver, oscillate, and break up** in the middle of the tube — indicating the **transition regime**.
3. **At high velocity:** The dye filament **immediately dispersed** across the entire cross-section, mixing chaotically — indicating **turbulent flow**.

By precisely measuring the velocity $V_c$ at the transition point, and using the formula $Re_c = \rho V_c D / \mu$, Reynolds calculated that the transition occurs at approximately **Re_c = 2000** for the lower critical value and **Re_c = 4000** for the upper critical value in circular pipes.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **critical velocity** in terms of the Critical Reynolds Number, pipe diameter, and kinematic viscosity. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A fluid of **density 900 kg/m³** and **dynamic viscosity 0.05 N·s/m²** flows through a pipe of diameter **100 mm**. Determine:
   - (i) The **critical velocity** at which the flow becomes turbulent. (Take Re_c = 2000)
   - (ii) The **maximum volumetric flow rate** for laminar flow.
   - (iii) The **Darcy friction factor** at the critical condition. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

The general Reynolds Number is defined as:

$$Re = \frac{\rho \cdot V \cdot D}{\mu}$$

At the **critical condition**, the velocity becomes $V_c$ and the Reynolds Number becomes $Re_c$:

$$Re_c = \frac{\rho \cdot V_c \cdot D}{\mu}$$

Rearranging to solve for $V_c$:

$$V_c = \frac{Re_c \cdot \mu}{\rho \cdot D}$$

Since kinematic viscosity is $\nu = \mu / \rho$, we substitute:

$$V_c = \frac{Re_c \cdot \nu}{D}$$

This is the **required expression** for the critical velocity.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- $\rho = 900 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\mu = 0.05 \text{ N·s/m}^2$
- $D = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$
- $Re_c = 2000$

**Step 1 — Kinematic Viscosity**

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{0.05}{900} = 5.556 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Critical Velocity (Sub-Part i)**

$$V_c = \frac{Re_c \cdot \nu}{D} = \frac{2000 \times 5.556 \times 10^{-5}}{0.1}$$

$$V_c = \frac{0.11111}{0.1} = 1.111 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Volumetric Flow Rate (Sub-Part ii)**

Cross-sectional area:

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.1)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

Volumetric flow rate:

$$Q_c = A \cdot V_c = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.111 = 8.726 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q_c \approx 8.73 \text{ L/s}}$$

**Step 4 — Darcy Friction Factor at Critical Condition (Sub-Part iii)**

For laminar flow, the Darcy friction factor is:

$$f = \frac{64}{Re_c} = \frac{64}{2000} = 0.032$$

$$\boxed{f_c = 0.032}$$

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **methodology of Osborne Reynolds' experiment** and the role of dye injection in determining the Critical Reynolds Number. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Crude oil of **kinematic viscosity 0.00005 m²/s** flows through a **150 mm** diameter pipe at a velocity of **1.5 m/s**. Calculate the Reynolds Number and classify the flow regime. If the same fluid is to be transported through a **75 mm** diameter pipe, find the **maximum velocity** that can be maintained to keep the flow laminar. Take Re_c = 2000. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Experimental Methodology (7 Marks):**

**Apparatus Setup:**
- A large **water reservoir** is placed at an elevated position to provide a constant head.
- A **glass test pipe** of uniform diameter is connected horizontally to the reservoir.
- A **flow control valve** regulates the discharge into the pipe.
- A **dye reservoir** containing a colored fluid (typically potassium permanganate solution or ink) is connected to a **fine injection needle** that enters the pipe at its centerline near the entrance.
- A **graduated scale** and **thermometer** are provided to measure flow parameters.

**Procedure:**
1. The valve is opened slightly to establish a **very low flow rate**.
2. The dye is injected as a thin filament at the pipe entrance.
3. **Observation:** The dye remains a **sharp, straight line** → **Laminar flow**.
4. The valve is gradually opened to **increase the flow rate**.
5. At a certain critical discharge, the dye filament begins to **waver and oscillate** → **Transition regime**.
6. With further increase, the dye **disperses immediately** → **Turbulent flow**.
7. The critical velocity $V_c$ is measured at the transition point, and $Re_c = \rho V_c D / \mu$ is computed.

**Role of Dye:** The dye acts as a **tracer** that visualizes the streamline patterns, allowing direct observation of the transition from ordered to chaotic motion.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- $\nu = 0.00005 \text{ m}^2/\text{s} = 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- $D_1 = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$
- $V_1 = 1.5 \text{ m/s}$
- $D_2 = 75 \text{ mm} = 0.075 \text{ m}$
- $Re_c = 2000$

**Step 1 — Reynolds Number in the 150 mm Pipe**

$$Re_1 = \frac{V_1 \cdot D_1}{\nu} = \frac{1.5 \times 0.15}{5 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$Re_1 = \frac{0.225}{5 \times 10^{-5}} = 4500$$

**Step 2 — Flow Classification for Pipe 1**

Since $Re_1 = 4500 > 4000$, the flow is **TURBULENT** in the 150 mm pipe.

**Step 3 — Maximum Velocity for Laminar Flow in 75 mm Pipe**

Using the critical velocity formula:

$$V_{c,2} = \frac{Re_c \cdot \nu}{D_2} = \frac{2000 \times 5 \times 10^{-5}}{0.075}$$

$$V_{c,2} = \frac{0.1}{0.075} = 1.333 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{V_{max} = 1.333 \text{ m/s for laminar flow in 75 mm pipe}}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the two critical values:** Many students interchange 2000 and 4000. Remember: **2000 is the LOWER** (laminar safety limit), **4000 is the UPPER** (turbulent certainty limit).
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units:** Pipe diameter must be in **meters** (not mm) when using SI units. Viscosity must be in **Pa·s or N·s/m²** (not poise or centipoise without conversion).
>
> 3. **Using the wrong characteristic length:** For circular pipes, use $D$ (diameter). For non-circular ducts, use **hydraulic diameter** $D_h = 4A/P$. For flat plates, use the **distance from the leading edge** $x$.
>
> 4. **Omitting the classification statement:** Even if the calculation is correct, students lose 1 mark for not explicitly stating *"The flow is laminar/turbulent because Re = ___ < 2000 / > 4000"*.
>
> 5. **Mixing up kinematic and dynamic viscosity:** The Reynolds Number can be written using either, but the formula form must be consistent: $Re = \rho V D / \mu$ (dynamic) **OR** $Re = V D / \nu$ (kinematic). Do not mix the two in the same calculation.
>
> 6. **Not stating the regime design criterion:** In design problems, always state *"For safe laminar operation, the design velocity is limited to $V_c = Re_c \nu / D$"*.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Critical Reynolds Number**

- **Definition:** Critical Reynolds Number ($Re_c$) is the threshold Reynolds Number at which flow transitions between laminar and turbulent regimes.

- **Standard Value for Circular Pipe:** $Re_c = 2000$ (lower) and $Re_c = 4000$ (upper). Use **2000** as the design limit for laminar flow.

- **Reynolds Number Formula (Two Equivalent Forms):**
  - $Re = \rho V D / \mu$ (using dynamic viscosity)
  - $Re = V D / \nu$ (using kinematic viscosity)

- **Critical Velocity Formula:** $V_c = Re_c \cdot \nu / D$ (or $V_c = Re_c \cdot \mu / (\rho D)$).

- **Critical Discharge Formula:** $Q_c = (\pi D \cdot Re_c \cdot \nu) / 4$.

- **Friction Factor in Laminar Flow:** $f = 64 / Re$ (valid for $Re < 2000$).

- **Three Flow Regimes:**
  - $Re < 2000$ → **Laminar** (parabolic profile, $h_f \propto V$).
  - $2000 \le Re \le 4000$ → **Transition** (avoid in design).
  - $Re > 4000$ → **Turbulent** (flat profile, $h_f \propto V^{1.75-2.0}$).

- **Geometry-Specific Critical Values (Memorize These):**
  - Circular pipe: **2000**
  - Parallel plates: **1000**
  - Flat plate: **5 × 10⁵** (based on $x$)
  - Sphere: **~1**

- **Osborne Reynolds' Experiment (1883):** Dye injection visualization — sharp filament = laminar, oscillating = transition, dispersed = turbulent.

- **Factors Lowering Re_c:** Surface roughness, sharp-edged entrances, external vibrations, adverse pressure gradients.

- **Key Insight:** $Re_c$ is **dimensionless and geometry-dependent**, not a universal constant. It represents the **competition between inertia (chaos) and viscosity (order)**.

- **Exam Mantra:** *"Calculate Re, compare with 2000/4000, declare the regime, justify with the formula used."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shear Stress and Velocity Distribution in a Pipe
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET413 - MECHANICS OF FLUID FLOW
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Shear Stress and Velocity Distribution in a Pipe

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the study of incompressible viscous flow, the **Shear Stress and Velocity Distribution in a Pipe** refers to the analytical determination of how internal fluid friction ($\tau$) and fluid velocity ($u$) vary across the cross-section of a circular conduit when the flow is **laminar** (streamline, $Re < 2000$). For a Newtonian fluid flowing steadily through a horizontal, straight pipe of constant cross-section, the velocity profile is **parabolic** (Hagen-Poiseuille flow), and the shear stress varies **linearly** from zero at the centerline to a maximum at the pipe wall.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This derivation assumes a **Newtonian fluid**, **steady**, **incompressible**, **axisymmetric**, **laminar** flow with **fully developed** (no-slip) boundary conditions at the rigid pipe wall.

## 1.2 Key Terminology

- **Newtonian Fluid:** A fluid in which the dynamic viscosity ($\mu$) is constant and the shear stress is linearly proportional to the rate of shear strain (e.g., water, air, thin oils).
- **Laminar Flow:** A flow regime characterized by smooth, parallel, non-intersecting streamlines where fluid layers slide over one another.
- **No-Slip Condition:** The principle that fluid in direct contact with a solid boundary has zero relative velocity to that boundary.
- **Shear Stress ($\tau$):** The tangential force per unit area acting between adjacent fluid layers, measured in **N/m²** or **Pa**.
- **Pressure Gradient ($\frac{\Delta P}{L}$):** The driving force per unit length that pushes the fluid through the pipe.
- **Reynolds Number ($Re$):** A dimensionless quantity that predicts flow regime, defined as $Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu}$.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Honey-Straw Analogy:** Imagine squeezing honey through a transparent straw. The thin film of honey touching the inner wall of the straw barely moves (no-slip condition), while the honey in the very center moves the fastest. The flow is fastest along the central axis, slowest at the walls, and follows a smooth, bullet-shaped (parabolic) profile. This is exactly the same physics as laminar water flow in a small-bore pipe.

**Real-World Engineering Examples:**
- **Blood Flow in Capillaries:** Red blood cells travel in a parabolic velocity profile through tiny blood vessels.
- **Lubrication Systems in Machinery:** Oil films in journal bearings and hydraulic lines.
- **Microfluidic Devices:** Lab-on-a-chip technology where laminar flow dominates.
- **Groundwater Flow in Aquifers:** Slow, viscous flow through porous media.

## 1.4 Physical Significance

The shear stress distribution governs the **frictional head loss** in pipelines, while the velocity distribution determines the **flow rate** and **discharge capacity** for a given pressure drop. The fundamental relationship is captured by the **Hagen-Poiseuille Equation**, a cornerstone result in fluid mechanics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU Board Examiner's Expectation:** A student must clearly state all assumptions (laminar, steady, incompressible, Newtonian, fully developed, axisymmetric) before starting any derivation. Marks are awarded for explicitly listing these conditions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Framework

The derivation of shear stress and velocity distribution in a pipe is built on a **fundamental force balance** applied to a cylindrical fluid element, combined with the **Newton's Law of Viscosity** as a constitutive equation.

### Logical Step 1: Isolate a Cylindrical Fluid Element

Consider a coaxial cylindrical fluid element of radius $r$ and length $L$ inside a pipe of radius $R$. The element is bounded by:
- An upstream cross-section at $x_1$ (pressure $P_1$)
- A downstream cross-section at $x_2$ (pressure $P_2$)
- A curved lateral surface of area $2\pi r L$ (where shear stress $\tau$ acts)

### Logical Step 2: Apply the Force Balance (The "Why")

Since the flow is **steady** and the velocity profile is **fully developed**, the net force on the element must equal zero (Newton's First Law for fluids in equilibrium). Two forces act along the axis:
- **Pressure Force (Driving):** $(P_1 - P_2) \times \pi r^2$ — pushes fluid forward.
- **Viscous Shear Force (Resisting):** $\tau \times 2\pi r L$ — opposes motion.

### Logical Step 3: Equate the Forces (The "How")

Setting the net force to zero:

$$P_1 \pi r^2 - P_2 \pi r^2 - \tau (2\pi r L) = 0$$

Solving for $\tau$ yields the **radial shear stress distribution** in the pipe.

### Logical Step 4: Apply Newton's Law of Viscosity

The shear stress is related to the velocity gradient by:

$$\tau = -\mu \frac{du}{dr}$$

The negative sign indicates that shear stress acts in the direction opposite to the velocity gradient (momentum diffuses from high-velocity regions to low-velocity regions).

### Logical Step 5: Integrate to Find Velocity Profile

By substituting the expression for $\tau$ from Step 3 into Newton's Law of Viscosity and integrating, with the boundary condition that $u = 0$ at $r = R$ (no-slip), we obtain the **parabolic velocity profile**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU Board Exams:** The following table contains every equation, boundary condition, and limiting case that the examiner expects you to know.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Shear stress distribution | $\tau = \frac{\Delta P}{2L} \cdot r$ | $\text{Pa}$; Linear in $r$ |
| 2 | Wall shear stress | $\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2L}$ | Maximum, occurs at $r = R$ |
| 3 | Centerline shear stress | $\tau_0 = 0$ | At $r = 0$ (axis of symmetry) |
| 4 | Newton's Law of Viscosity | $\tau = -\mu \dfrac{du}{dr}$ | $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity in $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ |
| 5 | Velocity distribution | $u = \dfrac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}\left(R^2 - r^2\right)$ | Parabolic profile |
| 6 | Maximum (centerline) velocity | $u_{\max} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{4\mu L}$ | Occurs at $r = 0$ |
| 7 | Average velocity | $u_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{8\mu L}$ | $u_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{u_{\max}}{2}$ |
| 8 | Volumetric flow rate (Q) | $Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P R^4}{8\mu L}$ | Hagen-Poiseuille Equation |
| 9 | Hagen-Poiseuille (in terms of D) | $\Delta P = \dfrac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi D^4}$ | $D = 2R$ |
| 10 | Friction factor (Fanning) | $f = \dfrac{16}{Re}$ | For laminar pipe flow |
| 11 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{4 \rho Q}{\pi \mu D}$ | $Re < 2000$ for laminar flow |
| 12 | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| 13 | Pressure gradient form | $u = \dfrac{-\partial P/\partial x}{4\mu}\left(R^2 - r^2\right)$ | Generalized 1-D form |
| 14 | Wall shear stress (alternative) | $\tau_w = \dfrac{4 \mu u_{\text{avg}}}{R}$ | From $u_{\text{avg}} = \tau_w R / 4\mu$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Reminder:** In all KTU board exam answers, you **must** explicitly state that this analysis applies only to **laminar flow** ($Re < 2000$). For turbulent flow, the velocity profile is logarithmic/flatter, and the average velocity is no longer equal to $u_{\max}/2$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydraulic Pipelines:** Sizing pumps and calculating pressure drop over long-distance oil/water pipelines.
- **Medical Devices:** Designing intravenous (IV) flow regulators and dialysis machines.
- **Microfluidics:** Designing lab-on-a-chip devices for biochemical analysis.
- **Aerospace:** Analyzing fuel flow in aircraft hydraulic systems.
- **Civil Engineering:** Computing head loss in water supply networks.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Shear Stress Distribution Across the Pipe Cross-Section

### Step 1: Define the Control Volume

Consider a horizontal, straight pipe of internal radius $R$ carrying a viscous, incompressible fluid in **steady, laminar, fully-developed flow**. Isolate a coaxial cylindrical fluid element of radius $r$ ($0 \le r \le R$) and length $L$ as the free body.

### Step 2: Identify All Forces Acting on the Element

**(a) Pressure force on the upstream face:**

$$F_1 = P_1 \times A = P_1 \times \pi r^2$$

**(b) Pressure force on the downstream face (acts in the negative x-direction):**

$$F_2 = P_2 \times \pi r^2$$

**(c) Shear force on the curved lateral surface (acts opposite to flow direction):**

$$F_{\text{shear}} = \tau \times 2\pi r L$$

The shear stress $\tau$ acts tangentially on the curved surface, and the area of this curved surface is $2\pi r L$.

### Step 3: Apply Newton's Second Law (Momentum Conservation for Steady Flow)

Since the flow is **steady** and **fully developed**, the acceleration of the fluid element is zero, so the sum of forces in the x-direction must vanish:

$$P_1 \pi r^2 - P_2 \pi r^2 - \tau (2\pi r L) = 0$$

### Step 4: Algebraic Simplification

Factor out $\pi r^2$ from the pressure terms:

$$\pi r^2 (P_1 - P_2) = \tau (2\pi r L)$$

The pressure drop over the length $L$ is defined as $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$:

$$\pi r^2 \Delta P = \tau (2\pi r L)$$

Cancel $\pi r$ from both sides:

$$r \Delta P = 2 \tau L$$

### Step 5: Solve Explicitly for the Shear Stress

$$\tau = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$$

This is the **linear shear stress distribution**. The shear stress is zero at the centerline ($r = 0$) and maximum at the wall ($r = R$).

### Step 6: Evaluate the Boundary Cases

**Case A: At the centerline ($r = 0$):**

$$\tau_0 = \frac{\Delta P \cdot 0}{2L} = 0$$

**Case B: At the pipe wall ($r = R$):**

$$\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** The maximum shear stress occurs at the wall, where the fluid "sticks" to the surface. This is the location of highest viscous dissipation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Velocity Distribution (Parabolic Profile)

### Step 1: Apply Newton's Law of Viscosity

For a Newtonian fluid, the shear stress is proportional to the velocity gradient:

$$\tau = -\mu \frac{du}{dr}$$

The negative sign arises because the velocity $u$ decreases as $r$ increases (i.e., $\frac{du}{dr} < 0$), while the actual shear stress on the fluid element is positive in the direction opposing flow.

### Step 2: Substitute the Expression for $\tau$ from Derivation 1

Equate the two expressions for $\tau$:

$$\frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L} = -\mu \frac{du}{dr}$$

### Step 3: Rearrange to Isolate the Velocity Gradient

Rearranging algebraically:

$$\frac{du}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2\mu L}$$

### Step 4: Separate Variables and Integrate

Separate the variables $u$ and $r$:

$$du = -\frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} \cdot r \, dr$$

Integrate both sides from $r = 0$ to $r = r$, with $u$ ranging from $u_{\max}$ to $u$:

$$\int_{u_{\max}}^{u} du = -\frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} \int_{0}^{r} r \, dr$$

Evaluate the right-hand integral:

$$u - u_{\max} = -\frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} \cdot \frac{r^2}{2} = -\frac{\Delta P \cdot r^2}{4\mu L}$$

Therefore:

$$u = u_{\max} - \frac{\Delta P \cdot r^2}{4\mu L}$$

### Step 5: Apply the No-Slip Boundary Condition

**Boundary Condition:** At the pipe wall, $r = R$, the fluid velocity equals zero:

$$u(R) = 0$$

Substituting into the velocity equation:

$$0 = u_{\max} - \frac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{4\mu L}$$

Solving for $u_{\max}$:

$$u_{\max} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{4\mu L}$$

### Step 6: Write the Final Velocity Distribution

Substitute $u_{\max}$ back into the velocity equation:

$$u = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{4\mu L} - \frac{\Delta P \cdot r^2}{4\mu L} = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}\left(R^2 - r^2\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Velocity Profile (KTU Board Format):**

$$u(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}\left(R^2 - r^2\right) = u_{\max}\left(1 - \frac{r^2}{R^2}\right)$$

This is a **parabolic profile** — a downward-opening parabola with maximum at the centerline.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Average Velocity and Hagen-Poiseuille Equation

### Step 1: Define Average Velocity

The average velocity is the total volumetric flow rate divided by the cross-sectional area:

$$u_{\text{avg}} = \frac{Q}{\pi R^2}$$

### Step 2: Compute the Volumetric Flow Rate

Consider a thin annular ring at radius $r$ of thickness $dr$. The area of this ring is $dA = 2\pi r \, dr$. The flow through this ring is:

$$dQ = u \cdot dA = u \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

Substitute the velocity distribution $u = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}(R^2 - r^2)$:

$$dQ = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}(R^2 - r^2) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L}(R^2 r - r^3) \, dr$$

### Step 3: Integrate Across the Full Cross-Section

Integrate from $r = 0$ (centerline) to $r = R$ (wall):

$$Q = \int_{0}^{R} \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L}(R^2 r - r^3) \, dr$$

Factor out the constants:

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \int_{0}^{R} (R^2 r - r^3) \, dr$$

Evaluate the integral:

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \left[ \frac{R^2 r^2}{2} - \frac{r^4}{4} \right]_{0}^{R}$$

Substitute the limits:

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \left( \frac{R^2 \cdot R^2}{2} - \frac{R^4}{4} \right) = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \left( \frac{R^4}{2} - \frac{R^4}{4} \right) = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \cdot \frac{R^4}{4}$$

### Step 4: Simplify to Obtain the Hagen-Poiseuille Equation

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P R^4}{8\mu L}$$

This is the famous **Hagen-Poiseuille Equation** for laminar flow in a circular pipe.

### Step 5: Compute the Average Velocity

$$u_{\text{avg}} = \frac{Q}{\pi R^2} = \frac{\pi \Delta P R^4}{8\mu L \pi R^2} = \frac{\Delta P R^2}{8\mu L}$$

### Step 6: Establish the Key Relationship

Compare $u_{\text{avg}}$ and $u_{\max}$:

$$u_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\Delta P R^2}{8\mu L}, \quad u_{\max} = \frac{\Delta P R^2}{4\mu L}$$

Therefore:

$$u_{\text{avg}} = \frac{u_{\max}}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize this relationship!** For laminar pipe flow, the average velocity is exactly **half** the maximum (centerline) velocity. This is a frequently tested 2-mark question in KTU exams.

### Step 7: Express the Hagen-Poiseuille Equation in Terms of Diameter

Substituting $R = D/2$ into the flow rate equation:

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P (D/2)^4}{8\mu L} = \frac{\pi \Delta P D^4}{128 \mu L}$$

Rearranging for pressure drop:

$$\Delta P = \frac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi D^4}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Wall Shear Stress in Terms of Average Velocity

Starting from the average velocity expression:

$$u_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\Delta P R^2}{8\mu L}$$

Solving for the pressure gradient:

$$\frac{\Delta P}{L} = \frac{8 \mu u_{\text{avg}}}{R^2}$$

Substitute this into the wall shear stress formula $\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P R}{2L}$:

$$\tau_w = \frac{R}{2} \cdot \frac{8 \mu u_{\text{avg}}}{R^2} = \frac{4 \mu u_{\text{avg}}}{R}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** This compact form $\tau_w = \frac{4 \mu u_{\text{avg}}}{R}$ is extremely useful in **fluid power systems** and **lubrication theory** for computing friction forces on bearings.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Quick Reference Summary

| Parameter | Symbol | Radial Dependence | Maximum Location | Functional Form |
|-----------|--------|-------------------|------------------|-----------------|
| Shear stress | $\tau$ | $\tau \propto r$ | Wall ($r = R$) | Linear |
| Velocity | $u$ | $u \propto (R^2 - r^2)$ | Centerline ($r = 0$) | Parabolic |
| Velocity gradient | $du/dr$ | $du/dr \propto r$ | Wall ($r = R$) | Linear |
| Pressure | $P$ | $P \propto x$ (axial) | Independent of $r$ | Linear in $x$ |
