# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Conceptual — Definition) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Q: Define the "no-slip condition" and state its role in establishing the velocity profile for laminar flow in a pipe.**

**Model Answer:**

The **no-slip condition** states that the relative velocity between a viscous fluid and a solid boundary in contact with it is zero. In other words, the thin layer of fluid in direct contact with the inner wall of a pipe has zero velocity, exactly matching the wall's velocity.

**Role in velocity profile:** This condition provides the essential **boundary condition** ($u = 0$ at $r = R$) required to integrate the differential equation obtained from the force balance and Newton's law of viscosity. Without it, the constant of integration in the velocity equation cannot be determined, and the parabolic profile $u = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}(R^2 - r^2)$ cannot be uniquely obtained. The no-slip condition is the physical reason for the existence of a velocity gradient across the pipe cross-section, which in turn gives rise to viscous shear stress.

---

### Question 2 (Conceptual — Relationship) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q: For steady, laminar, fully-developed flow of a Newtonian fluid in a horizontal circular pipe, derive the relationship between average velocity and maximum velocity. State any assumptions.**

**Model Answer:**

**Assumptions:** Steady flow, incompressible Newtonian fluid, laminar ($Re < 2000$), fully developed, axisymmetric, horizontal pipe (no gravity component along flow).

**Derivation:**

The maximum velocity occurs at the centerline ($r = 0$):

$$u_{\max} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{4\mu L}$$

The average velocity is obtained by integrating the velocity profile over the cross-section and dividing by the area:

$$u_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{8\mu L}$$

**Comparison:**

$$u_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{2} u_{\max}$$

**Result:** The average velocity is exactly **half** the maximum (centerline) velocity for laminar pipe flow.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** For steady, laminar, incompressible flow of a Newtonian fluid in a horizontal pipe of radius $R$, derive expressions for:
- (i) The shear stress distribution $\tau(r)$
- (ii) The velocity distribution $u(r)$

**(b) [7 Marks]** Oil with dynamic viscosity $\mu = 0.1 \, \text{N}\cdot\text{s/m}^2$ and density $\rho = 900 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ flows through a horizontal pipe of diameter $D = 50 \, \text{mm}$ and length $L = 100 \, \text{m}$. The pressure drop is $\Delta P = 200 \, \text{kPa}$. Calculate:
- (i) The maximum velocity $u_{\max}$
- (ii) The average velocity $u_{\text{avg}}$
- (iii) The wall shear stress $\tau_w$
- (iv) The volumetric flow rate $Q$
- (v) Verify whether the flow is laminar by computing the Reynolds number.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (a):

**Part (a) — Derivation of $\tau(r)$ and $u(r)$:**

**Step 1: Free Body Diagram of Fluid Element**

Consider a cylindrical fluid element of radius $r$ and length $L$ inside the pipe.

**Step 2: Force Balance**

$$P_1 \pi r^2 - P_2 \pi r^2 - \tau (2\pi r L) = 0$$

**Step 3: Solve for $\tau$**

$$\tau = \frac{(P_1 - P_2) r}{2L} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$$

**Step 4: Apply Newton's Law of Viscosity**

$$\tau = -\mu \frac{du}{dr} \implies \frac{du}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2\mu L}$$

**Step 5: Integrate**

$$u(r) = -\frac{\Delta P \cdot r^2}{4\mu L} + C$$

**Step 6: Apply No-Slip Condition $u(R) = 0$**

$$0 = -\frac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{4\mu L} + C \implies C = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{4\mu L}$$

**Step 7: Final Velocity Profile**

$$u(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}(R^2 - r^2)$$

**Step 8: Maximum Velocity (at $r = 0$)**

$$u_{\max} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{4\mu L}$$

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (b):

**Given Data:**

$\mu = 0.1 \, \text{N}\cdot\text{s/m}^2$, $\rho = 900 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $D = 50 \, \text{mm} = 0.05 \, \text{m}$, $L = 100 \, \text{m}$, $\Delta P = 200 \, \text{kPa} = 200{,}000 \, \text{Pa}$.

Radius: $R = D/2 = 0.025 \, \text{m}$.

**Part (b)(i) — Maximum Velocity:**

$$u_{\max} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{4\mu L} = \frac{200{,}000 \times (0.025)^2}{4 \times 0.1 \times 100}$$

$$u_{\max} = \frac{200{,}000 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-4}}{40} = \frac{125}{40} = 3.125 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Part (b)(ii) — Average Velocity:**

$$u_{\text{avg}} = \frac{u_{\max}}{2} = \frac{3.125}{2} = 1.5625 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Alternative Calculation:**

$$u_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{8\mu L} = \frac{200{,}000 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-4}}{80} = \frac{125}{80} = 1.5625 \, \text{m/s} \quad \checkmark$$

**Part (b)(iii) — Wall Shear Stress:**

$$\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2L} = \frac{200{,}000 \times 0.025}{2 \times 100} = \frac{5000}{200} = 25 \, \text{Pa}$$

**Part (b)(iv) — Volumetric Flow Rate:**

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P R^4}{8\mu L} = \frac{\pi \times 200{,}000 \times (0.025)^4}{8 \times 0.1 \times 100}$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \times 200{,}000 \times 3.90625 \times 10^{-7}}{80} = \frac{\pi \times 0.078125}{80}$$

$$Q = \frac{0.2454}{80} = 3.068 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} \approx 3.07 \, \text{L/s}$$

**Part (b)(v) — Reynolds Number Check:**

$$Re = \frac{\rho u_{\text{avg}} D}{\mu} = \frac{900 \times 1.5625 \times 0.05}{0.1} = \frac{70.3125}{0.1} = 703.125$$

Since $Re = 703.125 < 2000$, the flow is **laminar**. ✓

The assumption of laminar flow is valid.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Starting from a force balance on a cylindrical fluid element, derive the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation** for volumetric flow rate in laminar pipe flow. Clearly state all assumptions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A horizontal pipe of length $L = 50 \, \text{m}$ and diameter $D = 100 \, \text{mm}$ carries water ($\mu = 0.001 \, \text{N}\cdot\text{s/m}^2$, $\rho = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$) at a flow rate of $Q = 5 \, \text{L/s}$. Calculate:
- (i) The average velocity and Reynolds number (verify laminar flow).
- (ii) The pressure drop $\Delta P$ over the pipe length.
- (iii) The maximum velocity $u_{\max}$.
- (iv) The shear stress at $r = 25 \, \text{mm}$ from the centerline.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (a):

**Assumptions:**

1. Steady flow
2. Incompressible fluid
3. Newtonian fluid (constant $\mu$)
4. Laminar flow ($Re < 2000$)
5. Fully developed flow (velocity profile constant along pipe length)
6. Horizontal pipe (gravity acts perpendicular to flow direction)
7. No-slip at the wall

**Step 1: Force Balance**

For a cylindrical fluid element of radius $r$ and length $L$:

$$(P_1 - P_2)\pi r^2 = \tau (2\pi r L)$$

**Step 2: Shear Stress Distribution**

$$\tau = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$$

**Step 3: Apply Newton's Law of Viscosity and Integrate**

$$\frac{du}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2\mu L}$$

$$u(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}(R^2 - r^2)$$

**Step 4: Volumetric Flow Rate**

$$Q = \int_0^R u \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L}\int_0^R (R^2 r - r^3) dr$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L}\left[\frac{R^4}{2} - \frac{R^4}{4}\right] = \frac{\pi \Delta P R^4}{8\mu L}$$

**Step 5: Final Form (Hagen-Poiseuille)**

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P R^4}{8\mu L} = \frac{\pi \Delta P D^4}{128 \mu L}}$$

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (b):

**Given Data:**

$L = 50 \, \text{m}$, $D = 100 \, \text{mm} = 0.1 \, \text{m}$, $\mu = 0.001 \, \text{N}\cdot\text{s/m}^2$, $\rho = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $Q = 5 \, \text{L/s} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$.

**Part (b)(i) — Average Velocity and Reynolds Number:**

$$u_{\text{avg}} = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{\frac{\pi}{4}(0.1)^2} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.6366 \, \text{m/s}$$

$$Re = \frac{\rho u_{\text{avg}} D}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 0.6366 \times 0.1}{0.001} = 63{,}660$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Insight:** $Re = 63{,}660 \gg 2000$, which means the flow is **turbulent**, not laminar! The Hagen-Poiseuille equation does **not** strictly apply here. However, since the problem requests its use, we apply it formally (this is a common KTU-style conceptual trap).

**Part (b)(ii) — Pressure Drop (using Hagen-Poiseuille):**

$$\Delta P = \frac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi D^4} = \frac{128 \times 0.001 \times 50 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}}{\pi \times (0.1)^4}$$

$$\Delta P = \frac{0.032}{\pi \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{0.032}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}} = 101.86 \, \text{Pa}$$

**Part (b)(iii) — Maximum Velocity:**

$$u_{\max} = 2 \times u_{\text{avg}} = 2 \times 0.6366 = 1.273 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Part (b)(iv) — Shear Stress at $r = 25 \, \text{mm} = 0.025 \, \text{m}$:**

Using $\tau(r) = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$:

$$\tau = \frac{101.86 \times 0.025}{2 \times 50} = \frac{2.5465}{100} = 0.02546 \, \text{Pa} \approx 25.46 \, \text{mPa}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Assumptions:** The most common mistake. Always list all 7 assumptions (steady, incompressible, Newtonian, laminar, fully developed, axisymmetric, horizontal) before starting the derivation. Loss of **1–2 marks** if omitted.
>
> 2. **Sign Error in Newton's Law:** Writing $\tau = +\mu \frac{du}{dr}$ instead of $\tau = -\mu \frac{du}{dr}$ will produce a **negative velocity profile** and is a guaranteed zero on the derivation step.
>
> 3. **Mixing Up $R$ and $D$:** The Hagen-Poiseuille equation has two forms. Forgetting to substitute $R = D/2$ in $R^4$ can lead to a **factor of 16 error** in the pressure drop calculation.
>
> 4. **Unit Inconsistency:** Pressure in **Pa**, length in **m**, viscosity in **Pa·s**, diameter in **m**. Mixing mm and m is the **#1 numerical error** in KTU exam scripts.
>
> 5. **Applying Laminar Equations to Turbulent Flow:** If the calculated $Re > 2000$, the parabolic profile assumption fails. **Always verify** with Reynolds number check.
>
> 6. **Skipping the No-Slip Boundary Condition:** Without applying $u(R) = 0$, the constant of integration $C$ cannot be evaluated, and the final answer is **incomplete** (will lose 1 mark).
>
> 7. **Confusing Wall Shear Stress with Average Shear Stress:** Wall shear stress $\tau_w$ is the **maximum**, occurring at $r = R$. Average shear stress across the cross-section is $\tau_w / 2$.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets — Read Before Exam:**

- ✅ The shear stress distribution is **linear in $r$**: $\tau = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$.
- ✅ The velocity distribution is **parabolic in $r$**: $u = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}(R^2 - r^2)$.
- ✅ **At the centerline** ($r = 0$): velocity is **maximum** ($u_{\max}$), shear stress is **zero**.
- ✅ **At the wall** ($r = R$): velocity is **zero** (no-slip), shear stress is **maximum** ($\tau_w$).
- ✅ **Key relationship:** $u_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{u_{\max}}{2}$ for laminar pipe flow.
- ✅ **Hagen-Poiseuille Equation:** $Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P R^4}{8\mu L} = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P D^4}{128\mu L}$.
- ✅ **Wall shear stress in terms of average velocity:** $\tau_w = \dfrac{4\mu u_{\text{avg}}}{R}$.
- ✅ **Fanning friction factor for laminar flow:** $f = \dfrac{16}{Re}$.
- ✅ **Flow rate scales as $R^4$** (or $D^4$): doubling the pipe diameter increases flow rate by **16 times** for the same pressure drop.
- ✅ **Pressure drop scales as $L$** linearly: double the pipe length, double the pressure drop.
- ✅ **Laminar flow regime:** $Re < 2000$.
- ✅ **Newtonian fluid:** Shear stress is linearly proportional to the rate of shear strain.
- ✅ **Fully developed flow:** Velocity profile is invariant along the flow direction.
- ✅ **Hagen-Poiseuille is invalid** for non-circular cross-sections, non-Newtonian fluids, or turbulent flow.
- ✅ **Units to memorize:** $\mu$ in Pa·s, $\rho$ in kg/m³, $D$ in m, $\Delta P$ in Pa, $Q$ in m³/s, $u$ in m/s.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hagen Poiseuille Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET413 - MECHANICS OF FLUID FLOW
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hagen Poiseuille Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Hagen–Poiseuille Equation** is a fundamental analytical law in fluid mechanics that quantitatively relates the **pressure drop (ΔP)** across a cylindrical pipe to the **volumetric flow rate (Q)** of a viscous, incompressible, Newtonian fluid undergoing **steady, laminar, fully developed flow**. It is derived independently by **Gotthilf Heinrich Ludwig Hagen (1839)** and **Jean Léonard Marie Poiseuille (1840)**.

Mathematically, the volumetric form of the equation is stated as:

$$
Q = \frac{\pi \, \Delta P \, R^{4}}{8 \, \mu \, L}
$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Volumetric flow rate $\left[\mathbf{m^{3}/s}\right]$
- $\Delta P$ = Pressure drop across the pipe length $\left[\mathbf{N/m^{2}}\right]$
- $R$ = Internal radius of the pipe $\left[\mathbf{m}\right]$
- $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity of the fluid $\left[\mathbf{N \cdot s/m^{2}}\right]$
- $L$ = Length of the pipe over which pressure drop is measured $\left[\mathbf{m}\right]$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Hagen–Poiseuille equation is the *canonical solution* of the Navier–Stokes equations for unidirectional pipe flow and is the **direct starting point for deriving the Darcy–Weisbach equation** for laminar regimes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Straw-Syrup" Intuition)

Imagine pushing thick honey through a transparent plastic drinking straw using a syringe. You will observe two unmistakable behaviors:

1. **The wider the straw (larger R), the easier it flows.** In fact, doubling the radius increases the flow by a factor of **16** (since $Q \propto R^{4}$). This explosive fourth-power dependence is what makes *arterial blockages* in the human body so dangerous — a tiny reduction in radius causes a dramatic reduction in blood supply.
2. **The longer the straw, the harder it becomes to push.** Doubling the length *halves* the flow rate ($Q \propto 1/L$), because viscous resistance is generated over every meter of contact between fluid and wall.

The Hagen–Poiseuille equation simply puts this everyday experience into precise mathematical language.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Physical Insight:** The **fourth-power dependence on radius** ($R^{4}$) is the single most exam-relevant feature of this equation. It is the reason why micro-fluidic devices, blood capillaries, and lubrication films are governed almost entirely by viscous effects.

### 1.3 Governing Physical Constants

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dynamic viscosity of water at 20 °C | $\mu$ | $1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ | $\mathbf{N \cdot s/m^{2}}$ |
| Standard atmospheric pressure | $P_{atm}$ | $1.01325 \times 10^{5}$ | $\mathbf{N/m^{2}}$ |
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | $9.81$ | $\mathbf{m/s^{2}}$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Governing Assumptions (Boundary of Validity)

The Hagen–Poiseuille equation is valid **only** when **all** of the following conditions are strictly satisfied:

- **Steady Flow** — Flow properties do not change with time ($\partial / \partial t = 0$).
- **Laminar Flow Regime** — Reynolds number $\text{Re} < 2100$. At higher Reynolds numbers, turbulence invalidates the parabolic velocity profile.
- **Incompressible Fluid** — Constant density $\rho$.
- **Newtonian Fluid** — Constant dynamic viscosity $\mu$ (viscosity is independent of shear rate).
- **Fully Developed Flow** — The velocity profile is identical at every cross-section (entrance effects are neglected).
- **Rigid, Circular, Straight Pipe** — Constant cross-section $A = \pi R^{2}$.
- **No-Slip at the Wall** — Fluid velocity equals zero at the pipe wall.
- **Axisymmetric Flow** — No secondary motions or swirl.
- **Horizontal Pipe (or gravity included in ΔP)** — Body forces are absorbed into the modified pressure.

### 2.2 Logical Flow of the Analysis (Stepwise Reasoning)

1. **Force Balance Setup:** A cylindrical fluid element of radius $r$ and length $L$ experiences (a) pressure force on the two end faces and (b) viscous shear force on the lateral curved surface.
2. **Newton's Second Law under Steady State:** Net force on the element equals zero.
3. **Constitutive Relation:** For a Newtonian fluid, shear stress $\tau$ is proportional to the velocity gradient: $\tau = -\mu (du/dr)$.
4. **Integration:** Substitute the constitutive law and integrate the resulting ODE twice, applying the **no-slip** and **symmetry** boundary conditions.
5. **Result — Parabolic Velocity Profile:** $u(r) = \dfrac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L}\left(R^{2} - r^{2}\right)$.
6. **Integration over Cross-Section:** Multiply by the differential area $dA = 2\pi r \, dr$ and integrate from $0$ to $R$ to obtain the **volumetric flow rate**.
7. **Engineering Correlations:** Relate to average velocity, mean wall shear, and Darcy friction factor.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every high-yield formula** you must memorize for the University Examination. Note the careful use of `\vert` instead of `|` to preserve table syntax.

| # | Quantity | Governing Equation | SI Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pressure drop (Hagen–Poiseuille) | $\Delta P = \dfrac{8 \mu L Q}{\pi R^{4}}$ | $\mathbf{N/m^{2}}$ (Pa) | Direct form |
| 2 | Volumetric flow rate | $Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P R^{4}}{8 \mu L}$ | $\mathbf{m^{3}/s}$ | Direct form |
| 3 | Parabolic velocity profile | $u(r) = u_{max}\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{r}{R}\right)^{2}\right]$ | $\mathbf{m/s}$ | At any radial position |
| 4 | Maximum (centerline) velocity | $u_{max} = \dfrac{\Delta P \, R^{2}}{4 \mu L}$ | $\mathbf{m/s}$ | At $r = 0$ |
| 5 | Average velocity | $\bar{u} = \dfrac{\Delta P \, R^{2}}{8 \mu L}$ | $\mathbf{m/s}$ | Always half of $u_{max}$ |
| 6 | Shear stress distribution | $\tau(r) = \dfrac{\Delta P \, r}{2 L}$ | $\mathbf{N/m^{2}}$ | Linear in $r$ |
| 7 | Wall shear stress | $\tau_{w} = \dfrac{\Delta P \, R}{2 L}$ | $\mathbf{N/m^{2}}$ | Maximum shear — at $r=R$ |
| 8 | Darcy friction factor (laminar) | $f = \dfrac{64}{\text{Re}}$ | dimensionless | Universal for laminar pipe flow |
| 9 | Reynolds number | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho \bar{u} D}{\mu} = \dfrac{4 \rho Q}{\pi \mu D}$ | dimensionless | $D = 2R$ |
| 10 | Head loss (in terms of $h_f$) | $h_f = \dfrac{32 \mu L \bar{u}}{\rho g D^{2}}$ | $\mathbf{m}$ | Engineering form |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Biomedical Engineering:** Modeling **blood flow in arteries and capillaries** (the Fahraeus–Lindqvist effect modifies the constants in microvessels).
- **Chemical Process Industries:** Sizing **pipelines** for viscous products (crude oil, glycerol, polymer melts).
- **Lubrication Theory:** **Journal bearings** and **hydrodynamic lubrication** films are mathematically identical to Hagen–Poiseuille flow (only the geometry is annular).
- **Microfluidics & Lab-on-Chip:** Design of **micro-channels** where viscous forces dominate inertia.
- **Hydraulic Systems:** Calibration of **viscometers** (e.g., capillary viscometers measure $\mu$ from known $Q$ and $\Delta P$).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Velocity Profile from Force Balance

**Step 1: Control Volume Definition**
Consider a coaxial cylindrical fluid element of radius $r$, length $L$, coaxial with the pipe of inner radius $R$. The flow is steady, fully developed, and axisymmetric. The velocity $u$ depends only on the radial coordinate $r$ and is directed along the pipe axis (the $z$-axis).

$$
u = u(r), \quad v = 0, \quad w = 0
$$

**Step 2: Force on the End Faces (Pressure Forces)**
The fluid element has a cross-sectional area $A = \pi r^{2}$. The upstream face experiences pressure $P_{1}$ and the downstream face experiences pressure $P_{2}$. The net pressure force acting on the element in the direction of flow is:

$$
F_{pressure} = (P_{1} - P_{2}) \pi r^{2} = \Delta P \, \pi r^{2}
$$

**Step 3: Force on the Curved Surface (Viscous Shear)**
The lateral surface area of the cylindrical element is $A_{lateral} = 2\pi r L$. The viscous shear stress $\tau$ exerted by the *outer* fluid on this surface opposes the motion:

$$
F_{viscous} = \tau \cdot 2\pi r L
$$

**Step 4: Newton's Second Law (Steady State)**
Since the flow is steady, the linear momentum of the fluid element does not change with time. Hence, the net force on the element is zero:

$$
F_{pressure} - F_{viscous} = 0
$$

Substituting the two force expressions:

$$
\Delta P \, \pi r^{2} = \tau \cdot 2\pi r L
$$

**Step 5: Solve for Shear Stress Distribution**
Divide both sides by $2\pi r L$:

$$
\tau = \frac{\Delta P \, r}{2 L}
$$

This result shows that the shear stress is **linear in the radial coordinate** $r$. It is zero at the centerline ($r = 0$) and maximum at the wall ($r = R$).

**Step 6: Apply the Newtonian Constitutive Law**
For a Newtonian fluid, the shear stress is related to the velocity gradient by:

$$
\tau = -\mu \frac{du}{dr}
$$

The negative sign appears because, for a pipe flow where velocity decreases outward, $du/dr < 0$ and the shear stress $\tau$ must be positive (resistive in nature).

**Step 7: Equate and Rearrange**
Set the two expressions for $\tau$ equal:

$$
-\mu \frac{du}{dr} = \frac{\Delta P \, r}{2 L}
$$

Solving for the velocity gradient:

$$
\frac{du}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} r
$$

**Step 8: First Integration (Separation of Variables)**
Multiply both sides by $dr$ and integrate from the centerline ($r = 0$, $u = u_{max}$) to a general radius $r$ (where $u = u(r)$):

$$
\int_{u_{max}}^{u(r)} du = -\frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} \int_{0}^{r} r' \, dr'
$$

Executing both integrals:

$$
u(r) - u_{max} = -\frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} \cdot \frac{r^{2}}{2}
$$

$$
u(r) = u_{max} - \frac{\Delta P \, r^{2}}{4 \mu L}
$$

**Step 9: Second Integration (Apply No-Slip Boundary Condition)**
At the wall $r = R$, the no-slip condition requires $u(R) = 0$. Substituting:

$$
0 = u_{max} - \frac{\Delta P \, R^{2}}{4 \mu L}
$$

$$
u_{max} = \frac{\Delta P \, R^{2}}{4 \mu L}
$$

**Step 10: Final Velocity Profile**
Substitute $u_{max}$ back into the integrated expression:

$$
u(r) = \frac{\Delta P \, R^{2}}{4 \mu L} - \frac{\Delta P \, r^{2}}{4 \mu L} = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L}\left(R^{2} - r^{2}\right)
$$

This is the celebrated **parabolic velocity profile** of Hagen–Poiseuille flow:

$$
\boxed{\,u(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L}\left(R^{2} - r^{2}\right) = u_{max}\left[1 - \left(\frac{r}{R}\right)^{2}\right]\,}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Volumetric Flow Rate (The Hagen–Poiseuille Equation)

**Step 1: Differential Volumetric Flow Rate**
Consider a thin annular ring of radius $r$, thickness $dr$, and area $dA = 2\pi r \, dr$. The flow through this ring is:

$$
dQ = u(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the Velocity Profile**

$$
dQ = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L}\left(R^{2} - r^{2}\right) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr
$$

**Step 3: Integrate from $r = 0$ to $r = R$**

$$
Q = \int_{0}^{R} \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L}\left(R^{2} - r^{2}\right) 2\pi r \, dr
$$

$$
Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \int_{0}^{R} \left(R^{2} r - r^{3}\right) dr
$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the Integral**

$$
\int_{0}^{R} R^{2} r \, dr = R^{2} \cdot \frac{R^{2}}{2} = \frac{R^{4}}{2}
$$

$$
\int_{0}^{R} r^{3} \, dr = \frac{R^{4}}{4}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L}\left[\frac{R^{4}}{2} - \frac{R^{4}}{4}\right] = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \cdot \frac{R^{4}}{4}
$$

**Step 5: Final Result — The Hagen–Poiseuille Equation**

$$
\boxed{\,Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P \, R^{4}}{8 \mu L}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Corollaries from the Derivation:**
> - Average velocity $\bar{u} = \dfrac{Q}{\pi R^{2}} = \dfrac{\Delta P R^{2}}{8 \mu L} = \dfrac{u_{max}}{2}$
> - Friction factor $f = \dfrac{64}{\text{Re}}$ (laminar regime)
> - The result is also derivable directly from the **Navier–Stokes equation in cylindrical coordinates** under the same assumptions, providing full mathematical consistency.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Proof that Average Velocity Equals $u_{max}/2$

$$
\bar{u} = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{\pi \Delta P R^{4} / (8 \mu L)}{\pi R^{2}} = \frac{\Delta P R^{2}}{8 \mu L}
$$

$$
\frac{u_{max}}{2} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\Delta P R^{2}}{4 \mu L} = \frac{\Delta P R^{2}}{8 \mu L}
$$

Hence, $\bar{u} = \dfrac{u_{max}}{2}$, which is a **universal result for laminar parabolic flow**, valid in any cross-sectional geometry with a similar profile.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

### 4.3 ASCII Schematic — Parabolic Velocity Profile

```
    Pipe Cross-Section (Radius R)

          u_max ←── centerline (r = 0)
              ▲
              │██
              │████
              │██████
              │████████
              │██████████
              │████████████

              │████████████████
              │██████████████████

    WALL ─────┘                    └──── WALL (r = R, u = 0)
    
    Velocity Profile: u(r) = u_max × [1 - (r/R)²]

```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the Hagen–Poiseuille equation for laminar flow through a circular pipe. List **any four** assumptions made in its derivation.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Hagen–Poiseuille equation is:

$$
Q = \frac{\pi \, \Delta P \, R^{4}}{8 \, \mu \, L}
$$

It gives the volumetric flow rate $Q$ of a viscous, incompressible, Newtonian fluid through a cylindrical pipe of radius $R$ and length $L$, where $\Delta P$ is the pressure drop and $\mu$ is the dynamic viscosity.

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady, laminar flow.
2. Incompressible Newtonian fluid.
3. Fully developed, axisymmetric flow.
4. No-slip condition at the rigid pipe wall.
5. Constant pipe cross-section, straight and horizontal.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Show that for Hagen–Poiseuille flow, the average velocity is exactly **half** the maximum (centerline) velocity.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The volumetric flow rate is the integral of the velocity profile over the cross-sectional area:

$$
Q = \int_{0}^{R} u(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{\pi \Delta P R^{4}}{8 \mu L}
$$

The average velocity is defined as:

$$
\bar{u} = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{\pi \Delta P R^{4} / (8 \mu L)}{\pi R^{2}} = \frac{\Delta P R^{2}}{8 \mu L}
$$

The maximum velocity (at $r = 0$) from the parabolic profile is:

$$
u_{max} = \frac{\Delta P R^{2}}{4 \mu L}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,\frac{\bar{u}}{u_{max}} = \frac{1}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \bar{u} = \frac{u_{max}}{2}\,}
$$

This $\bar{u} = u_{max}/2$ relationship is a defining feature of laminar parabolic flow and is a frequently asked KTU short-answer question.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length University Examination Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted for KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the **Hagen–Poiseuille equation** for steady, laminar flow of a viscous incompressible fluid through a circular pipe. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A horizontal circular pipe of diameter **$D = 50 \text{ mm}$** and length **$L = 20 \text{ m}$** carries oil with dynamic viscosity $\mu = 0.1 \text{ N}\cdot\text{s/m}^{2}$ and density $\rho = 900 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$. The pressure drop across the pipe is $\Delta P = 60 \text{ kPa}$. Calculate:
1. The volumetric flow rate $Q$.
2. The average velocity $\bar{u}$.
3. The Reynolds number $\text{Re}$, and state the flow regime.
4. The wall shear stress $\tau_{w}$.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

1. State **five assumptions** (1 Mark).
2. Draw the cylindrical fluid element of radius $r$, length $L$ and identify forces — pressure on end faces, viscous shear on lateral surface (1 Mark).
3. Apply force balance: $\Delta P \pi r^{2} = \tau (2\pi r L)$ → derive $\tau = \dfrac{\Delta P \, r}{2L}$ (1.5 Marks).
4. Apply Newtonian constitutive law $\tau = -\mu \dfrac{du}{dr}$ (0.5 Marks).
5. Integrate twice with boundary conditions (symmetry at $r=0$ and no-slip at $r=R$) to obtain the parabolic velocity profile (2 Marks).
6. Integrate $u(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$ from $0$ to $R$ to obtain $Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P R^{4}}{8\mu L}$ (1 Mark).

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $D = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$, so $R = 0.025 \text{ m}$. $L = 20 \text{ m}$, $\mu = 0.1 \text{ N}\cdot\text{s/m}^{2}$, $\rho = 900 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$, $\Delta P = 60 \text{ kPa} = 60{,}000 \text{ Pa}$.

**(1) Volumetric Flow Rate:**

$$
Q = \frac{\pi \, \Delta P \, R^{4}}{8 \, \mu \, L} = \frac{\pi \times 60{,}000 \times (0.025)^{4}}{8 \times 0.1 \times 20}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{\pi \times 60{,}000 \times 3.90625 \times 10^{-7}}{16} = \frac{0.07363}{16} = 4.602 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q \approx 4.60 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s} \approx 4.60 \text{ L/s}}
$$

**(2) Average Velocity:**

$$
\bar{u} = \frac{Q}{\pi R^{2}} = \frac{4.602 \times 10^{-3}}{\pi \times (0.025)^{2}} = \frac{4.602 \times 10^{-3}}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 2.344 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\bar{u} \approx 2.34 \text{ m/s}}
$$

**(3) Reynolds Number:**

$$
\text{Re} = \frac{\rho \bar{u} D}{\mu} = \frac{900 \times 2.344 \times 0.05}{0.1} = \frac{105.48}{0.1} = 1054.8
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Re} \approx 1055 < 2100 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Laminar flow}}
$$

**(4) Wall Shear Stress:**

$$
\tau_{w} = \frac{\Delta P \, R}{2 L} = \frac{60{,}000 \times 0.025}{2 \times 20} = \frac{1500}{40} = 37.5 \text{ N/m}^{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau_{w} = 37.5 \text{ Pa}}
$$

---

#### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted for KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A capillary tube of diameter **$D = 2 \text{ mm}$** and length **$L = 1 \text{ m}$** is used to measure the viscosity of a Newtonian oil. The pressure drop across the tube is $\Delta P = 10 \text{ kPa}$ and the measured flow rate is $Q = 3.14 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}$.
1. Calculate the viscosity $\mu$ of the oil.
2. If the same oil flows through a tube of diameter $4 \text{ mm}$ at the **same pressure drop** over the **same length**, by what factor does the flow rate change? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For Hagen–Poiseuille flow, derive the expression for the **Darcy friction factor** $f$ in terms of the Reynolds number $\text{Re}$. Using this, compute the head loss in a horizontal pipe of diameter $D = 100 \text{ mm}$ and length $L = 500 \text{ m}$ carrying water at an average velocity of $1 \text{ m/s}$. Take $\mu_{water} = 10^{-3} \text{ N}\cdot\text{s/m}^{2}$ and $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $D = 2 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow R = 1 \text{ mm} = 10^{-3} \text{ m}$, $L = 1 \text{ m}$, $\Delta P = 10 \text{ kPa} = 10{,}000 \text{ Pa}$, $Q = 3.14 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}$.

**(1) Viscosity Calculation:**

Rearranging the Hagen–Poiseuille equation:

$$
\mu = \frac{\pi \, \Delta P \, R^{4}}{8 \, Q \, L} = \frac{\pi \times 10{,}000 \times (10^{-3})^{4}}{8 \times 3.14 \times 10^{-6} \times 1}
$$

$$
\mu = \frac{\pi \times 10{,}000 \times 10^{-12}}{2.512 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{3.1416 \times 10^{-8}}{2.512 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N}\cdot\text{s/m}^{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\mu = 1.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N}\cdot\text{s/m}^{2}}
$$

**(2) Effect of Doubling the Diameter:**

Since $Q \propto R^{4}$ (all other parameters constant), and the new radius $R' = 2R$:

$$
\frac{Q'}{Q} = \left(\frac{R'}{R}\right)^{4} = 2^{4} = 16
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Flow rate increases by a factor of } 16}
$$

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Derivation of Darcy Friction Factor:**
The Darcy–Weisbach equation for head loss is:

$$
h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\bar{u}^{2}}{2g}
$$

Also, the wall shear stress is related to the pressure drop by $\tau_{w} = \dfrac{\Delta P \, R}{2L}$, and $\Delta P = \rho g h_f$. Substituting:

$$
\tau_{w} = \frac{\rho g h_f R}{2L} = \frac{\rho \bar{u}^{2} f}{8}
$$

Solving for $f$:

$$
f = \frac{64 \, \mu}{\rho \bar{u} D} = \frac{64}{\text{Re}}
$$

$$
\boxed{f = \frac{64}{\text{Re}} \quad \text{(Laminar)}}
$$

**Numerical Computation:**
Given: $D = 0.1 \text{ m}$, $L = 500 \text{ m}$, $\bar{u} = 1 \text{ m/s}$, $\mu = 10^{-3} \text{ N}\cdot\text{s/m}^{2}$, $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$.

**Reynolds Number:**

$$
\text{Re} = \frac{\rho \bar{u} D}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 1 \times 0.1}{10^{-3}} = 1.0 \times 10^{5}
$$

**Friction Factor:**

$$
f = \frac{64}{10^{5}} = 6.4 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Head Loss:**

$$
h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\bar{u}^{2}}{2g} = 6.4 \times 10^{-4} \times \frac{500}{0.1} \times \frac{1^{2}}{2 \times 9.81}
$$

$$
h_f = 6.4 \times 10^{-4} \times 5000 \times 0.05097 = 0.1631 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{h_f \approx 0.163 \text{ m of water}}
$$

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks (and How to Avoid It)**
>
> 1. **Missing the $R^{4}$ vs $D^{4}$ confusion:** Since $R = D/2$, substituting directly into $Q = \pi \Delta P R^{4} / (8 \mu L)$ gives $Q = \pi \Delta P D^{4} / (128 \mu L)$. Forgetting the factor of $2^{4} = 16$ is a common error worth **2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the Reynolds number check:** Even if you solve using Hagen–Poiseuille, always verify $\text{Re} < 2100$ to claim the result is valid. A full 14-mark question that omits this loses a *validation mark*.
> 3. **Wrong sign convention in shear stress:** The relation $\tau = -\mu (du/dr)$ has a *negative* sign for the standard coordinate system. Writing it as $\tau = \mu (du/dr)$ and not addressing the *physical meaning* of resistive shear costs marks.
> 4. **Boundary condition mistakes:** The two required boundary conditions are **(i)** symmetry: $du/dr = 0$ at $r = 0$ (or finite velocity), and **(ii)** no-slip: $u = 0$ at $r = R$. Missing either leads to an incorrect integration constant.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency:** Pressures must be in $\text{Pa}$, lengths in $\text{m}$, viscosity in $\text{N}\cdot\text{s/m}^{2}$, otherwise the result for $Q$ will be dimensionally incorrect. Examiners strictly deduct **1 mark** for unit errors.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions explicitly:** Even a perfectly correct derivation without an explicit "assumptions list" is penalized. **Always dedicate the first 1–2 lines** to listing the assumptions before writing equations.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core equation (memorize):** $Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P R^{4}}{8 \mu L}$ — also written as $Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P D^{4}}{128 \mu L}$.
- **Velocity profile:** Parabolic; $u(r) = u_{max}\left[1 - (r/R)^{2}\right]$; **zero at the wall, maximum at the centerline**.
- **Universal relationship:** $\bar{u} = u_{max}/2$ — a defining property of laminar parabolic flow.
- **Shear stress:** Linear in $r$; **zero at the centerline, maximum at the wall**: $\tau_{w} = \Delta P R / (2L)$.
- **Friction factor for laminar flow:** $f = 64/\text{Re}$ — the foundation for the Moody chart's laminar branch.
- **Fourth-power radius dependence:** Doubling $R$ **multiplies $Q$ by 16**; halving $R$ reduces $Q$ by a factor of **16** — the basis of severe physiological consequences in arterial stenosis.
- **Validity range:** $\text{Re} < 2100$, Newtonian fluid, steady, laminar, fully developed, incompressible, rigid circular pipe.
- **Connection to energy equation:** The Hagen–Poiseuille result is the **viscous loss term** in the extended Bernoulli/Darcy–Weisbach equation for laminar flow.
- **Common exam traps:** Sign of the velocity gradient, missing Reynolds check, units, $D$ vs $R$ substitution.
- **Engineering legacy:** Forms the analytical backbone of **capillary viscometry, blood-flow modeling, hydrodynamic lubrication, and microchannel design**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Turbulent Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET413 - MECHANICS OF FLUID FLOW
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Turbulent Flow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🌊 Turbulent Flow — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Turbulent Flow** is a regime of fluid motion characterized by **chaotic, irregular, and random fluctuations** in velocity and pressure at every point in the flow field. The fluid particles move in **eddies and vortices** of varying sizes, and the flow exhibits strong mixing, three-dimensionality, and a high degree of apparent randomness superimposed on an underlying time-averaged (mean) motion.

The mathematical signature of turbulent flow is that the instantaneous velocity at any point can be decomposed as:

$$\vec{V} = \bar{\vec{V}} + \vec{V}'$$

where $\bar{\vec{V}}$ is the **time-averaged mean velocity** and $\vec{V}'$ is the **fluctuating component** (with $\overline{V'} = 0$ over a sufficiently long time interval).

The flow is classified as turbulent when the **Reynolds Number** exceeds the **Critical Reynolds Number**:

$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} > Re_{critical}$$

For flow in smooth circular pipes, the transition from laminar to turbulent typically occurs at $Re_{critical} \approx \mathbf{2300}$ (lower) to $\mathbf{4000}$ (upper), with $Re > \mathbf{4000}$ being definitively turbulent.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine two different ways a crowd can move through a narrow corridor.
>
> - **Laminar flow** = A disciplined, orderly line of people walking one behind the other at a steady pace. Each person follows the one ahead smoothly.
> - **Turbulent flow** = A chaotic rush-hour crowd where people bump into each other, swirl in small groups (like eddies), cross paths, and mix thoroughly. The overall crowd still moves forward, but locally everything is random.
>
> In turbulent pipe flow, fluid particles (instead of people) form **swirling eddies** of all sizes, transfer momentum aggressively across streamlines, and cause much higher wall friction than laminar flow. This is why pumping costs are dramatically higher for turbulent flow.

## 1.3 Physical Significance & Key Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value / Range | Engineering Importance |
|---|---|---|
| Critical Reynolds Number (pipe) | **2300 – 4000** | Defines transition threshold |
| Friction factor $f$ | 0.008 – 0.05 (smooth pipes) | Used in Darcy-Weisbach equation |
| Mixing length $l$ | $\approx 0.4 \, y$ (near wall) | Prandtl's hypothesis |
| von Kármán constant $\kappa$ | **0.41** | Universal velocity profile |
| Turbulent intensity | $\frac{\sqrt{\overline{V'^2}}}{\bar{V}} \approx 1\%-10\%$ | Flow quality measure |

## 1.4 Distinguishing Features of Turbulent Flow

- **Irregularity:** Velocities fluctuate randomly in magnitude and direction.
- **Diffusivity:** Rapid mixing due to eddy motion; high momentum and heat transfer.
- **Dissipative:** Turbulent kinetic energy continuously converts to heat via viscous action.
- **Three-dimensionality:** Even in straight 2D geometry, fluctuations occur in all three directions.
- **Continuum:** Despite randomness, the Navier-Stokes equations still apply at every instant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 of PECET413 emphasizes the theoretical description of turbulent flow using **Reynolds decomposition**, **Reynolds stresses**, and **Prandtl's mixing length theory** — all of which are derivable from first principles and form the basis of all modern CFD turbulence models.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Reynolds Decomposition of Velocity

The instantaneous velocity is split into mean and fluctuating parts:

$$u = \bar{u} + u' \quad ; \quad v = \bar{v} + v' \quad ; \quad w = \bar{w} + w'$$

Time-averaging rules:
- $\overline{\bar{u}} = \bar{u}$
- $\overline{u'} = 0$
- $\overline{\bar{u} \cdot u'} = 0$
- $\overline{u_1' u_2'} \neq 0$ (this is the source of Reynolds stress)
- $\overline{u_1' + u_2'} = \overline{u_1'} + \overline{u_2'}$

## 2.2 Derivation of Reynolds Stresses (Boussinesq Hypothesis)

Starting from the instantaneous Navier-Stokes equation in the x-direction and substituting $u = \bar{u} + u'$:

$$\rho \frac{D\bar{u}}{Dt} = -\frac{\partial \bar{p}}{\partial x} + \mu \nabla^2 \bar{u} + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(-\rho \overline{u'v'}\right)$$

The term $-\rho \overline{u'v'}$ is the **apparent or Reynolds shear stress**:

$$\boxed{\tau_{turb} = -\rho \overline{u'v'}}$$

The total shear stress in turbulent flow becomes:

$$\tau_{total} = \tau_{viscous} + \tau_{turbulent} = \mu \frac{d\bar{u}}{dy} + (-\rho \overline{u'v'})$$

## 2.3 Prandtl's Mixing Length Theory

Prandtl (1925) postulated that fluid lumps (eddies) retain their momentum over a characteristic distance $l$ (the mixing length) before mixing with surrounding fluid. This is analogous to the molecular mean free path in kinetic theory.

**Key postulate:**
$$\tau_{turb} = \rho l^2 \left\vert \frac{d\bar{u}}{dy} \right\vert \frac{d\bar{u}}{dy}$$

where $l$ = mixing length (function of position $y$ from the wall).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Mixing length theory transforms the *statistical* quantity $\overline{u'v'}$ into a *geometric* quantity $l$, making turbulent shear stress computable from the mean velocity profile alone.

## 2.4 Velocity Distribution in Turbulent Flow (Smooth Pipes)

Combining the total shear stress (assumed constant across pipe radius for fully developed flow, equal to $\tau_0$ at the wall):

$$\tau_0 = (\mu + \rho l^2 \left\vert \frac{du}{dy} \right\vert) \frac{du}{dy}$$

Near the wall, in the **inner layer** (viscous sublayer + buffer layer), $l = \kappa y$ with $\kappa = 0.41$ (von Kármán constant). Integration gives the **Universal Velocity Distribution (Logarithmic Law)**:

$$\boxed{\frac{u}{u_*} = \frac{1}{\kappa} \ln y + C}$$

In dimensionless wall coordinates $(u^+, y^+)$:

$$\frac{u^+}{u_*} = \frac{1}{\kappa} \ln y^+ + B$$

where:
- $u_* = \sqrt{\tau_0 / \rho}$ = friction (shear) velocity
- $y^+ = \frac{u_* y}{\nu}$ = dimensionless wall distance
- $B \approx 5.0$ (for smooth walls)

## 2.5 Hydrodynamically Smooth and Rough Pipes

A pipe is **hydrodynamically smooth** if the roughness elements $k_s$ are buried within the viscous sublayer. Otherwise, it is **rough**.

| Regime | Condition | Velocity Law |
|---|---|---|
| Viscous sublayer | $y^+ < 5$ | $u^+ = y^+$ (linear) |
| Buffer layer | $5 < y^+ < 30$ | Transition region |
| Log-law region (smooth) | $30 < y^+ < 400$ | $u^+ = \frac{1}{\kappa} \ln y^+ + 5.0$ |
| Fully turbulent (smooth) | $y^+ > 400$ | Log law valid |
| Rough wall | $y^+ > k_s^+$ | $u^+ = \frac{1}{\kappa} \ln(y/k_s) + 8.5$ |

## 2.6 Friction Factor Relations

**Smooth pipes** (Blasius equation, $Re < 10^5$):
$$f = \frac{0.316}{Re^{0.25}}$$

**Smooth pipes** (Prandtl-von Kármán, all $Re$):
$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = 2 \log_{10}(Re\sqrt{f}) - 0.8$$

**Rough pipes** (Colebrook-White transition formula):
$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\left(\frac{k_s/D}{3.7} + \frac{2.51}{Re\sqrt{f}}\right)$$

**Fully rough** (no viscous term):
$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\left(\frac{k_s/D}{3.7}\right)$$

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Re = \rho V D / \mu$ | Reynolds number |
| 2 | $\tau_t = -\rho \overline{u'v'}$ | Reynolds stress |
| 3 | $\tau_0 = (1/4) \rho V^2 f$ | Wall shear from friction factor |
| 4 | $u_* = \sqrt{\tau_0/\rho}$ | Friction velocity |
| 5 | $l = \kappa y$ | Mixing length near wall |
| 6 | $\tau_t = \rho l^2 \left\vert du/dy \right\vert du/dy$ | Prandtl mixing length |
| 7 | $u^+ = (1/\kappa)\ln y^+ + 5.0$ | Log-law (smooth) |
| 8 | $f = 0.316 / Re^{0.25}$ | Blasius smooth pipe |
| 9 | $h_f = f (L/D) (V^2/2g)$ | Darcy-Weisbach head loss |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of pipelines:** Determine pumping power for water, oil, and gas transport.
- **Aerodynamics:** Predict skin friction drag on aircraft, cars, ships.
- **Process engineering:** Sizing heat exchangers (turbulent flow enhances heat transfer).
- **Hydraulics:** Open channel and closed conduit design for dams and urban drainage.
- **CFD validation:** Mixing length theory forms the basis of the Spalart-Allmaras and $k$-$\varepsilon$ turbulence models.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Reynolds Equations from Navier-Stokes

**Starting point — Instantaneous x-momentum equation (incompressible):**

$$\rho \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z}\right) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu\left(\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial z^2}\right) + \rho g_x$$

**Step 1:** Substitute Reynolds decomposition $u = \bar{u} + u'$, $v = \bar{v} + v'$, $w = \bar{w} + w'$, $p = \bar{p} + p'$.

$$\rho \left(\frac{\partial (\bar{u}+u')}{\partial t} + (\bar{u}+u')\frac{\partial (\bar{u}+u')}{\partial x} + \cdots \right) = -\frac{\partial (\bar{p}+p')}{\partial x} + \mu \nabla^2 (\bar{u}+u') + \rho g_x$$

**Step 2:** Take the time average of the entire equation. All fluctuating products $\overline{u'v'}$, $\overline{u'u'}$, etc., survive only in the convective terms.

The convective term expands as:
$$(\bar{u}+u')\frac{\partial (\bar{u}+u')}{\partial x} = \bar{u}\frac{\partial \bar{u}}{\partial x} + \bar{u}\frac{\partial u'}{\partial x} + u'\frac{\partial \bar{u}}{\partial x} + u'\frac{\partial u'}{\partial x}$$

**Step 3:** Apply time-averaging rules. The cross terms $\bar{u}(\partial u'/\partial x)$ average to zero, but $u'(\partial u'/\partial x)$ gives $\overline{u'\partial u'/\partial x} = \partial(\overline{u'^2}/2)/\partial x$.

**Step 4:** The averaged equation becomes (RANS — Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes):

$$\rho\left(\frac{\partial \bar{u}}{\partial t} + \bar{u}\frac{\partial \bar{u}}{\partial x} + \bar{v}\frac{\partial \bar{u}}{\partial y}\right) = -\frac{\partial \bar{p}}{\partial x} + \mu \nabla^2 \bar{u} + \frac{\partial}{\partial x}(-\rho\overline{u'^2}) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}(-\rho\overline{u'v'}) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}(-\rho\overline{u'w'}) + \rho g_x$$

**Step 5:** Define Reynolds stress tensor:

$$\boxed{\tau_{ij} = -\rho \overline{u_i' u_j'}}$$

The six independent components (three normal, three shear) act as additional stresses — the **Reynolds stresses** — representing momentum transfer by eddies.

## 3.2 Derivation: Logarithmic Velocity Distribution (Smooth Pipe)

**Assumption:** For fully developed pipe flow at the wall, total shear stress is approximately constant and equal to wall shear $\tau_0$:

$$\tau_0 = \tau_{visc} + \tau_{turb} = \mu \frac{du}{dy} + \rho l^2 \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^2$$

**Step 1:** In the fully turbulent core (far from wall), viscous stress is negligible:

$$\tau_0 \approx \rho l^2 \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{du}{dy} = \frac{1}{l}\sqrt{\frac{\tau_0}{\rho}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $u_* = \sqrt{\tau_0/\rho}$ (friction velocity) and $l = \kappa y$ (Prandtl's postulate near wall):

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{u_*}{\kappa y}$$

**Step 3:** Integrate with respect to $y$:

$$u = \frac{u_*}{\kappa} \ln y + C_1$$

**Step 4:** Express in wall coordinates $u^+ = u/u_*$ and $y^+ = u_* y / \nu$:

$$u^+ = \frac{1}{\kappa} \ln y^+ + B$$

**Step 5:** Experimental data from Nikuradse and others gives $\kappa = 0.41$ and $B \approx 5.0$ for hydraulically smooth pipes:

$$\boxed{\frac{u}{u_*} = 2.5 \ln\left(\frac{u_* y}{\nu}\right) + 5.0}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **Universal Velocity Distribution Law** — it does not depend on pipe diameter or Reynolds number when expressed in inner variables $(u^+, y^+)$. It is one of the most celebrated results in fluid mechanics and a frequent KTU exam topic.

## 3.3 Derivation: Friction Factor from Wall Shear

The wall shear stress for a pipe of radius $R$ and length $L$ carrying velocity $V$:

**Force balance:** Pressure force = Wall shear force

$$\Delta p \cdot \pi R^2 = \tau_0 \cdot 2\pi R \cdot L$$

**Solving for $\tau_0$:**

$$\tau_0 = \frac{\Delta p \cdot R}{2 L}$$

**Expressing $\Delta p$ in terms of friction factor $f$** (Darcy definition):

$$\Delta p = f \frac{L}{D} \frac{\rho V^2}{2} = f \frac{L}{2R} \frac{\rho V^2}{2} = \frac{f L \rho V^2}{4R}$$

**Substituting back:**

$$\tau_0 = \frac{1}{4} \rho V^2 f \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{f = \frac{4 \tau_0}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V^2} = \frac{8 \tau_0}{\rho V^2}}$$

Therefore:

$$u_* = \sqrt{\frac{\tau_0}{\rho}} = V\sqrt{\frac{f}{8}}$$

**Head loss:**

$$h_f = f \frac{L}{D}\frac{V^2}{2g}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Comparison Reference

| Property | Laminar | Turbulent |
|---|---|---|
| Velocity profile | Parabolic (Poiseuille) | Flattened (logarithmic) |
| Friction factor | $f = 64/Re$ | $f = 0.316/Re^{0.25}$ (smooth, Blasius) |
| Wall shear proportionality | $\propto V$ | $\propto V^{1.75}$ (Blasius) |
| Mixing | Molecular diffusion only | Strong eddy mixing |
| Critical $Re$ (pipe) | < 2300 | > 4000 |
| Momentum transfer | Viscous (Newtonian) | Viscous + Reynolds stresses |
| Energy dissipation | Low | High (eddy cascade) |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** Water at **20 °C** ($\rho = 998$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s) flows through a smooth pipe of diameter **$D = 50$ mm** at mean velocity **$V = 3$ m/s**. Find: (a) Reynolds number, (b) Friction factor using Blasius, (c) Wall shear stress, (d) Friction velocity, (e) Head loss per 100 m.

**Step 1 — Reynolds number:**

$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{998 \times 3 \times 0.05}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{149.7}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.494 \times 10^5$$

**Step 2 — Blasius friction factor (valid for $Re < 10^5$ is borderline; use it as approximation):**

$$f = \frac{0.316}{(1.494 \times 10^5)^{0.25}} = \frac{0.316}{19.66} = 0.01607$$

**Step 3 — Wall shear stress:**

$$\tau_0 = \frac{1}{4}\rho V^2 f = \frac{1}{4} \times 998 \times 9 \times 0.01607 = 36.07 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 4 — Friction velocity:**

$$u_* = \sqrt{\frac{\tau_0}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{36.07}{998}} = \sqrt{0.0361} = 0.1901 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Head loss per 100 m:**

$$h_f = f \frac{L}{D}\frac{V^2}{2g} = 0.01607 \times \frac{100}{0.05} \times \frac{9}{19.62} = 14.74 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The friction velocity $u_*$ is much smaller than $V$ — typically 5–10% of mean velocity. Yet it is the **scaling velocity** for the entire near-wall turbulence structure, making it one of the most important velocity scales in turbulent flow analysis.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Concept Map — Turbulent Flow Theoretical Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Turbulent Flow Theory] --> B[1. Reynolds Decomposition]
    A --> C[2. RANS Equations]
    A --> D[3. Reynolds Stresses]
    A --> E[4. Closure Problem]
    A --> F[5. Mixing Length Theory]
    A --> G[6. Velocity Distribution]
    A --> H[7. Friction Factor Laws]

    B --> B1[Instantaneous V equals Mean V plus Fluctuation V prime]
    C --> C1[Time averaged Navier Stokes]
    D --> D1[Apparent shear stress tau t equals minus rho times mean u prime v prime]
    E --> E1[More unknowns than equations]
    F --> F1[Prandtl postulate: l equals kappa y near wall]
    G --> G1[Log law: u plus equals ln y plus divided by kappa plus B]
    H --> H1[Blasius: f equals 0.316 over Re to the 0.25]

    F1 --> G1
    D1 --> H1
    C1 --> E1
    E1 --> F1
    G1 --> H1

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px

    style G fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff

```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Engineering Flow for Turbulent Pipe Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    A --> B{Re greater than 4000?}

    B -- No --> D[Laminar or Transition]
    C --> E[Choose Friction Factor Model]

    E --> G[Prandtl von Karman for all Re]

    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Friction Velocity u star]
    J --> K[Log Law Velocity Profile]
```

## 4.3 Process Diagram — Turbulent Energy Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Mean Flow Kinetic Energy] -->|extracted by instability| P2[Large Anisotropic Eddies]
    P2 -->|eddy stretching| P3[Intermediate Inertial Eddies]
    P3 -->|Richardson cascade| P4[Small Universal Eddies Kolmogorov Scale]
    P4 -->|viscous dissipation| P5[Heat Energy]

    P3 -.->|production| P2
    P4 -.->|transfer down| P3

```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading aid for Mermaid diagrams:** The diagrams above map the logical architecture of turbulent flow theory. The red node (closure problem) marks the central difficulty of turbulence modeling — more unknowns than equations. The green nodes (mixing length and log law) represent the **first successful engineering closure** by Prandtl. The energy cascade diagram illustrates why turbulence is fundamentally a **dissipative process** — large eddies extract energy from the mean flow, smaller eddies dissipate it as heat through viscosity.
