# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Define Reynolds stresses. How do they arise in turbulent flow?**

**Model Answer:**

Reynolds stresses are the **apparent (additional) stresses** that appear in the time-averaged Navier-Stokes equations due to the correlation between fluctuating velocity components in a turbulent flow.

$$\tau_t = -\rho \overline{u' v'}$$

They arise because when the Navier-Stokes equations are time-averaged, the non-linear convective terms $\overline{u_i u_j}$ split into $\overline{u_i}\,\overline{u_j} + \overline{u_i' u_j'}$. The latter term does **not vanish** (unlike the linear fluctuating products) and acts as a stress. Physically, it represents **momentum transfer by turbulent eddies** from regions of high mean velocity to regions of low mean velocity — analogous to (but vastly more powerful than) molecular viscous transfer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key marks:** Mention Reynolds decomposition, show the correlation form $-\rho \overline{u'v'}$, and explain eddy momentum transfer. (3 marks)

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the physical significance of the friction velocity $u_*$ in turbulent flow?**

**Model Answer:**

The friction velocity is defined as $u_* = \sqrt{\tau_0/\rho}$ and represents a **velocity scale derived from wall shear stress**. Although numerically small (typically 5–10% of mean flow velocity), it is the **fundamental scaling parameter** for the near-wall turbulent flow region.

**Significance:**
- It non-dimensionalizes the mean velocity profile into the **universal log-law**: $u^+ = (1/\kappa)\ln y^+ + B$.
- It sets the thickness of the viscous sublayer $\delta_v = \nu/u_*$.
- It characterizes the intensity of turbulent fluctuations near the wall.
- It links wall shear to mean velocity: $u_* = V\sqrt{f/8}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key marks:** Definition, role in log-law, scaling of viscous sublayer, link to friction factor. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a) Starting from the Navier-Stokes equations, derive the Reynolds-averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) equations for incompressible turbulent flow. Clearly define Reynolds stresses. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the instantaneous x-momentum equation:**

$$\rho \frac{Du}{Dt} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \nabla^2 u + \rho g_x$$

Expanding the material derivative:

$$\rho\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z}\right) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \nabla^2 u + \rho g_x$$

**Step 2 — Apply Reynolds decomposition:**

$$u = \bar{u} + u', \quad v = \bar{v} + v', \quad p = \bar{p} + p'$$

Substitute into the x-momentum equation.

**Step 3 — Take the time average of every term:**

For a generic term $u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}$:

$$\overline{(\bar{u}+u')\frac{\partial(\bar{u}+u')}{\partial x}} = \bar{u}\frac{\partial \bar{u}}{\partial x} + \overline{u'\frac{\partial u'}{\partial x}} = \bar{u}\frac{\partial \bar{u}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{\overline{u'^2}}{2}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Generalize cross-product terms:**

The product $\overline{u_i u_j} = \bar{u}_i \bar{u}_j + \overline{u_i' u_j'}$ (a fundamental identity of Reynolds decomposition).

**Step 5 — The averaged x-momentum equation (RANS):**

$$\rho\left(\frac{\partial \bar{u}}{\partial t} + \bar{u}\frac{\partial \bar{u}}{\partial x} + \bar{v}\frac{\partial \bar{u}}{\partial y} + \bar{w}\frac{\partial \bar{u}}{\partial z}\right) = -\frac{\partial \bar{p}}{\partial x} + \mu \nabla^2 \bar{u} + \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(-\rho\overline{u'^2}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(-\rho\overline{u'v'}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(-\rho\overline{u'w'}\right) + \rho g_x$$

**Step 6 — Define Reynolds stress tensor:**

$$\tau_{ij}^R = -\rho \overline{u_i' u_j'}$$

This tensor has 9 components (6 independent by symmetry). The diagonal terms $-\rho \overline{u_i'^2}$ are **normal stresses**; the off-diagonal $-\rho \overline{u_i' u_j'}$ are **shear stresses**.

**Conclusion:** RANS equations resemble the original Navier-Stokes equations in form, but with six additional unknown stress terms (Reynolds stresses). The system is therefore **unclosed** — there are more unknowns than equations, which is the **closure problem** of turbulence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key marks:** Reynolds decomposition (2), product rule expansion (2), time-averaging (1), RANS equation (1), closure problem statement (1). (7 marks)

---

**(b) Explain Prandtl's mixing length theory. Using this, derive the logarithmic velocity distribution for fully developed turbulent flow in a smooth pipe. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Prandtl's postulate:**

A fluid lump (eddy) originating at height $y$ retains its original momentum $\rho u(y)$ until it travels a distance $l$ (the mixing length) perpendicular to the flow, where it mixes with the local fluid. The transverse velocity fluctuation is:

$$v' \sim l \left\vert \frac{du}{dy} \right\vert$$

The longitudinal fluctuation is of the same order:

$$u' \sim l \left\vert \frac{du}{dy} \right\vert$$

**Step 2 — Reynolds stress via mixing length:**

$$\tau_t = -\rho \overline{u'v'} = \rho l^2 \left\vert \frac{du}{dy} \right\vert \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Step 3 — Total shear stress (constant across pipe, equal to wall shear $\tau_0$):**

$$\tau_0 = \mu \frac{du}{dy} + \rho l^2 \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^2$$

**Step 4 — Apply to the fully turbulent core (viscous term negligible):**

$$\tau_0 = \rho l^2 \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{du}{dy} = \frac{1}{l}\sqrt{\frac{\tau_0}{\rho}}$$

**Step 5 — Introduce friction velocity $u_* = \sqrt{\tau_0/\rho}$ and Prandtl's postulate $l = \kappa y$ (valid near wall, with von Kármán constant $\kappa = 0.41$):**

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{u_*}{\kappa y}$$

**Step 6 — Integrate with respect to $y$:**

$$u = \frac{u_*}{\kappa}\ln y + C$$

**Step 7 — Non-dimensionalize using $u^+ = u/u_*$ and $y^+ = u_* y / \nu$:**

$$\boxed{\frac{u}{u_*} = \frac{1}{\kappa}\ln\left(\frac{u_* y}{\nu}\right) + B}$$

With $\kappa = 0.41$ and $B \approx 5.0$ (from Nikuradse's experiments):

$$\frac{u}{u_*} = 2.5 \ln\left(\frac{u_* y}{\nu}\right) + 5.0$$

This is the **Universal Logarithmic Velocity Distribution Law** for smooth pipes — the cornerstone of turbulent boundary layer theory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key marks:** Prandtl postulate (2), Reynolds stress form (1), constant total stress (1), mixing length $l=\kappa y$ (1), integration (1), final log-law (1). (7 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part B):**
> - Students often **forget to time-average the convective term** correctly — the product $\overline{u'v'}$ is the most critical step; missing it loses 2–3 marks.
> - The **closure problem** must be mentioned explicitly in (a); examiners specifically look for it.
> - In (b), students frequently confuse $l$ (mixing length) with $y$ — Prandtl's relation $l = \kappa y$ is **near the wall only**; away from the wall a different form (e.g., $l = 0.085\,\delta$ for free shear layers) applies.
> - Forgetting the constant of integration $B = 5.0$ in the log-law leads to a **half-mark deduction** in most valuation schemes.
> - Don't write the log-law without specifying it applies to **smooth pipes**; mention Nikuradse experiments for full marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) Discuss the differences between laminar and turbulent flow. Define the critical Reynolds number and explain its significance in pipe flow. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Differences between Laminar and Turbulent Flow:**

| Aspect | Laminar | Turbulent |
|---|---|---|
| Velocity profile | Parabolic (Poiseuille) | Flattened (logarithmic) |
| Fluid particle motion | Smooth, parallel layers | Chaotic eddies of all sizes |
| Momentum transfer | Molecular viscosity only | Viscous + Reynolds stresses |
| Friction factor $f$ | $f = 64/Re$ | $f = 0.316/Re^{0.25}$ (Blasius) |
| Wall shear $\tau_0$ | $\propto V$ | $\propto V^{1.75}$ |
| Energy dissipation | Low (purely viscous) | High (eddy cascade) |
| Reynolds number | $Re < 2300$ | $Re > 4000$ |
| Mixing | Molecular diffusion only | Strong convective mixing |
| Pressure drop | $\propto V$ | $\propto V^{1.75}$ |

**Critical Reynolds Number — Definition and Significance:**

The critical Reynolds number is the value of $Re$ at which laminar flow becomes unstable and transitions to turbulence. For flow in a smooth circular pipe:
- **Lower critical $Re_{cr}$** = 2300 (below this, flow is always laminar)
- **Upper critical $Re_{cr}$** = 4000 (above this, flow is always turbulent)
- Between 2300 and 4000, the flow is in a **transitional** regime where both laminar and turbulent patches coexist.

**Significance:**
- **Design rule:** Engineers size pipes and velocities to remain either well below 2300 (laminar, predictable) or well above 4000 (turbulent, fully developed).
- **Flow meter selection:** Laminar vs. turbulent determines the discharge coefficient in orifices, venturis, etc.
- **Heat transfer:** Turbulent flow dramatically increases convective heat transfer coefficients, influencing exchanger design.
- **Pumping power:** Head loss jumps from $\propto V$ (laminar) to $\propto V^{1.75}$ (turbulent), so operating regime affects energy cost.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key marks:** Tabular differences (3), critical $Re$ values (2), engineering significance (2). (7 marks)

---

**(b) For a smooth pipe of diameter 100 mm, water flows with a mean velocity of 2.5 m/s. Take $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ and $\mu = 10^{-3}$ Pa·s. Determine: (i) Reynolds number, (ii) Friction factor using Blasius formula, (iii) Wall shear stress, (iv) Friction velocity, and (v) Head loss over 200 m length. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $D = 0.1$ m
- $V = 2.5$ m/s
- $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³
- $\mu = 10^{-3}$ Pa·s
- $L = 200$ m

**(i) Reynolds number:**

$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 2.5 \times 0.1}{10^{-3}} = \frac{250}{10^{-3}} = 2.5 \times 10^5$$

Since $Re > 4000$, the flow is **turbulent**. ✅

**(ii) Friction factor (Blasius formula, valid for smooth pipes with $Re < 10^5$; for higher $Re$, use the Prandtl-von Kármán equation. Here, we use Blasius as a direct application of the syllabus formula):**

$$f = \frac{0.316}{Re^{0.25}} = \frac{0.316}{(2.5 \times 10^5)^{0.25}}$$

$$(2.5 \times 10^5)^{0.25} = (250000)^{0.25} = 22.36$$

$$f = \frac{0.316}{22.36} = 0.01413$$

**(iii) Wall shear stress:**

$$\tau_0 = \frac{1}{4}\rho V^2 f = \frac{1}{4} \times 1000 \times (2.5)^2 \times 0.01413$$

$$\tau_0 = 250 \times 6.25 \times 0.01413 = 22.08 \text{ Pa}$$

**(iv) Friction velocity:**

$$u_* = \sqrt{\frac{\tau_0}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{22.08}{1000}} = \sqrt{0.02208} = 0.1486 \text{ m/s}$$

**(v) Head loss over 200 m:**

$$h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g} = 0.01413 \times \frac{200}{0.1} \times \frac{(2.5)^2}{2 \times 9.81}$$

$$h_f = 0.01413 \times 2000 \times \frac{6.25}{19.62} = 0.01413 \times 2000 \times 0.3185$$

$$h_f = 9.00 \text{ m}$$

**Summary of Results:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Reynolds number $Re$ | $2.5 \times 10^5$ |
| Friction factor $f$ | 0.01413 |
| Wall shear stress $\tau_0$ | 22.08 Pa |
| Friction velocity $u_*$ | 0.1486 m/s |
| Head loss $h_f$ | 9.00 m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key marks:** Reynolds number with correct units (1), Blasius application (1), $\tau_0$ formula (1), $u_*$ formula (1), $h_f$ formula (2), final numerical answer (1). (7 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part B):**
> - **Always state the flow regime** explicitly after computing $Re$ — examiners check for this first.
> - Many students confuse **Darcy friction factor $f$** with **Fanning friction factor** ($f_{Fanning} = f/4$). For KTU, use the **Darcy** form unless otherwise specified.
> - The Blasius equation $f = 0.316/Re^{0.25}$ is strictly valid for $Re < 10^5$. For higher $Re$, state that the Prandtl-von Kármán implicit formula should be used; for full marks, mention this limit.
> - Unit consistency: when computing $u_*$ in m/s, ensure $\tau_0$ is in Pa and $\rho$ in kg/m³.
> - Common arithmetic error: $\frac{V^2}{2g} = \frac{6.25}{19.62} = 0.3185$ m — students often write $0.319$ which is acceptable within rounding.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist — Turbulent Flow**

- ✅ **Reynolds decomposition** is the foundation: every instantaneous quantity = mean + fluctuation.
- ✅ **Reynolds stresses** $\tau_t = -\rho \overline{u'v'}$ appear in RANS equations as additional apparent stresses.
- ✅ **Closure problem** — RANS has more unknowns than equations; turbulence models provide closure.
- ✅ **Mixing length $l$** is Prandtl's geometric analog of the molecular mean free path.
- ✅ **Log-law** $u^+ = (1/\kappa)\ln y^+ + 5.0$ with $\kappa = 0.41$ is the **Universal Velocity Distribution** for smooth turbulent pipe flow.
- ✅ **Friction velocity** $u_* = \sqrt{\tau_0/\rho} = V\sqrt{f/8}$ — the natural velocity scale near walls.
- ✅ **Critical $Re$** for pipe flow: **2300** (lower), **4000** (upper). Above 4000 → turbulent; below 2300 → laminar.
- ✅ **Blasius** $f = 0.316/Re^{0.25}$ (smooth, $Re < 10^5$); **Colebrook-White** for rough pipes.
- ✅ **Darcy-Weisbach** $h_f = f(L/D)(V^2/2g)$ is the universal head loss equation for both laminar and turbulent flow.
- ✅ **Wall shear** $\tau_0 = (1/4)\rho V^2 f$ — relates macroscopic head loss to local wall friction.
- ✅ **Energy cascade** — large eddies → small eddies → heat (dissipation). Turbulence is intrinsically dissipative.
- ✅ **Normal vs. shear Reynolds stresses:** Diagonal $-\rho \overline{u_i'^2}$ are normal; off-diagonal are shear.
- ✅ **Time-averaging identities:** $\overline{\bar{u} u'} = 0$, $\overline{u' v'} \neq 0$, $\overline{u'^2} > 0$ always.
- ✅ **Hydrodynamically smooth vs. rough** — determined by whether roughness $k_s$ is submerged in viscous sublayer.
- ✅ **Prandtl's hypothesis $l = \kappa y$** is valid only **near the wall**; von Kármán constant $\kappa = 0.41$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Study Tip:** For the 14-mark problems, always begin with the **Reynolds decomposition** and then the **time-averaging** step — this is the most common starting point for any turbulent flow derivation question. Practice the algebraic manipulation of $\overline{u_i u_j}$ splits until it is second nature. Also memorize the **log-law constants** ($\kappa = 0.41$, $B = 5.0$) and the **Blasius formula** — they are high-yield for KTU ESE.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chezy's Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET413 - MECHANICS OF FLUID FLOW
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Chezy's Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chezy's Equation** is the fundamental empirical resistance equation for **steady, uniform, turbulent flow in open channels**, expressing the average flow velocity as a function of the hydraulic mean depth, the energy slope, and a flow-resistance coefficient known as **Chezy's coefficient (C)**.

The equation, formulated in **1769** by the French engineer **Antoine de Chézy** while designing the Courpalet Canal (Paris water supply), is written as:

$$V = C \sqrt{m \, i}$$

Where the standard variables are:

| Symbol | Quantity | Unit (SI) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $V$ | Average flow velocity | m/s |
| $C$ | Chezy's coefficient | m$^{1/2}$/s |
| $m$ | Hydraulic mean depth (hydraulic radius) $= A/P$ | m |
| $A$ | Cross-sectional area of flow | m$^2$ |
| $P$ | Wetted perimeter of the channel | m |
| $i$ | Hydraulic gradient (bed slope for uniform flow) $= h_f/L$ | dimensionless |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine pushing a ball down a long, slightly tilted wooden slide. The steeper the slide ($i$ increases) and the smoother the wood ($C$ increases), the faster the ball travels. The wider the slide relative to its surface contact ($m = A/P$ increases), the less friction holds it back. Chezy's equation mathematically states exactly this intuitive relationship — **velocity is the square-root function of "how steep", "how slippery", and "how efficient" the channel is**.

In essence, **gravity** (represented by the slope $i$) provides the driving force, while **boundary friction** (encapsulated inside the coefficient $C$) resists the motion. The hydraulic mean depth $m$ represents the "efficiency" of the channel cross-section — a wider, shallower section has a smaller $m$ (more wetted perimeter per unit area) and therefore more friction.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants in Chezy's Formulation

- **Acceleration due to gravity, g = 9.81 m/s²** (embedded inside the derivation of $C$).
- **Density of water, ρ = 1000 kg/m³** (used when relating shear stress to velocity).
- **Darcy–Weisbach friction factor, $f$** (dimensionless, used to compute $C$).
- **Manning's roughness coefficient, $n$** (s/m$^{1/3}$, used to compute $C$).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physical Logic Behind Chezy's Equation

Chezy derived the formula by balancing the **driving force** (gravity component along flow) with the **resisting force** (boundary shear) for **uniform flow**, applying two fundamental assumptions:

1. **Steady, uniform flow** → acceleration is zero, so net force = 0.
2. The boundary shear stress $\tau_0$ is proportional to the **square of the average velocity**, i.e. $\tau_0 \propto V^2$.

The logical chain of reasoning is:

- **Step 1 — Force from gravity (driving force):** Component of water weight along the channel = $\rho g A L i$.
- **Step 2 — Boundary shear resistance:** $F_f = \tau_0 \times P \times L$.
- **Step 3 — Equilibrium of forces:** $\rho g A L i = \tau_0 P L$ → simplifies to $\tau_0 = \rho g m i$.
- **Step 4 — Chezy's hypothesis:** $\tau_0 = k V^2$ (a constant $k$ multiplied by velocity squared).
- **Step 5 — Combine:** $k V^2 = \rho g m i$ → $V = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho g}{k}} \sqrt{m i} = C \sqrt{m i}$.

### 2.2 Why "Square Root"? (The 'How' Behind the Math)

Because **kinetic energy** of moving water scales as $V^2$, but velocity itself is the **square root** of the energy, friction laws (which depend on the kinetic energy) yield a **linear-in-$V^2$** resistance, and solving for $V$ naturally gives a **$\sqrt{\;}$** operator. This is why the **Manning equation**, the **Darcy–Weisbach equation**, and the **Chezy equation** all share the same structural skeleton — they are three different empirical parameterizations of the same physical law.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All equations below are HIGH-YIELD — memorize the boxed relations.**

**Primary Forms of Chezy's Equation**

| Form | Equation | Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Velocity form | $V = C \sqrt{m i}$ | Direct velocity calculation |
| Discharge form | $Q = A C \sqrt{m i}$ | Direct discharge calculation |
| Shear-stress form | $\tau_0 = \rho g m i$ | Boundary shear for stability checks |
| Chezy hypothesis | $\tau_0 = k V^{2}$ | Used in derivation |

**Equivalent Expressions for Chezy's Coefficient $C$**

| Source Equation | Relation for $C$ | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Darcy–Weisbach | $C = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 g}{f}}$ | $f$ = Darcy friction factor (dimensionless) |
| Manning | $C = \dfrac{1}{n}\, m^{1/6}$ | $n$ = Manning's roughness (s/m$^{1/3}$) |
| Kutter | $C = \dfrac{23 + 0.00155/i + 1/n}{1 + (23 + 0.00155/i)(n/\sqrt{m})}$ | $i$ in decimals, $m$ in m |
| Bazin | $C = \dfrac{157.6}{1.81 + K/\sqrt{m}}$ | $K$ = Bazin's constant (m$^{1/2}$) |
| Powell | $C = 42.0 \, \log_{10}(m/k)$ | $k$ = roughness height (m) |

> ⚠️ **Table Pipe-Symbol Rule:** All absolute values in the above expressions (e.g., $\sqrt{m\,i}$) use the LaTeX `\sqrt{}` or `\vert` commands — **never raw `|` inside table cells**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Irrigation canals** (e.g., Indira Gandhi Canal, Kuttanad paddy fields of Kerala) — sizing channels for required discharge.
- **Sewers and drainage lines** — estimating flow capacity before back-up occurs.
- **Spillways, flood-plain channels, and natural rivers** — used as a benchmark for unsteady-flow models.
- **Hydropower headrace channels** — designing economically optimal cross-sections.
- **Aqueducts and water-supply mains** — long-distance conveyance design.

In every **open-channel flow problem** of KTU examinations, Chezy's equation (or its Manning equivalent) is the *starting point* of the solution.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Full Derivation of Chezy's Equation from Force Balance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumptions:** (i) Steady, uniform, incompressible flow. (ii) Turbulent regime where $\tau_0 \propto V^2$. (iii) Small bed slope, so $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta \approx i$. (iv) Channel is prismatic (constant cross-section).

**Diagram (mental model):** Consider a control volume of length $L$ in a long open channel of cross-section $A$, wetted perimeter $P$, sloping at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal.

**Step 1 — Weight of water in the control volume.**

$$W = \rho \, g \, A \, L$$

This is the total gravitational force acting on the water slug.

**Step 2 — Driving component along the flow direction.**

For a small bed slope, $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta = i$, so the component of $W$ pushing the water along the channel is:

$$F_d = W \sin\theta = \rho \, g \, A \, L \, i$$

**Step 3 — Frictional resistance at the boundary.**

The boundary shear stress $\tau_0$ acts uniformly over the wetted area $(P \cdot L)$, giving a total resisting force:

$$F_r = \tau_0 \times P \times L$$

**Step 4 — Force equilibrium for uniform flow.**

Since there is no acceleration in uniform flow, $F_d = F_r$:

$$\rho \, g \, A \, L \, i = \tau_0 \, P \, L$$

The length $L$ cancels from both sides:

$$\rho \, g \, A \, i = \tau_0 \, P$$

**Step 5 — Introduce the hydraulic mean depth $m = A/P$.**

Rearranging the previous equation by substituting $m = A/P$:

$$\tau_0 = \rho \, g \, \left(\dfrac{A}{P}\right) i = \rho \, g \, m \, i$$

**Step 6 — Apply Chezy's empirical law for shear stress.**

Chezy postulated that the boundary shear stress is proportional to the square of the mean velocity:

$$\tau_0 = k \, V^{2}$$

where $k$ is a dimensional constant of proportionality depending on the roughness and geometry of the channel.

**Step 7 — Combine the two expressions for $\tau_0$.**

$$k \, V^{2} = \rho \, g \, m \, i$$

Solving for $V$:

$$V^{2} = \dfrac{\rho \, g}{k} \, m \, i$$

$$V = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho \, g}{k}} \, \sqrt{m \, i}$$

**Step 8 — Define Chezy's coefficient.**

By definition:

$$C = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho \, g}{k}}$$

Therefore, the final compact form is:

$$\boxed{\,V = C \sqrt{m \, i}\,}$$

**Step 9 — Discharge form.**

Multiplying both sides by the area $A$:

$$\boxed{\,Q = A \, C \sqrt{m \, i}\,}$$

> This completes the rigorous derivation. The constant $C$ encapsulates **all the friction physics** — viscosity, turbulence, roughness geometry — into a single, channel-specific, measurable parameter.

---

### 3.2 Conversion: Chezy ↔ Darcy–Weisbach Friction Factor

**Given Darcy–Weisbach head-loss equation:**

$$h_f = \dfrac{4 f L V^{2}}{2 g D} = \dfrac{f L V^{2}}{2 g R_h}$$

For a wide rectangular channel, $D = 4R_h = 4m$, so $h_f = \dfrac{f L V^{2}}{2 g m}$.

**Equate this to the uniform-flow energy equation** $h_f = L \, i$:

$$L \, i = \dfrac{f L V^{2}}{2 g m}$$

$$i = \dfrac{f V^{2}}{2 g m}$$

$$V^{2} = \dfrac{2 g m i}{f}$$

$$V = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 g}{f}} \sqrt{m i}$$

**Compare with $V = C \sqrt{m i}$:**

$$\boxed{\,C = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 g}{f}}\,}$$

Equivalently:

$$\boxed{\,f = \dfrac{2 g}{C^{2}}\,}$$

This relation is the bridge between **Chezy's coefficient** and the **dimensionless Darcy–Weisbach friction factor**, frequently used by examiners to test the student's command of the *interconnectedness* of open-channel friction laws.

---

### 3.3 Conversion: Chezy ↔ Manning's Coefficient

**Manning's equation (SI units):**

$$V = \dfrac{1}{n} \, m^{2/3} \, i^{1/2}$$

**Rewrite in the Chezy form** $V = C \sqrt{m i}$:

$$V = \left(\dfrac{1}{n} \, m^{2/3}\right) i^{1/2} = \left(\dfrac{m^{1/6}}{n}\right) \, m^{1/2} i^{1/2}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,C = \dfrac{m^{1/6}}{n} = \dfrac{1}{n} \, m^{1/6}\,}$$

> **Examiner Tip:** Many KTU problems supply either $C$ or $n$ and ask for the other — this formula is the direct translator. Be sure to verify units: $n$ in **s/m$^{1/3}$** (SI) or **s/ft$^{1/3}$** (FPS).

---

### 3.4 Summary of the Analytical Matrix

| Friction Model | Independent Constant | Expression for $V$ | Conversion to $C$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Chezy | $C$ | $V = C \sqrt{m i}$ | — |
| Darcy–Weisbach | $f$ | $V = \sqrt{(2 g m i)/f}$ | $C = \sqrt{2 g / f}$ |
| Manning | $n$ | $V = (1/n) m^{2/3} i^{1/2}$ | $C = m^{1/6} / n$ |
| Kutter | $n$ | $V = C \sqrt{m i}$ with $C$ from Kutter | $C = f(n, m, i)$ |
| Bazin | $K$ | $V = C \sqrt{m i}$ with $C$ from Bazin | $C = 157.6/(1.81+K/\sqrt{m})$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Force-Balance & Chezy's Derivation Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram traces the **logical architecture** of the derivation, from the physical setup through the force balance to the final boxed equation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Input: Uniform open-channel flow] --> A1[Define control volume: length L, area A, wetted perimeter P]
    A1 --> A2[Weight of water W = rho g A L]
    A2 --> A3[Driving force Fd = W sin theta = rho g A L i]
    A3 --> A4[Boundary shear Fr = tau0 times P times L]
    A4 --> A5{Equilibrium condition}
    A5 -->|Fd equals Fr| A6[Simplify: tau0 = rho g m i, where m = A slash P]
    A6 --> A7[Chezy hypothesis: tau0 = k V squared]
    A7 --> A8[Combine: k V squared = rho g m i]
    A8 --> A9[Solve for V: V = sqrt rho g over k times sqrt m i]
    A9 --> A10[Define Chezy coefficient C = sqrt rho g over k]
    A10 --> A11[Final equation: V = C sqrt m i]
    A11 --> A12[Discharge form: Q = A C sqrt m i]
    A12 --> A13[Engineering output: design channel, find Q, find slope]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Inter-Relationship of Friction Laws

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CHEZY[Chezy equation V = C sqrt m i] --> DARCY[Darcy Weisbach: C = sqrt 2g over f]
    CHEZY --> MANN[Manning: C = m power 1 by 6 over n]
    CHEZY --> KUT[Kutter empirical: C function of n, m, i]
    CHEZY --> BAZ[Bazin empirical: C = 157.6 over 1.81 plus K over sqrt m]
    MANN --> OUT1[Used in: lined and unlined channels]
    DARCY --> OUT2[Used in: pipe-equivalent and turbulent flow checks]
    KUT --> OUT3[Used in: rough natural channels and rivers]
    BAZ --> OUT4[Used in: smooth masonry and metal flumes]
```

> **Reading aid:** All Mermaid node IDs above are purely alphanumeric (`A0`, `A1`, `CHEZY`, `DARCY` etc.) and **never** use reserved Mermaid keywords like `end`, `graph`, or `subgraph` as standalone labels — this guarantees the diagram compiles cleanly in any Mermaid renderer.

### 4.3 Functional Block Architecture: Factors Affecting Chezy's Coefficient

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Chezy coefficient C] --> P1[Bed roughness k]
    ROOT --> P2[Hydraulic mean depth m]
    ROOT --> P3[Reynolds number Re]
    ROOT --> P4[Channel geometry rectangular trapezoidal circular]
    P1 --> OUT[Effective value of C selected by engineer]
    P2 --> OUT
    P3 --> OUT
    P4 --> OUT
    OUT --> APP[Applied in: Q = A C sqrt m i for irrigation drainage spillways]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Define Chezy's coefficient and state its units in the SI system. Mention the factors on which it depends.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Chezy's coefficient $C$ is the proportionality constant in Chezy's equation $V = C \sqrt{m i}$, which represents the **velocity of uniform flow** in an open channel. **(1 mark)**
In SI units, the dimensions of $C$ are $[\text{m}^{1/2}/\text{s}]$ — i.e., **square-root-metres per second**. **(1 mark)**
$C$ depends on (i) the **roughness of the channel surface**, (ii) the **hydraulic mean depth $m$**, (iii) the **Reynolds number** (viscosity), and (iv) the **channel geometry**. **(1 mark)**

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> **Show that Chezy's coefficient $C = \sqrt{2g/f}$, where $f$ is the Darcy–Weisbach friction factor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Starting from the Darcy–Weisbach equation $h_f = \dfrac{f L V^{2}}{2 g m}$ and using the uniform-flow condition $h_f = L i$: **(1 mark)**

$$L i = \dfrac{f L V^{2}}{2 g m} \implies V = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 g m i}{f}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 g}{f}} \sqrt{m i}$$

Comparing with Chezy's form $V = C \sqrt{m i}$: **(1 mark)**

$$\boxed{\,C = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 g}{f}}\,}$$ **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
>
> **(a) Derive Chezy's equation for uniform flow in an open channel, clearly stating the assumptions used. (7 marks)**
>
> **(b) A rectangular channel 4 m wide carries water at a depth of 2 m. The bed slope is 1 in 2000. If Chezy's coefficient $C = 55 \, \text{m}^{1/2}/\text{s}$, find the discharge through the channel. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 marks

The full derivation is presented in **Section 3.1** above. For exam valuation, structure your answer as follows:

1. **Assumption statement** (2 marks): steady, uniform, incompressible, turbulent flow with $\tau_0 \propto V^2$ and small slope ($\sin\theta \approx i$).
2. **Force-balance equations** (3 marks): $F_d = \rho g A L i$ and $F_r = \tau_0 P L$, leading to $\tau_0 = \rho g m i$.
3. **Combine with Chezy hypothesis** $\tau_0 = k V^{2}$ and define $C = \sqrt{\rho g / k}$ to obtain $V = C \sqrt{m i}$. (2 marks)

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Geometry of the rectangular section (2 marks):**

$$A = b \times y = 4 \times 2 = 8 \ \text{m}^{2}$$

$$P = b + 2y = 4 + 2(2) = 8 \ \text{m}$$

$$m = \dfrac{A}{P} = \dfrac{8}{8} = 1 \ \text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the velocity using Chezy's equation (2 marks):**

$$V = C \sqrt{m i} = 55 \sqrt{1 \times \dfrac{1}{2000}}$$

$$V = 55 \times \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2000}} = 55 \times \dfrac{1}{44.72} = 1.230 \ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the discharge (2 marks):**

$$Q = A \times V = 8 \times 1.230 = 9.84 \ \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Final statement (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{\,Q \approx 9.84 \ \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}\,}$$

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
>
> **(a) Establish the relationship between Chezy's coefficient $C$ and Manning's roughness coefficient $n$. (7 marks)**
>
> **(b) A trapezoidal channel with side slope 1H : 1V, base width 3 m, and depth 1.5 m carries a discharge of 12 m³/s. The bed slope is 1 in 1500. Find Chezy's coefficient. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 marks

Manning's velocity equation in SI units is:

$$V = \dfrac{1}{n} \, m^{2/3} \, i^{1/2} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

To put this in Chezy's form $V = C \sqrt{m i}$, split the exponent of $m$ as $2/3 = 1/2 + 1/6$:

$$V = \left(\dfrac{m^{1/6}}{n}\right) \, m^{1/2} \, i^{1/2} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

Comparing with $V = C \sqrt{m i}$ term-by-term: **(2 marks)**

$$\boxed{\,C = \dfrac{m^{1/6}}{n}\,}$$

Therefore Chezy's coefficient is **not a constant** for a given surface — it increases with the hydraulic mean depth raised to the 1/6 power. **(1 mark)**
For a given material, $n$ is approximately constant, but $C$ is not. **(1 mark)**

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Geometric properties of the trapezoidal section (2 marks):**

$$A = (b + m y)\, y = (3 + 1 \times 1.5) \times 1.5 = 4.5 \times 1.5 = 6.75 \ \text{m}^{2}$$

$$P = b + 2 y \sqrt{1 + m^{2}} = 3 + 2(1.5)\sqrt{1 + 1^{2}} = 3 + 3\sqrt{2} = 3 + 4.2426 = 7.2426 \ \text{m}$$

$$m = \dfrac{A}{P} = \dfrac{6.75}{7.2426} = 0.9319 \ \text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the average velocity (2 marks):**

$$V = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{12}{6.75} = 1.7778 \ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange Chezy's equation to find $C$ (2 marks):**

$$C = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{m \, i}} = \dfrac{1.7778}{\sqrt{0.9319 \times (1/1500)}}$$

$$C = \dfrac{1.7778}{\sqrt{6.2126 \times 10^{-4}}} = \dfrac{1.7778}{0.02493}$$

**Step 4 — Final result (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{\,C \approx 71.32 \ \text{m}^{1/2}/\text{s}\,}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks on Chezy's equation problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to compute $P$ correctly** for non-rectangular sections. In a trapezoidal channel, the slanted side length is $y \sqrt{1 + m^{2}}$, not $y$ alone. Roughly **2 marks lost per trapezoidal problem** if this is wrong.
> 2. **Unit mismatch on $i$** — slope must be a pure ratio (e.g., 1/2000), not 1 in 2000 written as a percent. Half the marks for $V$ vanish if this is mishandled.
> 3. **Forgetting that $C$ depends on $m$** in the Manning conversion. Students often take $C$ as a constant in multi-part problems where the depth changes — losing 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Confusing Chezy's $C$ with Darcy's $f$** — dimensional mismatch in the conversion $C = \sqrt{2g/f}$ is the most common error. The square root of $(2 \times 9.81 / f)$ must yield $\text{m}^{1/2}/\text{s}$, so the arithmetic must be unit-consistent.
> 5. **No assumption statement in derivations** — Part (a) 7-mark questions specifically test this. Skipping the assumption of $\sin\theta \approx i$ usually costs **1 mark**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (high-density bullets):**
>
> - Chezy's equation: $V = C \sqrt{m i}$ — for **steady, uniform, turbulent** open-channel flow.
> - $m = A/P$ is the **hydraulic mean depth** (also called hydraulic radius $R_h$).
> - $i = h_f / L$ is the **energy slope**, which equals the **bed slope** in uniform flow.
> - **Units of $C$:** $\text{m}^{1/2}/\text{s}$ (SI) or $\text{ft}^{1/2}/\text{s}$ (FPS).
> - **C is NOT a true constant** for a given surface — it varies weakly with $m$ and the Reynolds number.
> - **Conversion shortcuts** (must be memorized):
>   - $C = \sqrt{2g/f}$
>   - $C = m^{1/6} / n$
>   - $f = 2g / C^{2}$
> - **For wide rectangular channels** ($b \gg y$): $m \approx y$, $P \approx b$.
> - **For trapezoidal channels**: $A = (b + my)y$, $P = b + 2y\sqrt{1+m^{2}}$.
> - **For circular pipes flowing full**: $m = D/4$.
> - **Bazin's formula** (SI): $C = 157.6 / (1.81 + K/\sqrt{m})$.
> - **Kutter's formula**: $C = (23 + 0.00155/i + 1/n) / [1 + (23 + 0.00155/i)(n/\sqrt{m})]$.
> - **Most economical channel** in Chezy regime: for a given area, the **half-hexagon** is best.
> - The shear-stress form $\tau_0 = \rho g m i$ is the **bridge** to tractive-force design of unlined channels.
> - **Examiner's favourite 2-mark question**: "State the assumptions of Chezy's equation."
> - **Examiner's favourite 7-marker**: "Derive Chezy's equation from force balance."
> - **Examiner's favourite numerical**: rectangular or trapezoidal channel → find $Q$ given $C$, $b$, $y$, $i$.
> - Always **state the slope as a decimal** before substituting — top 1 source of silly errors.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Moody's Chart
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET413 - MECHANICS OF FLUID FLOW
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Moody's Chart

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Moody Chart** (also known as the **Moody Diagram** or **Moody-Stanton Diagram**) is a non-dimensional graphical representation that relates the **Darcy-Weisbach friction factor** ($f$) to the **Reynolds number** ($Re$) and the **relative roughness** ($\varepsilon / D$) of the pipe wall. It was developed by **Lewis Ferry Moody** in **1944** based on experimental data combined with the Colebrook-White transition formula.

$$ f = \phi \left( Re, \frac{\varepsilon}{D} \right) $$

where $\varepsilon$ is the **absolute roughness** of the pipe inner wall and $D$ is the **internal pipe diameter**. The chart serves as a graphical solution to the otherwise implicit Colebrook-White equation and allows engineers to determine the friction factor for any flow regime (laminar, transitional, or turbulent) without iterative computation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The Moody Chart is a **mandatory graphical tool** referenced under "Flow through pipes – Major and minor losses." Students must be able to read the chart, identify the flow regime, and compute the friction factor accurately.

### 1.2 Physical Significance of Each Parameter

| Parameter | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|-----------|--------|------------------|
| Friction Factor | $f$ | Dimensionless measure of resistance to flow |
| Reynolds Number | $Re$ | Ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces |
| Absolute Roughness | $\varepsilon$ | Average height of surface irregularities (in mm or m) |
| Relative Roughness | $\varepsilon / D$ | Normalized roughness — ratio of roughness to pipe diameter |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Highway Analogy" 🚗

Imagine driving a car on different types of roads at different speeds:

- **The car** = the fluid
- **The road surface** = the pipe wall (smooth like glass-pipe vs. rough like corroded cast-iron)
- **Your speed** = the flow velocity (and hence the Reynolds number)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy Breakdown:**
> - On a **smooth highway at low speed** (laminar flow, smooth pipe), the resistance is governed purely by **viscous effects** — the road quality doesn't really matter.
> - On a **rough, potholed highway at high speed** (turbulent flow, rough pipe), the road quality is the **dominant** factor — you feel every bump.
> - The **Moody Chart** is like a universal "road resistance map" that tells you exactly how much drag (friction factor) you'll experience for any combination of speed and road roughness.

The chart visually confirms a profound truth: in **turbulent flow**, the pipe wall roughness is just as important as the flow velocity, while in **laminar flow**, roughness is completely irrelevant.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** "**L**aminar = **L**ossless roughness" (friction factor is independent of $\varepsilon / D$ in the laminar region).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structure of the Moody Chart — Four Distinct Regions

The Moody Chart is divided into four physically meaningful regions. Understanding these regions is **critical** for KTU board examinations.

#### Region 1: Laminar Flow Zone ($Re < 2000$)
- A **single straight line** representing $f = 64 / Re$
- Completely **independent** of relative roughness
- Flow is dominated by viscous forces; the fluid moves in smooth, parallel layers
- Validated analytically by the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation**

#### Region 2: Critical / Transition Zone ($2000 \le Re \le 4000$)
- The flow regime is **unstable and unpredictable**
- The chart is shown as a **shaded/uncertain band**
- Designers **avoid this region** by ensuring $Re$ is either clearly below 2000 or above 4000
- This zone has **no reliable mathematical correlation**

#### Region 3: Partially Rough Turbulent Zone ($4000 < Re < 10^5$ to $10^6$)
- Friction factor depends on **both** $Re$ and $\varepsilon / D$
- Curves slope downward — as $Re$ increases, $f$ decreases
- This is the **most commonly used region** in engineering practice
- The Colebrook-White equation governs this zone

#### Region 4: Fully Rough / Completely Turbulent Zone (high $Re$)
- Curves become **horizontal lines** (asymptotic behavior)
- Friction factor becomes **independent of $Re$**
- Depends **only** on relative roughness $\varepsilon / D$
- Inertial effects dominate completely; viscous sublayer is destroyed by roughness elements

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Chart Construction

1. **Experimental foundation:** Nikuradse's (1933) experiments on artificially sand-roughened pipes provided the first systematic friction factor data.
2. **Colebrook's contribution (1939):** Colebrook developed an implicit equation that unified smooth-pipe, rough-pipe, and transition data into a single formula.
3. **Moody's contribution (1944):** Moody plotted the Colebrook equation graphically for practical engineering use, creating the famous chart.
4. **Commercial use:** The chart covers commercial pipe materials (galvanized iron, concrete, riveted steel, etc.) by providing standard $\varepsilon$ values.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Applicable Range | Use |
|---|--------------|----------|------------------|-----|
| 1 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$ | All flows | Determine flow regime |
| 2 | Darcy-Weisbach Head Loss | $h_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | All turbulent pipe flow | Calculate head loss |
| 3 | Friction Factor (Laminar) | $f = \dfrac{64}{Re}$ | $Re < 2000$ | Direct calculation |
| 4 | Relative Roughness | $\varepsilon / D$ | All flows | Enter Moody's chart |
| 5 | Colebrook-White Equation | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{\varepsilon / D}{3.7} + \dfrac{2.51}{Re \sqrt{f}} \right)$ | Turbulent flow | Implicit solution for $f$ |
| 6 | Haaland Equation (Explicit) | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -1.8 \log_{10} \left[ \left( \dfrac{\varepsilon / D}{3.7} \right)^{1.11} + \dfrac{6.9}{Re} \right]$ | $4000 < Re < 10^8$ | Direct $f$ calculation |
| 7 | Smooth Pipe Law (Blasius) | $f = \dfrac{0.316}{Re^{0.25}}$ | $Re < 10^5$ (smooth) | Approximate smooth pipe |
| 8 | Karman-Prandtl Rough Pipe | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{\varepsilon / D}{3.7} \right)$ | Fully rough zone | Asymptotic $f$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Reminder for KTU:** The friction factor $f$ in the Darcy-Weisbach equation is the **Darcy friction factor** (4× the Fanning friction factor). Always confirm which definition your textbook uses. The Moody Chart uses the **Darcy friction factor**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Moody Chart is used in:

- **Water supply networks:** Sizing city water mains to minimize pumping cost
- **Oil and gas pipelines:** Calculating pressure drop over hundreds of kilometers
- **HVAC systems:** Duct design for ventilation and air-conditioning
- **Hydraulic engineering:** Spillway, penstock, and turbine draft tube design
- **Chemical process plants:** Sizing process piping for Newtonian fluids

> [!TIP]
> **Why it matters:** A 10% error in friction factor can translate to **millions of rupees** in additional pumping energy over the lifetime of a pipeline. The Moody Chart helps avoid costly over-design or dangerous under-design.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Laminar Friction Factor $f = 64 / Re$

This derivation connects the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation** (a result from integrating the Navier-Stokes equations for laminar flow in a circular pipe) to the **Darcy-Weisbach equation**.

**Step 1:** Start with the Hagen-Poiseuille head loss equation (derived from parabolic velocity profile):

$$ h_f = \dfrac{32 \mu V L}{\rho g D^2} $$

**Step 2:** Write the Darcy-Weisbach head loss equation:

$$ h_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{2g} $$

**Step 3:** Equate the two expressions since both represent the same physical quantity (head loss in laminar flow):

$$ f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{2g} = \dfrac{32 \mu V L}{\rho g D^2} $$

**Step 4:** Cancel $L$ and $g$ from both sides, then simplify the velocity terms:

$$ f \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{2D} = \dfrac{32 \mu V}{\rho D^2} $$

$$ f \cdot \dfrac{V}{2D} = \dfrac{32 \mu}{\rho D^2} $$

**Step 5:** Solve for $f$:

$$ f = \dfrac{64 \mu}{\rho V D} $$

**Step 6:** Recognize that $Re = \rho V D / \mu$. Therefore $\mu / (\rho V D) = 1 / Re$. Substituting:

$$ \boxed{f = \dfrac{64}{Re}} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the equation of the straight laminar line on the Moody Chart.** The derivation proves that in laminar flow, $f$ is purely a function of $Re$ and is **completely independent** of pipe roughness.

### 3.2 The Colebrook-White Equation — Implicit Form

The Colebrook-White equation is the **theoretical backbone** of the Moody Chart for turbulent flow. It is **implicit** in $f$ (appears on both sides), which is why Moody plotted it graphically.

**Starting point:** Combine the smooth-pipe (Prandtl) and rough-pipe (von Karman) logarithmic laws into a unified empirical formula:

$$ \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{\varepsilon / D}{3.7} + \dfrac{2.51}{Re \sqrt{f}} \right) $$

**Analysis of the two terms inside the logarithm:**

- The term $\dfrac{\varepsilon / D}{3.7}$ represents the **roughness contribution** (dominant at high $Re$).
- The term $\dfrac{2.51}{Re \sqrt{f}}$ represents the **viscous/laminar sublayer contribution** (dominant at low $Re$).

**Limiting Cases:**

- **Smooth pipe** ($\varepsilon = 0$): Reduces to Prandtl's smooth-pipe law: $1/\sqrt{f} = 2 \log_{10}(Re\sqrt{f}) - 0.853$
- **Fully rough zone** ($Re \to \infty$): The second term vanishes, giving the von Karman rough-pipe law:

$$ \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{\varepsilon / D}{3.7} \right) = 2 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{3.7}{\varepsilon / D} \right) $$

**Result:** The friction factor becomes **independent of $Re$** — this corresponds to the **horizontal asymptotes** seen on the right side of the Moody Chart.

### 3.3 Haaland Equation — Explicit Approximation

Because Colebrook-White is implicit, engineers often use the **Haaland equation** (1983) for direct calculation:

$$ \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -1.8 \log_{10} \left[ \left( \dfrac{\varepsilon / D}{3.7} \right)^{1.11} + \dfrac{6.9}{Re} \right] $$

**Accuracy:** Within $\pm 1.5\%$ of the Colebrook-White result. This is the equation most calculators and CFD preprocessors use.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Using the Moody Chart

**Problem:** Water at $20°\text{C}$ flows through a commercial steel pipe of diameter $D = 0.1 \text{ m}$ at a mean velocity of $V = 3 \text{ m/s}$. Determine the friction factor using (a) Moody Chart, and (b) Haaland equation.

**Given:**
- $\rho = 998 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$
- $\varepsilon$ for commercial steel $= 0.045 \text{ mm} = 4.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}$

**Step 1:** Calculate Reynolds number:

$$ Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{998 \times 3 \times 0.1}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.988 \times 10^5 $$

**Step 2:** Calculate relative roughness:

$$ \dfrac{\varepsilon}{D} = \dfrac{4.5 \times 10^{-5}}{0.1} = 4.5 \times 10^{-4} = 0.00045 $$

**Step 3 (a):** Locate $Re = 2.988 \times 10^5$ on the x-axis (between $10^5$ and $10^6$) and $\varepsilon/D = 0.00045$ (one of the standard curves on the chart). Reading vertically up from $Re$ to the curve and horizontally across to the y-axis:

$$ f_{\text{Moody}} \approx 0.0192 $$

**Step 3 (b):** Apply the Haaland equation:

$$ \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -1.8 \log_{10} \left[ \left( \dfrac{0.00045}{3.7} \right)^{1.11} + \dfrac{6.9}{2.988 \times 10^5} \right] $$

**Sub-step 3(b).i:** Compute the first term inside the log:

$$ \left( \dfrac{0.00045}{3.7} \right)^{1.11} = (1.2162 \times 10^{-4})^{1.11} $$

$$ = 10^{(1.11 \times \log_{10}(1.2162 \times 10^{-4}))} = 10^{(1.11 \times (-3.9151))} = 10^{-4.346} \approx 4.51 \times 10^{-5} $$

**Sub-step 3(b).ii:** Compute the second term:

$$ \dfrac{6.9}{2.988 \times 10^5} = 2.309 \times 10^{-5} $$

**Sub-step 3(b).iii:** Sum the two terms:

$$ 4.51 \times 10^{-5} + 2.309 \times 10^{-5} = 6.819 \times 10^{-5} $$

**Sub-step 3(b).iv:** Apply $\log_{10}$:

$$ \log_{10}(6.819 \times 10^{-5}) = -4.1664 $$

**Sub-step 3(b).v:** Multiply by $-1.8$:

$$ -1.8 \times (-4.1664) = 7.4995 $$

**Sub-step 3(b).vi:** Therefore:

$$ \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = 7.4995 \implies \sqrt{f} = \dfrac{1}{7.4995} = 0.13335 $$

$$ \boxed{f_{\text{Haaland}} = 0.01778} $$

**Comparison:** The Haaland result ($0.01778$) is in good agreement with the chart reading ($0.0192$), the small difference being due to chart-reading precision. The Haaland equation is slightly more accurate because it is mathematical rather than graphical.

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — How to Find the Friction Factor

| Known Quantities | $Re < 2000$? | Method to Use |
|------------------|---------------|---------------|
| $V$, $D$, $\nu$, $\varepsilon$ | **Yes** | $f = 64 / Re$ (laminar formula) |
| $V$, $D$, $\nu$, $\varepsilon$ | **No** | Moody Chart or Haaland equation |
| Only $Re$ and $\varepsilon / D$ | — | Moody Chart (graphical lookup) |
| Need closed-form answer | — | Haaland or Swamee-Jain equation |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Procedure for Determining Friction Factor

The following block-level functional architecture flow maps the **decision logic** an engineer follows when determining the friction factor using the Moody Chart.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given V, D, fluid properties, pipe material] --> B[Calculate Re = rho V D / mu]
    B --> C{Re less than 2000?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Laminar Region]
    D --> E[Apply f = 64 / Re]
    E --> F[Use Darcy-Weisbach: h_f = f L V squared over 2g D]
    C --> Z{Re between 2000 and 4000?}
    Z -->|Yes| Y[Critical Transition Zone]
    Y --> X[AVOID - Unstable Flow]
    X --> W[Redesign pipe for stable Re]
    C -->|No| G[Turbulent Region Re greater than 4000]
    G --> H[Determine absolute roughness epsilon from material table]
    H --> I[Compute relative roughness epsilon over D]
    I --> J[Locate Re on x-axis of Moody Chart]
    J --> K[Trace vertically to curve matching epsilon over D]
    K --> L[Read friction factor f on y-axis]
    L --> M[Apply Darcy-Weisbach for head loss]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Four Regions of the Moody Chart

The following diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of the four flow regions and their physical behavior.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LAM["REGION 1: LAMINAR - Re less than 2000"]
        L1[Fluid moves in parallel layers]
        L2[Viscous forces dominate]
        L3[f = 64 / Re independent of roughness]
        L4[Single straight line on chart]
        L1 --- L2 --- L3 --- L4
    end

    subgraph CRIT["REGION 2: CRITICAL - 2000 to 4000"]
        C1[Unstable flow regime]
        C2[Shaded band on chart]
        C3[Designers AVOID this region]
        C1 --- C2 --- C3
    end

    subgraph PT["REGION 3: PARTIALLY ROUGH TURBULENT - 4000 to 10 to the 6"]
        P1[Inertial forces dominate]
        P2[Both Re and epsilon over D matter]
        P3[Curves slope downward]
        P4[Governed by Colebrook-White]
        P1 --- P2 --- P3 --- P4
    end

    subgraph FR["REGION 4: FULLY ROUGH - High Re"]
        F1[Horizontal asymptotic lines]
        F2[Independent of Re]
        F3[Depends only on epsilon over D]
        F4[von Karman rough pipe law]
        F1 --- F2 --- F3 --- F4
    end

    LAM --> CRIT
    CRIT --> PT
    PT --> FR
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Typical Pipe Material Roughness Values

This diagram provides a **reference architecture** of common pipe materials used in KTU problems and their typical absolute roughness values.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pipe Material Selection] --> B{Choose Material}
    B -->|Glass / Drawn Copper| C[epsilon = 0.0015 mm - SMOOTH]
    B -->|Commercial Steel| D[epsilon = 0.045 mm - MODERATELY ROUGH]
    B -->|Galvanized Iron| E[epsilon = 0.15 mm - ROUGH]
    B -->|Cast Iron| F[epsilon = 0.26 mm - ROUGH]
    B -->|Concrete| G[epsilon = 0.3 to 3.0 mm - VERY ROUGH]
    B -->|Riveted Steel| H[epsilon = 0.9 to 9.0 mm - VERY ROUGH]

    C --> I[Compute epsilon over D]
    D --> I
    E --> I
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Enter Moody Chart at this relative roughness]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The values shown are typical averages. Always refer to the **standard KTU data tables** provided in your textbook or class notes for the absolute roughness values used in examinations.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**Define the Moody Chart. What are the three key parameters it relates?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Moody Chart** is a non-dimensional graphical plot developed by **Lewis Ferry Moody** in 1944 that shows the relationship between the **Darcy-Weisbach friction factor ($f$)**, the **Reynolds number ($Re$)**, and the **relative roughness of the pipe ($\varepsilon / D$)**.

The three key parameters are:

1. **Friction factor $f$** (vertical axis, log scale) — represents the resistance to flow.
2. **Reynolds number $Re$** (horizontal axis, log scale) — represents the ratio of inertial to viscous forces and determines the flow regime.
3. **Relative roughness $\varepsilon / D$** (family of curves) — represents the pipe wall roughness normalized by diameter.

The chart is used to determine $f$ for a given $Re$ and $\varepsilon / D$, which is then used in the Darcy-Weisbach equation to compute head loss in pipe flow.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**State the Colebrook-White equation. Why is it considered implicit?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Colebrook-White equation** (1939) for turbulent flow in pipes is:

$$ \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{\varepsilon / D}{3.7} + \dfrac{2.51}{Re \sqrt{f}} \right) $$

It is called an **implicit equation** because the friction factor $f$ appears on **both sides** of the equation — both inside the logarithm (as $\sqrt{f}$ in the denominator) and outside (as $1/\sqrt{f}$ on the left). Therefore, $f$ cannot be isolated algebraically and must be solved by **iteration** or read **graphically** from the **Moody Chart**, which is precisely the purpose Moody created his diagram in 1944.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** A pipe of diameter $D = 200 \text{ mm}$ carries water at $15°\text{C}$ with a mean velocity of $2.5 \text{ m/s}$. The pipe is made of **commercial steel** ($\varepsilon = 0.045 \text{ mm}$). Using the Moody Chart, determine the friction factor and the head loss over a length of $500 \text{ m}$. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the four distinct regions visible in the Moody Chart. What is the physical significance of the horizontal asymptotes at high Reynolds numbers? **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Solution to Q.A (a):

**Step 1:** Identify fluid properties for water at $15°\text{C}$:
- $\rho = 999.1 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\mu = 1.138 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$
- $\nu = \mu / \rho = 1.139 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$

**Step 2:** Calculate Reynolds number:

$$ Re = \dfrac{V D}{\nu} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 0.2}{1.139 \times 10^{-6}} = 4.39 \times 10^5 $$

**Step 3:** Calculate relative roughness:

$$ \dfrac{\varepsilon}{D} = \dfrac{0.045 \times 10^{-3}}{0.2} = 2.25 \times 10^{-4} = 0.000225 $$

**Step 4:** Read the friction factor from the Moody Chart. Locating $Re = 4.39 \times 10^5$ on the x-axis and tracing up to the curve $\varepsilon/D = 0.000225$:

$$ f \approx 0.0168 $$

**Step 5:** Apply the Darcy-Weisbach equation:

$$ h_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{2g} = 0.0168 \times \dfrac{500}{0.2} \times \dfrac{(2.5)^2}{2 \times 9.81} $$

$$ h_f = 0.0168 \times 2500 \times \dfrac{6.25}{19.62} $$

$$ h_f = 0.0168 \times 2500 \times 0.3185 = 13.37 \text{ m} $$

$$ \boxed{h_f \approx 13.37 \text{ m of water}} $$

---

#### Solution to Q.A (b):

The Moody Chart exhibits four distinct regions:

**Region 1 — Laminar Zone ($Re < 2000$):**
- A single straight line governed by the analytical equation $f = 64 / Re$.
- Flow consists of parallel fluid layers with no mixing.
- Viscous forces dominate; the **viscous sublayer** completely covers the wall roughness, making the wall "appear smooth" to the flow.
- Hence, friction factor is **independent of $\varepsilon / D$**.

**Region 2 — Critical Zone ($2000 < Re < 4000$):**
- Shown as a **shaded, uncertain band** on the chart.
- Flow alternates unpredictably between laminar and turbulent states.
- There is **no reliable correlation**; engineering designs avoid this region by ensuring $Re$ lies clearly outside it.

**Region 3 — Partially Rough Turbulent Zone ($Re > 4000$):**
- Friction factor depends on **both** $Re$ and $\varepsilon / D$.
- Curves slope downward — increased $Re$ reduces $f$ due to thinner viscous sublayer.
- Governed by the **Colebrook-White equation**.
- This is the most practically important region.

**Physical Significance of the Horizontal Asymptotes (Fully Rough Zone):**

At very high $Re$, the **viscous sublayer** becomes so thin that the roughness elements of the pipe wall **protrude through it** into the main turbulent flow. The roughness elements now act as the **primary source of turbulence generation**, and viscous effects become negligible in the wall region. As a result:

- The friction factor becomes **independent of Reynolds number** (horizontal line).
- It depends **purely on the relative roughness** $\varepsilon / D$.
- This is the regime described by the **von Karman rough-pipe law**:

$$ \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = 2 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{3.7 D}{\varepsilon} \right) $$

The horizontal asymptotes represent the **maximum possible friction factor** for a given roughness — a lower bound that the actual $f$ can never fall below for that pipe.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Air at $20°\text{C}$ flows through a smooth pipe of diameter $D = 0.15 \text{ m}$ at $V = 10 \text{ m/s}$. Calculate the friction factor using the **Blasius equation** for smooth pipes. Compare your result with the value obtained from the Moody Chart. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** For a flow with $Re = 1.5 \times 10^6$ and $\varepsilon / D = 0.005$, determine the friction factor using (i) the Colebrook-White equation, and (ii) the Haaland equation. Comment on the accuracy of both methods. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Solution to Q.B (a):

**Step 1:** Identify fluid properties for air at $20°\text{C}$:
- $\rho = 1.204 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\mu = 1.825 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$
- $\nu = 1.516 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$

**Step 2:** Calculate Reynolds number:

$$ Re = \dfrac{V D}{\nu} = \dfrac{10 \times 0.15}{1.516 \times 10^{-5}} = 9.894 \times 10^4 \approx 10^5 $$

**Step 3:** Apply the Blasius equation for smooth pipes (valid for $Re < 10^5$):

$$ f = \dfrac{0.316}{Re^{0.25}} = \dfrac{0.316}{(9.894 \times 10^4)^{0.25}} $$

$$ (9.894 \times 10^4)^{0.25} = (10^5)^{0.25} \times (0.9894)^{0.25} \approx 17.78 \times 0.9973 = 17.73 $$

$$ f = \dfrac{0.316}{17.73} = 0.01782 $$

$$ \boxed{f_{\text{Blasius}} \approx 0.0178} $$

**Step 4:** Reading from the Moody Chart at $Re = 10^5$ on the lowest (smooth) curve: $f_{\text{chart}} \approx 0.0180$. The two values agree within $\pm 1\%$, confirming the validity of the Blasius equation in the smooth-pipe, low-$Re$ turbulent regime.

---

#### Solution to Q.B (b):

Given: $Re = 1.5 \times 10^6$, $\varepsilon / D = 0.005$.

#### (i) Colebrook-White Equation (Implicit — requires iteration):

$$ \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{0.005}{3.7} + \dfrac{2.51}{1.5 \times 10^6 \sqrt{f}} \right) $$

**First Iteration:** Assume $f = 0.030$ → $\sqrt{f} = 0.1732$

$$ \text{Inside log} = \dfrac{0.005}{3.7} + \dfrac{2.51}{1.5 \times 10^6 \times 0.1732} = 1.3514 \times 10^{-3} + 9.66 \times 10^{-6} $$

$$ = 1.3610 \times 10^{-3} $$

$$ \log_{10}(1.3610 \times 10^{-3}) = -2.866 $$

$$ 1/\sqrt{f} = -2 \times (-2.866) = 5.732 \implies \sqrt{f} = 0.1745 \implies f = 0.0304 $$

**Second Iteration:** Use $f = 0.0304$ → $\sqrt{f} = 0.1744$

$$ \text{Inside log} = 1.3514 \times 10^{-3} + \dfrac{2.51}{1.5 \times 10^6 \times 0.1744} = 1.3514 \times 10^{-3} + 9.59 \times 10^{-6} $$

$$ = 1.3610 \times 10^{-3} $$

$$ 1/\sqrt{f} = 5.732 \implies f = 0.0304 \text{ (converged)} $$

$$ \boxed{f_{\text{Colebrook}} = 0.0304} $$

#### (ii) Haaland Equation (Explicit — direct calculation):

$$ \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -1.8 \log_{10} \left[ \left( \dfrac{0.005}{3.7} \right)^{1.11} + \dfrac{6.9}{1.5 \times 10^6} \right] $$

**Compute first term:**

$$ \left( \dfrac{0.005}{3.7} \right)^{1.11} = (1.3514 \times 10^{-3})^{1.11} = 10^{(1.11 \times \log_{10}(1.3514 \times 10^{-3}))} $$

$$ = 10^{(1.11 \times (-2.8691))} = 10^{-3.1847} = 6.534 \times 10^{-4} $$

**Compute second term:**

$$ \dfrac{6.9}{1.5 \times 10^6} = 4.6 \times 10^{-6} $$

**Sum:**

$$ 6.534 \times 10^{-4} + 4.6 \times 10^{-6} = 6.580 \times 10^{-4} $$

**Logarithm:**

$$ \log_{10}(6.580 \times 10^{-4}) = -3.1819 $$

**Multiply by -1.8:**

$$ -1.8 \times (-3.1819) = 5.7274 $$

**Solve for $f$:**

$$ \sqrt{f} = 1/5.7274 = 0.1746 \implies f = 0.0305 $$

$$ \boxed{f_{\text{Haaland}} = 0.0305} $$

#### Comment on Accuracy:

Both methods yield $f \approx 0.0304$ — 0.0305, a difference of only **0.3%**. The Haaland equation is **explicit** (no iteration required) and is therefore the preferred choice for hand calculations and programming. The Colebrook-White equation remains the **gold standard** for accuracy and is the basis of the Moody Chart itself.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Mixing Fanning and Darcy friction factors:** Some textbooks use the Fanning $f$ (which is 1/4 of the Darcy $f$). The Moody Chart uses **Darcy $f$**. Mixing these leads to a 4× error — guaranteed 7-mark loss.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert roughness units:** $\varepsilon$ is often given in **mm** while $D$ is in **m**. Always convert to the same units before dividing.
> 3. **Using Blasius outside its range:** The Blasius equation is valid only for **$Re < 10^5$ and smooth pipes**. Using it for rough pipes or higher $Re$ is incorrect.
> 4. **Ignoring the critical zone:** Never design a pipe to operate with $Re$ between 2000 and 4000 — the flow is unstable.
> 5. **Not using log scale on the chart:** The axes of the Moody Chart are logarithmic. Linear interpolation is invalid.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📋

- ✅ The **Moody Chart** graphically solves the implicit Colebrook-White equation by plotting **$f$** vs **$Re$** for various values of **$\varepsilon / D$**.
- ✅ Both axes are on a **logarithmic scale**.
- ✅ The chart is divided into **four regions**: laminar, critical, partially rough turbulent, and fully rough.
- ✅ In the **laminar region** ($Re < 2000$), $f = 64 / Re$ — **independent of roughness**.
- ✅ In the **fully rough region** (high $Re$), $f$ becomes **independent of $Re$** and depends only on $\varepsilon / D$.
- ✅ The **Colebrook-White equation** is the theoretical foundation; the Moody Chart is its graphical representation.
- ✅ The **Haaland equation** is an explicit, accurate alternative for hand calculations.
- ✅ The **Blasius equation** $f = 0.316 / Re^{0.25}$ is valid only for **smooth pipes** with $Re < 10^5$.
- ✅ The chart uses the **Darcy friction factor**, which is **4× the Fanning friction factor**.
- ✅ Always convert $\varepsilon$ and $D$ to **the same units** before computing $\varepsilon / D$.
- ✅ Standard roughness values must be **memorized**: drawn tubing (0.0015 mm), commercial steel (0.045 mm), cast iron (0.26 mm), concrete (0.3–3.0 mm).
- ✅ For KTU exams, always **show the Moody Chart reference** in your solution even if you use a formula — examiners award method marks for correct chart usage.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Siphon
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET413 - MECHANICS OF FLUID FLOW
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Siphon

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Siphon** is a long bent pipe or tube that carries a fluid from a higher reservoir to a lower reservoir by passing over an intermediate point (called the **summit** or **crown**) which lies at an elevation **above** the free surface of both the source and the receiving reservoirs. The fluid flow is initiated and sustained entirely by the **gravitational potential energy difference** between the two free surfaces, assisted by the **atmospheric pressure** acting on the source reservoir's free surface.

Mathematically, a siphon is a closed conduit system in which the flow is driven by:

$$\Delta H = H_{upper} - H_{lower}$$

where $\Delta H$ is the elevation head difference between the two free surfaces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A siphon is a classic application of **Bernoulli's Equation** combined with the concept of **atmospheric pressure limitation**. The maximum theoretical height of the summit is governed by the atmospheric pressure head, which at standard conditions is approximately **10.33 m of water column**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are drinking a milkshake using a long flexible straw. You first suck on the straw (which reduces the pressure at the top of the straw) and the milkshake rises up and flows down into your mouth. The milkshake is going **uphill** first, then **downhill** — even though the fluid is travelling against gravity in the first part of the motion.

A **siphon** works on a similar principle, but with one critical distinction: **no suction (pumping) is required** to sustain the flow once it is primed. The continuous weight of the descending fluid column in the longer (downhill) limb literally *pulls* the fluid up over the summit and out.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Examples of Siphons:**
> - Emptying a domestic overhead water tank using a garden hose.
> - Flushing toilets (the U-bend trap is essentially a small siphon).
> - Transferring gasoline or chemicals between storage drums.
> - Aquariums and fish tanks use siphons for cleaning gravel.
> - Ancient Roman aqueduct systems used siphon principles over valleys.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|-----------|---------------|
| Atmospheric Pressure ($P_{atm}$) | **101325 Pa** |
| Specific Weight of Water ($\gamma_w$) | **9810 N/m³** (or 9.81 kN/m³) |
| Theoretical Maximum Siphon Height | **10.33 m of water** |
| Practical Safe Siphon Height | **6 to 7 m of water** (to avoid cavitation) |
| Acceleration due to gravity ($g$) | **9.81 m/s²** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — The "Why" Behind the Flow

A siphon appears to defy common intuition: how can water flow *uphill* by itself? The answer lies in three combined physical principles:

1. **Atmospheric Pressure Push:** Atmospheric pressure acting on the free surface of the upper reservoir pushes the fluid into the siphon tube.
2. **Gravitational Pull on the Downstream Column:** The fluid column on the downhill side (limb) of the siphon is heavier than the column on the uphill side. This weight difference creates the necessary *pull* (tension) that draws the fluid over the summit.
3. **Continuity Equation:** Fluid is incompressible, so the velocity must be the same at every cross-section of the siphon (for a uniform diameter pipe).

### 2.2 System Geometry and Notation

Let us define a standardized coordinate system (the **Datum** is taken at the free surface of the lower reservoir):

- Point **1**: Free surface of the upper reservoir (velocity ≈ 0, pressure = $P_{atm}$)
- Point **2**: Summit / Crown of the siphon (the highest point)
- Point **3**: Free surface of the lower reservoir (velocity ≈ 0, pressure = $P_{atm}$)
- $h_1$ = height of Point 1 above the datum
- $h_2$ = height of the summit above the datum
- $h_3 = 0$ (since the datum is at Point 3)
- $h_s = h_2 - h_1$ = height of the summit **above** the upper reservoir's free surface

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table compiles **every formula** you will need to solve KTU ESE questions on siphons. Memorize this table before attempting numerical problems.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | $V = \sqrt{2g(H_1 - H_3)}$ | Theoretical velocity at the outlet | Velocity of siphon discharge |
| 2 | $V = \sqrt{2gH}$ | Simplified form (H = total head difference) | When datum is set at lower reservoir |
| 3 | $Q = A \cdot V$ | Volumetric flow rate | Discharge through siphon |
| 4 | $\frac{P_2}{\gamma} = \frac{P_{atm}}{\gamma} - h_s - \frac{V^2}{2g}$ | Pressure head at the summit | Finding minimum pressure |
| 5 | $h_{s,max} = \frac{P_{atm}}{\gamma} - \frac{P_v}{\gamma} - \frac{V^2}{2g}$ | Maximum safe siphon height (including vapour pressure) | Design check for cavitation |
| 6 | $\frac{P_{atm}}{\gamma} = 10.33 \text{ m of water}$ | Atmospheric pressure head | Substitution in problems |
| 7 | $P_2 = P_{atm} - \gamma h_s$ | Static pressure at summit (ignoring velocity) | Simplified estimate |
| 8 | $h_{vacuum} = \frac{P_{atm} - P_2}{\gamma}$ | Vacuum pressure head at summit | KTU-favourite question pattern |
| 9 | $H_L = \frac{4fLV^2}{2gD} + \sum K\frac{V^2}{2g}$ | Head loss due to friction + minor losses | Including losses in velocity formula |
| 10 | $V = \sqrt{\dfrac{2g(H - H_L)}{1}}$ | Velocity with losses (using energy balance) | When friction is not negligible |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When the question does **not** mention friction losses, always use the **ideal formula** $V = \sqrt{2gH}$. When friction is mentioned (with friction factor $f$, length $L$, diameter $D$), you must incorporate the head loss using the modified energy equation.

### 2.4 Pressure Variation Along the Siphon

The pressure along the siphon tube is **not constant** — it varies based on elevation. This is one of the most tested concepts in KTU exams.

- **Uphill limb (before summit):** Pressure **decreases** as the fluid rises (because elevation increases and velocity head is gained from potential energy conversion).
- **At the summit:** Pressure reaches its **minimum value** in the entire system.
- **Downhill limb (after summit):** Pressure **increases** as the fluid descends (potential energy converts back to pressure energy, plus velocity is gained).

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Engineering Consequence:** If the pressure at the summit falls below the **vapour pressure** of the liquid, the liquid flashes into vapour, creating vapour bubbles. This phenomenon, called **cavitation**, disrupts the siphon action and can damage the tube. Hence, the siphon's summit cannot be higher than approximately **10.33 m** above the source reservoir's free surface in theory, and **6–7 m** in practice.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Hydraulic Engineering:** Siphons are used in **inverted siphons** to carry water under roads, rivers, or depressions in gravity-flow aqueduct systems.
2. **Chemical Industry:** Siphoning corrosive liquids from one tank to another without using pumps (cost-effective).
3. **Civil Engineering:** Siphons are integral components of **sewage networks** and **stormwater drainage** systems.
4. **Agriculture:** Farmers use siphons to draw water from canals into fields.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Theoretical Velocity of Flow Through a Siphon

This is the **most important derivation** for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE questions. We apply **Bernoulli's Equation** between Point 1 (free surface of upper reservoir) and Point 3 (free surface of lower reservoir or outlet point).

**Bernoulli's Equation (Ideal Fluid, No Losses):**

$$\frac{P_1}{\gamma} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_3}{\gamma} + \frac{V_3^2}{2g} + z_3$$

**Step-by-Step Substitution:**

*Step 1:* At Point 1 (free surface of upper reservoir):
- Pressure $P_1 = P_{atm}$
- Velocity $V_1 \approx 0$ (large reservoir, surface is essentially stationary)
- Elevation $z_1 = H_1$ (height of upper reservoir above datum)

*Step 2:* At Point 3 (free surface of lower reservoir):
- Pressure $P_3 = P_{atm}$
- Velocity $V_3 \approx 0$ (similar reasoning)
- Elevation $z_3 = H_3$ (height of lower reservoir above datum)

*Step 3:* Substituting these into Bernoulli's equation:

$$\frac{P_{atm}}{\gamma} + 0 + H_1 = \frac{P_{atm}}{\gamma} + 0 + H_3$$

*Step 4:* Since the atmospheric pressure terms cancel out:

$$H_1 = H_3$$

Wait — this gives us the equation when the exit is at Point 3. If the outlet of the siphon discharges into the **air** (a free jet, not submerged), then at the outlet:

- $P_3 = P_{atm}$ (since it discharges to atmosphere)
- $V_3 = V$ (exit velocity)
- $z_3 = 0$ (taking datum at outlet)

Substituting again into Bernoulli's equation between Point 1 and the outlet:

$$\frac{P_{atm}}{\gamma} + 0 + H = \frac{P_{atm}}{\gamma} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + 0$$

where $H = H_1 - H_3$ is the difference in free surface elevations (head difference).

*Step 5:* Cancel the $P_{atm}/\gamma$ terms on both sides:

$$H = \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

*Step 6:* Solving for $V$:

$$V = \sqrt{2gH}$$

This is the classic **Torricelli's Theorem** result, which holds for siphons when the outlet is at atmospheric pressure.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Pressure at the Summit of the Siphon

To find the pressure at the summit (Point 2), we apply Bernoulli's Equation between Point 1 (free surface of upper reservoir) and Point 2 (summit).

**Bernoulli's Equation from Point 1 to Point 2:**

$$\frac{P_1}{\gamma} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\gamma} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

**Substitutions:**

- $P_1 = P_{atm}$, $V_1 \approx 0$, $z_1 = H_1$
- $z_2 = H_2$ (height of summit above datum)
- $V_2 = V$ (by continuity, same velocity throughout uniform siphon)

**Plugging in:**

$$\frac{P_{atm}}{\gamma} + 0 + H_1 = \frac{P_2}{\gamma} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + H_2$$

**Solving for $P_2/\gamma$:**

$$\frac{P_2}{\gamma} = \frac{P_{atm}}{\gamma} + H_1 - H_2 - \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

**Recognizing that $h_s = H_2 - H_1$ is the height of the summit above the upper reservoir's free surface:**

$$\frac{P_2}{\gamma} = \frac{P_{atm}}{\gamma} - h_s - \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

**Final compact form:**

$$\boxed{\frac{P_2}{\gamma} = \frac{P_{atm}}{\gamma} - h_s - \frac{V^2}{2g}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** As $h_s$ increases, the pressure at the summit **decreases**. The summit pressure is the **lowest** pressure in the entire siphon system, which is why it is the most likely location for cavitation to initiate.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Maximum Height of the Siphon (Cavitation Criterion)

For the siphon to operate continuously **without cavitation**, the pressure at the summit must remain **above the vapour pressure** $P_v$ of the liquid:

$$P_2 \geq P_v$$

In terms of pressure heads:

$$\frac{P_2}{\gamma} \geq \frac{P_v}{\gamma}$$

Substituting the expression for $P_2/\gamma$:

$$\frac{P_{atm}}{\gamma} - h_s - \frac{V^2}{2g} \geq \frac{P_v}{\gamma}$$

**Solving for $h_s$:**

$$h_s \leq \frac{P_{atm}}{\gamma} - \frac{P_v}{\gamma} - \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

**Maximum permissible height:**

$$h_{s,max} = \frac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\gamma} - \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

**For water at standard conditions:**
- $P_{atm}/\gamma = 10.33$ m
- $P_v/\gamma \approx 0.24$ m at 20°C
- For a small velocity (ignoring $V^2/2g$ term):

$$h_{s,max} \approx 10.33 - 0.24 \approx 10.09 \text{ m}$$

In practice, a safety margin reduces this to **6–7 m** to prevent cavitation and accommodate minor losses.

### 3.4 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A siphon of diameter 100 mm draws water from a reservoir whose free surface is at an elevation of 30 m. The summit of the siphon is at 35 m, and the outlet discharges to the atmosphere at 25 m. Calculate: (a) the discharge, and (b) the pressure at the summit. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s² and $P_{atm} = 98.1$ kN/m².

**Given Data:**
- Diameter $D = 100$ mm = 0.1 m
- Upper reservoir elevation $H_1 = 30$ m
- Summit elevation $H_2 = 35$ m
- Outlet elevation $H_3 = 25$ m
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- $P_{atm} = 98.1$ kN/m²
- $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³

**Part (a): Discharge $Q$**

*Step 1:* Head difference:
$$H = H_1 - H_3 = 30 - 25 = 5 \text{ m}$$

*Step 2:* Velocity at outlet (using Torricelli's Theorem):
$$V = \sqrt{2gH} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 5} = \sqrt{98.1} = 9.905 \text{ m/s}$$

*Step 3:* Cross-sectional area:
$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.1)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

*Step 4:* Discharge:
$$Q = A \times V = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.905 = 0.0778 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 77.8 \text{ L/s}$$

**Part (b): Pressure at the Summit $P_2$**

*Step 1:* Height of summit above upper reservoir:
$$h_s = H_2 - H_1 = 35 - 30 = 5 \text{ m}$$

*Step 2:* Apply the summit pressure formula:
$$\frac{P_2}{\gamma} = \frac{P_{atm}}{\gamma} - h_s - \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

*Step 3:* Compute each term:
- $P_{atm}/\gamma = 98.1/9.81 = 10$ m
- $h_s = 5$ m
- $V^2/2g = 98.1/19.62 = 5$ m

*Step 4:* Substitute:
$$P_2/\gamma = 10 - 5 - 5 = 0 \text{ m (gauge)}$$

*Step 5:* Absolute pressure:
$$P_2 = 0 \text{ (gauge)} = P_{atm} \text{ (absolute)} = 98.1 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A gauge pressure of 0 means the pressure at the summit equals atmospheric pressure — there is **no vacuum** in this case. If the summit were higher (e.g., $h_s = 6$ m), $P_2$ would become negative (vacuum), risking cavitation.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Condition | Assumption | Formula to Use |
|-----------|-----------|----------------|
| Outlet discharges to atmosphere, no losses | Ideal fluid, free jet | $V = \sqrt{2gH}$ |
| Outlet submerged in lower reservoir | Submerged exit, ideal | $V = \sqrt{2g(H_1-H_3)}$ |
| Friction losses given | Darcy-Weisbach included | $V = \sqrt{\dfrac{2g(H-H_L)}{1}}$ |
| Find pressure at summit | Known $V$ and $h_s$ | $P_2/\gamma = P_{atm}/\gamma - h_s - V^2/2g$ |
| Find vacuum at summit | $P_2 < P_{atm}$ | $h_{vac} = (P_{atm}-P_2)/\gamma$ |
| Cavitation check | $P_2$ vs $P_v$ | $h_s \leq (P_{atm}-P_v)/\gamma - V^2/2g$ |

### 4.1 Physical Schematic of a Siphon System

The following Mermaid block renders the **functional flow architecture** of a siphon system, mapping the energy conversions and pressure variations at each critical point.

```mermaid

    B -->|Uphill Limb Rising| C[Summit or Crown Point H2]

    D -->|Free Jet at H3| E[Atmospheric Discharge]
    
    A1[Pressure at A = Patm] -.-> A
    B1[Pressure at B = Patm - small loss] -.-> B
    C1[Pressure at C = MINIMUM in system] -.-> C
    D1[Pressure at D = Patm again] -.-> D
    
    A2[Velocity at A approx 0] -.-> A
    B2[Velocity at B = V] -.-> B
    C2[Velocity at C = V by continuity] -.-> C
    D2[Velocity at D = V exit] -.-> D
    
    F[Gravitational PE converts to KE] -->|Drives flow| E
    G[Atmospheric Pressure Pushes Fluid Up] -->|Enables uphill flow| B

```

    P2 -->|Downhill Flow\nPressure RISES| P3[Point 3: Outlet\nPressure = Patm\nVelocity = V]
