
### 4.3 Functional Architecture: Energy Conversion in a Siphon

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E1[Gravitational Potential Energy\nat Upper Reservoir] -->|Drives flow| E2[Conversion of PE to KE\n+ Pressure Energy]
    E2 --> E3[Atmospheric Pressure Push\nat Free Surface]
    E3 --> E4[Sustained Flow\nthrough Siphon Tube]
    E4 --> E5[Kinetic Energy at Outlet\nQ = A x V]
    E5 --> E6[Discharge to Lower\nReservoir or Free Jet]
    
    C1[Critical Constraint:\nSummit Pressure greater than Vapour Pressure] -.- E4
    C2[Maximum Summit Height:\nhs less than 10.33 m] -.- E1
    
    style E1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style E5 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style C1 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000
    style C2 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The red-colored boxes (Summit Point and Critical Constraints) highlight the **most failure-prone zones** in a siphon system. In KTU examinations, questions on siphons almost always target the summit analysis.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

*Define a siphon. State the maximum theoretical height of a siphon for water at standard atmospheric conditions.*

**Model Answer:**

A siphon is a long bent tube used to transfer liquid from a higher reservoir to a lower reservoir by passing through an intermediate point (summit) that is **above** the free surface of both reservoirs. The flow is driven by the difference in elevation between the two free surfaces, assisted by atmospheric pressure acting on the source reservoir.

The maximum theoretical height of a siphon summit above the upper reservoir's free surface is determined by the atmospheric pressure head:

$$h_{s,max} = \frac{P_{atm}}{\gamma_w} = \frac{101325}{9810} = 10.33 \text{ m of water}$$

This is the **theoretical limit** assuming no flow velocity and no vapour pressure effects. In practice, this is reduced to **6–7 m** to avoid cavitation.

**Question 2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

*Why does the pressure at the summit of a siphon fall below atmospheric pressure? What is the consequence if it falls below the vapour pressure of the liquid?*

**Model Answer:**

The pressure at the summit falls below atmospheric pressure because of two simultaneous effects:
1. **Elevation increase:** As the fluid rises from the upper reservoir to the summit, gravitational potential energy is gained at the expense of pressure energy.
2. **Velocity head gain:** The fluid accelerates from rest (at the free surface) to velocity $V$ inside the tube, consuming pressure energy to provide the kinetic energy.

Using Bernoulli's equation:

$$\frac{P_2}{\gamma} = \frac{P_{atm}}{\gamma} - h_s - \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

**Consequence of Pressure Falling Below Vapour Pressure:**

If $P_2 < P_v$, the liquid flashes into vapour, forming vapour bubbles. This phenomenon, called **cavitation**, causes:
- Disruption of the continuous liquid column
- The siphon action **breaks** and flow stops
- Possible damage to the tube walls (pitting and erosion)
- Noise and vibration

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Many students write $P_{atm} = 10.33$ m but forget to subtract the velocity head $V^2/2g$ when computing summit pressure. **Always include all three terms** ($P_{atm}/\gamma$, $h_s$, and $V^2/2g$) in the formula. Losing this step costs **1 full mark** in 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** A siphon of diameter 75 mm is used to draw water from a reservoir. The free surface of the reservoir is at an elevation of 50 m, the summit of the siphon is at 56 m, and the outlet is at 45 m. Calculate the discharge through the siphon and the pressure at the summit. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s² and atmospheric pressure = 98.1 kN/m². **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive an expression for the maximum height of a siphon summit above the free surface of the reservoir, considering the vapour pressure of the liquid. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Part (a):**

*Given:*
- $D = 75$ mm = 0.075 m
- $H_1 = 50$ m, $H_2 = 56$ m, $H_3 = 45$ m
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- $P_{atm} = 98.1$ kN/m²
- $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³

*Step 1: Compute head difference between upper and lower points.*

$$H = H_1 - H_3 = 50 - 45 = 5 \text{ m}$$

*Step 2: Apply Torricelli's Theorem to find exit velocity.*

$$V = \sqrt{2gH} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 5} = \sqrt{98.1} = 9.905 \text{ m/s}$$

*Step 3: Compute cross-sectional area of the siphon.*

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.075)^2 = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

*Step 4: Calculate discharge.*

$$Q = A \times V = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.905 = 4.376 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 43.76 \text{ L/s}$$

*Step 5: Compute the height of the summit above the upper reservoir's free surface.*

$$h_s = H_2 - H_1 = 56 - 50 = 6 \text{ m}$$

*Step 6: Apply the summit pressure formula.*

$$\frac{P_2}{\gamma} = \frac{P_{atm}}{\gamma} - h_s - \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

*Step 7: Compute each term individually.*
- $P_{atm}/\gamma = 98.1/9.81 = 10$ m
- $h_s = 6$ m
- $V^2/2g = 98.1/19.62 = 5$ m

*Step 8: Substitute.*

$$P_2/\gamma = 10 - 6 - 5 = -1 \text{ m (vacuum head)}$$

*Step 9: Convert to pressure.*

$$P_2 = -1 \times 9.81 = -9.81 \text{ kN/m}^2 \text{ (gauge)}$$

The **negative sign** indicates a vacuum (pressure below atmospheric).

**Absolute pressure at summit:**

$$P_{2,abs} = P_{atm} + P_{2,gauge} = 98.1 - 9.81 = 88.29 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** The vacuum head of 1 m at the summit means the pressure there is 1 m of water column below atmospheric. For water at 20°C (vapour pressure head = 0.24 m), this is still safe from cavitation. However, the value is approaching the practical limit.

**Final Answer for Part (a):** $Q = 0.0438$ m³/s and $P_2 = -9.81$ kN/m² (gauge) [i.e., 9.81 kN/m² vacuum].

---

**Solution to Part (b):**

*Step 1: Apply Bernoulli's equation between the upper reservoir's free surface (Point 1) and the summit (Point 2).*

$$\frac{P_1}{\gamma} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\gamma} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

*Step 2: Substitute boundary conditions.*
- $P_1 = P_{atm}$, $V_1 = 0$, $z_1 = 0$ (taking datum at upper free surface)
- $P_2 = P_v$ (vapour pressure, for the limiting case of incipient cavitation)
- $V_2 = V$ (siphon velocity), $z_2 = h_s$

*Step 3: Plug in the values.*

$$\frac{P_{atm}}{\gamma} + 0 + 0 = \frac{P_v}{\gamma} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + h_s$$

*Step 4: Solve for $h_s$.*

$$h_s = \frac{P_{atm}}{\gamma} - \frac{P_v}{\gamma} - \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

*Step 5: Express the maximum height.*

$$\boxed{h_{s,max} = \frac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\gamma} - \frac{V^2}{2g}}$$

*Step 6: Special case for stagnant siphon (V = 0).*

$$h_{s,max} = \frac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\gamma}$$

For water at standard atmospheric conditions:
$$h_{s,max} = 10.33 - 0.24 = 10.09 \text{ m}$$

This proves that the maximum theoretical siphon height for water is approximately **10.33 m** when vapour pressure is neglected, and **~10.09 m** when it is considered.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a siphon. Derive an expression for the velocity of flow through a siphon discharging to the atmosphere. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A siphon of length 60 m and diameter 150 mm connects two reservoirs whose free surface elevations differ by 4 m. If the summit of the siphon is 5 m above the upper reservoir's free surface, calculate the discharge, taking $f = 0.02$ into account. Assume the entry loss coefficient $K_{entry} = 0.5$. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Part (a):**

*Sketch:* (Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for a labeled diagram)

*Working Principle Explanation:*
A siphon consists of an **inlet limb** (rising from the upper reservoir), a **summit** (highest point), and an **outlet limb** (descending to the lower level). When the tube is initially filled with liquid (primed) and the outlet is open, the following sequence occurs:
1. Atmospheric pressure pushes the liquid into the inlet of the siphon tube.
2. The heavier column of liquid in the outlet limb exerts a "pulling" force due to gravity.
3. The combined action of atmospheric push and gravity pull sustains continuous flow over the summit.

*Derivation of Velocity:*

Apply Bernoulli's equation between Point 1 (free surface of upper reservoir) and Point 3 (outlet discharging to atmosphere):

$$\frac{P_1}{\gamma} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_3}{\gamma} + \frac{V_3^2}{2g} + z_3$$

Boundary conditions:
- $P_1 = P_{atm}$, $V_1 = 0$, $z_1 = H$ (height of upper reservoir above outlet)
- $P_3 = P_{atm}$ (discharge to atmosphere), $V_3 = V$, $z_3 = 0$ (datum at outlet)

Substituting:

$$\frac{P_{atm}}{\gamma} + 0 + H = \frac{P_{atm}}{\gamma} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + 0$$

Simplifying:

$$H = \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

$$\boxed{V = \sqrt{2gH}}$$

This is the **Torricelli velocity** for siphon discharge.

---

**Solution to Part (b):**

*Given:*
- Length $L = 60$ m, diameter $D = 0.15$ m
- Head difference $H = 4$ m
- Friction factor $f = 0.02$
- $K_{entry} = 0.5$
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

*Step 1: Write the energy equation including losses between the upper free surface (Point 1) and the outlet (Point 3).*

$$\frac{P_1}{\gamma} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_3}{\gamma} + \frac{V_3^2}{2g} + z_3 + H_L$$

*Step 2: Substitute boundary conditions.*
- $P_1 = P_{atm}$, $V_1 = 0$, $z_1 = H = 4$ m
- $P_3 = P_{atm}$, $V_3 = V$, $z_3 = 0$

$$\frac{P_{atm}}{\gamma} + 0 + 4 = \frac{P_{atm}}{\gamma} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + 0 + H_L$$

$$4 = \frac{V^2}{2g} + H_L$$

*Step 3: Express head losses.*
- Friction loss: $h_f = \frac{4fLV^2}{2gD} = \frac{4 \times 0.02 \times 60 \times V^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.15} = \frac{4.8V^2}{2.943} = 1.631 V^2$
- Entry loss: $h_e = K_{entry} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g} = 0.5 \times \frac{V^2}{19.62} = 0.0255 V^2$
- Total loss: $H_L = (1.631 + 0.0255)V^2 = 1.656 V^2$

*Step 4: Substitute and solve.*

$$4 = \frac{V^2}{2g} + 1.656 V^2$$

Converting $V^2/2g$ to a coefficient: $V^2/(2 \times 9.81) = 0.05097 V^2$

$$4 = 0.05097 V^2 + 1.656 V^2 = 1.707 V^2$$

$$V^2 = \frac{4}{1.707} = 2.343$$

$$V = 1.531 \text{ m/s}$$

*Step 5: Compute area and discharge.*

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.15)^2 = 0.01767 \text{ m}^2$$

$$Q = A \times V = 0.01767 \times 1.531 = 0.02705 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 27.05 \text{ L/s}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** In Part (b), students frequently forget to include the **entry loss** $K_{entry} \cdot V^2/2g$ and only account for friction. The full head loss equation is $H_L = h_f + h_{entry} + h_{bend} + h_{exit}$. When the problem explicitly gives $K_{entry}$, you **must** include it. Missing the entry loss will cost you **2 marks** out of 7. Also, never write $V^2/2g$ as $V^2 \times 2g$ — this is a common sign-error trap.

**Final Answer for Part (b):** $Q = 0.027$ m³/s ≈ 27 L/s.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Siphons (Module 2)**

- **Definition:** A siphon is a bent tube transferring liquid from a higher to a lower reservoir, with the **summit above** both free surfaces.
- **Driving Force:** Difference in free surface elevations ($H$) — same as Torricelli's theorem.
- **Velocity Equation (Ideal):** $V = \sqrt{2gH}$, where $H$ is the elevation difference between source and discharge point.
- **Discharge:** $Q = A \cdot V = (\pi D^2/4) \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$
- **Summit Pressure (Key Formula):** $\dfrac{P_2}{\gamma} = \dfrac{P_{atm}}{\gamma} - h_s - \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$
- **Atmospheric Pressure Head (Water):** $P_{atm}/\gamma = 10.33$ m
- **Maximum Siphon Height:** $h_{s,max} = \dfrac{P_{atm} - P_v}{\gamma} - \dfrac{V^2}{2g} \approx 10.33$ m (ideal), $6$–$7$ m (practical).
- **Vacuum Head at Summit:** $h_{vac} = \dfrac{P_{atm} - P_2}{\gamma}$
- **Cavitation:** Occurs when $P_2 < P_v$ (vapour pressure). It breaks siphon action.
- **Continuity:** Velocity is the same everywhere in a uniform siphon ($V_1 = V_2 = V_3$).
- **Pressure Trend:** Decreases from inlet to summit, then increases from summit to outlet. The **summit has the minimum pressure**.
- **With Friction Losses:** $V = \sqrt{\dfrac{2g(H - H_L)}{1}}$, where $H_L = h_f + h_{minor}$.
- **Real-World Use:** Inverted siphons in aqueducts, sewage networks, tank-emptying operations.
- **Sign Convention:** Always use gauge pressure unless absolute is specifically asked.
- **Frequent Mistake:** Forgetting the $V^2/2g$ term in the summit pressure equation.
- **KTU Favourite Question Patterns:** (i) Find discharge given dimensions, (ii) Find pressure at summit, (iii) Find maximum siphon height with vapour pressure, (iv) Find velocity with friction losses.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transmission of Power Through Pipes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET413 - MECHANICS OF FLUID FLOW
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Transmission of Power Through Pipes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Transmission of power through pipes** is the controlled conveyance of hydraulic (fluid) energy from a source (such as a reservoir or pump) to a load (such as a turbine, hydraulic press, or industrial process) through a closed conduit, where part of the input energy is inevitably dissipated as frictional head loss along the pipe wall.

Mathematically, it is the engineering process of converting **pressure energy (head)** and **kinetic energy (velocity/flow rate)** of a moving fluid into useful mechanical work at the downstream end of the pipeline.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECET413 – Module 2):**
> *"Power transmitted through a pipe is the product of the weight discharge ($\rho g Q$) and the net head available at the outlet (after deducting frictional losses). The transmission efficiency is the ratio of the head available at the outlet to the head supplied at the inlet."*

The hydraulic power transmitted (in Watts) is given by the fundamental energy-flux equation:

$$P_{transmitted} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H_{net}$$

where $\rho$ = mass density of fluid (kg/m³), $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (m/s²), $Q$ = volumetric discharge (m³/s), and $H_{net}$ = net head available at the outlet (m).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (The "Electrical Wire" Model)

Imagine a long electrical cable transmitting power from a generating station to a distant city. The cable has **resistance** that dissipates energy as heat, and the voltage at the receiving end is lower than at the sending end.

A pipe carrying pressurized water behaves in **exactly the same way**:

| Electrical Analogy | Hydraulic (Pipe) Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Voltage (V) | Pressure Head (H) |
| Current (I, Amperes) | Discharge (Q, m³/s) |
| Cable Resistance (R) | Pipe Friction Factor (f, L/D) |
| Power (V × I) | Power ($\rho g Q H$) |
| Voltage Drop ($IR$) | Frictional Head Loss ($h_f$) |
| Power Loss ($I^2R$) | Power Dissipated ($\rho g Q h_f$) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition (Student-Friendly):**
> Think of a long, thin drinking straw versus a wide garden hose. Pushing the same volume of water through the thin straw requires much more "push" (pressure) because the narrow walls create more friction. The **power** reaching the other end depends on both the push (head) and the flow rate — a delicate balance that engineers must optimize to avoid wasting energy as friction.

**Why does friction loss exist?**
Fluid layers sliding past one another and against the pipe wall generate shear stresses. The energy required to overcome this shear is permanently lost as heat. This is the **Darcy–Weisbach head loss**, denoted $h_f$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric to Remember:**
> - Darcy friction factor $f$ (dimensionless) for turbulent flow in smooth pipes: $f \approx 0.02$ to $0.03$ (typical)
> - Kinematic viscosity of water at 20°C: $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s
> - Density of water: $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ (often rounded to **$\rho = 10^3$ kg/m³** in exam problems)
> - Standard gravity: $g = 9.81$ m/s² (use **$g = 9.81$ m/s²** in numericals, never 10 m/s² unless explicitly stated)

---

## 1.3 Physical Setup of a Hydraulic Power Transmission System

A typical system consists of four key components:

1. **Source (Reservoir/Pump)** — supplies gross head $H$ (or pressure).
2. **Conduit (Pipe)** — of length $L$ and diameter $D$, with friction factor $f$.
3. **Load (Turbine/Machine)** — extracts useful work from the residual head.
4. **Tailrace/Delivery** — receives the spent fluid.

The gross head $H$ supplied at the inlet is split into two parts during flow:

$$H = h_f + H_{net}$$

where $h_f$ is the head lost to friction, and $H_{net}$ is the head available to do useful work at the outlet.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Head Loss (Why Friction Matters)

As fluid travels a length $L$ through a pipe of diameter $D$, the boundary layer near the wall creates shear stress $\tau_0$. The work done against this shear per unit weight of fluid is the **head loss**:

$$h_f = \frac{4 f L V^2}{2 g D}$$

> **Why the factor 4?** In the standard Darcy–Weisbach form, $4f$ is used (where $f$ is the Darcy friction factor). Some textbooks (especially older Indian editions) use $f'$ (Fanning factor) where $f_{Darcy} = 4 f'$. The KTU 2024 syllabus strictly adopts the **Darcy** convention.

### Expressing Head Loss in Terms of Discharge

Using continuity, $V = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$, so $V^2 = \dfrac{16 Q^2}{\pi^2 D^4}$. Substituting:

$$h_f = \frac{4 f L}{2 g D} \cdot \frac{16 Q^2}{\pi^2 D^4} = \frac{32 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5}$$

This is the **most important working formula** for power-transmission problems in KTU exams.

---

## 2.2 Power Transmitted and Transmission Efficiency

The hydraulic power available at the downstream end is:

$$P = \rho g Q \cdot H_{net} = \rho g Q (H - h_f)$$

The **efficiency of power transmission** is the ratio of power delivered to the power that *would* be delivered if there were no friction:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{delivered}}{P_{available}} = \frac{\rho g Q (H - h_f)}{\rho g Q H} = \frac{H - h_f}{H} = 1 - \frac{h_f}{H}$$

---

## 2.3 The Central Question: Is There an "Optimum"?

An engineer faces a design dilemma:
- **Small diameter** → high velocity → high friction loss → low efficiency.
- **Large diameter** → low velocity → low friction loss → high efficiency, BUT also higher pipe cost.

For a *fixed* flow rate $Q$, gross head $H$, and length $L$, is there a diameter that maximizes the *power actually delivered*? **Yes — and it is one of the most frequently asked questions in KTU examinations.**

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the core of what KTU examiners expect. Memorize these equations in SI units.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ | Continuity equation; velocity from discharge |
| 2 | $h_f = \dfrac{4 f L V^2}{2 g D}$ | Darcy–Weisbach head loss (velocity form) |
| 3 | $h_f = \dfrac{32 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5}$ | Head loss (discharge form) — **most used** |
| 4 | $P = \rho g Q H_{net}$ | Hydraulic power transmitted (Watts) |
| 5 | $P = \rho g Q (H - h_f)$ | Power with friction loss included |
| 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{H - h_f}{H} = 1 - \dfrac{h_f}{H}$ | Transmission efficiency (decimal or %) |
| 7 | $h_f = \dfrac{H}{3}$ | **Condition for maximum power transmission** |
| 8 | $\eta_{max\,P} = \dfrac{2}{3} \approx 66.67\%$ | Efficiency at max power (fixed) |
| 9 | $D_{opt} = \left(\dfrac{96 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g H}\right)^{1/5}$ | **Optimum diameter** for max power |
| 10 | $V_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{g D H}{6 f L}}$ | Optimum velocity at max power |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols and Units (for reference):**
> - $D$ = pipe diameter (m), $L$ = pipe length (m), $V$ = mean velocity (m/s)
> - $Q$ = discharge (m³/s), $H$ = gross head (m), $h_f$ = head loss (m)
> - $f$ = Darcy friction factor (dimensionless), $g$ = 9.81 m/s²
> - $\rho$ = 1000 kg/m³ (water), $P$ = power (W, or divide by 1000 for kW)

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles derived here are not merely academic. They directly govern the design of:

- **Hydroelectric penstocks** (e.g., Idukki, Mullaperiyar) — large-diameter pipes from dam to powerhouse.
- **Pumping mains** for water supply schemes (KWA projects in Kerala).
- **District cooling systems** where chilled water is piped over long distances.
- **Industrial hydraulic circuits** in steel plants and shipyards.

An undersized penstock wastes energy as heat; an oversized one wastes capital. The **optimum diameter** balances these — a classic economic–technical trade-off.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **exhaustive, line-by-line derivations** of every key result. No step is skipped. Use this as your "worked-example bible" for the 14-mark questions.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Head Loss in Terms of Discharge ($Q$) and Diameter ($D$)

**Starting point (Darcy–Weisbach):**

$$h_f = \frac{4 f L V^2}{2 g D}$$

**Step 1 — Express $V$ in terms of $Q$ and $D$ using continuity:**

For a circular pipe, cross-sectional area $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$, so:

$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{Q}{\pi D^2 / 4} = \frac{4 Q}{\pi D^2}$$

**Step 2 — Square the velocity:**

$$V^2 = \frac{16 Q^2}{\pi^2 D^4}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $V^2$ into the Darcy–Weisbach equation:**

$$h_f = \frac{4 f L}{2 g D} \cdot \frac{16 Q^2}{\pi^2 D^4}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the constants:**

$$h_f = \frac{4 \times 16 \cdot f L Q^2}{2 \cdot g \cdot \pi^2 D^5} = \frac{64 f L Q^2}{2 \pi^2 g D^5} = \frac{32 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5}$$

$$\boxed{\,h_f = \frac{32 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5}\,}$$

> **Why this matters:** This form directly relates head loss to discharge and diameter, both of which are the design variables in any pipe-flow problem. The **fifth-power dependence on $D$** is crucial: doubling the diameter reduces the head loss by a factor of $2^5 = 32$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Power Transmitted Through a Pipe

**Step 1 — Define hydraulic power:**

Power = (Weight rate of flow) × (Net head)

$$P = (\rho g Q) \times H_{net}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the energy balance $H_{net} = H - h_f$:**

$$P = \rho g Q (H - h_f)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the head-loss expression:**

$$P = \rho g Q \left( H - \frac{32 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5} \right)$$

$$\boxed{\,P = \rho g Q H - \frac{32 \rho f L Q^3}{\pi^2 D^5}\,}$$

This shows that $P$ is the **difference of two competing terms**: a "useful" term ($\rho g Q H$, increasing with $Q$) and a "loss" term (cubic in $Q$).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Efficiency of Power Transmission

**Step 1 — Power that *would* be delivered with no friction:**

$$P_{ideal} = \rho g Q H$$

**Step 2 — Actual power delivered (from Derivation 2):**

$$P_{actual} = \rho g Q (H - h_f)$$

**Step 3 — Take the ratio:**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{actual}}{P_{ideal}} = \frac{\rho g Q (H - h_f)}{\rho g Q H}$$

$$\boxed{\,\eta = \frac{H - h_f}{H} = 1 - \frac{h_f}{H}\,}$$

This efficiency is **dimensionless** and is often expressed as a percentage.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Condition for Maximum Power Transmission

This is the **derivation examiners most love to see in Part B (a)**.

**Step 1 — Start with the power equation:**

$$P = \rho g Q (H - h_f)$$

For a fixed pipe (i.e., fixed $D$ and $L$) and fixed gross head $H$, the only variable we can adjust to maximize $P$ is the discharge $Q$. (Recall: $h_f$ depends on $Q^2$.)

**Step 2 — Substitute $h_f = \dfrac{32 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5}$:**

$$P = \rho g Q H - \rho g Q \cdot \frac{32 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5} = \rho g Q H - \frac{32 \rho f L Q^3}{\pi^2 D^5}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate $P$ with respect to $Q$ and set to zero:**

$$\frac{dP}{dQ} = \rho g H - \frac{3 \times 32 \rho f L Q^2}{\pi^2 D^5} = 0$$

$$\rho g H = \frac{96 \rho f L Q^2}{\pi^2 D^5}$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $\rho$ from both sides:**

$$g H = \frac{96 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 D^5}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply both sides by the correct factor to expose $h_f$:**

We know that $h_f = \dfrac{32 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5}$. From Step 4, we have:

$$\frac{96 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 D^5} = g H \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{32 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5} = \frac{H}{3}$$

**Step 6 — Recognize the left-hand side as $h_f$:**

$$h_f = \frac{H}{3}$$

$$\boxed{\,\text{Condition for Maximum Power Transmission: } h_f = \frac{H}{3}\,}$$

**Step 7 — Calculate the efficiency at this condition:**

$$\eta_{max\,P} = \frac{H - h_f}{H} = \frac{H - H/3}{H} = \frac{2H/3}{H} = \frac{2}{3} = 66.67\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** Notice that at maximum power transmission, the efficiency is *fixed* at 66.67%, **independent** of $f$, $L$, $D$, or $Q$. This is a beautiful universal result.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Optimum Diameter for Maximum Power Transmission

**Step 1 — Start with the condition $h_f = H/3$:**

$$\frac{32 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g D_{opt}^5} = \frac{H}{3}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $D_{opt}^5$:**

$$D_{opt}^5 = \frac{32 f L Q^2 \times 3}{\pi^2 g H} = \frac{96 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g H}$$

**Step 3 — Take the fifth root:**

$$\boxed{\,D_{opt} = \left( \frac{96 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g H} \right)^{1/5}\,}$$

**Step 4 — Optional: Express in terms of the design variables only:**

$$D_{opt} = \left( \frac{96 f L}{\pi^2 g H} \right)^{1/5} \cdot Q^{2/5}$$

> **Why the $Q^{2/5}$ scaling?** This means doubling the discharge only increases the optimum diameter by a factor of $2^{2/5} \approx 1.32$, not by 2. This sub-linear scaling is a key insight for hydropower penstock design.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Optimum Velocity at Maximum Power

Starting from the same condition $h_f = H/3$:

$$\frac{4 f L V_{opt}^2}{2 g D} = \frac{H}{3}$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$12 f L V_{opt}^2 = 2 g D H \quad \Longrightarrow \quad V_{opt}^2 = \frac{2 g D H}{12 f L} = \frac{g D H}{6 f L}$$

$$\boxed{\,V_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{g D H}{6 f L}}\,}$$

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Variables to Power Output

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following "if-then" matrix summarizes the *qualitative behaviour* of power and efficiency — frequently tested as 3-mark concept questions.**

| Action Taken | Effect on $h_f$ | Effect on $P = \rho g Q (H - h_f)$ | Effect on $\eta$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Increase diameter $D$ (fixed $Q, H, L$) | Decreases (as $1/D^5$) | **Increases** monotonically | **Increases** monotonically |
| Increase discharge $Q$ (fixed $D, H, L$) | Increases (as $Q^2$) | Increases up to $Q_{opt}$, then **decreases** | Decreases monotonically |
| Increase length $L$ | Increases (linearly) | Decreases | Decreases |
| Use smoother pipe (lower $f$) | Decreases | Increases | Increases |

> **Key takeaway:** There is *no finite diameter* that gives maximum efficiency; efficiency is maximized in the limit of infinite $D$ (zero loss). But power *does* have a finite optimum, because as $D \to \infty$ with fixed $Q$, the term $\rho g Q H$ saturates while the loss term vanishes — and the *cost* of an infinite pipe is impractical. The $D_{opt}$ formula gives the engineering sweet spot.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The following Mermaid block diagrams illustrate the system architecture and the energy-flow partition. These are block-level functional schematics (not physical drawings) and are safe to render in any Mermaid-compatible viewer (GitHub, Obsidian, VS Code, etc.).

---

## 4.1 System Architecture — Hydraulic Power Transmission Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[RESERVOIR\nGross Head H]
    pipe[PIPE\nLength L, Diameter D]
    loss[FRICTION LOSS hf]
    turb[TURBINE\nNet Head available]
    pwr[POWER OUTPUT P]
    tail[TAILRACE]

    src -->|Available Head H| pipe
    pipe -->|H minus hf| turb
    turb -->|Spent Water| tail
    turb -.->|Power P| pwr
    pipe -.->|Head lost to wall shear| loss
```

**Interpretation of the schematic:**
- Solid arrows represent **actual fluid flow / energy transfer**.
- Dotted arrows represent **extracted (sankey-style) losses and outputs**.
- The reservoir acts as a constant-head source; friction draws energy out of the flow along the pipe length; the turbine converts the remaining head into mechanical/electrical power.

---

## 4.2 Energy Partition Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[GROSS HEAD H] --> B{FRICTION LOSS hf}
    B -->|hf| C[HEAD LOST\nCannot be recovered]
    A --> D{USEFUL HEAD}
    D -->|H minus hf| E[NET HEAD FOR POWER]
    E --> F[POWER TRANSMITTED P equals rho g Q times H minus hf]
    F --> G[EFFICIENCY eta equals H minus hf divided by H]
```

**Interpretation:**
- The gross head $H$ is **partitioned** into the friction loss $h_f$ (wasted) and the net head $H - h_f$ (useful).
- The efficiency $\eta$ quantifies what fraction of the supplied head is *actually* converted to power at the load.

---

## 4.3 Decision Flow for "Optimum Diameter" Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Given: Q, L, H, f] --> B{Goal: Max Power or Max Efficiency}
    B -->|Max Power| C[Use condition hf equals H over 3]
    B -->|Max Efficiency| D[No finite D optimum; eta increases with D]
    C --> E[Apply formula Dopt = 96 f L Q^2 over pi^2 g H all raised to 1/5]
    E --> F[Compute P = rho g Q times 2H over 3]
    F --> G[Efficiency is fixed at 66.67 percent]
```

This decision tree mirrors how a KTU examiner grades a 14-mark design problem: identify the goal, recall the right condition, and apply the correct formula in **SI units**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the fifth power of $D$** — Many students write $h_f \propto 1/D^2$ (using velocity form) instead of $1/D^5$ (using discharge form). When in doubt, always go back to $V = 4Q/(\pi D^2)$.
> 2. **Mixing Darcy and Fanning factors** — KTU 2024 uses Darcy's $f$. If the problem gives $f = 0.02$, it is *already* the Darcy value. Do not multiply by 4.
> 3. **Wrong optimum condition** — Maximum **power** transmission occurs at $h_f = H/3$. Maximum **efficiency** is unconstrained (→ 100% in the limit). Do not confuse these.
> 4. **Unit mismatch in $P$** — Power is in **Watts** if you use $g = 9.81$ m/s². To convert to kW, divide by 1000.
> 5. **Forgetting to subtract $h_f$ from $H$** — A classic blunder: writing $P = \rho g Q H$ instead of $P = \rho g Q (H - h_f)$.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU 2024 Scheme – Model Question]`

> Define the term *transmission efficiency* as applied to flow through pipes. Derive an expression for it in terms of gross head and head loss due to friction.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Transmission efficiency is the ratio of the head available at the outlet of a pipe (net head) to the head supplied at the inlet (gross head). It measures the fraction of input energy that is *not* lost to friction.

**Derivation:**

Let $H$ = gross head at inlet, and $h_f$ = head lost to friction along the pipe.

By the energy equation, the net head available at the outlet is:

$$H_{net} = H - h_f$$

Efficiency is defined as the ratio:

$$\eta = \frac{H_{net}}{H} = \frac{H - h_f}{H} = 1 - \frac{h_f}{H}$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 1 - \frac{h_f}{H}}$$

Since $h_f$ is always positive, $\eta < 1$ in practice. For a frictionless ideal pipe, $\eta = 1$ (100%).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Similar Pattern]`

> State the condition for **maximum power transmission** through a pipe. What is the corresponding transmission efficiency?

**Model Answer:**

**Condition for Maximum Power Transmission:**

$$\boxed{h_f = \frac{H}{3}}$$

i.e., the head loss due to friction must equal **one-third of the gross head** at the inlet.

**Corresponding Transmission Efficiency:**

At this optimum condition:

$$\eta = \frac{H - h_f}{H} = \frac{H - H/3}{H} = \frac{2}{3} = 66.67\%$$

> **Note:** This efficiency (66.67%) is **universal** — it holds for *all* pipe-flow systems, independent of $D$, $L$, $f$, or $Q$, provided the system is operating at the maximum-power point.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> KTU convention: Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each, with a clear choice between two full questions.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU 2024 Scheme – Model Question]`

**(a)** Derive the condition for **maximum power transmission** through a pipe. Hence, show that the efficiency at this condition is $\dfrac{2}{3}$. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A penstock of length $2$ km conveys water from a reservoir to a turbine. The available gross head is $80$ m and the Darcy friction factor is $0.022$. The required discharge is $0.5$ m³/s. Find the **optimum diameter** of the penstock and the **power** delivered at this optimum condition. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation [7 Marks]:**

The hydraulic power delivered at the outlet is:

$$P = \rho g Q (H - h_f)$$

Substituting $h_f = \dfrac{32 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5}$:

$$P = \rho g Q H - \frac{32 \rho f L Q^3}{\pi^2 D^5}$$

For a fixed pipe (i.e., fixed $D$, $L$, $f$, $H$), $P$ varies with $Q$. Differentiating w.r.t. $Q$:

$$\frac{dP}{dQ} = \rho g H - \frac{96 \rho f L Q^2}{\pi^2 D^5}$$

Setting $\dfrac{dP}{dQ} = 0$ for an extremum:

$$\rho g H = \frac{96 \rho f L Q^2}{\pi^2 D^5}$$

$$\therefore \frac{32 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5} = \frac{H}{3} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad h_f = \frac{H}{3}$$

**Condition for maximum power:** $h_f = \dfrac{H}{3}$

**Efficiency at maximum power:**

$$\eta = \frac{H - h_f}{H} = \frac{H - H/3}{H} = \frac{2}{3} = 66.67\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{max\,P} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 66.67\%}$$

> *Examiner note (1 mark):* Verify that the second derivative is negative to confirm a maximum. $d^2P/dQ^2 = -\dfrac{192 \rho f L Q}{\pi^2 D^5} < 0$ ✓ (for $Q > 0$).

---

**Part (b) — Numerical [7 Marks]:**

**Given:** $L = 2000$ m, $H = 80$ m, $f = 0.022$, $Q = 0.5$ m³/s, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Step 1 — Optimum diameter formula:**

$$D_{opt} = \left( \frac{96 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g H} \right)^{1/5}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$D_{opt} = \left( \frac{96 \times 0.022 \times 2000 \times (0.5)^2}{\pi^2 \times 9.81 \times 80} \right)^{1/5}$$

Compute the numerator:

$$96 \times 0.022 = 2.112; \quad 2.112 \times 2000 = 4224; \quad 4224 \times 0.25 = 1056$$

Compute the denominator:

$$\pi^2 = 9.8696; \quad 9.8696 \times 9.81 = 96.82; \quad 96.82 \times 80 = 7745.7$$

Ratio:

$$\frac{1056}{7745.7} = 0.1363$$

Fifth root:

$$D_{opt} = (0.1363)^{1/5} = 0.6748 \text{ m} \approx 0.675 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{D_{opt} \approx 0.675 \text{ m} \;(\approx 67.5 \text{ cm})}$$

**Step 3 — Power at optimum condition:**

At optimum, $h_f = H/3 = 80/3 = 26.67$ m, and net head = $2H/3 = 53.33$ m.

$$P = \rho g Q \cdot \frac{2H}{3} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.5 \times 53.33$$

$$P = 4905 \times 53.33 = 261,580 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 261.6 \text{ kW}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Similar Pattern]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the concept of **transmission of power through pipes**. Define the terms *gross head*, *net head*, *head loss*, and *transmission efficiency*. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Two reservoirs are connected by a pipe of length $500$ m. The head difference between them is $25$ m. If the discharge is $0.1$ m³/s and the Darcy friction factor is $0.024$, determine: (i) the head loss, (ii) the transmission efficiency, and (iii) whether the system is operating at the *maximum power transmission* condition. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Conceptual [7 Marks]:**

**Schematic (describe in words for written exam):**

A reservoir at elevation provides a gross head $H$. Water flows through a horizontal pipe of length $L$ and diameter $D$. Along the pipe, friction at the wall dissipates a head $h_f$. The remaining head $H_{net} = H - h_f$ reaches a hydraulic turbine at the outlet. The turbine extracts power $P = \rho g Q (H - h_f)$, and the spent water is discharged to a tailrace.

**Definitions:**

- **Gross Head ($H$):** The total head (energy per unit weight) supplied at the inlet of the pipe from the source reservoir or pump.
- **Net Head ($H_{net}$):** The head available at the outlet of the pipe, after deducting the frictional head loss. $H_{net} = H - h_f$.
- **Head Loss ($h_f$):** The energy (per unit weight of fluid) permanently dissipated as heat due to viscous friction at the pipe wall and between fluid layers. By Darcy–Weisbach:

$$h_f = \frac{4 f L V^2}{2 g D} = \frac{32 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5}$$

- **Transmission Efficiency ($\eta$):** The ratio of net head to gross head (or equivalently, the ratio of power delivered to the power that *would* be delivered with no friction):

$$\eta = \frac{H - h_f}{H} = 1 - \frac{h_f}{H}$$

**The "why" of power transmission:**

The fluid carries energy in two forms — **pressure energy** (proportional to head) and **kinetic energy** (proportional to $V^2$). The product of weight flow rate ($\rho g Q$) and net head gives the rate of energy transfer, i.e., power. Friction robs some of this energy, reducing the power available at the load.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical [7 Marks]:**

**Given:** $L = 500$ m, $H = 25$ m, $Q = 0.1$ m³/s, $f = 0.024$, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**(i) Head Loss:**

$$h_f = \frac{32 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5}$$

The diameter is not given; we can compute $D$ from $V$ or use a different route. The problem implicitly gives the diameter through the head-loss condition, but for clarity, we use the relationship:

$$D^5 = \frac{32 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g h_f}$$

Since $D$ is not given, we must interpret the problem: the head loss is *implicitly determined* by the head available. In reservoir-to-reservoir flow, the head difference equals the head loss: $h_f = H = 25$ m. This is a common interpretation.

> **Assumption:** For a reservoir-to-reservoir system with no turbine (or with the turbine extracting all remaining head), the entire head difference is dissipated as friction. So $h_f = H = 25$ m.

**If the system extracts no useful work (purely reservoir-to-reservoir), then $h_f = 25$ m.**

**Compute $D$ from the head loss formula:**

$$D^5 = \frac{32 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g h_f} = \frac{32 \times 0.024 \times 500 \times (0.1)^2}{9.8696 \times 9.81 \times 25}$$

Numerator: $32 \times 0.024 = 0.768$; $\times 500 = 384$; $\times 0.01 = 3.84$

Denominator: $9.8696 \times 9.81 = 96.82$; $\times 25 = 2420.5$

$$D^5 = \frac{3.84}{2420.5} = 0.001586$$

$$D = (0.001586)^{1/5} \approx 0.176 \text{ m} \approx 17.6 \text{ cm}$$

**(ii) Transmission Efficiency:**

Since *all* the head is dissipated (no useful extraction in reservoir-to-reservoir flow):

$$\eta = \frac{H - h_f}{H} = \frac{25 - 25}{25} = 0\%$$

> *Examiner note:* A reservoir-to-reservoir pipe transmits **no useful power**; efficiency is zero because $H_{net} = 0$.

**(iii) Maximum power condition:**

The condition for max power is $h_f = H/3 = 25/3 \approx 8.33$ m. But the actual $h_f = 25$ m, which is *not* equal to $H/3$. The system is therefore **NOT** operating at maximum power transmission. In fact, the system is over-frictioned (excessively long or small-diameter pipe), wasting all the head.

**Interpretation:** The pipe diameter $D \approx 0.176$ m is **too small** for this flow rate. To achieve max power, the pipe should be enlarged such that only $H/3$ of the head is lost to friction.

$$\boxed{\text{System is NOT at max power condition; pipe is undersized.}}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This and Pin It!**

**Core Concepts:**
- ☐ Power transmitted: $P = \rho g Q (H - h_f)$
- ☐ Darcy–Weisbach: $h_f = \dfrac{4 f L V^2}{2 g D} = \dfrac{32 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5}$
- ☐ Efficiency: $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{h_f}{H}$
- ☐ **Max power condition:** $h_f = H/3$
- ☐ **Efficiency at max power:** $\eta = 2/3 = 66.67\%$
- ☐ **Optimum diameter:** $D_{opt} = \left( \dfrac{96 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g H} \right)^{1/5}$
- ☐ **Optimum velocity:** $V_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{g D H}{6 f L}}$

**Critical Numerical Tricks:**
- ☐ Use $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ for water unless told otherwise.
- ☐ $g = 9.81$ m/s² (not 10) — KTU examiners are strict on this.
- ☐ Power is in **Watts**. Divide by 1000 for kW.
- ☐ The **fifth power** of $D$ is the most-missed exponent — write it carefully.
- ☐ For reservoir-to-reservoir flow (no turbine), $h_f = H$ and $\eta = 0$.

**Conceptual Distinctions (often confused in exams):**
- ☐ Max **power** ↔ Max **efficiency** are *different* problems.
  - Max power → finite $D_{opt}$, $\eta = 66.67\%$
  - Max efficiency → $D \to \infty$, $\eta \to 100\%$ (but $P \to$ finite value)
- ☐ "Optimum" always refers to **maximum power** in KTU syllabus, unless otherwise stated.

**Exam Strategy for 14-mark Questions:**
- ☐ Part (a): Always derive from the power equation, set $dP/dQ = 0$, and identify $h_f = H/3$.
- ☐ Part (b): State the formula, substitute, and show every arithmetic step. **Carry at least 4 significant figures** to avoid rounding errors.
- ☐ Always include units in the final answer.
- ☐ If asked for "optimum diameter," finish by computing $P_{max\,P}$ — full-mark credit.

**One-line memory aid (for the night before the exam!):**
> *"To transmit maximum hydraulic power, lose exactly **one-third** of your head to friction — you'll deliver the other **two-thirds** as useful work, every time."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Efficiency of Transmission
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET413 - MECHANICS OF FLUID FLOW
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Efficiency of Transmission

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Efficiency of Transmission (η)**: In fluid mechanics, the efficiency of transmission through a pipe is defined as the ratio of **power transmitted (or available) at any section** of the pipe to the **power supplied (or input)** at the inlet section of the pipe.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$\eta = \dfrac{\text{Power transmitted at section } x}{\text{Power supplied at inlet}} = \dfrac{P_x}{P_0}$$

Where, using the fundamental power equation $P = \rho g Q H$:

$$\eta = \dfrac{H_x}{H_0} = \dfrac{H - h_x}{H}$$

Here:
- $H$ = total head available at the inlet of the pipe (**m**)
- $H_x$ = residual head at a section located at distance $x$ from the inlet (**m**)
- $h_x$ = head lost due to friction up to that section (**m**)
- $\rho$ = mass density of the fluid (**kg/m³**)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- $Q$ = volumetric discharge (**m³/s**)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Garden Hose" Intuition

Imagine you are watering a garden using a long hose connected to a tap. The water enters the hose with a certain **pressure (head)**. As water travels down the hose, it loses some of this energy due to **friction** between the water and the pipe wall. By the time water reaches the nozzle, it has less "pushing power" than at the tap.

- **Power Supplied** = What the tap gives the hose at the inlet
- **Power Transmitted** = What is available to do useful work at the nozzle
- **Power Lost** = Energy converted to heat by friction (irrecoverable)

**Efficiency of Transmission** simply tells you: *"Of the total power you put into the pipe, how much of it actually arrives at the other end to do useful work?"*

## 1.3 Why is this Concept Important in Engineering?

> [!NOTE]
> In real engineering systems such as **hydroelectric penstocks**, **oil pipelines**, and **water supply mains**, fluid must often travel over long distances. Maximizing the transmitted power with minimum loss is a critical design goal. This concept allows engineers to:
> 1. Determine the **optimal pipe diameter** for a given head and discharge.
> 2. Find the **maximum power that can be transmitted** through a pipe.
> 3. Locate the **point of maximum power transmission** along the pipe.
> 4. Quantify **energy losses** in long-distance fluid transport systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Setup of the System

Consider a pipe of length $L$ and diameter $D$ connecting a higher reservoir to a lower reservoir, or simply fed from a source with a fixed head $H$ at the inlet. The fluid flows through the pipe with discharge $Q$.

The energy/head at any section decreases progressively due to friction. By the **Darcy–Weisbach equation**, the head loss in a pipe of length $L$ is:

$$h_f = \dfrac{8 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5}$$

where $f$ is the **coefficient of friction (dimensionless)**.

## 2.2 Key Conceptual Steps

1. **Head Distribution**: Assuming uniform friction, the head loss at a distance $x$ from the inlet is:
$$h_x = \dfrac{x}{L} \cdot h_f$$

2. **Available Head at Section x**: The remaining head that can do useful work is:
$$H_x = H - h_x = H - \dfrac{x}{L} h_f$$

3. **Power Transmitted at Section x**:
$$P_x = \rho g Q H_x = \rho g Q \left( H - \dfrac{x}{L} h_f \right)$$

4. **Power Supplied at Inlet (x = 0)**:
$$P_0 = \rho g Q H$$

5. **Efficiency of Transmission at Section x**:
$$\eta_x = \dfrac{H - \dfrac{x}{L} h_f}{H} = 1 - \dfrac{x h_f}{L H}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they are the backbone of every numerical problem in KTU exams on this topic.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Key Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-----------|
| 1 | Power transmitted | $P = \rho g Q H$ | W (or kW) | $H$ is net head at the section |
| 2 | Darcy–Weisbach head loss | $h_f = \dfrac{8 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5}$ | m | For circular full pipe |
| 3 | Discharge from head loss | $Q = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}\sqrt{\dfrac{g D h_f}{4 f L}}$ | m³/s | Inverted form |
| 4 | Head at section x | $H_x = H - \dfrac{x}{L} h_f$ | m | Linear distribution |
| 5 | Efficiency of transmission | $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{x h_f}{L H}$ | dimensionless | $0 \le \eta \le 1$ |
| 6 | Condition for max power | $H = 3 h_f \quad \Rightarrow \quad h_f = \dfrac{H}{3}$ | — | Found by differentiation |
| 7 | Maximum efficiency | $\eta_{\max} = \dfrac{2}{3} = 66.67\%$ | % | The famous textbook result |
| 8 | Efficiency at distance x (for max power case) | $\eta_x = 1 - \dfrac{x}{3L}$ | dimensionless | Linear variation along pipe |
| 9 | Max power transmitted | $P_{\max} = \dfrac{2}{3} \rho g Q H$ | W | Substituting $h_f = H/3$ |
| 10 | Power lost in friction | $P_L = \rho g Q h_f$ | W | Lost as heat |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Penstock Design in Hydro Projects**: Engineers design penstock diameters such that maximum hydraulic power reaches the turbines.
- **Long-Distance Oil/Gas Pipelines**: Used to size compressors and pumping stations.
- **Water Distribution Networks**: Helps in locating booster pumps along the network.
- **Cooling Systems in Power Plants**: Optimizes flow rates and pipe sizes for heat exchangers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Efficiency of Transmission at any Section x

**Given:**
- A pipe of length $L$ and diameter $D$
- Total head at inlet: $H$
- Head loss in the entire pipe: $h_f$
- Head loss varies uniformly along the length

**Step 1 — Head loss per unit length:**

The head loss per unit length is uniform if pipe diameter and roughness are constant:

$$\dfrac{h_f}{L} = \text{constant}$$

**Step 2 — Head loss at distance x from the inlet:**

At a section located at distance $x$ from the inlet, the head lost due to friction is:

$$h_x = \dfrac{x}{L} \cdot h_f$$

**Step 3 — Residual head at section x:**

$$H_x = H - h_x = H - \dfrac{x}{L} h_f$$

**Step 4 — Power transmitted at section x:**

$$P_x = \rho g Q H_x = \rho g Q \left( H - \dfrac{x}{L} h_f \right)$$

**Step 5 — Power supplied at inlet (x = 0):**

$$P_0 = \rho g Q H$$

**Step 6 — Efficiency of transmission:**

$$\eta_x = \dfrac{P_x}{P_0} = \dfrac{H - \dfrac{x}{L} h_f}{H} = 1 - \dfrac{x h_f}{L H}$$

**Inference:** Efficiency decreases **linearly** as we move away from the inlet.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Condition for Maximum Power Transmission

This is the **most important derivation** for KTU exams. We aim to find the head loss $h_f$ (or equivalently the relationship between $H$ and $h_f$) for which the power transmitted at the outlet of the pipe is maximum.

**Step 1 — Power available at the outlet of the pipe:**

$$P = \rho g Q (H - h_f)$$

**Step 2 — Express discharge Q in terms of head loss $h_f$ using Darcy–Weisbach:**

$$h_f = \dfrac{8 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5}$$

Rearranging for $Q$:

$$Q^2 = \dfrac{h_f \pi^2 g D^5}{8 f L}$$

$$Q = \sqrt{\dfrac{h_f \pi^2 g D^5}{8 f L}} = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \sqrt{\dfrac{g D h_f}{4 f L}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Q into the power equation:**

$$P = \rho g \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{h_f \pi^2 g D^5}{8 f L}} \cdot (H - h_f)$$

$$P = \rho g \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi^2 g D^5}{8 f L}} \cdot \sqrt{h_f} \cdot (H - h_f)$$

**Step 4 — Group constants and identify the variable:**

Let $K = \rho g \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi^2 g D^5}{8 f L}}$ (a constant for the given pipe and fluid).

Then:

$$P = K \cdot \sqrt{h_f} \cdot (H - h_f)$$

**Step 5 — Differentiate P with respect to $h_f$ and set to zero:**

$$\dfrac{dP}{d h_f} = K \cdot \left[ \dfrac{1}{2 \sqrt{h_f}} (H - h_f) + \sqrt{h_f} \cdot (-1) \right] = 0$$

$$\dfrac{(H - h_f)}{2 \sqrt{h_f}} - \sqrt{h_f} = 0$$

$$\dfrac{H - h_f}{2 \sqrt{h_f}} = \sqrt{h_f}$$

$$H - h_f = 2 h_f$$

$$\boxed{H = 3 h_f \quad \text{or} \quad h_f = \dfrac{H}{3}}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the maximum power transmitted:**

$$P_{\max} = \rho g Q \left( H - \dfrac{H}{3} \right) = \rho g Q \cdot \dfrac{2H}{3}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\max} = \dfrac{2}{3} \rho g Q H}$$

**Step 7 — Compute the maximum efficiency:**

The efficiency at the outlet:

$$\eta_{\max} = \dfrac{H - h_f}{H} = \dfrac{H - H/3}{H} = \dfrac{2H/3}{H} = \dfrac{2}{3}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{\max} = 66.67\%}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Maximum Power in Terms of H, D, L, f

Substituting $h_f = H/3$ into the discharge expression:

$$Q^2 = \dfrac{H}{3} \cdot \dfrac{\pi^2 g D^5}{8 f L} = \dfrac{\pi^2 g D^5 H}{24 f L}$$

$$Q = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}\sqrt{\dfrac{g D H}{24 f L}}$$

Therefore:

$$P_{\max} = \dfrac{2}{3} \rho g H \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}\sqrt{\dfrac{g D H}{24 f L}}$$

$$P_{\max} = \dfrac{\pi \rho g D^2 H}{6}\sqrt{\dfrac{g D H}{24 f L}}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\max} = \dfrac{\pi}{12\sqrt{6}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho \, g^{3/2} \, D^{5/2} \, H^{3/2}}{\sqrt{f L}}}$$

This compact formula is frequently asked in KTU as a 14-mark derivation.

---

## 3.4 Derivative Check (Second Derivative Test) — Confirming Maximum

$$\dfrac{d^2 P}{d h_f^2} = K \cdot \left[ -\dfrac{(H - h_f)}{4 h_f^{3/2}} - \dfrac{1}{2 \sqrt{h_f}} - \dfrac{1}{2 \sqrt{h_f}} \right]$$

At $h_f = H/3$:

$$\dfrac{d^2 P}{d h_f^2} = K \cdot \left[ -\dfrac{2H/3}{4 (H/3)^{3/2}} - \dfrac{2}{2 \sqrt{H/3}} \right] < 0$$

Since the second derivative is **negative**, the function is concave down — confirming that $P$ is at a **maximum** when $h_f = H/3$.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Critical Conditions

| Condition | Head Loss $h_f$ | Available Head $(H - h_f)$ | Efficiency $\eta$ | Power |
|-----------|----------------|---------------------------|-------------------|-------|
| Maximum Efficiency | $0$ | $H$ | $100\%$ | $0$ (no flow) |
| Maximum Discharge | $H$ | $0$ | $0\%$ | $0$ (no head) |
| **Maximum Power** | $\dfrac{H}{3}$ | $\dfrac{2H}{3}$ | $\dfrac{2}{3} = 66.67\%$ | $P_{\max}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The three conditions above form a classic KTU question pattern. Memorize the **second column** — it is the most-tested result in this module.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Power Transmission in a Pipe

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the energy/power flow through a pipe system, mapping the functional relationships between head, friction, and power transmission.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[RESERVOIR_OR_SOURCE] -->|H_total Head| B[PIPE_INLET]
    B -->|Input Power P0| C[FLOW_THROUGH_PIPE]
    C -->|Friction Loss hf| D[HEAD_LOSS_REGION]
    D -->|Heat Dissipation| E[ENERGY_LOST]
    C -->|Residual Head Hx| F[PIPE_SECTION_X]
    F -->|Power Px| G[OUTPUT_OR_TURBINE]
    H[FRICTION_FACTOR_f] -.->|governs| D
    I[PIPE_GEOMETRY_L_D] -.->|controls| C
    J[FLUID_PROPERTY_rho] -.->|affects| G

    A --- A_DESC[Total Head H at entry]
    B --- B_DESC[Power Input P0 = rho g Q H]
    D --- D_DESC[hf = 8fLQ2 / pi2 g D5]
    F --- F_DESC[Px = rho g Q H minus hf fraction]
    G --- G_DESC[Pmax when hf = H/3, eta = 2/3]
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow: Locating the Maximum Power Point

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S([Start: Given H, L, D, f]) --> P[Express P = rho g Q H minus hf]
    P --> Q[Use Darcy-Weisbach to express Q in hf]
    Q --> SUB[Substitute Q in P]
    SUB --> DIFF[Differentiate P wrt hf]
    DIFF --> SET[dP/dhf = 0]
    SET --> SOL[Obtain hf = H/3]
    SOL --> ETACalc[Compute eta_max = 2/3]
    SOL --> PMax[Compute P_max = 2 rho g Q H / 3]
    ETACalc --> END([Conclusion: Maximum Power Condition])
    PMax --> END
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Power-vs-Head-Loss Profile

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Power Curve: P vs hf"
        X0[hf = 0] --- X1[Power P = 0 since Q = 0]
        X2[hf = H/3] --- X3[Power P = P_max with eta = 2/3]
        X4[hf = H] --- X5[Power P = 0 since available head = 0]
    end
```

**Interpretation of the curve:**
- When $h_f = 0$: No friction, but **no flow** → $P = 0$
- When $h_f = H$: All head lost, **no useful head** at outlet → $P = 0$
- When $h_f = H/3$: **Maximum power** is transmitted with $\eta = 2/3$

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define efficiency of transmission. State its maximum theoretical value. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Efficiency of Transmission** is defined as the ratio of the power available at any section of the pipe to the power supplied at the inlet.

$$\eta = \dfrac{P_x}{P_0} = \dfrac{H - h_x}{H}$$

The **maximum theoretical value of efficiency of transmission** is $\boxed{66.67\%}$ (i.e., $\eta = 2/3$), which occurs when the head loss due to friction equals **one-third** of the total head at the inlet ($h_f = H/3$).

### Question 2: State the condition for maximum transmission of power through a pipe. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The condition for maximum transmission of power through a pipe of length $L$ and diameter $D$, with total head $H$ at the inlet, is:

$$H = 3 h_f \quad \text{or equivalently,} \quad h_f = \dfrac{H}{3}$$

This means that for **maximum power transmission**, the head lost due to friction should be exactly **one-third of the total head** at the inlet, and the remaining **two-thirds** of the head is available for useful work at the outlet.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**: A very common mistake students make is **forgetting to apply Darcy–Weisbach** to express $Q$ in terms of $h_f$ before differentiating. Without this step, the derivative $\frac{dP}{dh_f}$ is **incorrect** and you will lose 4–5 marks instantly. Always substitute $Q$ in terms of $h_f$ **first**, then differentiate.

---

### Question A: Maximum Power Transmission — Full Derivation `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` (14 Marks)

**(a) Derive the condition for maximum transmission of power through a pipe of length L and diameter D, having a head H at the inlet. (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

The power transmitted at the outlet of the pipe:

$$P = \rho g Q (H - h_f) \quad \cdots (1)$$

From Darcy–Weisbach equation:

$$h_f = \dfrac{8 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5}$$

$$Q^2 = \dfrac{\pi^2 g D^5 h_f}{8 f L}$$

$$Q = \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi^2 g D^5 h_f}{8 f L}} \quad \cdots (2)$$

Substituting (2) into (1):

$$P = \rho g (H - h_f) \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi^2 g D^5 h_f}{8 f L}}$$

Differentiating with respect to $h_f$ and setting $\frac{dP}{dh_f} = 0$:

$$\rho g \left[ -\sqrt{\dfrac{\pi^2 g D^5 h_f}{8 f L}} + (H - h_f) \cdot \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{h_f}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi^2 g D^5}{8 f L}} \right] = 0$$

Simplifying:

$$-h_f + \dfrac{H - h_f}{2} = 0$$

$$2 h_f = H - h_f \quad \Rightarrow \quad 3 h_f = H$$

$$\boxed{h_f = \dfrac{H}{3}}$$

**(b) Hence show that the maximum efficiency of transmission is 66.67%. Also obtain an expression for maximum power transmitted. (7 Marks)**

**Efficiency Calculation:**

$$\eta_{\max} = \dfrac{H - h_f}{H} = \dfrac{H - H/3}{H} = \dfrac{2H/3}{H} = \dfrac{2}{3}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{\max} = 66.67\%}$$

**Maximum Power Expression:**

Substituting $h_f = H/3$ in (2):

$$Q_{\max P} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi^2 g D^5 (H/3)}{8 f L}} = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}\sqrt{\dfrac{g D H}{24 f L}}$$

Therefore:

$$P_{\max} = \rho g \cdot \dfrac{2H}{3} \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}\sqrt{\dfrac{g D H}{24 f L}}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\max} = \dfrac{\pi \rho g D^2 H}{6}\sqrt{\dfrac{g D H}{24 f L}} = \dfrac{\pi}{12\sqrt{6}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho g^{3/2} D^{5/2} H^{3/2}}{\sqrt{f L}}}$$

---

### Question B: Alternative — Efficiency Along the Pipe `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` (14 Marks)

**(a) Derive an expression for the efficiency of transmission at a section x distance from the inlet of a pipe of length L, when the head loss in the pipe is hf and total head at inlet is H. (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

Assuming uniform friction, the head loss per unit length is:

$$\dfrac{h_f}{L}$$

Head loss at distance $x$ from the inlet:

$$h_x = \dfrac{x}{L} \cdot h_f$$

Head available at section $x$:

$$H_x = H - h_x = H - \dfrac{x h_f}{L}$$

Power transmitted at section $x$:

$$P_x = \rho g Q H_x = \rho g Q \left( H - \dfrac{x h_f}{L} \right)$$

Power supplied at inlet:

$$P_0 = \rho g Q H$$

Efficiency of transmission:

$$\eta_x = \dfrac{P_x}{P_0} = \dfrac{H - \dfrac{x h_f}{L}}{H} = 1 - \dfrac{x h_f}{L H}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_x = 1 - \dfrac{x h_f}{L H}}$$

**(b) A pipe of length 1000 m and diameter 0.3 m carries water with a head loss of 10 m. Find the efficiency of transmission at (i) the middle of the pipe and (ii) at the outlet. (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $L = 1000$ m
- $h_f = 10$ m

Assume $H$ is the total head at inlet. For this problem, we use the **maximum power transmission condition**, $H = 3 h_f = 30$ m.

**(i) At middle of the pipe (x = L/2 = 500 m):**

$$\eta_{L/2} = 1 - \dfrac{500 \times 10}{1000 \times 30} = 1 - \dfrac{5000}{30000} = 1 - 0.1667 = 0.8333$$

$$\eta_{L/2} = 83.33\%$$

**(ii) At the outlet (x = L = 1000 m):**

$$\eta_{L} = 1 - \dfrac{1000 \times 10}{1000 \times 30} = 1 - \dfrac{10000}{30000} = 1 - 0.3333 = 0.6667$$

$$\eta_L = 66.67\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> This confirms the famous result: at the **outlet** of a pipe operating under the **maximum power condition**, the efficiency of transmission is always **66.67%**, independent of pipe length, diameter, or friction factor.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Before walking into the KTU exam hall, tick off every point on this checklist:**

- ☐ **Definition**: Efficiency of transmission = (Power at section) / (Power at inlet) = $(H - h_x)/H$
- ☐ **Power Formula**: Always use $P = \rho g Q H$ where $H$ is the **net** head at the section
- ☐ **Darcy–Weisbach**: $h_f = \dfrac{8 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5}$ — this is the **most-cited equation** in this module
- ☐ **Maximum Power Condition**: $\boxed{H = 3 h_f}$ (one-third head lost in friction)
- ☐ **Maximum Efficiency**: $\boxed{\eta_{\max} = 2/3 = 66.67\%}$
- ☐ **Maximum Power**: $P_{\max} = \dfrac{2}{3} \rho g Q H = \dfrac{\pi}{12\sqrt{6}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho g^{3/2} D^{5/2} H^{3/2}}{\sqrt{f L}}$
- ☐ **Efficiency along pipe** (linear variation): $\eta_x = 1 - \dfrac{x h_f}{L H}$
- ☐ **Three Special Conditions to remember**:
  - $h_f = 0 \Rightarrow Q = 0, P = 0, \eta = 100\%$ (no flow, full head)
  - $h_f = H \Rightarrow \eta = 0\%$ (all head lost, no useful power)
  - $h_f = H/3 \Rightarrow P = P_{\max}, \eta = 66.67\%$ (optimum)
- ☐ **Common Mistake**: Don't forget to substitute $Q$ from Darcy–Weisbach **before** differentiating
- ☐ **Unit Vigilance**: $H$ in **m**, $Q$ in **m³/s**, $\rho$ in **kg/m³**, $g = 9.81$ **m/s²** → $P$ in **W**
- ☐ **Past Year Pattern**: KTU almost always asks the **derivation of $H = 3 h_f$** as a 14-mark question with internal choice between (a) the derivation and (b) numerical application
- ☐ **Engineering Insight**: For maximum power, design pipes so that friction loss is **one-third** of available head — this is the universal design rule for penstocks, oil pipelines, and water mains

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Water Hammer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET413 - MECHANICS OF FLUID FLOW
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Water Hammer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Water Hammer (Hydraulic Transient):** Water hammer is a hydraulic transient phenomenon characterized by the generation and propagation of high-pressure shock waves (pressure surges) within a confined fluid-conveying pipeline, caused by an abrupt alteration of the steady-state flow velocity—most commonly induced by the rapid closure (or opening) of a valve, the sudden stopping of a pump, or the failure of a system component.

The phenomenon is fundamentally governed by the **conservation of mass and momentum** combined with the **compressibility of the fluid** and the **elasticity of the pipe wall**. The associated pressure rise can rupture pipes, damage valves, pumps, and instrumentation if not properly engineered.

### 1.2 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Bulk Modulus of Water ($K$):** $2.19 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$ (at 20°C)
> - **Density of Water ($\rho$):** $1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - **Acceleration due to Gravity ($g$):** $9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
> - **Young's Modulus of Steel Pipe ($E$):** $2.0 \times 10^{11} \text{ N/m}^2$
> - **Typical Wave Celerity ($a$) in steel pipe carrying water:** $900$–$1200 \text{ m/s}$

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Train Analogy":**
> Imagine a long freight train traveling at high speed on a track. If the brakes are applied **instantly**, the couplers between the wagons experience a violent jerk because the kinetic energy of the moving cars has nowhere to go. Similarly, when water (a train of molecules) flowing at velocity $V$ in a pipe is stopped **instantly** by closing a valve, the kinetic energy of the entire column of fluid has to be absorbed almost instantaneously. This sudden conversion of **kinetic energy into pressure energy** creates a high-pressure shock wave that races back up the pipe — this is **water hammer**.

A practical real-world example is the **"thud"** you hear in household plumbing when a tap is closed rapidly — that is a miniature, low-magnitude water hammer effect.

### 1.4 Why Water Hammer Matters in Engineering

- Can **rupture pipelines** and damage pumping equipment.
- Causes **fatigue failure** in pipe walls due to repeated surge cycles.
- May lead to **column separation** (vapor cavity formation) and re-joining shocks.
- Critical consideration in **hydroelectric penstocks**, **pumping mains**, and **long-distance water transmission lines**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Physics — The "Why" and "How"

When a valve at the downstream end of a long pipe is closed rapidly:

1. **Step 1 — Kinetic Energy Conversion:** The fluid in immediate contact with the valve is brought to rest almost instantly. Its kinetic energy ($\frac{1}{2}\rho V^2$ per unit volume) has no time to dissipate, so it converts into **pressure energy** locally.
2. **Step 2 — Pressure Wave Generation:** This local pressure spike creates a **compression wave** that propagates **upstream** at the speed of sound in the fluid-pipe system (the **celerity**, $a$).
3. **Step 3 — Pipe Wall Expansion:** Because the pipe walls are elastic, they expand slightly to accommodate the compressed fluid. This expansion **reduces the effective celerity** below the pure sonic velocity in water ($1430 \text{ m/s}$).
4. **Step 4 — Wave Reflection:** When the pressure wave reaches the upstream reservoir (or pump), it reflects as a **relief (tension) wave** that travels back downstream, releasing the pressure.
5. **Step 5 — Oscillation:** The cycle repeats, with diminishing amplitude due to friction, until the system returns to steady state.

### 2.2 Classification by Closure Time

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Direct (Rigid) Water Hammer:** Occurs when valve closure time $t_c < \frac{2L}{a}$ (the **critical time**). Pressure rise is **maximum** and given by the Joukowsky equation.
> - **Indirect (Elastic) Water Hammer:** Occurs when $t_c > \frac{2L}{a}$. Pressure rise is **less than maximum** and depends on closure law (linear, parabolic, etc.).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Remarks** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wave Celerity in elastic pipe (water) | $a = \dfrac{\sqrt{K/\rho}}{\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{KD}{Ee}}}$ | m/s | $K$ = bulk modulus, $E$ = pipe material modulus, $D$ = diameter, $e$ = wall thickness |
| 2 | Wave Celerity (rigid pipe / pure fluid) | $a = \sqrt{K/\rho}$ | m/s | When $E \to \infty$ (no elasticity) |
| 3 | Celerity in terms of $K$, $E$ (Korteweg form) | $a = \dfrac{\sqrt{K/\rho}}{\sqrt{1 + \left(K/E\right)(D/e)}}$ | m/s | Same as #1, expanded notation |
| 4 | **Joukowsky Pressure Rise** | $\Delta P = \rho \, a \, \Delta V$ | Pa (N/m²) | For sudden (instantaneous) closure |
| 5 | Pressure Rise in Head of Fluid | $\Delta h = \dfrac{a \, \Delta V}{g}$ | m of fluid | Often called the **Joukowsky head** |
| 6 | Critical (Reflective) Time Period | $T_c = \dfrac{2L}{a}$ | s | Round-trip time of the pressure wave over pipe length $L$ |
| 7 | Critical Length of Pipe | $L_c = \dfrac{a \, t_c}{2}$ | m | If $L < L_c$, water hammer is direct |
| 8 | Number of Reflective Cycles | $N = \dfrac{t_c}{T_c} = \dfrac{t_c \, a}{2L}$ | dimensionless | $N < 1 \Rightarrow$ direct water hammer |
| 9 | Period of Oscillation in pipe | $T = \dfrac{4L}{a}$ | s | Full period (down + up) of a surge |
| 10 | Surge Velocity (gradual closure) | $\Delta V = V_0 \cdot \left(\dfrac{T_c}{t_c}\right)$ | m/s | For $t_c \geq T_c$ (indirect case) |
| 11 | Max Surge Pressure (indirect) | $\Delta P = \rho \cdot a \cdot V_0 \cdot \dfrac{T_c}{t_c}$ | Pa | Reduces with longer closure time |
| 12 | Bulk Modulus of Water (empirical) | $K \approx 2.19 \times 10^9 \, (1 - 0.004 \, \Delta T)$ | Pa | Approximately $2.2$ GPa at $20°C$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbols & Units Key:**
> - $K$ — Bulk modulus of fluid $[\text{N/m}^2]$
> - $E$ — Young's modulus of pipe material $[\text{N/m}^2]$
> - $\rho$ — Density of fluid $[\text{kg/m}^3]$
> - $D$ — Pipe internal diameter $[\text{m}]$
> - $e$ — Pipe wall thickness $[\text{m}]$
> - $L$ — Length of pipe from valve to reservoir $[\text{m}]$
> - $V_0$ — Initial steady-state velocity $[\text{m/s}]$
> - $a$ — Wave celerity $[\text{m/s}]$
> - $t_c$ — Valve closure time $[\text{s}]$
> - $T_c$ — Critical time period $[\text{s}]$
> - $\Delta P$ — Pressure rise $[\text{Pa}]$
> - $\Delta h$ — Pressure head rise $[\text{m}]$
> - $g$ — Gravitational acceleration $[\text{m/s}^2]$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Penstock Design in Hydropower:** Surge pressures can exceed static head by 30–50%; penstocks must be designed with adequate wall thickness and sometimes **surge tanks** or **air vessels** as protection.
- **Pumping Main Protection:** Sudden pump failure (power cut) causes **negative surge** (low pressure) which may cause column separation; **air vessels**, **check valves** with controlled closure, and **flywheels** on pumps are used.
- **Pipeline Networks (Water Supply):** Air release valves and **slow-closing valves** mitigate water hammer in municipal distribution.
- **Process Industries:** Quick-closing solenoid valves in chemical plants require **water hammer arrestors**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Wave Celerity in an Elastic Pipe (Micotty–Korteweg Equation)

**Starting Assumptions:**
- Fluid is slightly compressible (bulk modulus $K$).
- Pipe wall is elastic (Young's modulus $E$).
- Fluid motion is one-dimensional along the pipe axis $x$.

**Step 1 — Continuity Equation for Compressible Fluid in an Elastic Pipe**

For a pipe element of length $dx$ and cross-section $A$, the mass conservation gives:

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \rho \frac{\partial V}{\partial x} = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Pipe Wall Strain Consideration**

When pressure rises by $dP$, the pipe expands. The hoop stress causes radial expansion, increasing the cross-sectional area. For a thin-walled pipe, the circumferential strain is:

$$
\epsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{D \, dP}{2 e E}
$$

The corresponding area change is:

$$
\frac{dA}{A} = \frac{D \, dP}{e E}
$$

**Step 3 — Combined Compressibility**

The effective volumetric strain of the fluid in the pipe is the sum of the **fluid's own compressibility** and the **pipe's volumetric strain**:

$$
d\rho / \rho = \frac{dP}{K} + \frac{D \, dP}{e E}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into Continuity**

Substituting into the continuity equation and simplifying yields the wave equation:

$$
\frac{\partial^2 P}{\partial t^2} = a^2 \frac{\partial^2 P}{\partial x^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for Celerity $a$**

The wave propagation speed emerges as:

$$
a^2 = \frac{K / \rho}{1 + \left(\dfrac{K D}{E e}\right)}
$$

Taking the square root:

$$
\boxed{a = \frac{\sqrt{K/\rho}}{\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{KD}{Ee}\right)}}}
$$

This is the **Micotty–Korteweg formula** — the workhorse equation for water-hammer celerity in KTU examinations.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Joukowsky Equation (Sudden Closure)

**Step 1 — Apply the Unsteady Momentum Equation**

For a 1-D control volume of fluid in the pipe (length $L$, area $A$):

$$
\sum F = \frac{d}{dt}(mV)
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the Forces**

The pressure force acting on the fluid column when the valve closes suddenly produces a pressure rise $\Delta P$ at the valve. Neglecting friction (rigid column assumption):

$$
\Delta P \cdot A = \rho A L \cdot \frac{dV}{dt}
$$

**Step 3 — Relate Time to Wave Travel**

During the **time it takes the pressure wave to travel from the valve to the reservoir** (i.e., $L/a$), the **entire fluid column** of length $L$ decelerates from $V_0$ to $0$ (in the worst case). So:

$$
\frac{dV}{dt} \approx \frac{V_0}{L/a} = \frac{a \, V_0}{L}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Back**

$$
\Delta P \cdot A = \rho A L \cdot \frac{a \, V_0}{L} = \rho \, a \, A \, V_0
$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $A$ and Write the Final Form**

$$
\boxed{\Delta P = \rho \, a \, \Delta V}
$$

where $\Delta V = V_0 - 0 = V_0$ for sudden full closure.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pressure Head Form (Joukowsky Head)

Divide $\Delta P$ by $\rho g$:

$$
\Delta h = \frac{\Delta P}{\rho g} = \frac{\rho \, a \, \Delta V}{\rho g} = \frac{a \, \Delta V}{g}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta h = \frac{a \, \Delta V}{g}}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Critical Time Period

The **critical time** is the round-trip travel time of the pressure wave from the valve to the upstream boundary (reservoir or pump) and back:

$$
T_c = \frac{L}{a} + \frac{L}{a} = \frac{2L}{a}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_c = \frac{2L}{a}}
$$

If $t_c < T_c$, the valve closes **before** any relief wave can return — this is **direct (rigid) water hammer**, and full Joukowsky pressure is realized.

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Pressure Rise for Gradual (Indirect) Closure

For gradual closure where $t_c > T_c$, only a fraction $T_c/t_c$ of the original velocity change contributes to the surge at any instant:

$$
\Delta V_{\text{effective}} = V_0 \cdot \frac{T_c}{t_c}
$$

Substituting into the Joukowsky form:

$$
\boxed{\Delta P = \rho \, a \, V_0 \cdot \frac{T_c}{t_c} = \rho \, a \, V_0 \cdot \frac{2L}{a \, t_c} = \frac{2 \, \rho \, L \, V_0}{t_c}}
$$

This shows the powerful result: **doubling the closure time halves the pressure rise**.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A $1200 \text{ m}$ long steel pipe of diameter $D = 0.5 \text{ m}$ and wall thickness $e = 0.012 \text{ m}$ carries water with steady velocity $V_0 = 2.5 \text{ m/s}$. A downstream valve is closed in $t_c = 4 \text{ s}$. Compute: (i) wave celerity, (ii) critical time, (iii) pressure rise, (iv) head rise. Given: $K = 2.2 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$, $E = 2.0 \times 10^{11} \text{ N/m}^2$, $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

**Solution:**

**(i) Celerity:**

$$
\frac{KD}{Ee} = \frac{(2.2 \times 10^9)(0.5)}{(2.0 \times 10^{11})(0.012)} = \frac{1.1 \times 10^9}{2.4 \times 10^9} = 0.4583
$$

$$
a = \frac{\sqrt{2.2 \times 10^9 / 1000}}{\sqrt{1 + 0.4583}} = \frac{\sqrt{2.2 \times 10^6}}{\sqrt{1.4583}}
$$

$$
a = \frac{1483.2}{1.2076} \approx 1228.1 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(ii) Critical Time:**

$$
T_c = \frac{2L}{a} = \frac{2 \times 1200}{1228.1} \approx 1.954 \text{ s}
$$

**(iii) Type of Water Hammer:** Since $t_c = 4 \text{ s} > T_c = 1.954 \text{ s}$, this is **indirect (gradual) water hammer**.

**(iv) Pressure Rise (Indirect):**

$$
\Delta P = \frac{2 \, \rho \, L \, V_0}{t_c} = \frac{2 \times 1000 \times 1200 \times 2.5}{4}
$$

$$
\Delta P = 1{,}500{,}000 \text{ N/m}^2 = 1.5 \text{ MPa}
$$

**(v) Head Rise:**

$$
\Delta h = \frac{\Delta P}{\rho g} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^6}{1000 \times 9.81} \approx 152.9 \text{ m of water}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For comparison, if the same valve were closed **instantaneously** ($t_c \to 0$), the Joukowsky pressure would be:
> $$\Delta P_{\text{max}} = \rho \, a \, V_0 = 1000 \times 1228.1 \times 2.5 \approx 3.07 \text{ MPa} \quad (\sim 313 \text{ m head})$$
> This demonstrates why **rapid valve closure is dangerous**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Water Hammer Generation & Mitigation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reservoir or Pump] --> B[Long Pipeline with Fluid at Velocity V0]
    B --> C[Valve Closure Event]
    C --> D{Is Closure Time less than Critical Time tc less than Tc}
    D -- Yes --> E[DIRECT Water Hammer\nFull Joukowsky Pressure Rise\ndeltaP = rho a V0]
    D -- No --> F[INDIRECT Water Hammer\nReduced Surge Pressure\ndeltaP = 2 rho L V0 slash tc]
    E --> G[Consequences:\nPipe Rupture, Valve Damage,\nPump Failure]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Mitigation Strategies]
    H --> H1[Slow-Closing Valves]
    H --> H2[Surge Tanks]
    H --> H3[Air Cushion Vessels]
    H --> H4[Flywheel on Pump]
    H --> H5[Pressure Relief Valves]
    H1 --> I[Protected System]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    H5 --> I
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Pressure Wave Reflection in a Pipeline

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant V as Valve Downstream
    participant P as Pipe Column
    participant R as Reservoir Upstream
    Note over V,R: Initial state: Fluid moves at V0 from R to V
    V->>P: Sudden Closure at t = 0
    P->>P: Compression wave travels UPSTREAM at celerity a
    Note over P: Pressure at valve rises to P0 + deltaP
    P->>R: Wave reaches reservoir at t = L/a
    R->>P: Reflects as RELIEF wave traveling DOWNSTREAM
    Note over P: Pressure returns to P0
    P->>V: Relief wave reaches valve at t = 2L/a = Tc
    Note over V: Cycle repeats with diminishing amplitude
```

### 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Classification of Water Hammer

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SteadyFlow
    SteadyFlow --> Disturbance: Valve closure / Pump trip
    Disturbance --> DirectHammer: tc less than Tc
    Disturbance --> IndirectHammer: tc greater than Tc
    DirectHammer --> MaxPressure: Joukowsky eqn deltaP = rho a V0
    IndirectHammer --> ReducedPressure: deltaP = 2 rho L V0 slash tc
    MaxPressure --> Consequence: Pipe burst / Cavitation
    ReducedPressure --> Consequence
    Consequence --> Mitigation: Surge tank / Air vessel / Slow valve
    Mitigation --> SteadyFlow: System stabilizes
    SteadyFlow --> [*]
```

### 4.4 Physical Schematic Description (Textual)

A standard **water-hammer test rig** comprises:
- A **pressurized upstream reservoir** (or pump) maintaining head $H_0$.
- A **straight, horizontal pipe** of length $L$ and diameter $D$ with known elasticity $E$.
- A **downstream control valve** that can be closed either manually (slowly) or via a **quick-release mechanism** (suddenly).
- **Pressure transducers** at the valve and at mid-pipe to record transient pressure vs. time.
- A **data acquisition system** capturing the oscillatory pressure signal of period $T = 4L/a$.

The recorded pressure trace typically shows an **initial sharp rise** (Joukowsky spike) followed by a **damped sinusoidal decay** about the steady-state pressure — the envelope of which yields both the **celerity $a$** and the **friction damping coefficient**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define water hammer. List any two methods to control it.**

**Model Answer:**
Water hammer is a hydraulic transient phenomenon caused by the **abrupt change in fluid velocity** in a pipeline (typically by sudden valve closure or pump stoppage), which generates a high-pressure shock wave that propagates through the pipe.

**Two methods of control:**
1. Use of **slow-closing valves** to make closure time $t_c$ exceed the critical time $T_c$, thereby reducing the pressure rise.
2. Installation of **surge tanks** or **air vessels** near the valve to absorb the pressure surge.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] What is meant by critical time of a pipeline? Write the expression for it.**

**Model Answer:**
The **critical time** ($T_c$) of a pipeline is the **round-trip travel time** of the pressure wave from the valve (point of disturbance) to the upstream boundary (reservoir/pump) and back. It is the threshold time: if valve closure is faster than $T_c$, **direct (maximum) water hammer** occurs; if slower, **indirect (reduced) water hammer** results.

**Expression:**

$$
T_c = \frac{2L}{a}
$$

where $L$ is the pipe length and $a$ is the wave celerity.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model QP – Adapted]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **celerity of a pressure wave** in an elastic pipe conveying a compressible fluid. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A horizontal steel pipe of length $L = 1500 \text{ m}$, diameter $D = 0.6 \text{ m}$, and wall thickness $e = 0.015 \text{ m}$ conveys water at a steady velocity of $V_0 = 3.0 \text{ m/s}$. The downstream valve is closed **suddenly**. Compute: (i) the wave celerity, (ii) the critical time period, and (iii) the Joukowsky pressure rise and corresponding head rise.
**Given:** $K = 2.2 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$, $E = 2.0 \times 10^{11} \text{ N/m}^2$, $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **\[7 Marks\]**
