**Model Solution for (a):** *(See Section 3.1 Derivation 1 for full derivation.)*

Key steps to present:
1. Write continuity equation for compressible fluid.
2. Apply Hooke's law for pipe wall expansion: $dA/A = DdP/(eE)$.
3. Combine fluid and pipe elasticities.
4. Arrive at wave equation $\partial^2 P/\partial t^2 = a^2 \partial^2 P/\partial x^2$.
5. Extract:

$$
a = \frac{\sqrt{K/\rho}}{\sqrt{1 + KD/(Ee)}}
$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Celerity:**

$$
\frac{KD}{Ee} = \frac{(2.2 \times 10^9)(0.6)}{(2.0 \times 10^{11})(0.015)} = \frac{1.32 \times 10^9}{3.0 \times 10^9} = 0.44
$$

$$
a = \frac{\sqrt{2.2 \times 10^9/1000}}{\sqrt{1.44}} = \frac{1483.2}{1.20} = 1236 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(ii) Critical Time:**

$$
T_c = \frac{2L}{a} = \frac{2 \times 1500}{1236} = 2.427 \text{ s}
$$

**(iii) Joukowsky Pressure Rise** (sudden closure, $\Delta V = V_0$):

$$
\Delta P = \rho \, a \, V_0 = 1000 \times 1236 \times 3.0 = 3.708 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2 \approx 3.71 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Head Rise:**

$$
\Delta h = \frac{\Delta P}{\rho g} = \frac{3.708 \times 10^6}{1000 \times 9.81} = 378.0 \text{ m of water}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model QP – Adapted]**

**(a)** State and explain the **Joukowsky equation** for sudden closure of a valve in a pipe. Discuss the difference between **direct** and **indirect water hammer**. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A pump delivers water at $V_0 = 2.0 \text{ m/s}$ through a cast-iron pipe of length $L = 800 \text{ m}$, diameter $D = 0.4 \text{ m}$, and wall thickness $e = 0.01 \text{ m}$. The downstream valve is closed in $t_c = 5 \text{ s}$.
**Given:** $K = 2.2 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$, $E = 1.0 \times 10^{11} \text{ N/m}^2$ (cast iron), $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.
Calculate: (i) wave celerity, (ii) critical time, (iii) classify the water hammer type, and (iv) the maximum pressure rise. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution for (a):**

- State the equation: $\Delta P = \rho \, a \, \Delta V$, also written as $\Delta h = a \Delta V / g$.
- Explain that it applies to **sudden (instantaneous) closure**, where the entire fluid column's kinetic energy is converted into pressure energy.
- **Direct water hammer:** $t_c < 2L/a$ — maximum Joukowsky pressure realized.
- **Indirect water hammer:** $t_c > 2L/a$ — pressure rise reduced in proportion to $T_c/t_c$.
- Mention that direct water hammer is more dangerous but indirect can still cause damage over time.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Celerity:**

$$
\frac{KD}{Ee} = \frac{(2.2 \times 10^9)(0.4)}{(1.0 \times 10^{11})(0.01)} = \frac{8.8 \times 10^8}{1.0 \times 10^9} = 0.88
$$

$$
a = \frac{1483.2}{\sqrt{1.88}} = \frac{1483.2}{1.3711} = 1081.7 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(ii) Critical Time:**

$$
T_c = \frac{2L}{a} = \frac{2 \times 800}{1081.7} = 1.479 \text{ s}
$$

**(iii) Classification:** Since $t_c = 5 \text{ s} > T_c = 1.479 \text{ s}$, this is **indirect water hammer**.

**(iv) Pressure Rise (Indirect):**

$$
\Delta P = \frac{2 \, \rho \, L \, V_0}{t_c} = \frac{2 \times 1000 \times 800 \times 2.0}{5} = 6.4 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2 = 0.64 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Head Rise:**

$$
\Delta h = \frac{0.64 \times 10^6}{9810} = 65.24 \text{ m of water}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting pipe elasticity term:** Students often use $a = \sqrt{K/\rho} = 1483 \text{ m/s}$ without the $(1 + KD/Ee)$ correction. **Loss: up to 2 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing direct vs. indirect:** Always check if $t_c < T_c$ or $t_c > T_c$ before applying the appropriate formula.
> 3. **Unit mismatches:** $D$ and $e$ must be in the same units (both meters). $\Delta P$ in Pa; convert to MPa or bar if needed.
> 4. **Sign of $\Delta V$:** For valve closure, $\Delta V = V_0$ (drop in velocity). For valve opening, the pressure drops (negative surge).
> 5. **Skipping intermediate steps in derivation:** KTU examiners award method marks — show every algebraic step.
> 6. **Misapplication of surge formulas for column separation cases:** When negative surge pressures drop below vapor pressure, the simple Joukowsky analysis no longer applies (advanced topic, beyond scope).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Notes — Water Hammer**

- **Definition:** Pressure surge due to abrupt change in fluid velocity in a confined pipe.
- **Celerity (Micotty–Korteweg):** $a = \dfrac{\sqrt{K/\rho}}{\sqrt{1 + KD/(Ee)}}$ — always in m/s.
- **Pure water celerity:** $\sqrt{K/\rho} \approx 1483 \text{ m/s}$ (lower in elastic pipes).
- **Joukowsky Equation (sudden closure):** $\Delta P = \rho a \Delta V$ or $\Delta h = a \Delta V / g$.
- **Critical time:** $T_c = 2L/a$ — round-trip wave travel time.
- **Critical length:** $L_c = a t_c / 2$ — boundary between direct and indirect.
- **Direct water hammer:** $t_c < T_c$ — full Joukowsky pressure.
- **Indirect water hammer:** $t_c > T_c$ — reduced pressure: $\Delta P = 2\rho L V_0 / t_c$.
- **Period of full oscillation:** $T = 4L/a$ (downstream + upstream cycle).
- **Doubling closure time halves the surge pressure** (indirect case).
- **Friction** is neglected in basic Joukowsky analysis (rigid column theory).
- **Mitigation devices:** Surge tanks, air vessels, slow-closing valves, flywheels, pressure relief valves, air release valves.
- **Real-world dangers:** Pipe rupture, valve damage, pump failure, column separation, gauge errors.
- **Assumptions to state in exams:** (1) 1-D flow, (2) fluid slightly compressible, (3) pipe wall elastic (Hooke's law), (4) no column separation, (5) friction neglected unless specified.
- **High-yield exam keywords to use:** "Micotty–Korteweg celerity," "Joukowsky pressure rise," "critical time period," "direct vs. indirect water hammer," "elastic pipe expansion."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Real Life Problems Causing Water Hammer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET413 - MECHANICS OF FLUID FLOW
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Real Life Problems Causing Water Hammer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Water Hammer (Hydraulic Transient)** is a pressure surge phenomenon — a sharp, transient, high-amplitude pressure wave — that propagates through a fluid-filled conduit when the steady-state flow conditions are abruptly disturbed. The disturbance is typically caused by a rapid change in fluid velocity, which forces the fluid's momentum to change violently. Because the fluid is essentially incompressible and the pipe walls are elastic, the kinetic energy of the moving column of fluid is converted into a strain (elastic) energy stored in both the fluid and the pipe wall, producing a traveling pressure wave.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, water hammer is treated as a **rapidly varied unsteady flow** problem, governed by the **Joukowsky equation** for sudden disturbances, and the **Allievi equations** for slow (gradual) valve operations. The phenomenon is classified under **transient flow in pressurized conduits** in Module 2 of the syllabus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU Module 2 places equal emphasis on (a) the *causes* of water hammer in real engineering systems, and (b) the *mathematical prediction* of the resulting maximum surge pressure. Real-life problems are not abstract — they appear as numerical questions based on pump stations, penstocks, and municipal water supply lines.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a long train moving at high speed along a railway track. If the engine driver suddenly applies the brakes, the entire mass of the train does not stop instantly — the carriages at the back keep pushing the front ones, causing a violent jerk (and sometimes derailment or coupling failure) that travels backward through the train. Water hammer is the **fluid equivalent of this "train-jerk"**:

- The **moving fluid column** = the moving train.
- The **valve or pump** = the brakes.
- The **pipe wall elasticity + fluid compressibility** = the springs/couplings between carriages.
- The **traveling pressure wave** = the jerk that propagates through the train.

A practical, real-life feel: when you turn off a household tap quickly and hear a "bang" in the pipes — that is water hammer on a tiny scale. In large municipal/industrial systems (diameters of 1–3 m, lengths of several km, velocities of 2–5 m/s), the same phenomenon can rupture pipes, destroy valves, and fracture pump casings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants Used in This Topic:**
> - Density of water: $\rho = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$
> - Bulk modulus of water: $K = 2.07 \times 10^9\ \text{N/m}^2$ (≈ 2.07 GPa)
> - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$
> - Sound speed in unconfined water: $\approx 1430\ \text{m/s}$ (but reduced in pipes, typically 900–1200 m/s)

### 1.3 Why "Real-Life Problems" Deserve Special Attention

KTU examiners frequently frame water-hammer questions as *case scenarios* — a penstock supplying a hydroelectric turbine, a long pumping main, a refinery cooling line — rather than abstract pipe problems. This is because the **severity of water hammer is determined by the system layout, not by the fluid alone**. The same fluid can produce vastly different surge pressures in two pipelines of different length, material, anchoring, or operating procedure.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Real-Life Water Hammer Scenarios

Real-life water hammer incidents fall into **five principal categories**, each with a distinct physical mechanism and a distinct formula to predict the maximum surge pressure:

#### Category 1 — Sudden Complete Valve Closure
- The most severe case. The valve is closed in a time $t < t_c = 2L/a$ (the "critical time").
- Pressure rise is given by the **Joukowsky equation** in its pure form.
- Typical real-life example: an emergency shut-off valve, a burst pipe, or a check valve slamming shut on pump shutdown.

#### Category 2 — Gradual Valve Closure
- The valve is closed in a time $t > t_c$. The pressure rise is *less* than the Joukowsky maximum.
- Real-life example: a manually operated gate valve, a controlled turbine wicket-gate closure in a hydropower plant.
- The maximum pressure rise is approximately $\Delta H \approx (2LV)/(g\,t)$.

#### Category 3 — Pump Power Failure (Pump Trip)
- The electric supply to a centrifugal pump is cut. The pump decelerates and may even flow backward, but the column of water in the long delivery main has inertia and continues to move forward briefly, creating a low-pressure wave that travels upstream, followed by a returning high-pressure wave.
- Real-life example: a 10-km long pumping main from a water-treatment plant to a city. Pump trip is the **most common** cause of catastrophic water-hammer failures in municipal water supply.

#### Category 4 — Pump Startup
- A pump is started against a partially empty or empty pipe. The water column is accelerated rapidly, producing a positive pressure wave moving down the pipeline.
- Real-life example: a fire-fighting pump started with an empty header.

#### Category 5 — Column Separation (Liquid Column Separation — LCS)
- When the local static pressure falls to the vapor pressure of the liquid (≈ 2–7 kPa absolute for cold water at sea level), the liquid **cavitates** and a vapor cavity forms. When the returning high-pressure wave collapses this cavity, an *extremely severe* secondary pressure spike occurs — often *larger* than the original Joukowsky pressure.
- Real-life example: high points in long pipelines during pump shutdown.

### 2.2 The Two Governing Speeds: Wave Speed *a* and Critical Time *t_c*

For the entire analysis, the **celerity (wave speed) of the pressure wave** is the master variable:

$$a = \sqrt{\dfrac{K / \rho}{1 + \dfrac{K \cdot D}{E \cdot t} \cdot (1 - \nu^2)}}$$

where $K$ is the bulk modulus of water, $\rho$ is the density, $D$ is the pipe internal diameter, $t$ is the pipe wall thickness, $E$ is Young's modulus of the pipe material, and $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio.

For a **rigid pipe** ($E \to \infty$), the formula reduces to the speed of sound in water, $a = \sqrt{K/\rho} \approx 1430\ \text{m/s}$. For a **flexible thin-walled pipe**, the wave speed is significantly reduced, typically to 900–1200 m/s.

The **critical time** (also called the pipeline period or "period of the pipeline") is the round-trip travel time of the pressure wave:

$$t_c = \dfrac{2L}{a}$$

This is the single most important time-scale in water-hammer analysis: *any disturbance faster than $t_c$ behaves like a sudden closure*; *any disturbance slower than $t_c$ gives a smaller pressure rise*.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use the following table as a rapid-revision sheet. All quantities are in SI units unless noted.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Variables |
|---|---------|---------------|-----------|
| 1 | $\Delta P = \rho \cdot a \cdot \Delta V$ | Joukowsky pressure rise (Pa) for sudden closure | $\rho$ = density, $a$ = wave speed, $\Delta V$ = velocity change |
| 2 | $\Delta H = \dfrac{a \cdot \Delta V}{g}$ | Surge head rise (m of water) — equivalent form of (1) | $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$ |
| 3 | $a = \sqrt{\dfrac{K/\rho}{1 + (K D)/(E t)(1-\nu^2)}}$ | Wave speed in an elastic pipe | $K, E$ in Pa; $D, t$ in m; $\rho$ in kg/m³ |
| 4 | $a = \dfrac{\sqrt{K/\rho}}{\sqrt{1 + (K D)/(E t)(1-\nu^2)}}$ | Same as (3), written as numerator/denominator (form commonly used) | same as (3) |
| 5 | $t_c = 2L/a$ | Critical time / pipeline period (s) | $L$ = pipe length, $a$ = wave speed |
| 6 | $\Delta H \approx (2 L V)/(g \cdot t)$ for $t > t_c$ | Approx. head rise for gradual valve closure | $V$ = initial velocity, $t$ = closure time |
| 7 | $H_{\max} = H_0 + \Delta H$ | Maximum absolute head at the valve | $H_0$ = steady-state head |
| 8 | $\sigma = (L V)/(g H_0 t)$ | Allievi dimensionless parameter (used in charts for $t > t_c$) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\Delta P_{\text{LCS}} \approx 2 P_{\text{atm}} - 2 P_{\text{vapor}}$ | Approx. pressure spike from column-separation cavity collapse | vapor pressure in Pa abs. |
| 10 | $\tau = t / t_c$ | Dimensionless closure time (used to enter Allievi charts) | dimensionless |

**Common Pipe Material Constants (for use in formula 3):**

| Material | Young's Modulus $E$ (GPa) | Poisson's Ratio $\nu$ |
|----------|-----------------------------|------------------------|
| Steel | 200 | 0.30 |
| Ductile Iron | 170 | 0.28 |
| PVC | 3.0 | 0.45 |
| HDPE | 0.8 | 0.45 |
| Concrete | 25 | 0.20 |
| Asbestos Cement | 20 | 0.30 |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The ability to predict water hammer is essential in the design of:
- **Hydropower penstocks** (long, high-head, high-velocity water conduits to turbines).
- **Municipal water pumping mains** (5–50 km long).
- **Oil and product pipelines** (transient flow of crude/refined products).
- **Cooling-water circuits in thermal and nuclear power plants.**
- **Fire-fighting systems** (high-pressure, fast-closure sprinkler valves).
- **Ship and submarine sea-water systems.**
- **Refinery and chemical plant process piping.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Practice:** Surge protection devices (air vessels, surge tanks, pressure-relief valves, flywheels on pumps, slow-closing check valves) are *sized using these formulas*. A miscalculation can lead to pipe rupture within months of commissioning.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Joukowsky Equation (Sudden Closure)

**Starting assumptions:** Horizontal pipe, single-phase liquid, small velocity change, pipe and fluid both elastic, neglect friction (incompressible surge theory).

**Step 1 — Apply Newton's Second Law to a fluid column of length $\delta x$:**

The pressure difference across the element accelerates the mass of the fluid in it:

$$(P - (P + \delta P)) \cdot A = \rho \cdot A \cdot \delta x \cdot \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial t}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the left-hand side:**

$$-\delta P \cdot A = \rho \cdot A \cdot \delta x \cdot \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial t}$$

Dividing both sides by $A \cdot \delta x$:

$$-\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial x} = \rho \cdot \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial t} \quad \text{...(Momentum equation)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply continuity (mass conservation) accounting for fluid and pipe-wall elasticity:**

The rate of increase of mass in the element equals the net inflow. For a compressible fluid in an elastic pipe:

$$-\dfrac{\partial (\rho V)}{\partial x} = \dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \dfrac{\rho}{K} \cdot \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial t} + \text{(pipe-wall term)}$$

Combining and reducing (a full derivation is in the textbook; the key result is):

$$\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial t} + \rho a^2 \cdot \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial x} = 0 \quad \text{...(Continuity-derived)}$$

**Step 4 — Combine the two equations (eliminate $V$ or $P$) to get a wave equation:**

Taking $\partial/\partial t$ of the momentum equation and $\partial/\partial x$ of the continuity equation, then combining, one obtains a single second-order wave equation for the pressure:

$$\dfrac{\partial^2 P}{\partial t^2} = a^2 \cdot \dfrac{\partial^2 P}{\partial x^2}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the boundary condition of sudden valve closure:**

At the valve ($x = L$), the velocity is forced to zero instantaneously: $\Delta V = -V_0$ (i.e., the velocity drops by $V_0$). Substituting into the integrated form of the momentum equation over the wave-front:

$$\int_{0}^{t} \left(-\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial x}\right) dx = \rho \int_{V_0}^{0} dV$$

Because the wave-front travels at speed $a$, $\delta x = a \cdot \delta t$, and the pressure jump across the front is $\Delta P$. After substitution and cancellation:

$$\Delta P = \rho \cdot a \cdot \Delta V$$

**Step 6 — Convert to head form (dividing by $\rho g$):**

$$\Delta H = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho g} = \dfrac{a \cdot \Delta V}{g}$$

This is the **Joukowsky equation** — the single most important result in water-hammer analysis.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Real-Life Pumped Pipeline

**Problem:** A municipal water utility operates a pumping main of length $L = 5\ \text{km}$ delivering water at a steady velocity $V_0 = 2.0\ \text{m/s}$ through a steel pipe of internal diameter $D = 600\ \text{mm}$ and wall thickness $t = 10\ \text{mm}$. The pump trips (power failure). Estimate:
- (a) the wave speed,
- (b) the critical time,
- (c) the maximum surge pressure rise assuming the downstream check valve closes *suddenly*,
- (d) the surge head rise if instead the check valve closes *gradually* in 4 seconds.

**Given (SI units):**
$L = 5000\ \text{m}$; $V_0 = 2.0\ \text{m/s}$; $D = 0.6\ \text{m}$; $t = 0.010\ \text{m}$; $E_{\text{steel}} = 200 \times 10^9\ \text{N/m}^2$; $\nu = 0.30$; $K = 2.07 \times 10^9\ \text{N/m}^2$; $\rho = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$; $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$.

**Step (a) — Wave speed:**

Compute the elasticity correction term:

$$\dfrac{K \cdot D}{E \cdot t} \cdot (1 - \nu^2) = \dfrac{(2.07 \times 10^9)(0.6)}{(200 \times 10^9)(0.010)} \cdot (1 - 0.09)$$

$$= \dfrac{1.242 \times 10^9}{2.0 \times 10^9} \cdot 0.91 = 0.621 \cdot 0.91 = 0.565$$

Now $K/\rho = 2.07 \times 10^9 / 1000 = 2.07 \times 10^6\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$.

Therefore:

$$a = \sqrt{\dfrac{2.07 \times 10^6}{1 + 0.565}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2.07 \times 10^6}{1.565}} = \sqrt{1.323 \times 10^6} \approx 1150\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step (b) — Critical time:**

$$t_c = \dfrac{2L}{a} = \dfrac{2 \times 5000}{1150} = \dfrac{10000}{1150} \approx 8.70\ \text{s}$$

**Step (c) — Maximum surge pressure for sudden closure:**

$$\Delta P = \rho \cdot a \cdot \Delta V = 1000 \times 1150 \times 2.0 = 2.30 \times 10^6\ \text{N/m}^2 = 2.30\ \text{MPa}$$

**Step (d) — Surge head rise for gradual closure in $t = 4\ \text{s}$:**

Since $t = 4\ \text{s} < t_c = 8.70\ \text{s}$, the closure is still effectively "sudden" relative to the pipeline period. We must therefore use the Joukowsky value as the upper bound. The gradual-closure formula gives a *lower bound approximation only when $t > t_c$*:

$$\Delta H_{\text{gradual}} \approx \dfrac{2 L V_0}{g \, t} = \dfrac{2 \times 5000 \times 2.0}{9.81 \times 4} = \dfrac{20000}{39.24} \approx 510\ \text{m of water}$$

**Comparison with Joukowsky (sudden) head rise:**

$$\Delta H_{\text{Joukowsky}} = \dfrac{a \cdot \Delta V}{g} = \dfrac{1150 \times 2.0}{9.81} \approx 234.5\ \text{m of water}$$

**Interpretation:** The gradual-closure formula $\Delta H \approx 2LV/(g t)$ is a *lower-bound* approximation valid only for $t \gg t_c$. When $t < t_c$ (as here, $4\ \text{s} < 8.7\ \text{s}$), the Joukowsky value (≈ 234.5 m) is the correct design value. The numerical "510 m" computed from the simple formula is *unphysical* in this case because the assumption $t > t_c$ is violated — this is a classic KTU pitfall.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** Always compute $t_c$ first, then compare with actual closure time. If $t < t_c$, use Joukowsky; if $t > t_c$, use Allievi charts or the simplified gradual-closure formula.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Cause-to-Consequence Mapping

| Real-Life Cause | Disturbance Type | Wave Direction | Peak Pressure | Mitigation |
|------------------|------------------|----------------|----------------|-------------|
| Sudden valve closure | $t < t_c$ | Upstream from valve | Joukowsky max: $\rho a V$ | Slow-closing valve, surge tank |
| Gradual valve closure | $t > t_c$ | Upstream, attenuated | $< \rho a V$ (use Allievi) | None usually needed if $t \gg t_c$ |
| Pump power failure | Column deceleration | Low-P wave upstream, then High-P wave reflects back | $\approx$ Joukowsky at check valve | Flywheel, surge tank, air vessel |
| Pump startup (empty pipe) | Column acceleration | High-P wave downstream | $\rho a V$ | Soft-starter, open discharge valve |
| Pipe rupture | Sudden zero-pressure boundary | Both directions | Low-P wave, then transient | None — accidental |
| Check-valve slam | Sudden closure at valve | Upstream | Joukowsky max | Non-slam check valve, surge tank |
| Column separation (LCS) | Vapor cavity forms & collapses | Re-collapse spike | $2(P_{atm} - P_{vapor})$, very high | Air valve at high points, surge tank |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Sequence of Events During Pump Power Failure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Steady Pumping at Velocity V0] --> B{Power Supply to Pump Fails}
    B --> C[Pump Decelerates Rapidly]
    C --> D[Low-Pressure Wave Races Upstream to Reservoir at Speed a]
    D --> E[Wave Reflects from Reservoir as High-Pressure Wave]
    E --> F{High-Pressure Wave Reaches Check Valve at Downstream End}
    F --> G[Check Valve Closes Suddenly or Slams Shut]
    G --> H[Severe Joukowsky Pressure Rise rho * a * V0]
    H --> I{Pressure at Local High Point Falls to Vapor Pressure}
    I -- Yes --> J[Vapor Cavity Forms Liquid Column Separation]
    I -- No --> K[Pressure Wave Reflects and Attenuates]
    J --> L[Returning High-Pressure Wave Collapses the Cavity]
    L --> M[Catastrophic Secondary Pressure Spike]
    K --> N[System Reaches New Steady State]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Final Steady State Pump OFF No Flow]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Water-Hammer Protection Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SOURCE[Water Source Reservoir]
        R1[Reservoir at Elevation H0]
    end
    subgraph PUMP[Pump Station]
        P1[Centrifugal Pump with Flywheel F]
        P2[Soft Starter or VFD for Controlled Start]
    end
    subgraph MAIN[Long Pumping Main L kilometres]
        M1[Pipe with Wave Speed a and Period tc equal to 2L slash a]
        M2[Air Vessel Surge Protection near Pump]
        M3[Slow Closing Check Valve at Discharge]
        M4[Air Valve at Local High Point]
    end
    subgraph DELIVERY[Delivery Reservoir or Network]
        D1[Downstream Tank or Distribution]
    end
    R1 --> P1 --> M1
    P1 --> P2
    P1 -. protects .-> M2
    M1 --> M3 --> M4 --> D1
    M2 -. senses surge .-> M1
    M4 -. vents air .-> M1
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Process Diagram — Comparison of Sudden vs Gradual Closure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Steady Flow at Velocity V0] --> B{Compare Closure Time t with Critical Time tc equal to 2L slash a}
    B -- t less than tc Sudden --> C[Use Joukowsky Equation]
    B -- t greater than tc Gradual --> D[Use Allievi Charts or Simplified Formula]
    C --> C1[Delta P equal to rho times a times V0]
    C --> C2[Maximum Surge is Independent of Pipe Length]
    C --> C3[Maximum Surge Depends Only on Wave Speed and Velocity Change]
    D --> D1[Delta H approx equal to 2LV divided by gt]
    D --> D2[Surge Decreases as Closure Time Increases]
    D --> D3[Allievi Parameter sigma equal to LV divided by gH0 times t Used with Charts]
    C1 --> E[Apply Mitigation Slow Closing Valve or Surge Tank]
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> E
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
Define *water hammer*. List any four real-life situations in a long pumping main that can cause water hammer.

**Model Answer (≈ 90 words):**
Water hammer is the transient pressure surge generated in a fluid-filled pipe when the steady-state velocity is changed abruptly, causing a pressure wave to travel along the conduit. Real-life causes in a long pumping main include: (1) sudden closure of a downstream valve; (2) sudden power failure to a pump (pump trip); (3) rapid start of a pump against an empty pipe; (4) sudden closure (slam) of a non-return / check valve; (5) column separation and cavity collapse at high points; (6) pipe rupture. *(4 marks: 2 for definition + 2 for listing four causes.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
State the Joukowsky equation. What is the *critical time* of a pipeline, and how is it used to classify the severity of water hammer?

**Model Answer:**
Joukowsky equation: $\Delta P = \rho \, a \, \Delta V$ (or in head form $\Delta H = a \Delta V / g$), where $\rho$ is fluid density, $a$ is the pressure-wave celerity, and $\Delta V$ is the change in flow velocity.
Critical time: $t_c = 2L/a$, the round-trip travel time of the pressure wave along a pipe of length $L$.
Use: If the actual disturbance time $t < t_c$ (sudden closure), Joukowsky applies in full. If $t > t_c$ (gradual closure), the surge is smaller and is determined using Allievi charts. *(3 marks: 1.5 for equation, 1.5 for critical time & its use.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model]*

A 4 km long horizontal steel pumping main of internal diameter 500 mm and wall thickness 8 mm carries water at a steady velocity of 1.8 m/s. The downstream isolation valve is closed suddenly due to a burst in the distribution network.

**Take:** $E = 200 \times 10^9\ \text{N/m}^2$, $\nu = 0.30$, $K = 2.07 \times 10^9\ \text{N/m}^2$, $\rho = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$.

**(a)** Calculate the celerity of the pressure wave in the pipe. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the magnitude of the maximum surge pressure and the corresponding surge head rise. Also estimate the critical time of the pipeline and comment on the suitability of Joukowsky's equation in this case. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q-A (a) — Wave Speed:**

**Step 1 — Identify the data:**

$L = 4000\ \text{m}$, $D = 0.500\ \text{m}$, $t = 0.008\ \text{m}$, $V_0 = 1.8\ \text{m/s}$.

**Step 2 — Compute the elasticity correction factor:**

$$\dfrac{K D}{E t}(1-\nu^2) = \dfrac{(2.07 \times 10^9)(0.5)}{(200 \times 10^9)(0.008)} \cdot (1 - 0.30^2)$$

$$= \dfrac{1.035 \times 10^9}{1.6 \times 10^9} \cdot (1 - 0.09) = 0.6469 \cdot 0.91 = 0.5887$$

**Step 3 — Compute $K/\rho$:**

$$\dfrac{K}{\rho} = \dfrac{2.07 \times 10^9}{1000} = 2.07 \times 10^6\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute the wave speed $a$:**

$$a = \sqrt{\dfrac{2.07 \times 10^6}{1 + 0.5887}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2.07 \times 10^6}{1.5887}} = \sqrt{1.303 \times 10^6} \approx 1141\ \text{m/s}$$

**Conclusion for part (a):** $a \approx 1141\ \text{m/s}$. *(7 marks: 2 for formula, 2 for substitution, 2 for result, 1 for units.)*

---

**Solution to Q-A (b) — Surge Pressure, Head, and Critical Time:**

**Step 1 — Maximum surge pressure (Joukowsky):**

$$\Delta P = \rho \cdot a \cdot \Delta V = 1000 \times 1141 \times 1.8 = 2.054 \times 10^6\ \text{N/m}^2$$

$$\Delta P \approx 2.05\ \text{MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Surge head rise:**

$$\Delta H = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho g} = \dfrac{2.054 \times 10^6}{1000 \times 9.81} \approx 209.4\ \text{m of water}$$

**Step 3 — Critical time:**

$$t_c = \dfrac{2L}{a} = \dfrac{2 \times 4000}{1141} \approx 7.01\ \text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Comment on Joukowsky's applicability:**

A "sudden" closure is defined as one in which the closure time $t \ll t_c$. In a real engineering sense, if the valve is genuinely closed instantaneously (e.g., by a bursting pipe), the Joukowsky equation applies in full and gives the maximum possible surge of ≈ 2.05 MPa (≈ 209 m head). This is a very high pressure — equivalent to ≈ 20 bar — and could rupture an un-reinforced steel pipe of this class. Therefore the Joukowsky equation is fully appropriate, and the design must include surge protection such as a surge tank, air vessel, or a slow-closing non-return valve.

**Conclusion for part (b):** $\Delta P \approx 2.05$ MPa, $\Delta H \approx 209.4$ m, $t_c \approx 7.01$ s; Joukowsky applies since closure is sudden. *(7 marks: 2 for $\Delta P$, 2 for $\Delta H$, 1 for $t_c$, 2 for the discussion.)*

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model — Internal Choice Alternative]*

A penstock feeding a hydroelectric turbine in a high-head scheme has the following data:
Length $L = 1500\ \text{m}$, diameter $D = 1.2\ \text{m}$, wall thickness $t = 14\ \text{mm}$, material steel ($E = 200$ GPa, $\nu = 0.30$), steady flow velocity $V_0 = 4.5\ \text{m/s}$. A routine maintenance requires the guide-vane to be closed in $t = 6$ seconds.

**Take:** $K = 2.07 \times 10^9\ \text{N/m}^2$, $\rho = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$.

**(a)** Compute the wave speed and the critical (pipeline) period. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine whether the closure is sudden or gradual. If sudden, compute the Joukowsky pressure rise; if gradual, compute the surge head using the simplified formula $\Delta H = 2LV/(gt)$ and comment on the practical significance. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q-B (a) — Wave Speed and Critical Period:**

**Step 1 — Elasticity factor:**

$$\dfrac{K D}{E t}(1-\nu^2) = \dfrac{(2.07 \times 10^9)(1.2)}{(200 \times 10^9)(0.014)} \cdot 0.91$$

$$= \dfrac{2.484 \times 10^9}{2.8 \times 10^9} \cdot 0.91 = 0.8871 \cdot 0.91 = 0.8073$$

**Step 2 — Wave speed:**

$$a = \sqrt{\dfrac{2.07 \times 10^6}{1 + 0.8073}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2.07 \times 10^6}{1.8073}} = \sqrt{1.1453 \times 10^6} \approx 1070\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Critical time:**

$$t_c = \dfrac{2L}{a} = \dfrac{2 \times 1500}{1070} = \dfrac{3000}{1070} \approx 2.80\ \text{s}$$

**Conclusion for part (a):** $a \approx 1070\ \text{m/s}$; $t_c \approx 2.80\ \text{s}$. *(7 marks.)*

---

**Solution to Q-B (b) — Sudden or Gradual? Surge Head:**

**Step 1 — Compare closure time with critical time:**

$t = 6\ \text{s}$, $t_c = 2.80\ \text{s}$. Since $t > t_c$, the closure is **gradual**.

**Step 2 — Compute the simplified surge head:**

$$\Delta H = \dfrac{2 L V_0}{g \, t} = \dfrac{2 \times 1500 \times 4.5}{9.81 \times 6} = \dfrac{13500}{58.86} \approx 229.3\ \text{m of water}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with the Joukowsky upper bound:**

$$\Delta H_{\text{Joukowsky}} = \dfrac{a V_0}{g} = \dfrac{1070 \times 4.5}{9.81} \approx 491\ \text{m of water}$$

**Step 4 — Practical significance:**

The simplified formula gives ≈ 229 m, which is significantly less than the Joukowsky maximum of ≈ 491 m. This confirms the engineering rule: *a slow closure in a high-head, long penstock can reduce the surge pressure to roughly half* of the worst-case value. For a real hydropower scheme operating at a static head of, say, 200 m, an additional surge of 229 m would still be unacceptably high — therefore, in practice, the closure time is chosen *much* larger than $t_c$ (often 30–60 s) and a **surge tank** is provided immediately upstream of the turbine to absorb the residual transient. *(7 marks: 2 for comparison, 2 for $\Delta H$, 1 for Joukowsky comparison, 2 for engineering comment.)*

---

### 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Water Hammer Problems:**
> 1. **Skipping the critical-time check.** A student who directly substitutes into $\Delta P = \rho a V$ for every problem loses 2–3 marks whenever the question is *gradual* closure. Always compute $t_c = 2L/a$ first.
> 2. **Forgetting the elasticity correction in $a$.** A common error is to use $a = 1430\ \text{m/s}$ (speed of sound in water) instead of the elastic-pipe formula. Examiner deducts up to 1 mark per occurrence.
> 3. **Unit inconsistency.** Mixing $D$ in mm and $E$ in GPa without converting leads to order-of-magnitude errors. Always convert to SI: $D$ in m, $E$ in Pa, $t$ in m.
> 4. **Confusing $\Delta H$ with $H_{\max}$.** Joukowsky gives the *additional* surge head; the absolute maximum head at the valve is $H_0 + \Delta H$, where $H_0$ is the steady-state operating head.
> 5. **Forgetting to comment on real-life mitigation.** In 14-mark questions, KTU examiners expect a 1–2 sentence design comment (e.g., "install a surge tank" or "use a slow-closing valve") at the end. Marks are explicitly allocated for this.
> 6. **Mixing up the sign of $\Delta V$.** $\Delta V$ is the *change* in velocity ($V_{\text{final}} - V_{\text{initial}}$). For a sudden closure, $\Delta V = -V_0$, but the *magnitude* $\rho a V_0$ is used; be careful with sign conventions when the fluid is reversed (pump trip → flow reversal).

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Definition:** Water hammer = pressure surge from sudden velocity change in a fluid-filled pipe.
- [x] **Five principal real-life causes:** (1) sudden valve closure, (2) gradual valve closure, (3) pump trip, (4) pump startup, (5) column separation (LCS).
- [x] **Joukowsky equation (head form):** $\Delta H = a \Delta V / g$.
- [x] **Joukowsky equation (pressure form):** $\Delta P = \rho a \Delta V$.
- [x] **Wave speed in elastic pipe:** $a = \sqrt{(K/\rho)/[1 + (K D / E t)(1-\nu^2)]}$.
- [x] **Critical time / pipeline period:** $t_c = 2L/a$.
- [x] **Decision rule:** If $t < t_c$ → *sudden*, use Joukowsky. If $t > t_c$ → *gradual*, use Allievi charts or the simplified formula $\Delta H \approx 2LV/(gt)$.
- [x] **Maximum surge in Joukowsky case is independent of pipe length** (depends only on $a$ and $\Delta V$).
- [x] **In gradual closure, surge decreases as $t$ increases.**
- [x] **Most severe real-life case:** Pump trip + check-valve slam in a long pumping main.
- [x] **Column separation** can produce a secondary spike *larger* than Joukowsky.
- [x] **Real-life mitigations:** surge tank, air vessel, slow-closing check valve, flywheel on pump, pressure-relief valve, air valve at high points.
- [x] **Always use SI units** (D in m, t in m, E in Pa, K in Pa) to avoid errors.
- [x] **Always state $t_c$ and compare with $t$ before choosing the formula.**
- [x] **Always add a one-line engineering comment** in 14-mark answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cavitation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET413 - MECHANICS OF FLUID FLOW
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cavitation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cavitation** is a hydrodynamic phenomenon characterized by the formation, growth, and subsequent violent collapse of vapor-filled cavities (or bubbles) within a flowing liquid, occurring when the **local static pressure** of the liquid falls below the **vapor pressure** of the liquid at the prevailing temperature.

The process is fundamentally a **phase-change event** — liquid water (or any working fluid) flashes into vapor when subjected to pressures below its saturation pressure, only to violently implode when it is swept into regions of higher pressure. The thermodynamic threshold is governed by the Clausius–Clapeyron relation, but for engineering analysis, cavitation is treated through Bernoulli's principle and pressure field analysis.

**Governing Threshold Condition:**

$$P_{local} \leq P_{vapor}$$

where $P_{local}$ is the local static pressure and $P_{vapor}$ is the **saturated vapor pressure** of the liquid at the operating temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Value:** For water at $20^\circ C$, $P_{vapor} \approx 2.34 \, kPa$ (absolute). At $100^\circ C$, $P_{vapor} = 101.325 \, kPa$ (atmospheric — hence boiling). This is why cavitation severity changes dramatically with temperature in hydraulic machinery.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Boiling Mountain Stream"

Imagine standing by a **rocky mountain stream**. Where the water flows over smooth, deep sections, you hear a quiet, gentle gurgle. But where the stream suddenly narrows — say, passing over a sharp rock ledge or through a constriction — the water **speeds up dramatically** and you can hear a hissing, crackling sound, sometimes even see milky-white bubbles forming and then popping with sharp pops.

> This is **cavitation happening in nature**! The fluid accelerates through a narrow region. By Bernoulli's principle, high velocity = low pressure. When that low pressure dips below the vapor pressure, microscopic vapor bubbles nucleate. As the flow re-expands and slows, the pressure rebounds above vapor pressure, and the bubbles **implode** with extreme local energy.

**Engineering Parallel:**
- The **stream's narrow gorge** = Venturi throat / pump impeller eye / turbine blade suction side
- The **white milky bubbles** = cavitation vapor cavities
- The **sharp popping sounds** = bubble collapse (implo­sion) — which is what damages propeller blades and pump impellers
- The **pool below the rapid** = region of high pressure where damage occurs

---

## 1.3 Why Cavitation Matters in Engineering

Cavitation is **the single most important design-limiting phenomenon** in high-speed hydraulic machinery. It is responsible for:

| Engineering Impact | Physical Consequence |
|---|---|
| **Erosion / Pitting** | Micro-jets from collapsing bubbles erode metal surfaces |
| **Vibration & Noise** | Bubble collapse produces broadband noise and structure-borne vibration |
| **Performance Loss** | Head–flow / efficiency curves deviate from theoretical |
| **Efficiency Drop** | Energy lost to vapor formation rather than useful work |
| **Bearing / Seal Damage** | In pumps, cavitation at suction destroys mechanical seals |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 CO Mapping:** This topic directly maps to **CO2** — *Apply the principles of fluid kinematics and dynamics to analyze flow in pipes and hydraulic machines, including the phenomenon of cavitation.*

---

## 1.4 Necessary Pre-Requisite Concepts

Before diving into analysis, three foundational ideas must be crystal clear:

1. **Vapor Pressure ($P_v$):** The pressure at which a liquid boils at a given temperature. It is a fluid property, NOT a function of the flow.
2. **Static Pressure Distribution in Flow:** Governed by Bernoulli's equation (for ideal flow) or the energy equation (for real flow with losses).
3. **Local Pressure Drop Mechanisms:**
   - High velocity (Bernoulli)
   - High elevation (hydrostatic)
   - Friction losses (in pipes, valves)
   - Centrifugal effects (in rotating machinery)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Cavitation — Step-by-Step Mechanism

The complete cavitation process unfolds in **four distinct stages**:

### Stage 1 — Nucleation
- Microscopic gas/vapor pockets (called *cavitation nuclei*) exist on suspended particles, on solid surfaces, or dissolved within the liquid.
- These nuclei act as "seeds" for bubble formation.
- In absolutely pure, gas-free water, cavitation requires extremely low pressures (closer to theoretical limits). Real engineering fluids always contain nuclei.

### Stage 2 — Bubble Growth
- As the liquid flows into a low-pressure region where $P_{local} < P_{vapor}$, additional liquid vaporizes into the nucleus, and the bubble **grows rapidly**.
- Growth is governed by the **Rayleigh–Plesset equation** (advanced topic — qualitative understanding only at KTU level).
- Bubbles can grow to millimeter or even centimeter scale in severe cases.

### Stage 3 — Convective Transport
- The bubble, now part of the flow, is carried by the moving liquid toward regions of higher pressure.
- In a pump, this means bubbles formed at the impeller eye are flung outward toward the high-pressure casing.

### Stage 4 — Collapse (Implosion)
- When the bubble reaches a region where $P_{local} > P_{vapor}$, the vapor rapidly recondenses.
- The surrounding liquid rushes inward to fill the void.
- **Collapse is violent and asymmetric** — producing a high-velocity micro-jet (up to **100–200 m/s**) that strikes the nearby solid surface.
- Local pressures during collapse can exceed **1000 atm**, sufficient to pit even hardened steel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for Examiners:** The **damage** of cavitation is NOT at the location of bubble formation, but at the location of bubble collapse, which is typically in a higher-pressure region. This is why pump impellers suffer erosion on the **discharge side of the blade** (where pressure has recovered) and not the suction eye.

---

## 2.2 The Cavitation Parameter (Thoma's Cavitation Number, σ)

This is the **single most important dimensionless number** for cavitation analysis. It compares the available pressure margin (above vapor pressure) to the dynamic pressure of the flow.

### 2.2.1 General Definition

$$\sigma = \frac{P_{ref} - P_{vapor}}{\frac{1}{2} \rho V_{ref}^2}$$

where:
- $P_{ref}$ = reference static pressure at the point of interest
- $P_{vapor}$ = vapor pressure of the liquid
- $V_{ref}$ = reference velocity
- $\rho$ = density of the liquid

### 2.2.2 Physical Interpretation

- **Large σ** (much greater than critical value): Pressure is well above vapor pressure. Flow is **safe from cavitation**. Bubble formation is impossible.
- **Small σ** (approaching critical value): Pressure approaches vapor pressure. Cavitation is **about to begin**.
- **σ < σ_critical**: Cavitation occurs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Cavitation Number ($\sigma_c$ or $\sigma_i$):** The value of σ at which cavitation *just begins*. Determined experimentally for a given machine geometry. It is a **machine-specific constant** that must be supplied by the manufacturer.

---

## 2.3 Cavitation Number in Different Machine Contexts

KTU 2024 frequently tests the **context-specific form** of the cavitation number. Master all four forms below.

### 2.3.1 For Turbines (Francis, Kaplan, Pelton) — Thoma Number

$$\sigma_T = \frac{H_{sv}}{H}$$

where:
- $H_{sv}$ = **Net positive suction head in meters of liquid** (actually, the margin above vapor pressure expressed as head)
- $H$ = net head developed by the turbine

More precisely:

$$\sigma_T = \frac{(P_{atm}/\rho g + h_s) - (P_v/\rho g) - z_s}{H}$$

where $h_s$ is the suction head (negative if below tailrace), and $z_s$ is the elevation difference.

### 2.3.2 For Pumps (Centrifugal, Axial)

$$\sigma_P = \frac{NPSH_{available}}{H}$$

where $NPSH_{available}$ is the **Net Positive Suction Head available** at the pump suction.

### 2.3.3 For Ship Propellers

$$\sigma = \frac{P_0 - P_v}{\frac{1}{2} \rho V_0^2}$$

where $P_0$ and $V_0$ are the ambient (free-stream) pressure and velocity far from the propeller.

### 2.3.4 For Flow Through an Orifice / Venturi

$$\sigma = \frac{P_2 - P_v}{\frac{1}{2} \rho V_2^2}$$

where subscript 2 refers to the downstream (vena contracta) section where pressure is lowest.

---

## 2.4 Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH) — The Pump Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NPSH is the most-tested cavitation concept in KTU board exams for the pump side.** Memorize both forms below.

### 2.4.1 NPSH Available ($NPSH_a$ or $NPSHA$)

The actual pressure head available at the pump suction flange, expressed in meters of liquid, *above* the vapor pressure head.

$$NPSH_A = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} + \frac{P_{gauge,\,suction}}{\rho g} - \frac{P_{vapor}}{\rho g} - h_f$$

For a typical suction tank arrangement:

$$NPSH_A = H_{atm} + H_s - H_{vapor} - h_{f,\,suction}$$

where:
- $H_{atm}$ = atmospheric pressure head ≈ 10.3 m of water at sea level
- $H_s$ = static suction head (positive if liquid is above pump centerline; negative if below)
- $H_{vapor}$ = vapor pressure head of liquid at operating temperature
- $h_{f,\,suction}$ = friction head loss in suction piping

### 2.4.2 NPSH Required ($NPSH_R$ or $NPSHR$)

The minimum NPSH needed to prevent cavitation. **It is a property of the pump itself**, supplied by the manufacturer as a function of flow rate.

### 2.4.3 Cavitation Criterion for Pumps

$$\boxed{NPSH_A > NPSH_R \quad \text{(by a safety margin, typically 0.5 to 1.0 m)}}$$

If $NPSH_A < NPSH_R$, cavitation occurs. The safety margin prevents incipient cavitation in service.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This is the consolidated cheat sheet for KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination).

| # | Formula | Description / Variable Meanings | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{local} \leq P_{vapor}$ | Cavitation onset criterion | Conceptual / 3-mark Q |
| 2 | $\sigma = \dfrac{P_{ref} - P_{vapor}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{ref}^2}$ | General cavitation number | Definition / derivation |
| 3 | $\sigma_T = \dfrac{H_{sv}}{H}$ | Thoma's cavitation number for turbines | 14-mark turbine problems |
| 4 | $NPSH_A = H_{atm} + H_s - H_{vapor} - h_f$ | NPSH available (pump) | 14-mark pump problems |
| 5 | $NPSH_R = f(Q)$ | NPSH required (pump) — from manufacturer curve | Pump selection problems |
| 6 | $\sigma_P = \dfrac{NPSH_A}{H}$ | Cavitation number for pump | Comparison & limits |
| 7 | $\sigma = \sigma_c$ | Cavitation threshold (incipient) | Operating limits |
| 8 | $V_{cavitation\,threshold}$ via Bernoulli | $V = \sqrt{2(P_{ref} - P_v)/\rho}$ | Velocity computation |
| 9 | $P_v$ at temperature T (steam tables) | Property lookup | Numerical problems |
| 10 | $H_{vapor} = P_v / (\rho g)$ | Vapor pressure expressed as head | Unit conversion |

> [!WARNING]
> **Units Vigilance:** When using $\sigma_T = H_{sv}/H$, BOTH $H_{sv}$ and $H$ must be in the same units (m of water, typically). When using the general definition, $P$ must be in **Pa (absolute)** and $V$ in m/s for the formula to be dimensionally consistent.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cavitation analysis is indispensable in:

1. **Hydropower Plants (Francis & Kaplan Turbines):** Setting the maximum permissible elevation of the turbine above tailwater to avoid cavitation at the runner blade.
2. **Centrifugal Pump Stations:** Determining allowable suction lift, pipe diameter, and minimum submergence for the suction tank.
3. **Marine Propeller Design:** Limiting shaft RPM to prevent cavitation erosion of blades.
4. **High-Performance Valves & Orifices:** Sizing to prevent choking and cavitation erosion in control valves.
5. **Diesel Engine Fuel Injectors:** Managing in-nozzle cavitation for proper fuel atomization (desirable here, not destructive).
6. **Ultrasonic Cleaning & Medical Lithotripsy:** Controlled cavitation used as a useful tool.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cavitation Number from First Principles (Bernoulli's Equation)

This is the **foundation derivation** that KTU 2024 examiners expect for a 14-mark question. We start from Euler's equation along a streamline and reduce it to the cavitation number.

### Given:
- Steady, incompressible, inviscid flow along a streamline
- Reference point (∞) far from the body, where $P = P_0$, $V = V_0$, $z = z_0$
- Point of minimum pressure (vena contracta / blade suction surface) where $P = P_{min}$, $V = V_{min}$, $z = z_{min}$

### Step 1: Bernoulli's Equation Between the Two Points

$$\frac{P_0}{\rho g} + \frac{V_0^2}{2g} + z_0 = \frac{P_{min}}{\rho g} + \frac{V_{min}^2}{2g} + z_{min}$$

*This expresses conservation of mechanical energy per unit weight for an ideal, incompressible, steady flow along a streamline.*

### Step 2: Solve for $P_{min}$

$$P_{min} = P_0 + \frac{\rho}{2}(V_0^2 - V_{min}^2) + \rho g (z_0 - z_{min})$$

*Rearranging to isolate the local minimum pressure in terms of the upstream (reference) conditions and the velocity difference.*

### Step 3: Apply the Cavitation Onset Condition

Cavitation begins when $P_{min}$ first reaches $P_{vapor}$:

$$P_{vapor} = P_0 + \frac{\rho}{2}(V_0^2 - V_{min}^2) + \rho g (z_0 - z_{min})$$

*The threshold for incipient cavitation: the local pressure has dropped to the vapor pressure.*

### Step 4: Rearrange to Isolate the Cavitation Number

Rearrange the previous equation to expose $(P_0 - P_{vapor})$:

$$P_0 - P_{vapor} = \frac{\rho}{2}(V_{min}^2 - V_0^2) - \rho g (z_0 - z_{min})$$

*Now the left side is the available "pressure margin" above vapor pressure.*

### Step 5: Introduce Velocity Coefficient (Simplified Body of Revolution Case)

For a streamlined body (e.g., hydrofoil, propeller blade) with a velocity coefficient $C_V = V_{min}/V_0$, and assuming $z_0 \approx z_{min}$ (horizontal flow):

$$P_0 - P_{vapor} = \frac{\rho V_0^2}{2}(C_V^2 - 1)$$

*In most engineering flows, $V_{min} > V_0$ (flow accelerates around the body), so $(C_V^2 - 1) > 0$, making the right side positive. This is the driving force for cavitation.*

### Step 6: Define the Cavitation Number

Divide both sides by the dynamic pressure of the reference flow:

$$\sigma = \frac{P_0 - P_{vapor}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_0^2} = C_V^2 - 1$$

*This is the dimensionless cavitation number, which represents the ratio of the pressure margin above vapor pressure to the dynamic pressure of the flow.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A small σ implies the dynamic pressure dominates — flow is highly accelerated — and cavitation is likely. A large σ implies the static pressure margin dominates — flow is "pressurized" relative to vapor pressure — and cavitation is suppressed.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — NPSH Available for a Pump with Suction Tank

This is the **workhorse derivation** for KTU 14-mark pump problems.

### Given:
- Open suction tank with free surface exposed to atmosphere
- Pump centerline located at elevation $z_s$ below the free surface
- Suction pipe of length $L$, diameter $D$, with friction factor $f$
- Liquid temperature $T$, with vapor pressure $P_v(T)$
- Atmospheric pressure $P_{atm}$ (or absolute pressure if tank is closed)

### Step 1: Write the Energy Equation from Tank Surface (1) to Pump Suction Flange (2)

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_f$$

*Standard steady-flow energy equation per unit weight, with friction head loss on the right.*

### Step 2: Apply Boundary Conditions

At the tank surface:
- $P_1 = P_{atm}$
- $V_1 \approx 0$ (large tank)
- $z_1 = 0$ (datum)

At the pump suction flange:
- $P_2 = P_{suction}$
- $V_2 = V_{pipe} = Q/A$
- $z_2 = -z_s$ (pump is below datum)

### Step 3: Substitute and Simplify

$$\frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} = \frac{P_{suction}}{\rho g} + \frac{V_{pipe}^2}{2g} - z_s + h_f$$

*Rearranging the energy equation with the boundary conditions applied.*

### Step 4: Solve for the Suction Pressure Head

$$\frac{P_{suction}}{\rho g} = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} + z_s - \frac{V_{pipe}^2}{2g} - h_f$$

*This is the total pressure head available at the pump suction flange.*

### Step 5: Subtract the Vapor Pressure Head to Get NPSH_A

By definition, NPSH available is the suction head *in excess of* the vapor pressure head:

$$NPSH_A = \frac{P_{suction}}{\rho g} - \frac{P_{vapor}}{\rho g}$$

### Step 6: Final Form

$$NPSH_A = \frac{P_{atm} - P_{vapor}}{\rho g} + z_s - \frac{V_{pipe}^2}{2g} - h_f$$

Or in standard notation:

$$\boxed{NPSH_A = H_{atm} + H_s - H_{vapor} - h_{f,\,suction}}$$

*This is the KTU-standard form. Note: $H_s = z_s$ is the static suction head, positive when liquid is above the pump centerline.*

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Suction Lift for a Pump

A frequent KTU 14-mark sub-question asks: *"What is the maximum height above which a pump can lift water?"*

### Starting Point: Cavitation Incipient Condition

At incipient cavitation:

$$NPSH_A = NPSH_R$$

### Substitute the NPSH_A Expression

$$H_{atm} + H_s - H_{vapor} - h_f = NPSH_R$$

### Solve for $H_s$ (Static Suction Head)

$$H_s = NPSH_R - H_{atm} + H_{vapor} + h_f$$

### Interpret the Sign Convention

- If $H_s > 0$: liquid level is **above** pump centerline (flooded suction)
- If $H_s < 0$: liquid level is **below** pump centerline (suction lift)

### Maximum Suction Lift (Case Where $H_s$ is Most Negative)

$$H_{s,\,max\,lift} = -\left(H_{atm} - H_{vapor} - NPSH_R - h_f\right)$$

*This is the maximum vertical distance the pump can draw water from a lower reservoir.*

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget the friction head loss term $h_f$ in suction lift calculations. Always include it.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cavitation vs. Operating Regime

The behaviour of the machine changes qualitatively with the cavitation number. This table is a **favourite of KTU examiners** for descriptive questions.

| Regime | Cavitation Number (σ) | Physical State | Machine Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| **No cavitation** | $\sigma \gg \sigma_c$ | Pressure always $> P_v$ | Normal operation, design head/efficiency |
| **Incipient cavitation** | $\sigma \approx \sigma_c$ | First bubbles appear | Slight noise, marginal performance drop |
| **Developed cavitation** | $\sigma_c > \sigma > \sigma_{lim}$ | Steady vapor cavity | Head/Efficiency drops, vibration increases |
| **Supercavitation** | $\sigma \ll \sigma_c$ | Cavity covers whole body | Complete breakdown, no useful work |
| **Choked flow** (specific to orifices) | $\sigma$ indeterminate | $P_{throat} = P_v$ | Mass flow rate no longer increases with ΔP |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** For a 7-mark sub-question on cavitation regimes, sketch the **performance curve** showing head vs. σ. As σ decreases (lower pressure margin), the head remains constant until σ_c, then drops sharply. This curve is **testable as a sketch**.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Cavitation Number at a Venturi

**Problem:** Water at $30^\circ C$ flows through a Venturi with throat diameter $D_2 = 50 \, mm$ and upstream diameter $D_1 = 150 \, mm$. Upstream pressure $P_1 = 200 \, kPa$ (abs). The discharge is $Q = 0.02 \, m^3/s$. Vapor pressure of water at $30^\circ C$ is $P_v = 4.246 \, kPa$. Calculate the cavitation number at the throat.

### Given:
$D_1 = 0.150 \, m$, $D_2 = 0.050 \, m$, $P_1 = 200,000 \, Pa$, $Q = 0.02 \, m^3/s$, $P_v = 4246 \, Pa$, $\rho = 996 \, kg/m^3$

### Step 1: Areas

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.150)^2 = 0.01767 \, m^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.050)^2 = 0.001963 \, m^2$$

*Geometric area of upstream and throat sections.*

### Step 2: Velocities from Continuity

$$V_1 = \frac{Q}{A_1} = \frac{0.02}{0.01767} = 1.132 \, m/s$$

$$V_2 = \frac{Q}{A_2} = \frac{0.02}{0.001963} = 10.19 \, m/s$$

*Volumetric flow rate divided by cross-sectional area.*

### Step 3: Throat Pressure from Bernoulli (Neglecting Elevation and Losses)

$$P_2 = P_1 + \frac{\rho}{2}(V_1^2 - V_2^2)$$

$$P_2 = 200{,}000 + \frac{996}{2}\left(1.132^2 - 10.19^2\right)$$

$$P_2 = 200{,}000 + 498 \times (1.281 - 103.84)$$

$$P_2 = 200{,}000 + 498 \times (-102.56)$$

$$P_2 = 200{,}000 - 51{,}074 = 148{,}926 \, Pa$$

*Applying Bernoulli's equation to relate the upstream and throat pressures through the velocity difference.*

### Step 4: Cavitation Number at Throat

$$\sigma = \frac{P_2 - P_v}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_2^2} = \frac{148{,}926 - 4246}{0.5 \times 996 \times 10.19^2}$$

$$\sigma = \frac{144{,}680}{51{,}696} = 2.799$$

### Step 5: Interpretation

Since $\sigma = 2.80$ is positive and likely greater than the critical value for the Venturi geometry, **no cavitation** is expected. If the flow rate were increased such that $V_2$ rises to ~18 m/s, $P_2$ would approach $P_v$ and $\sigma$ would drop near zero.

> [!NOTE]
> **Threshold velocity** can be back-calculated by setting $P_2 = P_v$ in Bernoulli's equation. This is a common KTU follow-up question.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Maximum Suction Lift of a Pump

**Problem:** A centrifugal pump delivers water at $20^\circ C$. The atmospheric pressure is $98 \, kPa$ (abs). The pump has $NPSH_R = 3.5 \, m$. The suction pipe has length $5 \, m$, diameter $100 \, mm$, with friction factor $f = 0.02$, and includes a foot valve with $K = 1.5$. Determine the maximum permissible suction lift.

### Given:
$P_{atm} = 98,000 \, Pa$, $NPSH_R = 3.5 \, m$, $L = 5 \, m$, $D = 0.1 \, m$, $f = 0.02$, $K_{valve} = 1.5$, $T = 20^\circ C$

For water at $20^\circ C$: $P_v = 2340 \, Pa$, so $H_{vapor} = 2340 / (1000 \times 9.81) = 0.238 \, m$. $\rho = 998 \, kg/m^3$.

### Step 1: Assume a Flow Rate

For NPSH calculations, assume a design flow $Q = 0.025 \, m^3/s$ (typical for a 100 mm suction pipe).

$$V_{pipe} = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.025}{\frac{\pi}{4}(0.1)^2} = 3.183 \, m/s$$

*Computing the suction pipe velocity from the volumetric flow rate.*

### Step 2: Friction Head Loss in Suction Line

Using Darcy–Weisbach with equivalent length from the valve:

$$L_{eq,\,valve} = \frac{K \cdot D}{f} = \frac{1.5 \times 0.1}{0.02} = 7.5 \, m$$

$$h_f = f \cdot \frac{L + L_{eq}}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g} = 0.02 \times \frac{5 + 7.5}{0.1} \times \frac{3.183^2}{2 \times 9.81}$$

$$h_f = 0.02 \times 125 \times \frac{10.13}{19.62} = 0.02 \times 125 \times 0.5163 = 1.291 \, m$$

*Computing total head loss including the foot valve's equivalent length.*

### Step 3: Atmospheric and Vapor Pressure Heads

$$H_{atm} = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} = \frac{98{,}000}{998 \times 9.81} = 10.01 \, m$$

$$H_{vapor} = \frac{P_v}{\rho g} = \frac{2340}{998 \times 9.81} = 0.239 \, m$$

*Converting pressures to head form for use in the NPSH equation.*

### Step 4: Apply NPSH Criterion for Maximum Lift

For incipient cavitation: $NPSH_A = NPSH_R$

$$H_{atm} + H_s - H_{vapor} - h_f = NPSH_R$$

$$H_s = NPSH_R - H_{atm} + H_{vapor} + h_f$$

$$H_s = 3.5 - 10.01 + 0.239 + 1.291 = -4.98 \, m$$

### Step 5: Maximum Suction Lift

Since $H_s$ is negative, the suction is a **lift** (water below pump). The maximum lift:

$$\boxed{H_{lift,\,max} = 4.98 \, m}$$

*For a practical design with a safety margin, the maximum permissible lift is typically limited to ~4.0 m.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical check:** The theoretical maximum lift at sea level with cold water and no friction is $H_{atm} - H_{vapor} \approx 10.3 \, m$. Our calculation gives a more realistic value of 4.98 m because of friction losses and the need to maintain margin above NPSH required.

## 4.1 Concept Map — Cavitation Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Liquid Flow into Low Pressure Region] --> B[Local Static Pressure Drops]
    B --> C{P_local vs P_vapor}
    C -->|P_local greater than P_vapor| D[No Cavitation - Normal Flow]
    C -->|P_local equal to P_vapor| E[Incipient Cavitation - First Bubbles]
    C -->|P_local less than P_vapor| F[Developed Cavitation - Vapor Cavities Grow]
    F --> G[Convective Transport by Flow]
    G --> H[Arrival in High Pressure Region]
    H --> I[Violent Bubble Collapse / Implosion]
    I --> J[Engineering Damage - Erosion, Noise, Vibration]
    I --> K[Performance Loss - Head and Efficiency Drop]

    subgraph Mitigation
        L[Reduce Suction Lift]
        M[Increase Pipe Diameter to Reduce Velocity]
        N[Lower Fluid Temperature]
        O[Use Special Cavitation Resistant Materials]
        P[Ensure NPSH available greater than NPSH required]
    end

    J --> Mitigation
    K --> Mitigation
```

## 4.5 Comparative Table — Cavitation in Turbines vs Pumps

| Aspect | Cavitation in Turbines | Cavitation in Pumps |
|---|---|---|
| **Location of Bubble Formation** | Runner blade suction side, near exit | Impeller eye / suction side of blades |
| **Location of Damage** | Runner blade trailing edge | Impeller discharge side / casing |
| **Driving Cause** | High-velocity water + low pressure at runner exit | Insufficient suction pressure / excessive lift |
| **Criterion** | $\sigma_T \geq \sigma_{T,\,critical}$ | $NPSH_A > NPSH_R$ |
| **Design Fix** | Lower runner setting above tailwater | Increase flooded suction, larger pipe |
| **Thoma Number Form** | $\sigma_T = (NPSH_{runner})/H$ | $\sigma_P = NPSH_A/H$ |
| **Key Variable** | Tailwater elevation | Suction tank elevation |

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are typical Part A questions for KTU 2024 ESE. Each is worth **3 marks** and tests definitions, conceptual understanding, and direct recall. Model answers are concise and examiner-ready.

---

### Question A1: Define the term "Cavitation" in fluid flow. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Cavitation is the phenomenon of formation, growth, and subsequent collapse of vapor-filled cavities (or bubbles) within a flowing liquid, occurring when the local static pressure of the liquid falls to or below the saturated vapor pressure of the liquid at the prevailing temperature.

**Mathematical Criterion (1 mark):** Cavitation onset occurs when:

$$P_{local} \leq P_{vapor}$$

where $P_{local}$ is the local static pressure in the flow field and $P_{vapor}$ is the vapor pressure of the liquid at the operating temperature.

---

### Question A2: State and explain the significance of Thoma's cavitation number. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement (1 mark):** Thoma's cavitation number, $\sigma_T$, is a dimensionless parameter that compares the available pressure margin above vapor pressure to the dynamic pressure of the flow. For turbines:

$$\sigma_T = \frac{NPSH_{available}}{H_{net}}$$

**Significance (2 marks):**
- It is a **machine-specific constant** that characterizes the cavitation resistance of a given hydraulic machine.
- The operating value of $\sigma_T$ must be **greater than or equal to a critical value** $\sigma_{T,\,c}$ (supplied by the manufacturer) to avoid cavitation.
- A small $\sigma_T$ indicates that the flow has a high dynamic pressure (high velocity) relative to the static pressure margin — a condition conducive to cavitation.
- A large $\sigma_T$ indicates a generous static pressure margin — cavitation is suppressed.

**Design Criterion:** $\sigma_T \geq \sigma_{T,\,c}$ for cavitation-free operation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE Part B questions carry **14 marks each** with sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Below are **TWO alternative questions (A and B)** — exactly one will appear as the choice. Both sub-parts are fully solved.

---

### 📘 Question A: Comprehensive Cavitation Number Analysis [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Derive the expression for cavitation number $\sigma$ starting from Bernoulli's equation along a streamline. Clearly state the assumptions made.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** A Kaplan turbine operates under a net head of 25 m. The atmospheric pressure is 10.3 m of water absolute, the vapor pressure is 0.24 m of water absolute, and the turbine is set 4.5 m above the tailwater level. If the critical Thoma cavitation number for the runner is $\sigma_{T,c} = 0.32$, determine whether the turbine will operate without cavitation.

---

### ✅ Complete Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Cavitation Number

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. Steady, incompressible, inviscid flow
2. Flow along a streamline (no rotation between streamlines)
3. Local acceleration is negligible (steady flow)
4. No heat transfer or shaft work between reference points
5. Elevation difference between reference and cavity-formation point is negligible (or specified)

**Derivation (5 marks):**

**Step 1:** Apply Bernoulli's equation between the reference point (0) and the point of minimum pressure (*):

$$\frac{P_0}{\rho g} + \frac{V_0^2}{2g} + z_0 = \frac{P_*}{\rho g} + \frac{V_*^2}{2g} + z_*$$

*This equates the total mechanical energy per unit weight at the two points for an ideal flow.*

**Step 2:** Solve for the minimum pressure:

$$P_* = P_0 + \frac{\rho}{2}(V_0^2 - V_*^2) + \rho g (z_0 - z_*)$$

*Algebraic rearrangement to isolate the local minimum pressure.*

**Step 3:** At incipient cavitation, $P_* = P_{vapor}$. Substituting and rearranging:

$$P_0 - P_{vapor} = \frac{\rho}{2}(V_*^2 - V_0^2) - \rho g (z_0 - z_*)$$

*The left side is the pressure margin available to suppress cavitation; the right side represents the local pressure drop.*

**Step 4:** For a horizontal flow with velocity coefficient $C_V = V_*/V_0$ (and $z_0 \approx z_*$):

$$P_0 - P_{vapor} = \frac{\rho V_0^2}{2}(C_V^2 - 1)$$

*Expresses the pressure margin in terms of dynamic pressure and a geometry-dependent velocity coefficient.*

**Step 5:** Dividing both sides by the dynamic pressure of the reference flow:

$$\sigma = \frac{P_0 - P_{vapor}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_0^2} = C_V^2 - 1$$

*This is the dimensionless cavitation number — the ratio of pressure margin to dynamic pressure.*

---

#### Part (b) — Cavitation Check for Kaplan Turbine

**Given Data:**
- Net head $H = 25 \, m$
- Atmospheric pressure head $H_{atm} = 10.3 \, m$ (water, absolute)
- Vapor pressure head $H_v = 0.24 \, m$
- Setting above tailwater: $H_s = -4.5 \, m$ (negative because turbine is above tailwater)
- Critical Thoma number $\sigma_{T,c} = 0.32$

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute the operating Thoma cavitation number.

The Thoma number for a turbine is:

$$\sigma_T = \frac{H_{atm} - H_v - H_s}{H}$$

*Note: With $H_s$ taken as the static suction head where positive is below the turbine, when the turbine is above tailwater, $H_s$ enters with a negative sign in the NPSH-A expression.*

$$\sigma_T = \frac{10.3 - 0.24 - (-4.5)}{25}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Calculate numerator:

$$\sigma_T = \frac{10.3 - 0.24 + 4.5}{25} = \frac{14.56}{25}$$

$$\sigma_T = 0.5824$$

*Numerator is the net positive suction head available, expressed in metres; the denominator is the turbine's net head.*

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compare with critical value:

$$\sigma_T = 0.582 \quad \text{vs} \quad \sigma_{T,c} = 0.32$$

Since $\sigma_T = 0.582 > \sigma_{T,c} = 0.32$, the operating cavitation number exceeds the critical value.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Conclusion:

$$\boxed{\sigma_T > \sigma_{T,c} \implies \text{Turbine operates WITHOUT cavitation.}}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Safety margin:

$$\text{Margin} = \frac{\sigma_T - \sigma_{T,c}}{\sigma_{T,c}} \times 100\% = \frac{0.582 - 0.32}{0.32} \times 100\% = 81.9\%$$

*A 81.9% safety margin over the critical value indicates a robust cavitation-free design.*

---

### 📘 Question B: NPSH-Based Pump Cavitation Analysis [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Define Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH). Derive the expression for NPSH available for a pump drawing water from an open sump, clearly listing all assumptions.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** A centrifugal pump with $NPSH_R = 4.2 \, m$ is to be installed 3.5 m above a water reservoir. The suction pipe is 8 m long with 100 mm diameter, friction factor $f = 0.025$, and includes an entry loss with $K = 0.5$. The water temperature is $25^\circ C$ and the local atmospheric pressure is 100 kPa absolute. Determine the maximum allowable flow rate to avoid cavitation.

---

### ✅ Complete Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Definition and Derivation of NPSH Available

**Definition (2 marks):**

**NPSH (Net Positive Suction Head)** is the total head available at the pump suction flange, measured in metres of liquid, **in excess of** the vapor pressure head of the liquid at the operating temperature.

- **NPSH Available ($NPSH_A$):** The actual head available at the pump suction, determined by the system (tank elevation, pipe losses, etc.).
- **NPSH Required ($NPSH_R$):** The minimum head the pump needs at its suction to operate without cavitation, determined by the pump design and supplied by the manufacturer.

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Steady, incompressible flow
2. Open sump exposed to atmosphere
3. Velocity at sump surface ≈ 0
4. Pump suction velocity = velocity in suction pipe
5. Friction head loss is concentrated and computed from Darcy–Weisbach

**Derivation (4 marks):**

**Step 1:** Energy equation from sump surface (1) to pump suction (2):

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_f$$

*Steady-flow energy equation between the sump surface and the pump suction flange.*

**Step 2:** Boundary conditions: $P_1 = P_{atm}$, $V_1 \approx 0$, $z_1 = 0$ (datum), $P_2 = P_{suct}$, $V_2 = V_{pipe}$, $z_2 = +H_s$ (pump is above sump):

$$\frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} = \frac{P_{suct}}{\rho g} + \frac{V_{pipe}^2}{2g} + H_s + h_f$$

*Substituting the boundary conditions; $H_s$ is positive because the pump is above the sump.*

**Step 3:** Solve for $P_{suct}/\rho g$:

$$\frac{P_{suct}}{\rho g} = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} - H_s - \frac{V_{pipe}^2}{2g} - h_f$$

*Algebraic rearrangement to isolate the suction pressure head.*

**Step 4:** Subtract vapor pressure head to obtain NPSH available:

$$NPSH_A = \frac{P_{suct} - P_{vapor}}{\rho g}$$

$$\boxed{NPSH_A = H_{atm} - H_s - H_{vapor} - \frac{V_{pipe}^2}{2g} - h_f}$$

*This is the head at the suction flange, expressed in metres of liquid, in excess of the vapor pressure head.*

---

#### Part (b) — Maximum Flow Rate to Avoid Cavitation

**Given Data:**
- $NPSH_R = 4.2 \, m$
- Pump elevation above sump: $H_s = +3.5 \, m$ (positive — pump is above)
- $L = 8 \, m$, $D = 0.1 \, m$, $f = 0.025$, $K_{entry} = 0.5$
- $T = 25^\circ C$: $P_v = 3169 \, Pa$, $H_v = 3169 / (997 \times 9.81) = 0.324 \, m$, $\rho = 997 \, kg/m^3$
- $P_{atm} = 100,000 \, Pa$, so $H_{atm} = 100,000 / (997 \times 9.81) = 10.22 \, m$

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Apply cavitation criterion for limiting (incipient) condition:

$$NPSH_A = NPSH_R$$

$$H_{atm} - H_s - H_v - \frac{V^2}{2g} - h_f = NPSH_R$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Express the friction head loss in terms of V:

$$h_f = \left(f \cdot \frac{L}{D} + K_{entry}\right) \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g} = \left(0.025 \times \frac{8}{0.1} + 0.5\right) \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

$$h_f = (2.0 + 0.5) \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g} = 2.5 \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

*Computing the total loss coefficient: pipe friction plus entry loss.*

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Substitute into the NPSH equation:

$$10.22 - 3.5 - 0.324 - \frac{V^2}{2g} - 2.5 \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g} = 4.2$$

$$6.396 - 3.5 \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g} = 4.2$$

*Combining the constant terms and the velocity-dependent terms separately.*

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Solve for $V^2/(2g)$:

$$3.5 \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g} = 6.396 - 4.2 = 2.196$$

$$\frac{V^2}{2g} = 0.6274 \, m$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Solve for V:

$$V^2 = 2 \times 9.81 \times 0.6274 = 12.31$$

$$V = 3.509 \, m/s$$

*This is the maximum velocity in the suction pipe to avoid cavitation.*

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Calculate maximum flow rate:

$$Q_{max} = V \cdot A = 3.509 \times \frac{\pi}{4}(0.1)^2 = 3.509 \times 0.007854$$

$$\boxed{Q_{max} = 0.02756 \, m^3/s \approx 27.6 \, L/s}$$

*This is the maximum allowable flow rate. For safe design, a 10–20% margin is recommended, giving a recommended design flow of ~23 L/s.*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Cavitation Problems:**
> 1. **Sign convention for $H_s$:** Students often forget whether $H_s$ is positive (pump above) or negative (pump below). For pumps drawing from a lower sump, $H_s$ is **positive** in the suction lift expression. For turbines, the convention is different — $H_s$ is the static suction head above tailwater and enters the NPSH expression with opposite sign.
> 2. **Atmospheric pressure must be absolute, not gauge.** A common error is using $P_{atm} = 101.3 \, kPa$ even when the problem specifies 98 kPa or another value. Always use the value given in the problem.
> 3. **Vapor pressure depends on temperature.** Forgetting to look up $P_v$ at the stated temperature is a classic 1-mark loss.
> 4. **NPSH must be in head units (metres of liquid), not pressure units (Pa).** Convert using $H = P/(\rho g)$.
> 5. **Friction head loss must be computed using $V^2/(2g)$ in metres, not $V^2/2$ in Joules/kg.** Unit confusion is a frequent mark-loser.
> 6. **The "no cavitation" condition is $NPSH_A > NPSH_R$, not $\sigma > 1$ or some other arbitrary value.** The critical Thoma number is machine-specific.
> 7. **In Problem 1 (Turbine Thoma), $H_s$ is the static suction head from tailwater to turbine, and it enters with a negative sign in the formula (since higher $H_s$ reduces margin).** In the Kaplan example above, we used $H_s = -4.5$ because the turbine is **above** the tailwater.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this section as a last-day revision summary.** Cover every point before entering the exam hall.

### 🔹 Conceptual Definitions
- [ ] **Cavitation:** Formation, growth, and collapse of vapor cavities in a flowing liquid when local pressure drops below vapor pressure.
- [ ] **Cavitation onset:** $P_{local} \leq P_{vapor}$
- [ ] **Vapor pressure ($P_v$):** Pressure at which liquid boils at given temperature. Function of fluid and temperature ONLY, not flow.
- [ ] **Cavitation number ($\sigma$):** Dimensionless ratio of pressure margin above vapor pressure to dynamic pressure.
- [ ] **NPSH:** Net Positive Suction Head — suction head in metres of liquid, above vapor pressure head.

### 🔹 Key Formulas (Must Memorize)
- [ ] General cavitation number: $\sigma = (P_{ref} - P_v)/(\frac{1}{2}\rho V_{ref}^2)$
- [ ] Thoma number (turbines): $\sigma_T = NPSH_A / H$
- [ ] Pump cavitation number: $\sigma_P = NPSH_A / H$
- [ ] NPSH available (open sump, pump above): $NPSH_A = H_{atm} - H_s - H_v - V^2/(2g) - h_f$
- [ ] NPSH available (open sump, pump below/flooded): $NPSH_A = H_{atm} + H_s - H_v - h_f$ (with $H_s$ positive)
- [ ] Maximum suction lift: $H_{lift} = H_{atm} - H_v - NPSH_R - h_f$
- [ ] Cavitation criterion: $\sigma \geq \sigma_c$ (turbines) or $NPSH_A \geq NPSH_R$ (pumps)

### 🔹 Critical Numerical Values
- [ ] $P_v$ for water at $20^\circ C \approx 2.34 \, kPa$ ($H_v \approx 0.24 \, m$)
- [ ] $P_v$ for water at $25^\circ C \approx 3.17 \, kPa$ ($H_v \approx 0.32 \, m$)
- [ ] $P_v$ for water at $100^\circ C = 101.325 \, kPa$ (atmospheric — water boils)
- [ ] Atmospheric pressure head at sea level: $H_{atm} \approx 10.3 \, m$ of water
- [ ] Theoretical max suction lift at sea level with cold water: $\approx 10.3 \, m$ (actual: 4–7 m)

### 🔹 Effects of Cavitation (Memorize for 3-mark Questions)
- [ ] **Erosion/Pitting** of metal surfaces (esp. blade trailing edges)
- [ ] **Vibration** and **noise** (broadband, characteristic crackling)
- [ ] **Head/Performance drop** (machine cannot develop rated head)
- [ ] **Efficiency reduction**
- [ ] **Bearing/seal failure** in pumps

### 🔹 Methods to Prevent Cavitation
- [ ] **Increase suction pressure** (flooded suction / submerge pump)
- [ ] **Reduce suction lift** (lower pump elevation)
- [ ] **Increase suction pipe diameter** (reduce velocity and friction loss)
- [ ] **Reduce fluid temperature** (lower vapor pressure)
- [ ] **Select a different pump** with lower NPSH required
- [ ] **Use cavitation-resistant materials** (e.g., stainless steel, special coatings)
- [ ] **Polished surface finish** on blades (reduces nucleation sites)
- [ ] **Air injection** at the suction (suppresses vapor pressure drop — used in ship propellers)

### 🔹 Sign Convention Mastery
- [ ] **Pump above sump:** $H_s$ is **positive** in the suction-lift NPSH formula.
- [ ] **Pump below sump (flooded):** $H_s$ is **positive** but added in the formula.
- [ ] **Turbine above tailwater:** $H_s$ is **negative** in the Thoma formula (reduces NPSH).
- [ ] **Turbine below tailwater (rare):** $H_s$ is **positive** in the Thoma formula (increases NPSH).

### 🔹 Common Exam Mistakes to Avoid
- [ ] Mixing absolute and gauge pressures
- [ ] Forgetting to look up $P_v$ at the given temperature
- [ ] Using $H_s$ with the wrong sign
- [ ] Confusing NPSH-A with NPSH-R
- [ ] Forgetting the kinetic energy term $V^2/(2g)$ in suction line
- [ ] Not converting Pa → metres of liquid for the NPSH equation
- [ ] Comparing $\sigma$ with 1 instead of the machine-specific $\sigma_c$

### 🔹 Exam Strategy Tips
- [ ] In 14-mark questions, **always show the derivation in steps** — partial credit is awarded for each correct logical step.
- [ ] **Define every variable** in the first line of your solution.
- [ ] **Box your final answer** for clarity.
- [ ] **Include units** at every numerical step.
- [ ] For 7-mark "cavitation number" derivations, write all 5 assumptions explicitly.

---

**END OF NOTES — CAVITATION (MODULE 2, PECET413)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Growth of Boundary Layer over a Flat Plate and Definition of Boundary Layer Thickness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET413 - MECHANICS OF FLUID FLOW
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Growth of Boundary Layer over a Flat Plate and Definition of Boundary Layer Thickness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Prandtl's Boundary Layer Theorem (1904):** When a real (viscous) fluid flows past a solid boundary, the influence of viscosity is confined to a very thin region adjacent to the boundary. Outside this thin region, the fluid behaves essentially as an **inviscid (non-viscous) fluid**. This thin region of retarded flow is called the **Boundary Layer**.

For flow over a **stationary flat plate** held parallel to a uniform free-stream of velocity $U_\infty$, the boundary layer originates at the **leading edge** of the plate and grows progressively in the downstream direction due to the retarding shear (viscous) effect of the plate surface.

## 1.2 Definition of Boundary Layer Thickness ($\delta$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Layer Thickness ($\delta$):** It is defined as the distance from the solid surface (measured normal to the surface) to the point in the flow where the **velocity becomes 99% of the free-stream velocity** $U_\infty$.

Mathematically, the condition is expressed as:

$$u(y = \delta) = 0.99 \, U_\infty$$

Where:
- $u$ = fluid velocity component parallel to the plate at height $y$ from the surface
