for turbulent, $\delta^{*}/\delta \approx 1/8$ (1/7 power law). **Momentum Thickness (θ) — 2 marks:** - Distance representing the loss of momentum flux in the boundary layer. - Formula: $\theta = \int_{0}^{\delta}\dfrac{u}{U}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{u}{U}\right)dy$ - It is **always smaller** than $\delta^{*}$, because the integrand $\eta(1-\eta)$ is smaller than $(1-\eta)$ for $0 < \eta < 1$. - Physical meaning: thickness of an inviscid layer carrying the same momentum deficit. **Energy Thickness (δ\*\*) — 2 marks:** - Distance representing the loss of kinetic energy flux. - Formula: $\delta^{**} = \int_{0}^{\delta}\dfrac{u}{U}\!\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{u}{U}\right)^{2}\right]dy$ - Always the **smallest** of the three thicknesses. **Comparison Table (1 mark):** | Property | Laminar | Turbulent | |----------|---------|-----------| | $\delta^{*}/\delta$ | $\approx 0.33$ | $\approx 0.125$ | | $\theta/\delta$ | $\approx 0.083$ | $\approx 0.097$ | | $\delta^{**}/\delta$ | $\approx 0.067$ | $\approx 0.175$ | | $H = \delta^{*}/\theta$ | $\approx 2.0 - 2.6$ | $\approx 1.3 - 1.4$ | --- **Part (b) — 1/7-th Power Law Calculations (7 marks)** **Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Setup. With $u/U = (y/\delta)^{1/7} = \eta^{1/7}$: **Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Momentum thickness integral: $$\frac{\theta}{\delta} = \int_{0}^{1}(\eta^{1/7} - \eta^{2/7})\,d\eta = \frac{7}{8} - \frac{7}{9} = \frac{7}{72} \approx 0.0972$$ **Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Energy thickness integral: $$\frac{\delta^{**}}{\delta} = \int_{0}^{1}(\eta^{1/7} - \eta^{3/7})\,d\eta = \frac{7}{8} - \frac{7}{10} = \frac{7}{40} = 0.175$$ **Step 4 (1.5 marks):** For a flat plate, $\delta = 0.376\,x\,\text{Re}_{x}^{-1/5}$. Differentiating: $$\frac{d\theta}{dx} = \frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{7}{72}\cdot 0.376\,x\,\text{Re}_{x}^{-1/5}\right) = \frac{7\cdot 0.376}{72}\cdot\text{Re}_{x}^{-1/5}\left(1 - \frac{1}{5}\right) = \frac{7\cdot 0.376\cdot 4}{72\cdot 5}\text{Re}_{x}^{-1/5}$$ $$= \frac{7 \cdot 1.504}{360}\text{Re}_{x}^{-1/5} = 0.0292\,\text{Re}_{x}^{-1/5}$$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Skin friction coefficient: $$C_{f} = \frac{2\tau_{w}}{\rho U^{2}} = \frac{2\rho U^{2}\,d\theta/dx}{\rho U^{2}} = 2\cdot 0.0292\,\text{Re}_{x}^{-1/5} = 0.0584\,\text{Re}_{x}^{-1/5}$$ This matches the KTU-recommended value $C_{f} = 0.0576\,\text{Re}_{x}^{-1/5}$ within 1.4% — the small discrepancy arises from rounding $\delta/\delta$ coefficients. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cause Mark Deductions in KTU Exams:** > 1. **Forgetting the dimensionless form** — students write $\theta = \int u(U-u)dy$ without dividing by $U$ or $U^{2}$. This is wrong! The correct form is $\theta = \int_{0}^{\delta}\dfrac{u}{U}\!\left(1-\dfrac{u}{U}\right)dy$ so that the units of $\theta$ are length, not momentum. > 2. **Mixing up displacement and momentum thickness integrals** — these are easy to confuse under exam pressure. Memorize the *integrand* patterns: > - Displacement: $(1 - \eta)$ > - Momentum: $\eta(1 - \eta)$ > - Energy: $\eta(1 - \eta^{2})$ > 3. **Skipping the Bernoulli step** in the Von Kármán derivation. The $U\,dU/dx = -(1/\rho)dp/dx$ link is the **bridge** between momentum thickness and pressure gradient — losing this loses 1–2 marks. > 4. **Ignoring the $(2 + H)$ factor** — this is the most-missed part. Many students write just $\dfrac{\tau_{w}}{\rho U^{2}} = \dfrac{d\theta}{dx}$, which is only valid for **flat plates**. > 5. **Sign errors in $dU/dx$** — for adverse pressure gradients, $dU/dx < 0$, so the second term is *negative* and $\tau_{w}$ becomes zero at separation. > 6. **Unit inconsistency** — always express $\theta$ in metres, $\tau_{w}$ in Pa, $C_{f}$ is dimensionless. > 7. **In Part B derivations**, examiners award marks for **boundary conditions** and **assumptions** stated at the start. Skipping these can cost 0.5 to 1 mark even if the math is right. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Momentum & Energy Thickness:** **Core Definitions** - ☐ **Displacement thickness** δ\* = $\int_{0}^{\delta}(1-\eta)\,dy$ — *mass flux loss* - ☐ **Momentum thickness** θ = $\int_{0}^{\delta}\eta(1-\eta)\,dy$ — *momentum flux loss* - ☐ **Energy thickness** δ\*\* = $\int_{0}^{\delta}\eta(1-\eta^{2})\,dy$ — *kinetic energy flux loss* **Ordering Rule** - ☐ Always: $\delta^{*} > \theta > \delta^{**}$ (because integrands are ordered: $(1-\eta) > \eta(1-\eta) > \eta(1-\eta^{2})$ for $0 < \eta < 1$) **Governing Equation** - ☐ **Von Kármán:** $\dfrac{\tau_{w}}{\rho U^{2}} = \dfrac{d\theta}{dx} + (2 + H)\dfrac{\theta}{U}\dfrac{dU}{dx}$ - ☐ **Flat plate special case:** $\tau_{w} = \rho U^{2}\,d\theta/dx$ **Shape Factors** - ☐ $H = \delta^{*}/\theta$ — separates **laminar** ($H \approx 2 - 2.6$) from **turbulent** ($H \approx 1.3 - 1.4$) - ☐ $K = \delta^{**}/\theta$ — energy shape factor - ☐ **Separation criterion:** $H \to 3.5$ (laminar) or $H \to 2.5$ (turbulent) **Energy / Momentum Loss Formulas** - ☐ Momentum loss per unit width: $\Delta M = \rho U^{2}\theta$ - ☐ Energy loss per unit width: $\Delta E = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho U^{3}\delta^{**}$ **Common Velocity Profiles — Standard Results** - ☐ **Linear** $u/U = \eta$: $\delta^{*}/\delta = 1/2$, $\theta/\delta = 1/6$, $\delta^{**}/\delta = 1/4$ - ☐ **Parabolic** $u/U = 2\eta - \eta^{2}$ (Pohlhausen): $\delta^{*}/\delta = 1/3$, $\theta/\delta = 1/12$ - ☐ **1/7 Power Law** $u/U = \eta^{1/7}$: $\delta^{*}/\delta = 1/8$, $\theta/\delta = 7/72$, $\delta^{**}/\delta = 7/40$ - ☐ **Sinusoidal** $u/U = \sin(\pi\eta/2)$: $\delta^{*}/\delta = 1 - 2/\pi$, $\theta/\delta = (4-\pi)/(2\pi)$ **Bernoulli Connection** - ☐ Outside the BL: $U\,dU/dx = -(1/\rho)\,dp/dx$ — links momentum equation to pressure gradient **Engineering Significance** - ☐ Momentum thickness at trailing edge = **drag per unit span / $\rho U^{2}$** - ☐ Energy thickness = **dissipation coefficient** in turbomachinery loss models - ☐ Shape factor $H$ is the **single most useful diagnostic** for boundary-layer state **Assumptions to Always State in Derivations** - ☐ Steady, 2D, incompressible - ☐ Thin boundary layer: $\delta \ll L$ - ☐ $u = 0$ at $y = 0$ (no-slip) - ☐ $u \to U$ as $y \to \delta$ - ☐ $\partial p / \partial y = 0$ across boundary layer > [!TIP] > **Final Exam Tip:** When asked *"Define momentum thickness"* or *"Define energy thickness"* in 3-mark questions, ALWAYS include (i) the verbal definition, (ii) the formula with integrand, and (iii) the physical loss equation. This 3-part answer guarantees full marks. ---

topics.title: Laminar and Turbulent Boundary Layers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET413 - MECHANICS OF FLUID FLOW
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Laminar and Turbulent Boundary Layers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Boundary Layer Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Prandtl's Boundary Layer Hypothesis (1904):** When a real fluid flows past a solid boundary, the effects of fluid viscosity are confined to a thin region adjacent to the surface called the **Boundary Layer**. Outside this thin layer, the fluid behaves as an inviscid (frictionless) flow.

The **boundary layer** is the thin fluid layer adjacent to a solid surface in which the fluid velocity changes from **zero** at the surface (due to the no-slip condition) to approximately **99% of the free-stream velocity** ($U_\infty$) at the outer edge.

### Formal Mathematical Definition

For a flat plate aligned with a uniform free-stream of velocity $U_\infty$, the boundary layer thickness $\delta(x)$ at any station $x$ is defined as the value of $y$ where:

$$u(x, y) = 0.99 \, U_\infty$$

where:
- $u(x, y)$ = velocity component in the $x$-direction at location $(x, y)$
- $U_\infty$ = free-stream velocity (m/s)
- $\delta(x)$ = boundary layer thickness (m)

## 1.2 Types of Boundary Layers

> [!NOTE]
> **Laminar Boundary Layer:** The layer in which fluid particles move in smooth, parallel layers (laminae) with no macroscopic mixing between them. The flow is orderly and predictable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Turbulent Boundary Layer:** The layer in which fluid particles undergo intense chaotic mixing, with rapid fluctuations in velocity, pressure, and eddying motion. The mean flow is unidirectional but superimposed with random fluctuations.

### Transition Criteria

The transition from laminar to turbulent boundary layer is governed by the **local Reynolds number**:

$$Re_x = \frac{U_\infty \, x}{\nu}$$

The critical Reynolds number for transition over a flat plate is:

$$Re_{x, \text{crit}} \approx 5 \times 10^5 \quad \text{(standard value for KTU problems)}$$

For $Re_x < Re_{x, \text{crit}}$: **Laminar** boundary layer
For $Re_x > Re_{x, \text{crit}}$: **Turbulent** boundary layer

## 1.3 Real-World Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: The Boat in a River** — Imagine a stationary boat in a flowing river. Right next to the boat's hull, water is essentially stuck to the surface (zero velocity). As you move your hand outward into the river, the water speed gradually increases until it matches the river's main current. That thin transition region — where the water "realizes" it has to slow down because of the boat — is the boundary layer. If the river is slow, the water near the hull slides smoothly past (laminar). If the river is fast, you'll see swirls, eddies, and chaotic mixing right near the hull (turbulent).

### Engineering Significance

| Application | Boundary Layer Role |
|-------------|---------------------|
| Aircraft wings | Determines lift and drag |
| Ship hulls | Determines frictional resistance |
| Turbine blades | Affects efficiency and heat transfer |
| Pipeline flows | Controls pressure drop |
| Wind turbine blades | Governs power extraction efficiency |

### Important Physical Constants

- **Standard kinematic viscosity of air:** $\nu = 1.46 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ at 15°C
- **Standard kinematic viscosity of water:** $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ at 20°C
- **Density of air:** $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at sea level

## 1.4 Laminar vs. Turbulent Boundary Layer — A Snapshot

| Feature | Laminar BL | Turbulent BL |
|---------|------------|--------------|
| Velocity profile | Smooth, parabolic-like | Flatter, fuller profile |
| Thickness | Thinner | Thicker (≈5–10x laminar) |
| Wall shear stress $\tau_w$ | Lower | Higher (4–5 times) |
| Mixing | Negligible | Intense |
| Momentum transfer | Viscous only | Viscous + turbulent eddies |
| Energy dissipation | Less | More |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Boundary Layer Thickness Definitions

### (a) Boundary Layer Thickness ($\delta$)

The nominal distance from the wall to the point where $u = 0.99\,U_\infty$.

$$\delta = y \quad \text{when} \quad u = 0.99\,U_\infty$$

### (b) Displacement Thickness ($\delta^*$)

The **displacement thickness** represents the distance by which the external streamlines are shifted away from the boundary due to the reduction in flow rate within the boundary layer.

$$\delta^* = \int_0^{\delta} \left(1 - \frac{u}{U_\infty}\right) dy$$

**Physical meaning:** If we could remove the boundary layer and replace it with an inviscid flow, the streamlines would shift outward by $\delta^*$.

### (c) Momentum Thickness ($\theta$)

The **momentum thickness** represents the loss of momentum flux per unit width due to the formation of the boundary layer.

$$\theta = \int_0^{\delta} \frac{u}{U_\infty} \left(1 - \frac{u}{U_\infty}\right) dy$$

**Physical meaning:** It quantifies the momentum deficit in the boundary layer.

### (d) Energy Thickness ($\delta^{**}$ or $\theta_E$)

Represents the loss of kinetic energy flux within the boundary layer.

$$\delta^{**} = \int_0^{\delta} \frac{u}{U_\infty} \left(1 - \left(\frac{u}{U_\infty}\right)^2\right) dy$$

## 2.2 The Prandtl Boundary Layer Equations

For steady, two-dimensional, incompressible flow with constant properties, the Navier-Stokes equations reduce to the **Prandtl boundary layer equations**:

$$u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2}$$

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$$

**Boundary Conditions:**

$$y = 0: \quad u = 0, \quad v = 0$$
$$y = \delta: \quad u = U_\infty, \quad v = \text{finite}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** Outside the boundary layer ($y > \delta$), viscous terms are negligible, and the flow obeys Euler's equation. Inside the layer, viscosity is dominant and the streamwise pressure gradient equals the free-stream value: $\partial p/\partial x = \partial p_\infty/\partial x$.

## 2.3 The von Kármán Momentum Integral Equation

This is the **most important equation in boundary layer analysis** for KTU exams:

$$\frac{\tau_w}{\rho U_\infty^2} = \frac{d\theta}{dx}$$

For flow with a pressure gradient, the more general form is:

$$\frac{\tau_w}{\rho U_\infty^2} = \frac{d\theta}{dx} + (2 + \Lambda) \frac{\theta}{U_\infty} \frac{dU_\infty}{dx}$$

where $\Lambda = \frac{\delta^*}{\theta}$ is the boundary layer shape factor.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description / Validity |
|---|---------|------------------------|
| 1 | $Re_x = \frac{U_\infty x}{\nu}$ | Local Reynolds number |
| 2 | $\delta = \frac{5.0\,x}{\sqrt{Re_x}}$ | Laminar BL thickness (Blasius) |
| 3 | $\delta = \frac{0.37\,x}{Re_x^{1/5}}$ | Turbulent BL thickness (1/7 power law) |
| 4 | $\delta^* = \frac{1.721\,x}{\sqrt{Re_x}}$ | Displacement thickness (laminar) |
| 5 | $\delta^* = \frac{\delta}{8}$ | Displacement thickness (turbulent, 1/7 power law) |
| 6 | $\theta = \frac{0.664\,x}{\sqrt{Re_x}}$ | Momentum thickness (laminar) |
| 7 | $\theta = \frac{7}{72}\,\delta$ | Momentum thickness (turbulent, 1/7) |
| 8 | $C_f = \frac{0.664}{\sqrt{Re_L}}$ | Local skin friction coefficient (laminar) |
| 9 | $C_f = \frac{0.0576}{Re_x^{1/5}}$ | Local skin friction coefficient (turbulent) |
| 10 | $\bar{C}_D = \frac{1.328}{\sqrt{Re_L}}$ | Average drag coefficient (laminar) |
| 11 | $\bar{C}_D = \frac{0.074}{Re_L^{1/5}}$ | Average drag coefficient (turbulent) |
| 12 | $F_D = \frac{1}{2}\rho U_\infty^2 A \bar{C}_D$ | Drag force on flat plate |
| 13 | $H = \frac{\delta^*}{\theta}$ | Shape factor (Laminar $\approx 2.59$, Turbulent $\approx 1.3$) |
| 14 | $\tau_w = 0.332\,\rho U_\infty^2 \, Re_x^{-1/2}$ | Wall shear stress (laminar) |
| 15 | $\tau_w = 0.0225\,\rho U_\infty^2 \, Re_x^{-1/5}$ | Wall shear stress (turbulent) |

> [!TIP]
> **Memorization Strategy for KTU:** Commit equations 2, 6, 8, 10, 11 to memory — these five formulas solve ~80% of flat plate boundary layer problems.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Boundary Layer Theory

- **Aerospace:** Predicting drag on aircraft wings; designing laminar-flow airfoils to delay transition.
- **Marine:** Estimating frictional resistance of ship hulls for fuel efficiency.
- **Civil/Hydraulic:** Computing head loss in large conduits and open channels.
- **Turbomachinery:** Blade design for compressors and turbines; understanding stall and separation.
- **Heat Transfer:** Convective heat transfer coefficients depend directly on boundary layer behavior.
- **Wind Engineering:** Designing tall buildings and bridges against wind-induced vibrations.

## 2.6 Logical Flow of Boundary Layer Analysis (How & Why)

1. **Identify flow regime:** Compute $Re_x$ and compare with $Re_{\text{crit}}$.
2. **Assume a velocity profile:** Use Blasius (laminar) or 1/7 power law (turbulent).
3. **Compute $\theta$:** Integrate the assumed profile.
4. **Apply momentum integral:** Substitute $\theta$ into von Kármán equation.
5. **Solve for $\delta(x)$:** Obtain boundary layer growth.
6. **Compute $\tau_w$ and $C_f$:** Determine wall friction.
7. **Calculate drag:** Integrate wall shear over the plate area.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: von Kármán Momentum Integral Equation

### Setup

Consider a steady, two-dimensional, incompressible boundary layer flow over a flat plate. Apply the integral form of the momentum equation to a control volume bounded by:

- Bottom: the plate surface from $x$ to $x + dx$
- Top: streamline at $y = \delta$
- Left: vertical line at $x$ (entry)
- Right: vertical line at $x + dx$ (exit)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Write the integral momentum equation in $x$-direction:

$$F_x = \oint \rho u (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = \dot{m}_{out} V_{out} - \dot{m}_{in} V_{in}$$

**Step 2:** Identify the forces acting on the control volume:
- Pressure force on left face: $p_\infty \cdot \delta$
- Pressure force on right face: $\left(p_\infty + \frac{dp_\infty}{dx}dx\right)\delta$
- Pressure on top: $p_\infty \, dx$ (constant pressure outside BL)
- Wall shear stress (resisting): $-\tau_w \, dx$

Net force per unit width:

$$F_x = p_\infty \delta - \left(p_\infty + \frac{dp_\infty}{dx}dx\right)\delta - \tau_w \, dx$$

**Step 3:** Neglecting the $dp_\infty/dx$ term (zero pressure gradient on flat plate):

$$F_x = -\tau_w \, dx$$

**Step 4:** Compute the momentum flux across each face.

- Mass flow entering at $x$ per unit width: $\int_0^\delta \rho u \, dy$
- Mass flow leaving at $x + dx$: $\int_0^\delta \rho u \, dy + d\left(\int_0^\delta \rho u \, dy\right)$

Mass flow added through top (due to streamline displacement):

$$\dot{m}_{top} = \rho U_\infty \frac{d\delta}{dx} dx$$

**Step 5:** Net momentum flux out:

$$\Delta \dot{M} = \int_0^\delta \rho u^2 \, dy + d\left(\int_0^\delta \rho u^2 \, dy\right) - \int_0^\delta \rho u^2 \, dy - \rho U_\infty^2 \frac{d\delta}{dx} dx$$

**Step 6:** Combining with force balance and simplifying:

$$-\tau_w \, dx = d\left(\int_0^\delta \rho u^2 \, dy\right) - \rho U_\infty^2 \, d\delta$$

**Step 7:** Recall that the momentum thickness is defined as:

$$\theta = \int_0^\delta \frac{u}{U_\infty}\left(1 - \frac{u}{U_\infty}\right) dy = \int_0^\delta \frac{u}{U_\infty} dy - \int_0^\delta \frac{u^2}{U_\infty^2} dy$$

Using the continuity equation (mass flow above the boundary layer must equal the deficit below):

$$U_\infty \delta = \int_0^\delta u \, dy$$

**Step 8:** Therefore:

$$\int_0^\delta u \, dy = U_\infty \delta \quad \Rightarrow \quad \int_0^\delta \frac{u^2}{U_\infty^2} dy = \int_0^\delta \frac{u}{U_\infty} dy - \theta = \delta - \theta$$

**Step 9:** Substituting and simplifying the momentum equation:

$$-\tau_w \, dx = \rho d\left[U_\infty^2 \delta - U_\infty^2 \theta\right] - \rho U_\infty^2 d\delta = -\rho U_\infty^2 d\theta$$

**Step 10:** Final result — **The von Kármán Momentum Integral Equation:**

$$\boxed{\tau_w = \rho U_\infty^2 \frac{d\theta}{dx}}$$

Or equivalently:

$$\frac{\tau_w}{\rho U_\infty^2} = \frac{d\theta}{dx} = \frac{C_f}{2}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Laminar Boundary Layer — Blasius Solution (Flat Plate)

### Velocity Profile Assumption (Blasius)

Blasius proposed a similarity solution for laminar flow. Assume:

$$u = U_\infty f'(\eta), \quad \eta = y\sqrt{\frac{U_\infty}{\nu x}}$$

The solution to the Blasius equation $f''' + \frac{1}{2} f f'' = 0$ gives:

$$f''(0) = 0.332$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation of $\delta$ and $C_f$

**Step 1:** Wall shear stress from Blasius:

$$\tau_w = \mu \left.\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right|_{y=0} = \mu U_\infty \sqrt{\frac{U_\infty}{\nu x}} \cdot f''(0)$$

$$\tau_w = 0.332 \, \mu U_\infty \sqrt{\frac{U_\infty}{\nu x}} = 0.332 \, \rho U_\infty^2 \, Re_x^{-1/2}$$

**Step 2:** Wall shear as $\tau_w = \frac{1}{2}\rho U_\infty^2 C_f$:

$$C_f = \frac{2\tau_w}{\rho U_\infty^2} = \frac{0.664}{\sqrt{Re_x}}$$

**Step 3:** Boundary layer thickness from $C_f = 2d\theta/dx$ and $\theta \approx 0.664 x / \sqrt{Re_x}$:

$$\delta \approx \frac{5.0 \, x}{\sqrt{Re_x}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Turbulent Boundary Layer (1/7 Power Law)

### Velocity Profile Assumption

For a turbulent boundary layer, the empirical **1/7th power law** is widely used:

$$\frac{u}{U_\infty} = \left(\frac{y}{\delta}\right)^{1/7}$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Compute the momentum thickness $\theta$:

$$\theta = \int_0^\delta \frac{u}{U_\infty}\left(1 - \frac{u}{U_\infty}\right) dy = \delta \int_0^1 \eta^{1/7}(1 - \eta^{1/7}) d\eta$$

where $\eta = y/\delta$.

**Step 2:** Evaluate the integral:

$$\int_0^1 \eta^{1/7} d\eta = \frac{7}{8}, \quad \int_0^1 \eta^{2/7} d\eta = \frac{7}{9}$$

$$\theta = \delta \left[\frac{7}{8} - \frac{7}{9}\right] = \delta \cdot \frac{63 - 56}{72} = \frac{7\delta}{72}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the von Kármán momentum integral equation. Wall shear for turbulent flow:

$$\tau_w = \mu \left.\frac{du}{dy}\right|_{y=0} + \rho \overline{u'v'}$$

For the 1/7 power law, the wall shear is given by the empirical relation (or by combining viscous + turbulent contributions at the wall):

$$\tau_w = 0.0225 \, \rho U_\infty^2 \, Re_x^{-1/5}$$

**Step 4:** From the momentum integral:

$$\frac{\tau_w}{\rho U_\infty^2} = \frac{d\theta}{dx} = \frac{7}{72} \frac{d\delta}{dx}$$

**Step 5:** Equate and integrate:

$$0.0225 \, U_\infty^2 \left(\frac{\nu}{U_\infty x}\right)^{1/5} = \frac{7}{72} U_\infty^2 \frac{d\delta}{dx}$$

$$0.0225 \, \left(\frac{\nu}{U_\infty}\right)^{1/5} x^{-1/5} = \frac{7}{72} \frac{d\delta}{dx}$$

**Step 6:** Separate and integrate from $0$ to $x$:

$$\delta = 0.0225 \times \frac{72}{7} \times \frac{5}{4} \times \left(\frac{\nu}{U_\infty}\right)^{1/5} x^{4/5}$$

$$\delta = 0.37 \, x \, Re_x^{-1/5}$$

$$\boxed{\delta = \frac{0.37 \, x}{Re_x^{1/5}}}$$

**Step 7:** Average drag coefficient for a plate of length $L$:

$$\bar{C}_D = \frac{2}{\rho U_\infty^2 L} \int_0^L \tau_w \, dx = \frac{0.074}{Re_L^{1/5}}$$

---

## 3.4 Summary Table — Derivations at a Glance

| # | Derivation | Key Result | KTU Marks Weight |
|---|------------|------------|------------------|
| 1 | von Kármán Momentum Integral | $\tau_w = \rho U_\infty^2 \, d\theta/dx$ | 7–10 marks |
| 2 | Blasius (Laminar Flat Plate) | $\delta = 5x/\sqrt{Re_x}$ | 5–7 marks |
| 3 | 1/7 Power Law (Turbulent Flat Plate) | $\delta = 0.37x/Re_x^{1/5}$ | 7–10 marks |
| 4 | Displacement Thickness (Laminar) | $\delta^* = 1.721x/\sqrt{Re_x}$ | 3–5 marks |
| 5 | Drag Force Integration | $F_D = \frac{1}{2}\rho U_\infty^2 A \bar{C}_D$ | 5–7 marks |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Boundary Layer Flow Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inlet([Free Stream U Infinity]) --> outer[Outer Inviscid Region]
    outer --> desc1[Pressure and Velocity follow Euler equations]
    outer --> bl[Boundary Layer Region Thickness Delta]
    bl --> regime{Re x vs Re critical}
    regime -->|Re x less than 5e5| lam[Laminar BL]
    regime -->|Re x greater than 5e5| turb[Turbulent BL]
    lam --> lprof[Blasius Similarity Profile]
    lprof --> lshear[Wall Shear tau w Low]
    turb --> tprof[1 by 7 Power Law Profile]
    tprof --> tshear[Wall Shear tau w High 4 to 5 times]
    lshear --> tlayer[Thin Laminar Sublayer]
    tshear --> sub[Three Sublayers Viscous Buffer Log Law]
    sub --> wall([Wall No Slip Condition])
    tlayer --> wall
    lam --> trn[Transition Region]
    turb --> trn
    trn --> sep{Separation Check}
    sep -->|Adverse pressure gradient| yes[Flow Separation]
    sep -->|Favorable pressure gradient| no[Attached Flow]
    yes --> wake[Turbulent Wake Region]
    no --> wake
    wake --> out([Downstream Flow])
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Boundary Layer Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Step 1 Compute Re x] --> B[Step 2 Compare with Re critical]
    B --> C{Regime}
    C -->|Laminar| D[Step 3a Blasius Profile]
    C -->|Turbulent| E[Step 3b Power Law 1 by 7]
    D --> F[Step 4 Compute Theta]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Step 5 von Karman Equation]
    G --> H[Step 6 Solve for Delta x]
    H --> I[Step 7 Compute tau w]
    I --> J[Step 8 Compute Cf and CD]
    J --> K[Step 9 Compute Drag Force]
    K --> L([Final Answer])
```

## 4.3 Velocity Profile Comparison (Schematic Description)

The figure below describes the qualitative behavior of velocity profiles. (In an exam, draw this clearly with axes $y/\delta$ vs $u/U_\infty$.)

| Zone | $y/\delta$ | Laminar Profile | Turbulent Profile |
|------|------------|------------------|---------------------|
| Wall | 0 | $u = 0$ | $u = 0$ |
| Inner layer | 0 – 0.2 | Smooth, parabolic | Steep (viscous sublayer) |
| Middle | 0.2 – 0.6 | Gradual rise | Logarithmic |
| Outer | 0.6 – 1.0 | Approaches $U_\infty$ smoothly | Approaches $U_\infty$ rapidly (flatter) |
| Edge | 1.0 | $u \approx 0.99 U_\infty$ | $u \approx 0.99 U_\infty$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Visual Takeaway:** The turbulent profile is **fuller** (more uniform across the layer) than the laminar profile. This fuller shape produces higher wall shear stress, thicker boundary layer, but better resistance to separation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Boundary Layer Thickness Definitions `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define the following boundary layer thickness terms with neat sketches: (i) Boundary layer thickness $\delta$, (ii) Displacement thickness $\delta^*$, (iii) Momentum thickness $\theta$.**

**Model Answer:**

> **(i) Boundary layer thickness ($\delta$):** The distance from the solid surface to the point where the velocity reaches 99% of the free-stream velocity $U_\infty$. Beyond this, viscous effects are negligible.
>
> **(ii) Displacement thickness ($\delta^*$):** The distance by which the external streamlines are displaced outward from the wall due to the reduction in mass flow rate within the boundary layer.
>
> $$\delta^* = \int_0^\delta \left(1 - \frac{u}{U_\infty}\right) dy$$
>
> **(iii) Momentum thickness ($\theta$):** Represents the loss of momentum flux per unit width in the boundary layer compared to an inviscid flow.
>
> $$\theta = \int_0^\delta \frac{u}{U_\infty}\left(1 - \frac{u}{U_\infty}\right) dy$$
>
> **Physical significance:** $\delta$ shows the extent of viscous influence, $\delta^*$ represents the streamline displacement, and $\theta$ indicates momentum deficit.

---

### Question 2: Laminar vs. Turbulent Boundary Layer `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between laminar and turbulent boundary layers. State the criterion for transition.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Laminar BL | Turbulent BL |
|-----------|------------|--------------|
| Flow pattern | Smooth, orderly | Chaotic, fluctuating |
| Mixing | None | Intense |
| Thickness | Thin | Thick |
| Wall shear stress | Low | High (4–5 times) |
| Energy loss | Less | More |
| Velocity profile | Parabolic-like | Flatter, fuller |

**Transition Criterion:** Transition occurs when the local Reynolds number exceeds a critical value:

$$Re_x = \frac{U_\infty \, x}{\nu} \geq Re_{x, \text{crit}} \approx 5 \times 10^5$$

Below this value, the flow remains laminar; above it, the flow becomes turbulent.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) Derive the von Kármán momentum integral equation for a two-dimensional steady incompressible flow over a flat plate. (7 marks)**

**(b) Using the 1/7th power law velocity profile, derive expressions for the boundary layer thickness, wall shear stress, and average drag coefficient for turbulent flow over a flat plate. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1: Control Volume Setup.** Consider a control volume of unit width bounded by the plate from $x$ to $x+dx$, the streamline $y = \delta$, and the two vertical faces.

**Step 2: Apply Momentum Equation.** Net force in $x$-direction = rate of change of momentum flux.

**Step 3: Forces.** Only the wall shear stress $\tau_w$ acts in $x$-direction (since $p_\infty$ is uniform on a flat plate):

$$F_x = -\tau_w \, dx$$

**Step 4: Momentum Flux Balance.** Subtract inlet from outlet momentum flux, accounting for the mass entering through the top of the control volume.

**Step 5: Substitute velocity profile and integrate.** Using the definition of momentum thickness:

$$\theta = \int_0^\delta \frac{u}{U_\infty}\left(1 - \frac{u}{U_\infty}\right) dy$$

**Step 6: Final result:**

$$\boxed{\frac{\tau_w}{\rho U_\infty^2} = \frac{d\theta}{dx}}$$

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1: Assume the 1/7th power law:**

$$\frac{u}{U_\infty} = \left(\frac{y}{\delta}\right)^{1/7}$$

**Step 2: Compute momentum thickness:**

$$\theta = \delta \int_0^1 \eta^{1/7}(1-\eta^{1/7}) d\eta = \frac{7\delta}{72}$$

**Step 3: Use the empirical wall shear relation for turbulent flow:**

$$\tau_w = 0.0225 \, \rho U_\infty^2 \, Re_x^{-1/5}$$

**Step 4: Apply von Kármán equation:**

$$\frac{\tau_w}{\rho U_\infty^2} = \frac{d\theta}{dx} = \frac{7}{72} \frac{d\delta}{dx}$$

**Step 5: Integrate to obtain:**

$$\boxed{\delta = \frac{0.37 \, x}{Re_x^{1/5}}}$$

**Step 6: Substitute back to obtain wall shear:**

$$\boxed{\tau_w = 0.0225 \, \rho U_\infty^2 \, Re_x^{-1/5}}$$

**Step 7: Average drag coefficient for length $L$:**

$$\bar{C}_D = \frac{1}{L}\int_0^L C_f \, dx = \frac{1}{L} \int_0^L 0.0576 \, Re_x^{-1/5} dx$$

$$\bar{C}_D = \frac{0.0576 \times 5}{4} \, Re_L^{-1/5} = \frac{0.074}{Re_L^{1/5}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2022]`

**(a) Explain Prandtl's boundary layer concept. Discuss the boundary layer equations and their assumptions with the physical significance of each. (7 marks)**

**(b) A flat plate of length 2 m and width 1.5 m is placed in a stream of oil flowing at 3 m/s. Calculate the boundary layer thickness at the trailing edge, the wall shear stress, and the total drag force. Given: $\nu = 0.6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$, $\rho = 880 \text{ kg/m}^3$. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Prandtl's Concept (1904):** Ludwig Prandtl proposed that for a real fluid flowing past a solid body, the effects of viscosity are confined to a thin layer adjacent to the body called the **boundary layer**. Outside this layer, the fluid behaves as an inviscid flow obeying Euler's equations.

**Key Assumptions of Boundary Layer Theory:**

1. The boundary layer is thin: $\delta \ll L$
2. Streamwise velocity gradient $\partial u/\partial y$ is large; normal gradient $\partial v/\partial x$ is small.
3. Pressure across the boundary layer is constant: $p = p(x)$ only.
4. Viscous terms $\partial^2 u/\partial y^2$ dominate over $\partial^2 u/\partial x^2$.

**Prandtl's Boundary Layer Equations:**

$$u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2}$$

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$$

**Physical significance:** These equations simplify the full Navier-Stokes equations by neglecting small terms, making analytical and numerical solutions tractable while retaining essential physics.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1: Compute the local Reynolds number at the trailing edge ($x = L = 2$ m):**

$$Re_L = \frac{U_\infty L}{\nu} = \frac{3 \times 2}{0.6 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.0 \times 10^5$$

**Step 2: Compare with critical Reynolds number.** $Re_L = 10^5 < 5 \times 10^5$, so the flow is **laminar**.

**Step 3: Compute boundary layer thickness using Blasius formula:**

$$\delta = \frac{5.0 \, L}{\sqrt{Re_L}} = \frac{5.0 \times 2}{\sqrt{10^5}} = \frac{10}{316.23} = 0.0316 \text{ m} = 31.6 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Compute wall shear stress:**

$$\tau_w = 0.332 \, \rho U_\infty^2 \, Re_L^{-1/2} = 0.332 \times 880 \times 3^2 \times (10^5)^{-1/2}$$

$$\tau_w = 0.332 \times 880 \times 9 \times 3.162 \times 10^{-3} = 8.31 \text{ N/m}^2$$

**Step 5: Average drag coefficient:**

$$\bar{C}_D = \frac{1.328}{\sqrt{Re_L}} = \frac{1.328}{316.23} = 4.2 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 6: Total drag force:**

$$F_D = \frac{1}{2}\rho U_\infty^2 A \bar{C}_D = \frac{1}{2} \times 880 \times 9 \times (2 \times 1.5) \times 4.2 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$F_D = 0.5 \times 880 \times 9 \times 3 \times 4.2 \times 10^{-3} = 49.9 \text{ N}$$

**Final Answers:**
- $\delta = 31.6 \text{ mm}$
- $\tau_w = 8.31 \text{ N/m}^2$
- $F_D \approx 49.9 \text{ N}$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the regime check:** Always compute $Re_L$ first and compare with $5 \times 10^5$. Mixing laminar and turbulent formulas is the #1 reason students lose 4–6 marks per question.
> 2. **Using wrong $\nu$ value:** Oil viscosity is much higher than water/air. KTU problems often specify $\nu$ — use the given value, do not assume water/air defaults.
> 3. **Area calculation errors:** Drag force uses **planform area** $A = L \times b$, where $b$ is plate width. Students often use only $L$ or include both sides incorrectly.
> 4. **Sign of $d\theta/dx$:** In von Kármán derivation, ensure the wall shear is positive; check your force direction convention.
> 5. **Missing units:** Always quote SI units (N, m, m²/s, Pa). Marks deducted for missing units.
> 6. **Forgetting "average" vs "local":** $\bar{C}_D$ is the average over the plate, $C_f$ is local. The factor of 2 difference ($1.328$ vs $0.664$) is the most frequently missed constant.
> 7. **Mixing up $0.664$ and $0.074$:** $0.664/\sqrt{Re_L}$ is **laminar**; $0.074/Re_L^{1/5}$ is **turbulent**. Cross-using them leads to wrong answers.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **boundary layer** is the thin region near a surface where viscous effects matter (Prandtl, 1904).
- ✅ Transition is governed by **$Re_{x,\text{crit}} \approx 5 \times 10^5$** over a flat plate.
- ✅ **Laminar** BL: smooth, thin, low wall shear; governed by **Blasius** solution.
- ✅ **Turbulent** BL: chaotic, thick, high wall shear; modeled using **1/7th power law**.
- ✅ Three characteristic thicknesses: **$\delta$** (nominal), **$\delta^*$** (displacement), **$\theta$** (momentum).
- ✅ **von Kármán momentum integral** ($\tau_w = \rho U^2 d\theta/dx$) is the master equation for boundary layer analysis.
- ✅ Key laminar formulas: $\delta = 5x/\sqrt{Re_x}$, $C_f = 0.664/\sqrt{Re_x}$, $\bar{C}_D = 1.328/\sqrt{Re_L}$.
- ✅ Key turbulent formulas: $\delta = 0.37x/Re_x^{1/5}$, $C_f = 0.0576/Re_x^{1/5}$, $\bar{C}_D = 0.074/Re_L^{1/5}$.
- ✅ **Shape factor** $H = \delta^*/\theta$ is ~2.59 for laminar and ~1.3 for turbulent — useful for checking work.
- ✅ **Separation** occurs under adverse pressure gradients ($dP/dx > 0$); turbulent BL resists separation better.
- ✅ Drag on a flat plate is **skin friction drag only** (no pressure drag in 2-D aligned flow).
- ✅ Always check **regime first**, then pick the **correct formula set**, then compute.
- ✅ Remember: **laminar** problems use $\sqrt{Re_L}$ dependencies; **turbulent** problems use $Re_L^{1/5}$ dependencies.
- ✅ Typical KTU question: 7 marks for derivation, 7 marks for numerical — practice both types.
- ✅ Use the given $\nu$ and $\rho$ for the specific fluid (air, water, oil) — never assume.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laminar Sub Layer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET413 - MECHANICS OF FLUID FLOW
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Laminar Sub Layer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Laminar Sublayer** (also called the **Viscous Sublayer** or **Linear Sublayer**) is the thin region of fluid that exists immediately adjacent to a solid boundary in a fully developed turbulent flow, where the viscous forces overwhelmingly dominate the inertial and turbulent mixing forces. In this zone, the velocity profile is essentially **linear** (not logarithmic), and the fluid motion is essentially laminar in character, even though the bulk flow outside is turbulent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECET413 – Module 3):**
> The laminar sublayer is defined as the thin fluid layer of thickness $\delta'$ adjacent to the wall in turbulent flow, within which viscous shear stress is the dominant mechanism of momentum transfer and the velocity varies linearly with distance from the wall. It is bounded by the wall on one side and the **buffer layer** on the other, transitioning into the **logarithmic (turbulent) core**.

Mathematically, the laminar sublayer thickness is given by:
$$\delta' = \frac{11.6 \, \nu}{u_*}$$

where $\nu$ is the kinematic viscosity and $u_*$ is the **shear (friction) velocity** at the wall.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The River-Bank Effect**
> Imagine standing beside a fast-flowing river. At the very edge where the water touches the bank, you can see the water is *almost still* — you can clearly distinguish individual leaves drifting slowly. But a metre away, the water rushes past in a churning, turbulent mass. The "thin quiet layer" right next to the bank behaves almost as if the river were laminar, even though the main flow is wildly turbulent. **That quiet, sluggish layer is the laminar sublayer.**

The "thickness" of this calm band is microscopic — for water in a typical pipe it is on the order of **0.01 mm to 0.1 mm**. Yet despite being paper-thin, it controls almost everything we care about in pipe friction: pressure drop, pumping cost, and the entire classification of pipes as hydraulically *smooth*, *transitional*, or *rough*.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (in Bold)

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value (Water, 20°C) | Units |
|----------|--------|------------------------------|-------|
| Kinematic viscosity of water | $\nu$ | $\mathbf{1.004 \times 10^{-6}}$ | m²/s |
| Wall shear stress | $\tau_0$ | $\mathbf{0.1 - 100}$ | N/m² |
| Shear (friction) velocity | $u_*$ | $\mathbf{0.01 - 0.3}$ | m/s |
| Laminar sublayer thickness | $\delta'$ | $\mathbf{0.01 - 0.1}$ | mm |
| Critical sublayer Reynolds number | $Re_{\delta'}$ | $\mathbf{\approx 11.6}$ (dimensionless) | — |
| Von Kármán constant | $\kappa$ | $\mathbf{0.40}$ (dimensionless) | — |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Architecture of a Turbulent Boundary Layer

In a fully developed turbulent flow over a flat plate or inside a pipe, the boundary layer is **not** a single uniform zone. It consists of **three distinct sub-regions** stacked above the wall:

1. **Laminar (Viscous) Sublayer** — $0 \leq y \leq \delta'$
2. **Buffer (Transition) Layer** — $\delta' \leq y \leq 0.2\delta$ (where $\delta$ is the total BL thickness)
3. **Turbulent (Logarithmic) Core** — $0.2\delta \leq y \leq \delta$

**Why does this layering exist?**
- Near the wall, the no-slip condition forces the velocity to be zero. Turbulent eddies, being large and inertia-driven, **cannot fit into** the tiny space adjacent to the wall — there simply isn't enough room for them to rotate. So the very thin region right next to the wall behaves in a strictly **viscous, laminar** manner.
- As $y$ increases, eddies get room to develop, momentum exchange by turbulent fluctuations becomes significant, and the profile bends from linear to logarithmic.
- The **buffer layer** is the meeting zone where viscous and turbulent stresses are of comparable magnitude.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Physical Reasoning

- **Step 1 — Wall boundary condition:** At $y = 0$, the no-slip condition demands $u = 0$. The fluid in direct molecular contact with the wall is glued to it.
- **Step 2 — Shear stress dominance:** The total shear stress $\tau$ at any point in the boundary layer is the sum of viscous stress $\tau_v$ and turbulent (Reynolds) stress $\tau_t$: $\tau = \tau_v + \tau_t$.
- **Step 3 — In the sublayer:** Because turbulent eddies are suppressed, $\tau_t \to 0$, so $\tau \approx \tau_v = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy} = \tau_0$ (constant, equal to the wall shear stress).
- **Step 4 — Integration:** Since $\mu$ is constant, $\dfrac{du}{dy} = \dfrac{\tau_0}{\mu}$ is a **constant** — hence $u$ varies **linearly** with $y$.
- **Step 5 — The edge of the sublayer:** At $y = \delta'$, the linear profile is matched to the logarithmic profile, and the criterion $\dfrac{u_* \delta'}{\nu} \approx 11.6$ is obtained.
- **Step 6 — Engineering use:** Knowing $\delta'$ lets us predict whether wall roughness $k$ projects *through* the sublayer (rough pipe) or is *buried* inside it (smooth pipe).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **complete set of formulas** that KTU examiners expect a student to reproduce and apply for this topic. All marks in numerical problems come from these equations.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Condition | Units |
|---|---------|---------------------|-------|
| 1 | $u_* = \sqrt{\dfrac{\tau_0}{\rho}}$ | Shear (friction) velocity definition | m/s |
| 2 | $\delta' = \dfrac{11.6 \, \nu}{u_*}$ | Laminar sublayer thickness | m |
| 3 | $Re_{\delta'} = \dfrac{u_* \, \delta'}{\nu} = 11.6$ | Critical sublayer Reynolds number (constant) | — |
| 4 | $u = \dfrac{\tau_0}{\mu} \, y = \dfrac{u_*^{\,2}}{\nu} \, y$ | Linear velocity distribution inside sublayer | m/s |
| 5 | $\tau_0 = \mu \, \dfrac{du}{dy} = \mu \, \dfrac{u}{\,y\,}$ | Shear stress inside the sublayer | N/m² |
| 6 | $f = \dfrac{0.0791}{Re^{0.25}}$ | Blasius equation (smooth pipe, $Re < 10^5$) | — |
| 7 | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = 2 \log_{10} (Re \sqrt{f}) - 0.8$ | Smooth pipe law (universal) | — |
| 8 | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = 2 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{r}{k} \right) + 1.74$ | Fully rough pipe law (von Kármán) | — |
| 9 | $\dfrac{u}{u_*} = \dfrac{1}{\kappa} \ln \left( \dfrac{u_* \, y}{\nu} \right) + C$ | Logarithmic velocity distribution (turbulent core) | — |
| 10 | $k < \delta' \Rightarrow$ hydraulically smooth; $k > \delta' \Rightarrow$ rough | Pipe classification criterion | — |

**Reminder for safe markdown rendering:** In the formulas above, expressions like $\vert y \vert$ or $\vert u \vert$ should be written as $\lvert y \rvert$ and $\lvert u \rvert$ — never use a bare vertical pipe `|` inside a table cell, since it breaks the markdown column separator.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Why Laminar Sublayer Matters |
|-------|------------------------------|
| **Water supply pipelines** | Determines whether old corroded pipes (with $k$ comparable to $\delta'$) act as smooth or rough — affects pumping cost dramatically. |
| **Aerospace (aircraft fuselages)** | Surface paint smoothness is designed so $k \ll \delta'$ to keep the boundary layer laminar for as long as possible (delay transition to drag). |
| **Heat exchangers** | The thermal resistance of the sublayer is *the* controlling resistance in convective heat transfer — entire correlations (Dittus-Boelter, Sieder-Tate) are essentially formulations of sublayer behaviour. |
| **Hydraulic turbines & ship hulls** | Bio-fouling increases $k$; once $k$ exceeds $\delta'$, the surface "switches" from smooth to rough and fuel/npower consumption jumps. |
| **Blood flow in arteries** | Arterial walls experience a sublayer; understanding it helps design stents and cardiovascular devices. |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Linear Velocity Distribution in the Laminar Sublayer

**Statement to prove:** *Within the laminar sublayer, the velocity varies linearly with distance from the wall, given by $u = \dfrac{\tau_0}{\mu} \, y$.*

**Starting point — Newton's law of viscosity** for a unidirectional flow:
$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Step 1 — Accept that inside the sublayer, turbulent fluctuations are negligible.** Hence the total shear stress $\tau$ equals the viscous shear stress and is constant (equal to the wall shear stress $\tau_0$):
$$\tau = \tau_v + \tau_t \quad \text{and} \quad \tau_t \approx 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \tau \approx \tau_v = \tau_0$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into Newton's law:**
$$\tau_0 = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Step 3 — Separate variables and integrate** from the wall ($y = 0, \; u = 0$) to an arbitrary point ($y, \; u$):
$$\int_{0}^{u} du = \frac{\tau_0}{\mu} \int_{0}^{y} dy$$

**Step 4 — Carry out the integration:**
$$u = \frac{\tau_0}{\mu} \, y$$

**Step 5 — Express in terms of shear velocity** $u_* = \sqrt{\tau_0 / \rho}$, so that $\tau_0 = \rho u_*^{\,2}$. Then since $\nu = \mu / \rho$:
$$u = \frac{\rho u_*^{\,2}}{\mu} \, y = \frac{u_*^{\,2}}{\nu} \, y$$

**Final boxed result:**
$$\boxed{\,u = \frac{u_*^{\,2}}{\nu} \, y \quad \text{(linear profile in laminar sublayer)}\,}$$

This derivation is a **favourite 5–7 mark KTU question**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Thickness of the Laminar Sublayer $\delta'$

**Statement to prove:** *The thickness of the laminar sublayer is $\delta' = \dfrac{11.6 \, \nu}{u_*}$.*

**Step 1 — Define the edge of the sublayer.** By convention, the laminar sublayer is assumed to extend up to the point where the linear velocity profile intersects the logarithmic (turbulent-core) velocity profile, OR up to the point where viscous and turbulent stresses are of equal magnitude. A widely used criterion is that the **sublayer Reynolds number equals a critical value**:
$$Re_{\delta'} = \frac{u_* \, \delta'}{\nu} \approx 11.6$$

**Step 2 — Justify the constant 11.6.** It arises from matching the linear sublayer velocity $u = \dfrac{u_*^{\,2}}{\nu} \, y$ with the logarithmic law $\dfrac{u}{u_*} = \dfrac{1}{\kappa} \ln \left( \dfrac{u_* y}{\nu} \right) + C$, where $\kappa = 0.4$ and $C = 5.0$ (or 5.5 depending on convention). Substituting $y = \delta'$ and $u = u_*$ into the linear law first gives:
$$u_* = \frac{u_*^{\,2}}{\nu} \, \delta' \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta' = \frac{\nu}{u_*}$$

But more refined matching (or the condition $\tau_v = \tau_t$ at the sublayer edge) yields the factor 11.6. We accept this as the **experimentally validated KTU-standard** result.

**Step 3 — Rearrange to obtain thickness:**
$$\delta' = \frac{11.6 \, \nu}{u_*}$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of the wall shear stress** for computational convenience. Since $u_* = \sqrt{\tau_0 / \rho}$:
$$\delta' = \frac{11.6 \, \nu}{\sqrt{\tau_0 / \rho}} = 11.6 \sqrt{\frac{\rho \, \nu^{\,2}}{\tau_0}}$$

**Step 5 — Check dimensional consistency.** The numerator has units of m²/s, the denominator m/s → result is in metres. ✔

**Final boxed result:**
$$\boxed{\,\delta' = \frac{11.6 \, \nu}{u_*} = 11.6 \sqrt{\frac{\rho \, \nu^{\,2}}{\tau_0}}\,}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Velocity at the Edge of the Sublayer

**Step 1 — Use the linear law at $y = \delta'$:**
$$u_{\delta'} = \frac{u_*^{\,2}}{\nu} \, \delta'$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\delta' = \dfrac{11.6 \, \nu}{u_*}$:**
$$u_{\delta'} = \frac{u_*^{\,2}}{\nu} \cdot \frac{11.6 \, \nu}{u_*} = 11.6 \, u_*$$

**Interpretation:** *The fluid velocity at the outer edge of the laminar sublayer equals 11.6 times the shear velocity.* This is an important checkpoint for numerical problems.

$$\boxed{\,u_{\delta'} = 11.6 \, u_*\,}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example (Full Solution)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example (Full Marks Model Solution):**
> *"Water at 20°C flows over a smooth flat plate. The wall shear stress is measured as $\tau_0 = 2 \, \text{N/m}^2$. Take $\rho = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ and $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}$. Determine: (i) the shear velocity, (ii) the laminar sublayer thickness, (iii) the velocity at the edge of the sublayer, and (iv) the velocity at $y = 0.05 \, \text{mm}$ from the wall."*

**Part (i) — Shear velocity:**
$$u_* = \sqrt{\frac{\tau_0}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{1000}} = \sqrt{0.002} = 0.0447 \;\text{m/s}$$

**Part (ii) — Laminar sublayer thickness:**
$$\delta' = \frac{11.6 \, \nu}{u_*} = \frac{11.6 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-6}}{0.0447} = \frac{1.16 \times 10^{-5}}{0.0447} = 2.595 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m} = 0.2595 \;\text{mm}$$

**Part (iii) — Velocity at edge of sublayer:**
$$u_{\delta'} = 11.6 \, u_* = 11.6 \times 0.0447 = 0.5186 \;\text{m/s}$$

**Part (iv) — Velocity at $y = 0.05 \, \text{mm} = 5 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}$:**  
First check whether the point is inside the sublayer: $y = 0.05 \, \text{mm} < \delta' = 0.2595 \, \text{mm}$ → **Yes, use linear law.**
$$u = \frac{u_*^{\,2}}{\nu} \, y = \frac{(0.0447)^2}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} \times 5 \times 10^{-5} = \frac{1.998 \times 10^{-3}}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} \times 5 \times 10^{-5}$$
$$u = 1998.0 \times 5 \times 10^{-5} = 0.0999 \;\text{m/s} \approx 0.10 \;\text{m/s}$$

**Final answers (boxed):**
$$\boxed{\,u_* = 0.0447 \;\text{m/s}, \quad \delta' = 0.2595 \;\text{mm}, \quad u_{\delta'} = 0.5186 \;\text{m/s}, \quad u_{0.05\,\text{mm}} \approx 0.10 \;\text{m/s}\,}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Three-Regime Classification (Smooth / Transitional / Rough)

| Regime | Condition | Velocity Law | Friction Law |
|--------|-----------|--------------|--------------|
| **Hydraulically Smooth** | $k < 0.4 \, \delta'$ (or $k^+ < 5$) | Logarithmic (depends on $Re$) | Blasius / Prandtl smooth law |
| **Transitional** | $0.4 \, \delta' < k < 6 \, \delta'$ | Mixed (depends on $Re$ and $k$) | Colebrook-White equation |
| **Fully Rough** | $k > 6 \, \delta'$ (or $k^+ > 70$) | Logarithmic (independent of $Re$) | von Kármán rough law |

where $k^+ = k u_* / \nu$ is the **roughness Reynolds number**.
