<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Mechanics of Fluid Flow**, the term **"Geometric"** refers to the study of the **geometric properties (also called hydraulic elements) of fluid-conveying cross-sections**, along with the principles of **geometric similarity** used in dimensional analysis, model prototyping, and scale-up studies of fluid systems. It encompasses the relationship between the *physical shape* of a conduit (pipe, duct, channel, or packed bed) and the *hydraulic parameters* that govern fluid behaviour within it.

The fundamental geometric/hydraulic parameters of any flow cross-section are:

| Parameter | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| Cross-sectional area of flow | $A$ | Area of the fluid column perpendicular to the direction of flow (m²) |
| Wetted Perimeter | $P$ | Length of the boundary of the cross-section that is in actual contact with the flowing fluid (m) |
| Hydraulic Radius (Hydraulic Mean Depth) | $R_h$ | Ratio of the cross-sectional flow area to the wetted perimeter: $R_h = A/P$ (m) |
| Equivalent (Hydraulic) Diameter | $D_e$ | Four times the hydraulic radius: $D_e = 4R_h = 4A/P$ (m) |
| Hydraulic Depth | $D_h$ | Ratio of area to the free-surface (top) width: $D_h = A/T$ (m) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Geometric properties form the *bridge* between conduit shape and head-loss/friction-factor correlations. The **Reynolds number** is universally defined using the *equivalent diameter* $D_e$ for **non-circular conduits**, making geometric properties indispensable for industrial design.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are walking barefoot along the **edge of a swimming pool**, then turning and walking along the **edge of a small kitchen sink** filled to the same depth. Even though the water depth is identical, the pool "feels" like it has much more capacity to move water than the sink.

**Why?** The pool has a *larger wetted perimeter spread over a much larger cross-sectional area* — meaning each metre of "wet wall" carries a much larger volume of water. This intuition is captured exactly by the **Hydraulic Radius** $R_h = A/P$ — a *large* $R_h$ means "fat, open" flow, while a *small* $R_h$ means "narrow, restricted" flow.

For a **full circular pipe** of diameter $D$:
- Area $A = \pi D^2/4$
- Wetted perimeter $P = \pi D$
- So $R_h = D/4$, and the **equivalent diameter equals the pipe diameter itself** ($D_e = D$).

For a **square duct** of side $a$ flowing full:
- $A = a^2$, $P = 4a$
- $D_e = 4(a^2)/(4a) = a$ — i.e., the equivalent diameter equals the side length, **not** the diagonal!

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Insight:** A circular cross-section is the *most hydraulically efficient* shape for a closed conduit because, for a given area, it minimises the wetted perimeter — and hence minimises frictional head loss. Any deviation from circularity *increases* $P$ for the same $A$, which *decreases* $R_h$ and increases wall friction.

## 1.3 Geometric Similarity — The Scaling Concept

Beyond hydraulic elements, **Geometric Similarity** is the foundation of model studies:

> **Geometric Similarity** exists between a *model* (subscript $m$) and a *prototype* (subscript $p$) when the ratio of all corresponding linear dimensions is a *constant*. This constant is called the **scale factor** $\lambda$.

$$\lambda_L = \frac{L_m}{L_p} = \text{constant for all lengths}$$

All corresponding angles must be identical, and all area ratios must equal $\lambda_L^2$ while volume ratios equal $\lambda_L^3$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Convention:** Throughout KTU and most fluid mechanics texts, the **prototype** refers to the *full-scale real-world system* (e.g., a dam spillway, an aircraft wing, a chemical reactor) and the **model** refers to the *small-scale laboratory replica* used to predict prototype behaviour.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Breakdown of Geometric Properties

### Step 1 — Identify the Flow Conduit
The first decision is whether the conduit is:
- **Closed (pressurised)** — e.g., pipes, ducts running full.
- **Open (free-surface)** — e.g., channels, spillways, partially filled pipes.

This decides which perimeter portions are *wetted* and which are *free surface*.

### Step 2 — Compute the Wetted Perimeter
$ P $ is the **sum of all boundary lengths that touch the flowing fluid**. The free surface of an open channel is *not* included.

### Step 3 — Compute the Cross-sectional Flow Area
$A$ is the geometric area *occupied by the fluid* in the cross-section.

### Step 4 — Derive Hydraulic Radius and Equivalent Diameter
Apply the definitions $R_h = A/P$ and $D_e = 4R_h$.

### Step 5 — Use $D_e$ in Standard Correlations
Substitute $D_e$ into Reynolds number, friction factor, and head-loss equations that were originally derived for circular pipes.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Parameter

- **Why $R_h$ matters:** Frictional resistance is generated *along the wetted boundary* (proportional to $P$) but the fluid *carried* is proportional to $A$. The ratio $A/P$ therefore expresses "fluid volume per unit wall contact" — a direct measure of how efficiently a cross-section moves fluid.
- **Why $D_e = 4R_h$:** The factor of 4 normalises the hydraulic radius to coincide with the actual diameter for a *full circular pipe*. This way, $D_e$ and $D$ are interchangeable, allowing use of all circular-pipe charts and equations (Moody diagram, Blasius, Colebrook) for non-circular sections.

## 2.3 Geometric Similarity — Logical Flow

1. Identify corresponding points, lines, angles between model and prototype.
2. Confirm **all linear scale ratios** are equal: $L_m/L_p = \lambda_L$ (constant).
3. Confirm all corresponding angles are identical.
4. The resulting **area ratio** is $\lambda_L^2$ and **volume ratio** is $\lambda_L^3$.
5. Geometric similarity is the *first* (and easiest) condition required for **dynamic similarity**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Geometric Formulas for KTU PECET413**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hydraulic Radius | $R_h = \dfrac{A}{P}$ | m | Universal for any cross-section |
| 2 | Equivalent (Hydraulic) Diameter | $D_e = \dfrac{4A}{P}$ | m | Reduces to $D$ for full circular pipe |
| 3 | Reynolds Number (non-circular) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \, v \, D_e}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Use $D_e$ instead of $D$ |
| 4 | Friction Factor (Blasius) | $f = \dfrac{0.316}{Re^{0.25}}$ | dimensionless | Valid for smooth pipes, $Re < 10^5$ |
| 5 | Head Loss (Darcy–Weisbach) | $h_f = f \dfrac{L}{D_e} \dfrac{v^2}{2g}$ | m | Uses equivalent diameter |
| 6 | Geometric Scale Factor | $\lambda_L = \dfrac{L_m}{L_p}$ | dimensionless | Constant across all dimensions |
| 7 | Area Scale Ratio | $\dfrac{A_m}{A_p} = \lambda_L^{\,2}$ | dimensionless | Derived from length scale |
| 8 | Volume Scale Ratio | $\dfrac{V_m}{V_p} = \lambda_L^{\,3}$ | dimensionless | Derived from length scale |
| 9 | Hydraulic Radius — Annulus | $R_h = \dfrac{D_o - D_i}{4}$ | m | For concentric annular flow |
| 10 | Hydraulic Radius — Square (full) | $R_h = \dfrac{a}{4}$ | m | For square duct running full |
| 11 | Hydraulic Radius — Rectangular (full) | $R_h = \dfrac{ab}{2(a+b)}$ | m | For rectangular duct |
| 12 | Hydraulic Depth | $D_h = \dfrac{A}{T}$ | m | $T$ = top (free-surface) width |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These geometric relations are used in the design of *heat exchanger tube banks*, *ventilation ducts*, *spillways*, *sewers*, *packed columns*, and *process piping* — anywhere fluid flows through a non-circular or partially filled cross-section.

## 2.5 Real-World Industrial Utility

- **Chemical process industries:** Designing non-circular mixers and reactors where equivalent diameter predicts transition from laminar to turbulent flow.
- **Civil/Hydraulic engineering:** Open channel flow in irrigation canals, spillways, and rivers where hydraulic radius dictates the Chezy/Manning flow resistance.
- **HVAC:** Rectangular air-conditioning ducts use $D_e$ to size fans and pressure drops.
- **Scale-up & Pilot plants:** Geometric similarity ensures that the *flow patterns* observed in a small pilot reactor can be extrapolated to the full-scale plant.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Equivalent Diameter of a Full Circular Pipe

**Statement to prove:** For a circular pipe of internal diameter $D$ running completely full, $D_e = D$.

**Step 1 — Write the area of the circular cross-section.**

The cross-sectional area of a circle of diameter $D$ is:

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

**Step 2 — Write the wetted perimeter.**

The wetted perimeter is the entire circumference, because the entire wall is in contact with the fluid:

$$P = \pi D$$

**Step 3 — Apply the definition of equivalent diameter.**

The equivalent diameter is defined as four times the hydraulic radius:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_e &= 4 R_h \\
    &= 4 \left( \frac{A}{P} \right) \\
    &= 4 \left( \frac{\dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}}{\pi D} \right) \\
    &= 4 \left( \frac{\pi D^2}{4 \cdot \pi D} \right) \\
    &= 4 \left( \frac{D}{4} \right) \\
    &= D
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion.**

The factor of 4 in the definition $D_e = 4A/P$ is chosen *specifically* so that equivalent diameter reduces to the actual diameter for a full circular pipe. This allows the same friction-factor charts (Moody diagram) to be applied universally.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Equivalent Diameter of a Square Duct (Flowing Full)

**Statement to prove:** For a square duct of side $a$ flowing full, $D_e = a$.

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of a square.**

$$A = a \times a = a^2$$

**Step 2 — Wetted perimeter of a square (all four sides touch the fluid).**

$$P = 4a$$

**Step 3 — Apply the definition of equivalent diameter.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_e &= \frac{4A}{P} \\
    &= \frac{4 \, a^2}{4a} \\
    &= a
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

The equivalent diameter of a square duct is *equal to its side length*, not its diagonal. This is because the diagonal is a *geometric* length, not a *hydraulic* length.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Equivalent Diameter of a Rectangular Duct ($a \times b$, Full Flow)

**Step 1 — Area and perimeter of the rectangle.**

$$A = a \cdot b \qquad P = 2(a+b)$$

**Step 2 — Equivalent diameter.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_e &= \frac{4A}{P} \\
    &= \frac{4ab}{2(a+b)} \\
    &= \frac{2ab}{a+b}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Limiting case check.**

For a square ($a = b$): $D_e = \dfrac{2a^2}{2a} = a$ ✓ (matches Derivation 2).

For a *very wide* duct ($b \to \infty$, $a$ fixed):

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_e &= \lim_{b \to \infty} \frac{2ab}{a+b} = \lim_{b \to \infty} \frac{2a}{\frac{a}{b}+1} = 2a
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the well-known result that the equivalent diameter of an infinitely wide parallel-plate channel equals *twice* the gap between the plates.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Equivalent Diameter of a Concentric Annulus

**Statement:** For flow through the annular space between an inner pipe of outer diameter $D_i$ and an outer pipe of inner diameter $D_o$, derive $D_e$.

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional flow area (outer minus inner).**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D_o^2 - D_i^2\right)$$

**Step 2 — Wetted perimeter (inner + outer walls, since both touch the fluid).**

$$P = \pi D_o + \pi D_i = \pi(D_o + D_i)$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent diameter.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_e &= \frac{4A}{P} \\
    &= \frac{4 \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2)}{\pi(D_o + D_i)} \\
    &= \frac{D_o^2 - D_i^2}{D_o + D_i} \\
    &= \frac{(D_o - D_i)(D_o + D_i)}{D_o + D_i} \\
    &= D_o - D_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion.**

For a concentric annulus, the equivalent diameter is simply the **gap width** $D_o - D_i$. This is one of the cleanest results in fluid mechanics and is heavily tested in KTU.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Geometric Similarity Ratios

**Step 1 — Definition of linear scale factor.**

$$\lambda_L = \frac{L_m}{L_p} \quad \text{(constant for all corresponding lengths)}$$

**Step 2 — Derive the area scale ratio.**

Since area is a product of two lengths:

$$\frac{A_m}{A_p} = \frac{(L_m)^2}{(L_p)^2} = \lambda_L^{\,2}$$

**Step 3 — Derive the volume scale ratio.**

Since volume is a product of three lengths:

$$\frac{V_m}{V_p} = \frac{(L_m)^3}{(L_p)^3} = \lambda_L^{\,3}$$

**Step 4 — Velocity scale ratio (from continuity).**

For an *incompressible* fluid with identical density in model and prototype, continuity gives $Q = A v$. With $Q_m/Q_p = (A_m v_m)/(A_p v_p)$ and $Q_m/Q_p$ depending on the time scale $\lambda_T$:

$$\frac{v_m}{v_p} = \frac{\lambda_L}{\lambda_T}$$

**Step 5 — Discharge scale ratio.**

$$\frac{Q_m}{Q_p} = \frac{A_m v_m}{A_p v_p} = \lambda_L^{\,2} \cdot \frac{\lambda_L}{\lambda_T} = \frac{\lambda_L^{\,3}}{\lambda_T}$$

**Step 6 — Force scale ratio (Newton's second law).**

$$F = m a = \rho L^3 \cdot \frac{L}{T^2} = \rho \frac{L^4}{T^2}$$

Hence:

$$\frac{F_m}{F_p} = \frac{\rho_m}{\rho_p} \cdot \frac{\lambda_L^{\,4}}{\lambda_T^{\,2}}$$

If the same fluid is used ($\rho_m = \rho_p$):

$$\frac{F_m}{F_p} = \frac{\lambda_L^{\,4}}{\lambda_T^{\,2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these derivations matter for KTU:** Each ratio above is a potential sub-part of a 7 or 14 mark question. Memorising only the length/area/volume ratios is *not* enough — students frequently lose marks by failing to derive velocity, discharge, and force ratios from first principles.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Geometric Property Computation

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the *decision logic* used to compute equivalent diameter for any given cross-section.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Conduit Geometry] --> B{Is the conduit closed or open?}
    B -- Closed --> C[Compute full cross-section area A]
    B -- Open --> D[Compute wetted area A excluding free surface]
    C --> E[Compute wetted perimeter P all walls]
    D --> F[Compute wetted perimeter P excluding free surface]
    E --> G[Compute hydraulic radius Rh = A divided by P]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Compute equivalent diameter De = 4 Rh]
    H --> I[Substitute De in Reynolds number Re]
    I --> J{Is Re less than 2300?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Laminar regime: use f = 64 divided by Re]
    J -- No --> L{Is Re less than 100000 and pipe smooth?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Turbulent smooth: use Blasius f = 0.316 divided by Re power 0.25]
    L -- No --> N[Use Colebrook equation or Moody chart]
    K --> O[Compute head loss using Darcy Weisbach with De]
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[Output: final design head loss in metres]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Geometric Similarity Hierarchy

This diagram shows the *three tiers of similarity* required for full dynamic similitude, with geometric similarity as the foundation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Geometric Similarity] --> L2[Kinematic Similarity]
    L2 --> L3[Dynamic Similarity]
    L1 --> M1[Same shape: all L ratios equal lambda L]
    L1 --> M2[Same angles at all corresponding points]
    L2 --> M3[Same streamline pattern: velocity ratios constant]
    L2 --> M4[Same time scale: acceleration ratios consistent]
    L3 --> M5[Same force ratios at all corresponding points]
    L3 --> M6[Same dimensionless groups: Re Fr We Eu equal]
    L1 -.->|Foundation| L2
    L2 -.->|Foundation| L3
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Common Cross-Sections and their Equivalent Diameters

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CIRC[Full Circular Pipe]
        A1[Area A equals pi D squared divided by 4]
        A2[Perimeter P equals pi D]
        A3[Equivalent diameter De equals D]
        A1 --> A3
        A2 --> A3
    end
    subgraph SQ[Full Square Duct]
        B1[Area A equals a squared]
        B2[Perimeter P equals 4a]
        B3[Equivalent diameter De equals a]
        B1 --> B3
        B2 --> B3
    end
    subgraph REC[Full Rectangular Duct]
        C1[Area A equals a times b]
        C2[Perimeter P equals 2 times a plus b]
        C3[Equivalent diameter De equals 2ab divided by a plus b]
        C1 --> C3
        C2 --> C3
    end
    subgraph ANN[Concentric Annulus]
        D1[Area A equals pi divided by 4 times Do squared minus Di squared]
        D2[Perimeter P equals pi times Do plus Di]
        D3[Equivalent diameter De equals Do minus Di]
        D1 --> D3
        D2 --> D3
    end
```

## 4.4 Process Flow — Applying Geometric Similarity in Model Studies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Define prototype dimensions Lp] --> S2[Choose scale factor lambda L]
    S2 --> S3[Compute model dimensions Lm equals lambda L times Lp]
    S3 --> S4[Identify governing dimensionless group Re Fr We]
    S4 --> S5{Choose fluid and velocity to match group}
    S5 --> S6[Run experiment on model]
    S6 --> S7[Measure model force Fm velocity vm discharge Qm]
    S7 --> S8[Apply force and velocity scale ratios]
    S8 --> S9[Predict prototype behaviour Fp vp Qp]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper]
**Define equivalent diameter. Derive its value for a square duct of side 0.2 m flowing full.**

**Model Answer:**

The **equivalent (or hydraulic) diameter** of a non-circular conduit is defined as four times the hydraulic radius:

$$D_e = \frac{4A}{P}$$

where $A$ is the cross-sectional flow area and $P$ is the wetted perimeter.

**Derivation for a square duct (side $a$):**
- Area $A = a^2 = (0.2)^2 = 0.04 \text{ m}^2$
- Wetted perimeter $P = 4a = 4 \times 0.2 = 0.8 \text{ m}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_e &= \frac{4 \times 0.04}{0.8} \\
    &= \frac{0.16}{0.8} \\
    &= 0.2 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence the equivalent diameter of a square duct equals its side length.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**What is geometric similarity? State the scale ratios for length, area, and volume between a model and prototype.**

**Model Answer:**

**Geometric similarity** exists between a model and a prototype when the ratio of all corresponding linear dimensions is a constant, and all corresponding angles are identical.

The scale ratios are:
- **Length scale ratio:** $\lambda_L = \dfrac{L_m}{L_p}$
- **Area scale ratio:** $\lambda_A = \dfrac{A_m}{A_p} = \lambda_L^{\,2}$
- **Volume scale ratio:** $\lambda_V = \dfrac{V_m}{V_p} = \lambda_L^{\,3}$

These ratios form the foundation for deriving all other kinematic and dynamic scale ratios in dimensional analysis.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Define hydraulic radius and equivalent diameter. For a concentric annulus with outer diameter 100 mm and inner diameter 60 mm, carrying oil of density 880 kg/m³ and viscosity 0.05 Pa·s at a velocity of 1.5 m/s, determine:
- (i) Equivalent diameter
- (ii) Reynolds number and state the flow regime
- (iii) Friction factor (using the Blasius equation if applicable) **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A trapezoidal open channel has a bottom width of 2 m, side slopes of 1 horizontal : 1.5 vertical, and carries water at a depth of 1.2 m. Calculate the hydraulic radius and equivalent diameter. If the water flows at 0.8 m/s, compute the head loss over a 50 m length using the Darcy–Weisbach equation with $f = 0.02$. **(7 marks)**

### MODEL SOLUTION

**Part (a):**

**Definitions:**
- **Hydraulic radius** $R_h$ is the ratio of cross-sectional flow area to the wetted perimeter: $R_h = A/P$.
- **Equivalent diameter** $D_e$ is defined as $D_e = 4R_h = 4A/P$.

**(i) Equivalent diameter:**

For a concentric annulus (derived in Section 3.4): $D_e = D_o - D_i$

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_e &= 0.100 - 0.060 \\
    &= 0.040 \text{ m} = 40 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Reynolds number:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Re &= \frac{\rho \, v \, D_e}{\mu} \\
   &= \frac{880 \times 1.5 \times 0.040}{0.05} \\
   &= \frac{52.8}{0.05} \\
   &= 1056
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $Re < 2300$, the flow is **laminar**.

**(iii) Friction factor:**

For laminar flow in a non-circular duct, $f = \dfrac{C}{Re}$ where $C = 64$ for a circular pipe; for a concentric annulus, $C$ depends on diameter ratio. Using the standard circular-pipe value (as is common in KTU problems unless otherwise specified):

$$
\begin{aligned}
f &= \frac{64}{Re} = \frac{64}{1056} \\
  &= 0.0606
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Part (a):** Many students write "$D_e$ for an annulus is $\sqrt{D_o^2 - D_i^2}$" — this is **wrong**. The correct expression is the *gap width* $D_o - D_i$. Memorise the derivation in Section 3.4.

---

**Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Geometry of the trapezoidal channel.**

Bottom width $b = 2$ m, depth $y = 1.2$ m, side slope 1H : 1.5V (horizontal : vertical).

The horizontal projection of each slanted side:

$$x = 1.5 \times y = 1.5 \times 1.2 = 1.8 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Cross-sectional flow area.**

The trapezoid consists of the bottom rectangle plus two right-triangular sides:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= b \cdot y + x \cdot y \\
  &= (2 \times 1.2) + (1.8 \times 1.2) \\
  &= 2.4 + 2.16 \\
  &= 4.56 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Wetted perimeter.**

The free surface (top) is *not* included. The wetted perimeter consists of the bottom and the two slanted sides.

Length of each slanted side:

$$\ell = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} = \sqrt{(1.8)^2 + (1.2)^2} = \sqrt{3.24 + 1.44} = \sqrt{4.68} = 2.163 \text{ m}$$

Wetted perimeter:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= b + 2\ell \\
  &= 2 + 2 \times 2.163 \\
  &= 2 + 4.327 \\
  &= 6.327 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Hydraulic radius.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_h &= \frac{A}{P} = \frac{4.56}{6.327} \\
    &= 0.7206 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Equivalent diameter.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_e &= 4 R_h = 4 \times 0.7206 \\
    &= 2.882 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Head loss using Darcy–Weisbach.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_f &= f \frac{L}{D_e} \frac{v^2}{2g} \\
    &= 0.02 \times \frac{50}{2.882} \times \frac{(0.8)^2}{2 \times 9.81} \\
    &= 0.02 \times 17.35 \times \frac{0.64}{19.62} \\
    &= 0.02 \times 17.35 \times 0.03262 \\
    &= 0.01132 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $R_h = 0.721$ m, $D_e = 2.882$ m, $h_f = 0.0113$ m over 50 m.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Part (b):** Students commonly include the **top free-surface width** in the wetted perimeter. Remember: the free surface is *not* a wall in contact with the fluid — it is excluded from $P$. Also, use the *slanted* length of the side (not its horizontal projection) when computing the perimeter.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** What is meant by geometric similarity? A 1:25 scale model of a harbour breakwater is tested in a laboratory. The prototype is expected to experience a wave force of 1500 kN. If the experiment uses the same fluid as the prototype (sea water), and the time scale is chosen such that the Froude number is preserved, determine the model force. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rectangular air duct of dimensions 0.4 m × 0.3 m carries air at 12 m/s. Air properties: $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s. Calculate the equivalent diameter, Reynolds number, and the head loss per 10 m of duct length using the Blasius formula. **(7 marks)**

### MODEL SOLUTION

**Part (a):**

**Geometric similarity** means all corresponding linear dimensions between model and prototype are in a fixed ratio $\lambda_L$, and all corresponding angles are equal. The model is a *scaled geometric replica* of the prototype.

**Given:**
- Scale ratio: $\lambda_L = L_m / L_p = 1/25$
- Same fluid: $\rho_m = \rho_p$
- Froude similarity: $Fr_m = Fr_p$ ⇒ $\dfrac{v_m^2}{L_m g} = \dfrac{v_p^2}{L_p g}$

**Step 1 — Velocity scale ratio from Froude similarity.**

$$
\frac{v_m}{v_p} = \sqrt{\frac{L_m}{L_p}} = \sqrt{\lambda_L} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{25}} = \frac{1}{5}
$$

**Step 2 — Time scale ratio.**

$$
\lambda_T = \frac{T_m}{T_p} = \frac{L_m/v_m}{L_p/v_p} = \frac{\lambda_L}{\lambda_v} = \frac{\lambda_L}{\sqrt{\lambda_L}} = \sqrt{\lambda_L} = \frac{1}{5}
$$

**Step 3 — Force scale ratio.**

For the same fluid, with $F \propto \rho L^2 v^2$ (from pressure × area):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{F_m}{F_p} &= \frac{\rho_m}{\rho_p} \cdot \lambda_L^{\,2} \cdot \lambda_v^{\,2} \\
                &= 1 \times \left(\frac{1}{25}\right)^2 \times \left(\frac{1}{5}\right)^2 \\
                &= \frac{1}{625} \times \frac{1}{25} \\
                &= \frac{1}{15625}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Model force.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_m &= F_p \times \frac{1}{15625} \\
    &= 1500 \times 10^3 \text{ N} \times \frac{1}{15625} \\
    &= \frac{1{,}500{,}000}{15625} \\
    &= 96 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** Model force = **96 N**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Part (a):** A very common error is to use $F \propto L^3$ (from hydrostatic pressure) instead of $F \propto L^2 v^2$ (from dynamic pressure for wave forces). Wave forces are *dynamic*, so use $\rho L^2 v^2$. Also, students forget to convert 1500 kN into Newtons before substituting.

---

**Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Equivalent diameter of the rectangular duct.**

Using the formula from Section 3.3:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_e &= \frac{2ab}{a+b} = \frac{2 \times 0.4 \times 0.3}{0.4 + 0.3} \\
    &= \frac{0.24}{0.7} \\
    &= 0.3429 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Reynolds number.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Re &= \frac{\rho \, v \, D_e}{\mu} \\
   &= \frac{1.2 \times 12 \times 0.3429}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}} \\
   &= \frac{4.937}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}} \\
   &= 2.743 \times 10^{5}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Friction factor (Blasius — valid since $Re < 10^5$? Actually, $Re = 2.74 \times 10^5$ exceeds the Blasius limit of $10^5$).**

Since the question explicitly instructs use of the Blasius formula, we proceed (KTU questions often use this as a learning exercise even when the validity range is slightly exceeded):

$$
\begin{aligned}
f &= \frac{0.316}{Re^{0.25}} \\
  &= \frac{0.316}{(2.743 \times 10^5)^{0.25}} \\
  &= \frac{0.316}{22.91} \\
  &= 0.01379
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Head loss over 10 m.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_f &= f \frac{L}{D_e} \frac{v^2}{2g} \\
    &= 0.01379 \times \frac{10}{0.3429} \times \frac{(12)^2}{2 \times 9.81} \\
    &= 0.01379 \times 29.16 \times \frac{144}{19.62} \\
    &= 0.01379 \times 29.16 \times 7.339 \\
    &= 2.952 \text{ m of air}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $D_e = 0.343$ m, $Re = 2.74 \times 10^5$, $h_f = 2.95$ m.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Part (b):** Students often compute $D_e$ as the *diagonal* of the rectangle ($\sqrt{0.4^2 + 0.3^2} = 0.5$ m). This is the *geometric* length, not the *hydraulic* equivalent diameter. Use $\dfrac{2ab}{a+b}$ only. Also remember that for gases, $g$ is still used in $v^2/2g$ to obtain head in metres of the gas (or you may convert to pressure drop using $\Delta P = \rho g h_f$).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Geometric Properties & Similarity**

- **Hydraulic radius** is always $R_h = A/P$, where $P$ is the wetted perimeter only (no free surface).
- **Equivalent diameter** is $D_e = 4R_h = 4A/P$, defined such that it reduces to the pipe diameter $D$ for a *full circular pipe*.
- **Annulus** has $D_e = D_o - D_i$ (gap width) — not $\sqrt{D_o^2 - D_i^2}$.
- **Square duct (full)** has $D_e = a$ (side length, not diagonal).
- **Rectangular duct (full)** has $D_e = \dfrac{2ab}{a+b}$.
- **Parallel plates (infinite width)** have $D_e = 2a$ where $a$ is the gap.
- **Hydraulic depth** $D_h = A/T$ is used for *open channels* and is different from $D_e$.
- **Geometric similarity** requires a *constant length ratio* $\lambda_L$ between model and prototype, and identical corresponding angles.
- **Area scale ratio** = $\lambda_L^{\,2}$; **Volume scale ratio** = $\lambda_L^{\,3}$.
- **Velocity scale ratio** depends on the *governing dimensionless group* (Froude gives $\sqrt{\lambda_L}$, Reynolds gives $1/\lambda_L$, etc.).
- **Force scale ratio** uses $F \propto \rho L^2 v^2$ for dynamic forces; for hydrostatic use $F \propto \rho L^3 g$.
- **Discharge scale ratio** is $Q_m/Q_p = \lambda_L^{\,2} \cdot \lambda_v$.
- **Equivalent diameter** is *the* bridge that allows circular-pipe friction charts to be used for non-circular conduits.
- For **laminar flow** in non-circular ducts, the constant in $f = C/Re$ may differ from 64 (e.g., annulus gives 96 for concentric; rectangular ducts give 56.9 for square, etc.) — KTU usually accepts 64 unless stated.
- A *circular* cross-section is the **most hydraulically efficient** (minimum wetted perimeter for a given area) and hence gives the **lowest head loss**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kinematic and Dynamic Similarity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET413 - MECHANICS OF FLUID FLOW
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Kinematic and Dynamic Similarity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Types of Similarity — Formal Definitions

In fluid mechanics, the principle of similitude allows engineers to study the behavior of a **prototype** (full-scale system) by experimenting on a **model** that is geometrically smaller (or sometimes larger) but behaves in a dynamically analogous manner. There are three hierarchical levels of similarity:

> [!IMPORTANT] Core KTU Definition — Hierarchy of Similitude
> 1. **Geometric Similarity** — Exists when the model and prototype have identical shapes, and all linear dimensions bear a constant scale ratio.
> 2. **Kinematic Similarity** — Exists when, in addition to geometric similarity, the velocities at corresponding points have the same ratios in magnitude and direction at corresponding times.
> 3. **Dynamic Similarity** — Exists when, in addition to kinematic similarity, the forces acting on corresponding fluid elements (and boundaries) bear a constant ratio at corresponding points and times.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy — The Boating Pond:**
Imagine two boats — one a giant cruise ship (**prototype**) and the other a toy boat (**model**) floating in a pool. For the toy boat to truly *represent* the cruise ship's wave pattern, drag behavior, and turning characteristics:

- **Geometric similarity** → The toy boat must look like a miniature cruise ship (same hull shape, just smaller).
- **Kinematic similarity** → When the cruise ship moves at 20 km/h, the toy boat must move at a *scaled* speed so that water particles flow past them in the *same patterns*. If a wave at the bow of the ship is 2 m tall, the wave at the bow of the toy should be a proportional (scaled) height at the corresponding time.
- **Dynamic similarity** → The ratio of the *forces* (inertia, gravity, viscosity, surface tension) acting on the ship and toy must be the same, so that the toy experiences miniature versions of the same *types* of physical effects.

> [!NOTE] Intuitive Summary
> Geometric similarity is about **shape**, kinematic similarity is about **motion**, and dynamic similarity is about **forces**. Dynamic similarity is the *most complete* and *most difficult* to achieve — it is the ultimate goal of dimensional analysis-based model testing.

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities & Standard Metrics

The following fundamental quantities govern fluid flow similarity and are to be remembered in **bold** throughout this module:

- Characteristic length: **L** (m)
- Characteristic velocity: **V** (m/s)
- Fluid density: **ρ** (kg/m³)
- Dynamic viscosity: **μ** (N·s/m²)
- Kinematic viscosity: **ν** (m²/s)
- Acceleration due to gravity: **g** (m/s²) = **9.81 m/s²**
- Surface tension: **σ** (N/m)
- Bulk modulus of elasticity: **K** (N/m²)
- Speed of sound in fluid: **c** (m/s)

### 1.4 Significance for KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU Syllabus Highlight
> In PECET413, Module 4 specifically tests your ability to:
> - State the three types of similitude.
> - Derive expressions for model-prototype scale ratios for dominant forces.
> - Apply Reynolds, Froude, Euler, Weber, Mach, and Cauchy model laws to practical problems.
> - Recognize the dominant force ratio for a given engineering scenario (e.g., dams → Froude; pipes → Reynolds; ships → Froude + Reynolds; hydraulic structures with surface tension → Weber).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Prerequisites: The Three Fundamental Steps of Similitude

For complete dynamic similarity between model (**m**) and prototype (**p**), the following must hold simultaneously:

1. **Geometric similitude**: All linear scale ratios are equal.

$$
\lambda_L = \frac{L_m}{L_p} = \text{constant}
$$

2. **Kinematic similitude**: Velocity ratio is constant and time ratio follows a fixed pattern.

$$
\lambda_V = \frac{V_m}{V_p} = \frac{\lambda_L}{\lambda_T}
$$

3. **Dynamic similitude**: All force ratios are equal. The dominant forces include:

| Force Type | Symbol | Physical Origin |
|---|---|---|
| Inertia force | $F_i$ | Mass × acceleration of fluid |
| Viscous force | $F_\mu$ | Internal friction of fluid |
| Gravity force | $F_g$ | Weight of fluid element |
| Pressure force | $F_p$ | Surface pressure distribution |
| Surface tension force | $F_\sigma$ | Interfacial molecular attraction |
| Elastic force | $F_K$ | Fluid compressibility |
| Inertial-gravitational | – | Free-surface flows |
| Inertial-elastic | – | Compressible / high-speed flows |

### 2.2 Dimensionless Numbers — The Heart of Dynamic Similarity

Each dimensionless number is a ratio of two competing forces. When two flows are dynamically similar, these numbers are **equal** for model and prototype.

$$
\text{Re} = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \frac{V L}{\nu} \quad \text{(Reynolds number — inertia / viscous force ratio)}
$$

$$
\text{Fr} = \frac{V}{\sqrt{gL}} \quad \text{(Froude number — inertia / gravity force ratio)}
$$

$$
\text{Eu} = \frac{p}{\rho V^2} \quad \text{(Euler number — pressure / inertia force ratio)}
$$

$$
\text{We} = \frac{\rho V^2 L}{\sigma} \quad \text{(Weber number — inertia / surface tension force ratio)}
$$

$$
\text{M} = \frac{V}{c} = \frac{V}{\sqrt{K/\rho}} \quad \text{(Mach number — inertia / elastic (compressibility) force ratio)}
$$

$$
\text{Ca} = \frac{\rho V^2}{K} \quad \text{(Cauchy number — inertia / elastic force ratio, often } \text{Ca} = \text{M}^2\text{)}
$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick-Reference Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU High-Yield Formula Table

| Model Law | Dominant Force Ratio | Equating the dimensionless number gives (Velocity Scale) | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reynolds Model Law** | Inertia = Viscous | $V_m = V_p \cdot \dfrac{\nu_m}{\nu_p} \cdot \dfrac{L_p}{L_m}$ | Pipe flow, flow past submerged bodies (aerodynamics, hydraulics) |
| **Froude Model Law** | Inertia = Gravity | $V_m = V_p \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{L_m}{L_p}}$ | Free-surface flows: spillways, weirs, ships, harbours |
| **Euler Model Law** | Inertia = Pressure | $V_m = V_p \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_p}{\rho_m}}$ | Flow where pressure forces dominate (cavitation studies) |
| **Weber Model Law** | Inertia = Surface tension | $V_m = V_p \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_p} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_p}{\rho_m} \cdot \dfrac{L_p}{L_m}}$ | Flow with free-surface tension effects (capillary jets, small weirs) |
| **Mach Model Law** | Inertia = Elastic (compressibility) | $V_m = V_p \cdot \dfrac{c_m}{c_p} = V_p \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{K_m}{K_p} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_p}{\rho_m}}$ | High-speed gas dynamics, supersonic flows |
| **Cauchy Model Law** | Inertia = Elastic (compressibility) | Similar to Mach; same form | Compressible flow regimes |

> [!NOTE] Force-Scale Ratio Master Relation
> The general force-scale ratio is given by Newton's second law analogy:
> $$
> \lambda_F = \lambda_\rho \cdot \lambda_L^2 \cdot \lambda_V^2
> $$
> where the subscripted λ denotes the model-to-prototype ratio. Any other force ratio must equal this for dynamic similarity to be satisfied.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydraulic Engineers** use Froude-scaled models of **dams, spillways, and harbours** because gravity dominates.
- **Aerospace/Aeronautical Engineers** use Reynolds-scaled wind-tunnel tests for **aircraft, automobiles, and submarines**.
- **Civil Engineers** use Reynolds-scaled models of **pipelines, penstocks, and water-distribution networks**.
- **Naval Architects** use Froude-Reynolds compromises for **ship resistance** testing.
- **Compressible Flow Engineers** use Mach-scaled tests in **blow-down and shock tunnels** for supersonic aircraft and rocket nozzles.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reynolds Number from Navier-Stokes

**Step 1:** The full Navier-Stokes equation for an incompressible Newtonian fluid along one flow direction is:

$$
\rho \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w \frac{\partial u}{\partial z} \right) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \left( \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial z^2} \right) + \rho g_x
$$

**Step 2:** To make this equation dimensionless, we normalize every variable by a characteristic scale. Let:

$$
u = V \cdot \bar{u}, \quad x = L \cdot \bar{x}, \quad t = \frac{L}{V} \cdot \bar{t}, \quad p = \rho V^2 \cdot \bar{p}
$$

**Step 3:** Substituting into the inertial term $\rho \, u \, \partial u / \partial x$:

$$
\rho \cdot (V\bar{u}) \cdot \frac{(V\bar{u})}{L\bar{x}} = \frac{\rho V^2}{L} \left( \bar{u} \frac{\partial \bar{u}}{\partial \bar{x}} \right)
$$

The numerator has the **inertia force per unit volume** scaling: $\rho V^2 / L$.

**Step 4:** Substituting into the viscous term $\mu \, \partial^2 u / \partial x^2$:

$$
\mu \cdot \frac{V \bar{u}}{L^2 \bar{x}^2} = \frac{\mu V}{L^2} \left( \frac{\partial^2 \bar{u}}{\partial \bar{x}^2} \right)
$$

The viscous force per unit volume scales as: $\mu V / L^2$.

**Step 5:** Taking the ratio of inertial to viscous terms:

$$
\frac{\text{Inertia force}}{\text{Viscous force}} = \frac{\rho V^2 / L}{\mu V / L^2} = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \text{Re}
$$

> [!NOTE] Physical Meaning
> The Reynolds number compares the **inertia** of the moving fluid (tendency to keep moving) to the **viscous resistance** (tendency to slow it down through internal friction). When Re is small, viscous effects dominate (laminar flow). When Re is large, inertia dominates (turbulent flow).

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Froude Number from Energy / Gravity Comparison

**Step 1:** Consider a fluid element of characteristic length $L$, moving with velocity $V$ under gravity. Two competing effects are:

- **Inertial energy per unit mass** ~ $V^2$
- **Gravitational potential energy per unit mass** ~ $gL$

**Step 2:** Forming the ratio:

$$
\frac{\text{Inertia effect}}{\text{Gravity effect}} = \frac{V^2}{gL} = \text{Fr}^2
$$

Hence,

$$
\text{Fr} = \frac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}
$$

**Step 3:** For dynamic similarity, Fr must be equal for model and prototype:

$$
\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{g_m L_m}} = \frac{V_p}{\sqrt{g_p L_p}}
$$

Since $g_m = g_p$ (same planet, typically Earth):

$$
V_m = V_p \cdot \sqrt{\frac{L_m}{L_p}}
$$

> [!NOTE] Physical Meaning
> The Froude number compares the **flow inertia** to the **gravitational pull**. It governs free-surface phenomena — waves, hydraulic jumps, ship waves, spillway flow. Whenever a free surface is present, Fr is the relevant scaling parameter.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Euler Number (Pressure-Inertia Comparison)

**Step 1:** The pressure force per unit area (relevant for a control volume of characteristic length $L$) scales as $\Delta p \cdot L^2$.

**Step 2:** The inertia force per unit volume is $\rho V^2 / L$, multiplied by volume $L^3$ to get $\rho V^2 L^2$.

**Step 3:** The pressure-inertia force ratio is:

$$
\text{Eu} = \frac{\Delta p}{\rho V^2} = \frac{\text{Pressure force}}{\text{Inertia force}}
$$

**Step 4:** Equating Eu for model and prototype (assuming the same fluid $\rho_m = \rho_p$):

$$
\frac{\Delta p_m}{\rho_m V_m^2} = \frac{\Delta p_p}{\rho_p V_p^2} \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{\Delta p_m}{\Delta p_p} = \frac{V_m^2}{V_p^2}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Weber Number (Surface-Tension-Inertia)

**Step 1:** Surface-tension force scales as: $\sigma \cdot L$.

**Step 2:** Inertia force scales as: $\rho V^2 L^2$.

**Step 3:** Their ratio gives:

$$
\text{We} = \frac{\rho V^2 L^2}{\sigma L} = \frac{\rho V^2 L}{\sigma}
$$

Equating We for model and prototype:

$$
V_m = V_p \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_p} \cdot \frac{\rho_p}{\rho_m} \cdot \frac{L_p}{L_m}}
$$

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Mach / Cauchy Number (Elastic-Inertia)

**Step 1:** Elastic (compressibility) force per unit area is the bulk modulus $K$.

**Step 2:** The dimensionless Cauchy number:

$$
\text{Ca} = \frac{\rho V^2}{K}
$$

**Step 3:** The Mach number uses the speed of sound $c = \sqrt{K/\rho}$:

$$
\text{M} = \frac{V}{c} = \frac{V}{\sqrt{K/\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{\rho V^2}{K}} = \sqrt{\text{Ca}}
$$

Equating Mach numbers for model and prototype:

$$
V_m = V_p \cdot \sqrt{\frac{K_m}{K_p} \cdot \frac{\rho_p}{\rho_m}}
$$

### 3.6 The Reynolds Model Law — Detailed Derivation

**Step 1:** We require that the ratio of inertia to viscous force is the same in model and prototype:

$$
\left( \frac{F_i}{F_\mu} \right)_m = \left( \frac{F_i}{F_\mu} \right)_p
$$

**Step 2:** Writing the inertia force as $F_i = \rho L^2 V^2$ (mass × acceleration scaled), and viscous force as $F_\mu = \mu L V$ (shear stress × area), we have:

$$
\frac{\rho_m L_m^2 V_m^2}{\mu_m L_m V_m} = \frac{\rho_p L_p^2 V_p^2}{\mu_p L_p V_p}
$$

**Step 3:** Simplifying:

$$
\frac{\rho_m L_m V_m}{\mu_m} = \frac{\rho_p L_p V_p}{\mu_p}
$$

**Step 4:** Solving for $V_m$:

$$
V_m = V_p \cdot \frac{\rho_p}{\rho_m} \cdot \frac{L_p}{L_m} \cdot \frac{\mu_m}{\mu_p} = V_p \cdot \frac{L_p}{L_m} \cdot \frac{\nu_m}{\nu_p}
$$

where we used $\nu = \mu/\rho$.

> [!NOTE] Most useful KTU form
> The KTU textbook convention is:
> $$
> V_r = V_m / V_p = \dfrac{L_r}{\nu_r} \quad \text{where } L_r = L_p/L_m, \; \nu_r = \nu_m / \nu_p
> $$
> A higher viscosity in the model fluid *and* a larger model both *reduce* the required model velocity.

### 3.7 The Froude Model Law — Detailed Derivation

**Step 1:** Equate Froude numbers:

$$
\frac{V_m^2}{g_m L_m} = \frac{V_p^2}{g_p L_p}
$$

**Step 2:** Take $g_m = g_p$ (full-scale gravity = model gravity on Earth):

$$
V_m^2 = V_p^2 \cdot \frac{L_m}{L_p}
$$

**Step 3:** Hence:

$$
V_m = V_p \cdot \sqrt{\frac{L_m}{L_p}}
$$

The discharge scale ratio (using $Q = V \cdot A$, with $A \propto L^2$):

$$
Q_m = Q_p \cdot \left( \frac{L_m}{L_p} \right)^{5/2}
$$

The force scale ratio (using $F \propto \rho L^2 V^2$):

$$
F_m = F_p \cdot \left( \frac{L_m}{L_p} \right)^{3} \cdot \frac{\rho_m}{\rho_p}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] Why Froude models use the same fluid
> For most hydraulic structures, the same fluid (water) is used in model and prototype. If we also use $g_m = g_p$, the Froude model automatically implies Reynolds similarity will be **satisfied only if** the same fluid is used AND the scale is not too small. This is the famous "Froude-Reynolds dilemma" for ship models.

### 3.8 The Type-Studies Summary Matrix

| Flow Regime | Dominant Forces | Relevant Numbers | Typical Model Setup |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flow in closed conduits | Inertia, Viscous | Re | Same fluid (water), geometric scaling |
| Free-surface flows (spillways, weirs) | Inertia, Gravity | Fr | Same fluid, geometric scaling |
| Flow past immersed bodies (low Re) | Inertia, Viscous | Re | May use air/water/glycerin |
| Cavitation studies | Inertia, Pressure | Eu, $\sigma$ | Tunnels with adjustable pressure |
| Wave/wind-driven ships | Inertia, Gravity, Viscous | Fr, Re | Compromise between Fr and Re |
| Capillary flows (small weirs) | Inertia, Surface tension | We | Special fluids; small models |
| Compressible / supersonic | Inertia, Elastic | M, Ca | Pressurized or cryogenic tunnels |

### 3.9 The Buckingham Pi Theorem (Background Tool)

**Statement:** If a physical problem involves $n$ physical variables and $k$ fundamental dimensions, then the relationship can be expressed in terms of $(n - k)$ dimensionless $\pi$-groups.

**Example — Pipe flow:** Pressure drop $\Delta p$ depends on $L$, $V$, $D$ (diameter), $\rho$, $\mu$. So $n = 6$ variables in dimensions M, L, T ($k = 3$); expect $6 - 3 = 3$ dimensionless groups. These are: $\Delta p / (\rho V^2)$, $L/D$, and $\rho V D / \mu = \text{Re}$. The functional form:

$$
\frac{\Delta p}{\rho V^2} = f\!\left( \frac{L}{D}, \text{Re} \right)
$$

For long pipes, $L/D$ is absorbed into the friction factor:

$$
f = \phi(\text{Re})
$$

> [!NOTE] KTU Examiner's Tip
> The Buckingham Pi theorem is a **frequently-asked 7- or 14-mark question** in KTU exams. Be ready to:
> 1. List all variables.
> 2. Identify fundamental dimensions.
> 3. Compute the number of $\pi$-groups.
> 4. Form dimensionless groups by inspection or by writing a system of linear equations for the exponents.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Hierarchy of Similitude

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PROTOTYPE Full Scale System] --> B[Determine Dominant Forces in Flow]
    B --> C{Select Appropriate Model Law}
    C -->|Viscous Dominant| D[Reynolds Model Law Re]
    C -->|Gravity Dominant| E[Froude Model Law Fr]
    C -->|Pressure Dominant| F[Euler Model Law Eu]
    C -->|Surface Tension Dominant| G[Weber Model Law We]
    C -->|Compressibility Dominant| H[Mach Model Law M]
    D --> I[Equate Re_m = Re_p]
    E --> J[Equate Fr_m = Fr_p]
    F --> K[Equate Eu_m = Eu_p]
    G --> L[Equate We_m = We_p]
    H --> M[Equate M_m = M_p]
    I --> N[Compute Velocity Scale V_m / V_p]
    J --> N
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Compute Discharge Scale Q_m / Q_p]
    N --> P[Compute Force Scale F_m / F_p]
    N --> Q[Compute Time Scale T_m / T_p]
    O --> R[MODEL with Verified Dynamic Similarity]
    P --> R
    Q --> R
    R --> S[Measure Model Response]
    S --> T[Scale Up to Prototype Behavior]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Force-Ratio Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engineering Problem] --> B{Is there a free surface?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Use Froude Law]
    B -->|No| D{Is flow in closed conduit?}
    D -->|Yes| E{Is it highly viscous or low Re?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Use Reynolds Law]
    E -->|No| G{Is compressibility significant?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Use Mach or Cauchy Law]
    G -->|No| I[Use Reynolds Law default]
    D -->|No| J{Is surface tension dominant?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Use Weber Law]
    J -->|No| L[Use Reynolds Law]
    C --> M[Apply V_m = V_p sqrt L_m / L_p]
    F --> N[Apply V_m = V_p times nu_m / nu_p times L_p / L_m]
    H --> O[Apply V_m = V_p times c_m / c_p]
    I --> N
    K --> P[Apply V_m = V_p times sqrt sigma_m / sigma_p times rho_p / rho_m times L_p / L_m]
    L --> N
    M --> Q[Compute Q_m Q_p and F_m F_p scales]
    N --> Q
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — The Reynolds Model Law Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Given L_p V_p rho_p mu_p] --> B[Choose L_m and model fluid]
    B --> C[Compute Re_p = rho_p V_p L_p / mu_p]
    C --> D[Set Re_m = Re_p]
    D --> E[Solve for V_m = V_p times nu_m / nu_p times L_p / L_m]
    E --> F[Compute Q_m = V_m times A_m]
    E --> G[Compute F_m = rho_m V_m squared A_m]
    E --> H[Compute T_m = L_m / V_m]
    F --> I[Final Scaled Model Parameters]
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Buckingham Pi Theorem Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[List all relevant physical variables n] --> B[Identify fundamental dimensions k]
    B --> C[Compute number of pi groups n minus k]
    C --> D[Select k repeating variables]
    D --> E[Form pi_1 pi_2 ... pi_n_minus_k]
    E --> F[Express final relation f pi_1 pi_2 ... pi_3 = 0]
    F --> G[Identify known dimensionless numbers Re Fr We etc]
    G --> H[Use in model design or correlation]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define the three types of similitude used in fluid flow model studies. Mention the condition for complete dynamic similarity.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Geometric similarity** (1 mark): Model and prototype have the same shape, with all linear dimensions in a constant ratio $\lambda_L = L_m / L_p$.
2. **Kinematic similarity** (1 mark): Velocities at corresponding points in model and prototype are in a constant ratio in magnitude and direction: $\lambda_V = V_m / V_p = \lambda_L / \lambda_T$.
3. **Dynamic similarity** (1 mark): All forces (inertia, viscous, gravity, pressure, etc.) acting on corresponding fluid elements are in a constant ratio: $\lambda_F = F_m / F_p$. **Complete dynamic similarity** requires the ratios of all relevant forces (inertia, viscosity, gravity, pressure, surface tension, elasticity) to be equal between model and prototype.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
State the Reynolds Model Law. For what kind of flows is it applicable?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Statement (2 marks):** When viscous forces are dominant, dynamic similarity is achieved by equating the **Reynolds number** of the model and prototype:
$$
\text{Re}_m = \text{Re}_p \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{\rho_m V_m L_m}{\mu_m} = \frac{\rho_p V_p L_p}{\mu_p}
$$
The velocity scale becomes:
$$
V_m = V_p \cdot \frac{\nu_m}{\nu_p} \cdot \frac{L_p}{L_m}
$$
- **Application (1 mark):** Applicable to **flow in closed conduits** (pipes, channels), **flow past submerged bodies** (submarines, aircraft, automobiles), and any flow where **viscous forces dominate** over gravity, surface tension, and compressibility.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** Derive the Reynolds Model Law from dimensional analysis. Obtain the velocity, discharge, and force scale ratios. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** A submarine is to move at 36 km/h in seawater ($\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.05 \times 10^{-3}$ N·s/m²). A 1:25 scale model is to be tested in a freshwater towing tank ($\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.00 \times 10^{-3}$ N·s/m²). Find the required model speed, the drag force on the prototype if the model drag is measured as 25 N, and the power required by the prototype at its operating speed. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Derivation of Reynolds Model Law (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify the variables governing the flow. For a viscous flow around a body, the force $F$ depends on length $L$, velocity $V$, density $\rho$, and viscosity $\mu$:

$$
F = f(L, V, \rho, \mu)
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Write the functional form using dimensional analysis (Buckingham Pi):

$$
F = C \cdot L^a V^b \rho^c \mu^d
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Equate dimensions in M, L, T:

$$
[M L T^{-2}] = [L]^a [L T^{-1}]^b [M L^{-3}]^c [M L^{-1} T^{-1}]^d
$$

This gives the system:
- M: $1 = c + d$
- L: $1 = a + b - 3c - d$
- T: $-2 = -b - d$

Solving: $b = 2 - d$, $c = 1 - d$, $a = 2 - b + 3c + d = 2 - (2-d) + 3(1-d) + d = 3 - d$.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Choosing $d = 0$ to expose the leading scaling:

$$
F = C \cdot L^2 V^2 \rho
$$

The constant $C$ is dimensionless and depends on the remaining dimensionless group $\rho V L / \mu = \text{Re}$:

$$
F = C(\text{Re}) \cdot \rho L^2 V^2
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** For dynamic similarity, $\text{Re}_m = \text{Re}_p$:

$$
\frac{\rho_m V_m L_m}{\mu_m} = \frac{\rho_p V_p L_p}{\mu_p}
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Solve for velocity scale:

$$
V_m = V_p \cdot \frac{\mu_m}{\mu_p} \cdot \frac{\rho_p}{\rho_m} \cdot \frac{L_p}{L_m} = V_p \cdot \frac{\nu_m}{\nu_p} \cdot \frac{L_p}{L_m}
$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Compute other scale ratios:

- Discharge ratio ($Q = V \cdot A \propto V L^2$):

$$
Q_m = Q_p \cdot \frac{\nu_m}{\nu_p} \cdot \left( \frac{L_m}{L_p} \right)
$$

Equivalently:

$$
Q_r = \frac{\nu_r}{L_r}, \quad \text{where } L_r = L_p / L_m
$$

- Force ratio ($F \propto \rho L^2 V^2$):

$$
F_m = F_p \cdot \frac{\rho_m}{\rho_p} \cdot \left( \frac{L_m}{L_p} \right)^2 \cdot \left( \frac{\nu_m}{\nu_p} \cdot \frac{L_p}{L_m} \right)^2 = F_p \cdot \frac{\rho_m}{\rho_p} \cdot \left( \frac{\nu_m}{\nu_p} \right)^2
$$

- Time ratio ($T = L / V$):

$$
T_m = T_p \cdot \frac{L_m}{L_p} \cdot \frac{\nu_p}{\nu_m}
$$

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- Prototype speed: $V_p = 36$ km/h = **10 m/s**
- $L_r = L_p / L_m = 25$, so $L_m / L_p = 1/25$
- $\rho_p = 1025$ kg/m³, $\mu_p = 1.05 \times 10^{-3}$ N·s/m²
- $\rho_m = 1000$ kg/m³, $\mu_m = 1.00 \times 10^{-3}$ N·s/m²
- Measured model drag: $F_m = 25$ N

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute kinematic viscosities:

$$
\nu_p = \frac{\mu_p}{\rho_p} = \frac{1.05 \times 10^{-3}}{1025} = 1.0244 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}
$$

$$
\nu_m = \frac{\mu_m}{\rho_m} = \frac{1.00 \times 10^{-3}}{1000} = 1.00 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute model velocity:

$$
V_m = V_p \cdot \frac{\nu_m}{\nu_p} \cdot \frac{L_p}{L_m} = 10 \times \frac{1.00 \times 10^{-6}}{1.0244 \times 10^{-6}} \times 25
$$

$$
V_m = 10 \times 0.9761 \times 25 = 244.03 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute prototype drag force:

$$
F_p = F_m \cdot \frac{\rho_p}{\rho_m} \cdot \left( \frac{\nu_p}{\nu_m} \right)^2
$$

$$
F_p = 25 \times \frac{1025}{1000} \times \left( \frac{1.0244 \times 10^{-6}}{1.00 \times 10^{-6}} \right)^2
$$

$$
F_p = 25 \times 1.025 \times 1.0494 = 26.89 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Compute prototype power:

$$
P_p = F_p \cdot V_p = 26.89 \times 10 = 268.9 \text{ W} \approx 0.269 \text{ kW}
$$

**Final Answer:** $V_m \approx 244$ m/s, $F_p \approx 26.89$ N, $P_p \approx 268.9$ W.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** Explain the Froude Model Law. Derive the velocity, discharge, and force scale ratios. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** A spillway model built to a scale of 1:50 is tested in a laboratory. The discharge over the model spillway at a particular head is measured to be 0.40 m³/s. Determine the corresponding prototype discharge, velocity, and the force on a particular element of the prototype spillway, if the corresponding model force is 8 N. Assume the same fluid (water) is used. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Froude Model Law — Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** When gravity dominates (free-surface flow), the relevant forces are inertia $F_i$ and gravity $F_g$. The ratio:

$$
\text{Fr}^2 = \frac{F_i}{F_g} = \frac{\rho V^2 L^2}{\rho g L^3} = \frac{V^2}{gL}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Equate Froude numbers:

$$
\frac{V_m^2}{g_m L_m} = \frac{V_p^2}{g_p L_p}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** With $g_m = g_p$ (same gravitational field):

$$
V_m = V_p \cdot \sqrt{\frac{L_m}{L_p}}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Discharge ratio ($Q = V A \propto V L^2$):

$$
Q_m = Q_p \cdot \left( \frac{L_m}{L_p} \right)^{5/2}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Force ratio (using $F \propto \rho L^2 V^2$):

$$
F_m = F_p \cdot \frac{\rho_m}{\rho_p} \cdot \left( \frac{L_m}{L_p} \right)^2 \cdot \left( \frac{V_m}{V_p} \right)^2 = F_p \cdot \frac{\rho_m}{\rho_p} \cdot \left( \frac{L_m}{L_p} \right)^3
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Time ratio ($T = L / V$):

$$
T_m = T_p \cdot \sqrt{\frac{L_m}{L_p}}
$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** State the assumptions: (i) same fluid in model and prototype, (ii) $g_m = g_p$, (iii) viscous and surface tension effects negligible.

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- Scale ratio: $L_m / L_p = 1/50$, so $L_p / L_m = 50$
- $Q_m = 0.40$ m³/s
- $F_m = 8$ N
- Same fluid: $\rho_m = \rho_p$

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute prototype discharge:

$$
Q_p = Q_m \cdot \left( \frac{L_p}{L_m} \right)^{5/2} = 0.40 \times (50)^{2.5}
$$

$$
(50)^{2.5} = 50^2 \cdot 50^{0.5} = 2500 \times 7.071 = 17677.7
$$

$$
Q_p = 0.40 \times 17677.7 = 7071 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute prototype velocity (note: we cannot directly get $V_p$ from $Q_p$ without an area; instead use the Froude scale):

Since the model is geometrically similar, $A_m / A_p = (L_m / L_p)^2 = (1/50)^2 = 1/2500$, so $A_p = 2500 \, A_m$.

$$
V_p = \frac{Q_p}{A_p} = \frac{Q_m (L_p/L_m)^{5/2}}{(L_p/L_m)^2 A_m} = V_m \cdot \sqrt{\frac{L_p}{L_m}} = V_m \cdot \sqrt{50}
$$

To find $V_m$, we need $A_m$. Assume the spillway has a representative area; alternatively, the velocity scale alone:

$$
V_p = V_m \cdot \sqrt{50} = 7.071 \, V_m
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute prototype force:

$$
F_p = F_m \cdot \left( \frac{L_p}{L_m} \right)^3 = 8 \times (50)^3 = 8 \times 125000 = 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ N} = 1 \text{ MN}
$$

**Final Answer:** $Q_p \approx 7071$ m³/s, $V_p = 7.071 \, V_m$, $F_p = 1 \times 10^6$ N = 1 MN.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PECET413
> 1. **Confusing scale-ratio directions:** KTU consistently uses $L_m / L_p$ in the *model-to-prototype* sense. Some textbooks reverse this. Always state your convention explicitly at the start of the solution. (Penalty: 0.5–1 mark per occurrence.)
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units:** Prototypes are often given in km/h, m, and cm. Convert *every* quantity to SI (m, s, kg) before substituting into formulas. (Penalty: 1 mark.)
> 3. **Mixing up Reynolds and Froude applicability:** Reynolds is for closed-conduit / fully-submerged flows; Froude is for *free-surface* flows. If you apply Froude law to a pipe-flow problem, you lose 2–3 marks instantly.
> 4. **Omitting the force-scale derivation:** Many students correctly compute $V_m$ but forget the corresponding $F_m / F_p$ ratio. The question almost always asks for *all three*: velocity, discharge, force.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions:** Always list the assumptions (e.g., $g_m = g_p$, same fluid, negligible surface tension, etc.) explicitly. Examiners allocate 1 mark just for this.
> 6. **Using the wrong prototype/property symbols:** In Reynolds-law problems, watch the difference between $\nu = \mu/\rho$ and $\mu$ itself. Substituting $\mu$ where $\nu$ is required is a frequent, costly error.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Three types of similitude** (in order of strength): Geometric → Kinematic → Dynamic.
- **Geometric similarity** = same shape; $\lambda_L = $ constant.
- **Kinematic similarity** = same motion; $\lambda_V = $ constant.
- **Dynamic similarity** = same force ratios; all relevant dimensionless numbers match.
- **Reynolds number** (Re) = inertia / viscous; governs closed-conduit and fully-submerged flows.
- **Froude number** (Fr) = inertia / gravity; governs free-surface flows (spillways, ships, weirs).
- **Euler number** (Eu) = pressure / inertia; governs cavitation and pressure-driven flows.
- **Weber number** (We) = inertia / surface tension; governs capillary-scale and droplet flows.
- **Mach number** (M) = velocity / speed of sound; governs compressible / supersonic flows.
- **Cauchy number** (Ca) = inertia / elastic force; related to Mach as $\text{Ca} = \text{M}^2$.
- **Reynolds velocity scale:** $V_m = V_p \cdot (\nu_m / \nu_p) \cdot (L_p / L_m)$.
- **Froude velocity scale:** $V_m = V_p \cdot \sqrt{L_m / L_p}$ (with $g_m = g_p$).
- **Froude discharge scale:** $Q_m = Q_p \cdot (L_m / L_p)^{5/2}$.
- **Froude force scale:** $F_m = F_p \cdot (\rho_m / \rho_p) \cdot (L_m / L_p)^3$.
- **Buckingham Pi theorem** reduces $n$ variables to $n - k$ dimensionless groups.
- **Type study** choice: free surface → Fr; closed conduit → Re; high speed → M; small scale → We.
- **Always convert** to SI units, **always list assumptions**, and **always state the scale-ratio convention** at the start.
- **The Froude-Reynolds dilemma** in ship modelling: cannot satisfy both with a single fluid on the same gravity field — compromise is needed (e.g., pressurized towing tanks, or compensation factors).
- **Force-scale master relation:** $\lambda_F = \lambda_\rho \cdot \lambda_L^2 \cdot \lambda_V^2$ — use this to derive other force ratios in 30 seconds.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Model Studies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET413 - MECHANICS OF FLUID FLOW
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Model Studies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Model Studies** in fluid mechanics refer to the systematic experimental and analytical investigation of a *prototype* (full-scale system) by examining a *model* (geometrically similar, scaled-down or scaled-up replica) under dynamically equivalent conditions, with the primary objective of predicting the hydrodynamic, kinematic, and thermodynamic performance of the prototype without resorting to expensive, hazardous, or impractical full-scale testing.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for *Mechanics of Fluid Flow*, model studies form the culminating application of **Dimensional Analysis** and **Similitude Theory**, allowing engineers to relate forces, velocities, pressures, and flow rates between the model and the prototype through a finite set of **dimensionless parameters** (also called *Π-terms* or *similarity numbers*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Examiner Standard):**
> "Model analysis is the technique of investigating the behaviour of a full-scale prototype by studying the behaviour of a smaller or larger model, provided the conditions of geometric, kinematic and dynamic similarity are satisfied between the two."

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

**Analogy 1 — The Architect's Wind Tunnel:**
Imagine an architect designing a skyscraper. Instead of building a 200 m tower and waiting for typhoon winds to test it (dangerous, expensive, slow), engineers construct a **1:100 scale wooden model** and place it in a wind tunnel. They blow air over it and measure pressure, drag, and vortex shedding. From these small-scale observations, they predict what will happen at full scale. The key question is: *"What wind speed must I use on the model so that the model behaves exactly like the prototype under real winds?"* That "magic speed" is dictated by **dynamic similarity** (matching Reynolds number, Froude number, etc.).

**Analogy 2 — The Toy Ship in a Bathtub:**
When naval architects test a model ship in a towing tank, the *gravity waves* on the water surface behave differently on a small scale. If they just used a tiny model at the prototype's speed, the waves would be too small. So, they deliberately slow the model down so that the *wave pattern* (Froude number) matches. They accept that *viscous effects* (Reynolds number) will not match perfectly — this is the famous **Reynolds–Froude dilemma** in ship hydrodynamics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:**
> The *model* and *prototype* do NOT need to be the same size, the same speed, or even use the same fluid. They only need to be governed by the same **dimensionless governing equations** and have the **same values of relevant dimensionless groups** (Π-terms).

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following physical quantities form the **primary variables** of model analysis in fluid flow:

| Primary Variable | Symbol | SI Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Length (characteristic) | $L$ | **m** | Sets the geometric scale |
| Velocity | $V$ | **m/s** | Kinematic reference |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** | Drives free-surface & buoyancy effects |
| Fluid density | $\rho$ | **kg/m³** | Inertial property |
| Fluid dynamic viscosity | $\mu$ | **N·s/m² (Pa·s)** | Viscous resistance |
| Surface tension | $\sigma$ | **N/m** | Capillary effects |
| Bulk modulus of elasticity | $K$ | **N/m² (Pa)** | Compressibility |
| Pressure difference | $\Delta P$ | **N/m² (Pa)** | Driving force |

**Universal constants** used in model studies (kept in mind but typically absorbed in the dimensionless groups):

- **Standard gravity:** $g = 9.80665 \, \text{m/s}^2$
- **Density of water (4 °C):** $\rho_w = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$
- **Kinematic viscosity of water (20 °C):** $\nu = 1.004 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}$
- **Density of air (15 °C, sea level):** $\rho_a \approx 1.225 \, \text{kg/m}^3$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Rule of Three" in KTU Board Examinations:**
> Any model analysis question in the KTU 2024 scheme will almost certainly revolve around three core dimensionless numbers:
> 1. **Reynolds number** $Re$ — viscous/inertial force ratio
> 2. **Froude number** $Fr$ — gravitational/inertial force ratio
> 3. **Euler number** $Eu$ — pressure/inertial force ratio
> Plus (for special cases) **Mach, Weber, Cauchy, Strouhal** numbers.

---

## 1.4 Why Model Studies Are Indispensable in Engineering

1. **Economy** — A 1:10 model costs vastly less than a full-scale dam/spillway/aircraft.
2. **Safety** — Real prototype failure (e.g., nuclear reactor coolant study) cannot be risked.
3. **Time Compression** — A 100-year flood can be simulated in minutes in a hydraulic model.
4. **Process Optimization** — Multiple design alternatives can be tested rapidly.
5. **Validation of CFD** — Experimental model data serve as the *gold standard* benchmark for validating computer simulations.
6. **Scale-up/Scale-down of Industrial Equipment** — Chemical reactors, mixers, pumps, turbines, and biomedical devices are routinely scaled.

**Real-world applications (KTU-favoured examples):**

- Hydraulic jumps in **spillway design** (Froude similitude)
- **Ship resistance** in towing tanks (Reynolds–Froude)
- **Subsonic & supersonic flow** past aircraft (Mach similitude)
- **Flow meters, weirs, and venturimeters** in calibration labs
- **Sediment transport** in river engineering
- **Hydraulic turbines and pumps** in model test rigs
- **Wind loading** on tall buildings and bridges

---

## 1.5 The Three Pillars of Similitude (Master Diagram in Words)

For a model to truly represent a prototype, the following **three types of similarity** must exist simultaneously:

```
PILLAR 1: GEOMETRIC SIMILARITY  →  Shape similarity
             (All linear dimensions scaled by constant factor λ)

PILLAR 2: KINEMATIC SIMILARITY  →  Motion similarity
             (Streamlines are geometrically similar;
              Velocity ratio constant at all corresponding points)

PILLAR 3: DYNAMIC SIMILARITY   →  Force similarity
             (Ratios of all relevant forces constant at
              corresponding points and times)
```

Dynamic similarity automatically ensures kinematic similarity (which in turn rests on geometric similarity), but the converse is **not** true.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logic Chain of Model Analysis

The complete logical flow of a KTU model-studies problem proceeds in **five explicit steps**:

1. **Identify the dependent variable** (the unknown we want — e.g., drag force, discharge, head loss).
2. **List the independent governing variables** (the parameters that influence it — e.g., $L$, $V$, $\rho$, $\mu$, $g$).
3. **Form the dimensional equation** and reduce variables to dimensionless groups using either:
   - **Rayleigh's Method** (for simple problems with one repeating variable, or fewer than 4 variables), or
   - **Buckingham's Π-Theorem** (for complex problems — the KTU-preferred method).
4. **Establish similarity conditions** by equating each dimensionless group in the model to that in the prototype.
5. **Solve for the unknown scale factor** (e.g., model velocity, model discharge, model force) and predict prototype behaviour.

---

## 2.2 Geometric Similarity — Detailed Analysis

**Definition:** Model and prototype have the same shape, with all linear dimensions in a constant ratio.

$$\boxed{\lambda_L = \frac{L_m}{L_p} = \text{constant for all dimensions}}$$

**Consequences:**

$$\frac{A_m}{A_p} = \lambda_L^2, \qquad \frac{\forall_m}{\forall_p} = \lambda_L^3$$

where $\forall$ denotes volume. **Surface roughness** does NOT scale linearly with $\lambda_L$ in practice (a fundamental limitation of distorted models — discussed later).

---

## 2.3 Kinematic Similarity — Detailed Analysis

**Definition:** Velocities at corresponding points are in a constant ratio, and accelerations are also in a constant ratio. This means the *streamline pattern* is identical.

$$\boxed{\lambda_V = \frac{V_m}{V_p} = \text{constant}}$$

Streamline equation: $\psi_m(x, y, z) = \lambda_L^2 \lambda_V \, \psi_p(x/\lambda_L, y/\lambda_L, z/\lambda_L)$

> [!NOTE]
> Kinematic similarity implies the **same flow regime** — both model and prototype must be laminar, or both turbulent, with comparable transition characteristics.

---

## 2.4 Dynamic Similarity — Detailed Analysis

**Definition:** The ratios of all forces acting on corresponding fluid elements in the model and prototype are identical.

For a typical fluid-flow problem, the *inertial force* is balanced by one or more of the following:

| Force Type | Symbol | Expression | Dimensionless Group |
|---|---|---|---|
| Inertial | $F_I$ | $\rho L^2 V^2$ | 1 (defining group) |
| Viscous | $F_V$ | $\mu L V$ | **Reynolds number** $Re$ |
| Gravitational | $F_G$ | $\rho L^3 g$ | **Froude number** $Fr$ |
| Pressure | $F_P$ | $\Delta P \, L^2$ | **Euler number** $Eu$ |
| Surface tension | $F_S$ | $\sigma L$ | **Weber number** $We$ |
| Elastic (compressibility) | $F_E$ | $K L^2$ | **Cauchy number** $Ca$ (= $1/Ma^2$) |
| Unsteady (local inertia) | $F_{U}$ | $\rho L^3 \, \dot{V}$ | **Strouhal number** $St$ |

Dynamic similarity requires:

$$\boxed{\frac{F_V}{F_I}\bigg|_m = \frac{F_V}{F_I}\bigg|_p, \quad \frac{F_G}{F_I}\bigg|_m = \frac{F_G}{F_I}\bigg|_p, \quad \text{etc.}}$$

---

## 2.5 Derivation of the Major Dimensionless Numbers

The board examiner expects students to be able to **derive** the following five numbers from first principles. Each derivation is presented in Section 3; the *physical meaning* is summarized here.

### 2.5.1 Reynolds Number ($Re$)

$$Re = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \frac{V L}{\nu} = \frac{\text{Inertial force}}{\text{Viscous force}} = \frac{F_I}{F_V}$$

- **Dominant in:** Pipe flow, boundary layers, submerged bodies where viscosity is the dominant resistance.
- **Critical for:** Laminar-turbulent transition ($Re_{crit} \approx 2300$ in pipes, $\approx 5 \times 10^5$ on flat plates).

### 2.5.2 Froude Number ($Fr$)

$$Fr = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g L}} = \sqrt{\frac{\rho V^2 L^2}{\rho g L^3}} = \sqrt{\frac{F_I}{F_G}}$$

- **Dominant in:** Free-surface flows — open channels, spillways, ship waves, hydraulic jumps, wave forces on coastal structures.

### 2.5.3 Euler Number ($Eu$)

$$Eu = \frac{\Delta P}{\rho V^2} = \frac{\text{Pressure force}}{\text{Inertial force}} = \frac{F_P}{F_I}$$

- **Dominant in:** Flow past bodies where pressure distribution dictates the force — airfoils, venturimeters, orifices, building aerodynamics.

### 2.5.4 Mach Number ($Ma$)

$$Ma = \frac{V}{c} = \frac{V}{\sqrt{K/\rho}}$$

where $c$ is the speed of sound in the fluid and $K$ is the bulk modulus.

- **Dominant in:** Compressible flows, high-speed aerodynamics, gas dynamics, shock-wave phenomena.

### 2.5.5 Weber Number ($We$)

$$We = \frac{\rho V^2 L}{\sigma} = \frac{\text{Inertial force}}{\text{Surface tension force}} = \frac{F_I}{F_S}$$

- **Dominant in:** Flows with significant interfacial tension — droplet formation, capillary jets, spilling breakers, small-scale hydraulic models using mercury.

### 2.5.6 Strouhal Number ($St$)

$$St = \frac{f L}{V} = \frac{\text{Local acceleration force}}{\text{Inertial force}}$$

- **Dominant in:** Oscillatory/unsteady flows — vortex shedding behind cylinders, flag flutter, pulsating flows.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — these are the 14 formulas the KTU board examiner tests on PECET413 Module 4.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Application Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Geometric scale ratio | $\lambda_L = L_m / L_p$ | All model studies |
| 2 | Area ratio | $\lambda_A = \lambda_L^2$ | Geometric similarity |
| 3 | Volume ratio | $\lambda_\forall = \lambda_L^3$ | Geometric similarity |
| 4 | Velocity scale (Reynolds) | $V_m = V_p \, (L_p/L_m) \cdot (\nu_m/\nu_p)$ | Viscous-dominated |
| 5 | Velocity scale (Froude) | $V_m = V_p \, \sqrt{L_m / L_p}$ | Free-surface flows |
| 6 | Velocity scale (Mach) | $V_m = V_p \cdot (c_m / c_p)$ | Compressible flows |
| 7 | Discharge scale (Reynolds) | $Q_m = Q_p \, (L_m/L_p) \cdot (\mu_p/\mu_m)$ | Pipe flow models |
| 8 | Discharge scale (Froude) | $Q_m = Q_p \, (L_m/L_p)^{5/2}$ | Open-channel models |
| 9 | Force scale (general) | $F_m = F_p \, \lambda_\rho \, \lambda_L^2 \, \lambda_V^2$ | All dynamic models |
| 10 | Force scale (Reynolds) | $F_m = F_p \, \lambda_\mu^2 / \lambda_\rho$ | Viscous models |
| 11 | Force scale (Froude) | $F_m = F_p \, \lambda_\rho \, \lambda_L^3$ | Gravity models |
| 12 | Time scale (Reynolds) | $t_m = t_p \, (L_m/L_p)^2 \cdot (\rho_m/\mu_m) \cdot (\mu_p/\rho_p)$ | Viscous unsteady |
| 13 | Time scale (Froude) | $t_m = t_p \, \sqrt{L_m / L_p}$ | Free-surface unsteady |
| 14 | Pressure scale (Euler) | $\Delta P_m = \Delta P_p \, (\rho_m/\rho_p) \cdot (V_m/V_p)^2$ | Pressure-driven flow |

---

## 2.7 Buckingham's Π-Theorem — The Master Tool

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Buckingham's Π-Theorem (the exam favourite):**
> If a physical phenomenon involves $n$ independent variables in $m$ fundamental dimensions, then the phenomenon can be completely described by $(n - m)$ independent dimensionless groups (Π-terms), and the relationship can be written as:
> $$f(\Pi_1, \Pi_2, \ldots, \Pi_{n-m}) = 0$$

**Systematic Procedure (the 7 steps to write in the exam):**

1. List all variables (including the dependent variable).
2. Choose a set of $m$ fundamental dimensions (usually M, L, T).
3. Determine the number of Π-terms: $k = n - m$.
4. Select $m$ *repeating variables* (collectively spanning all $m$ dimensions; never include the dependent variable).
5. Form each Π-term by combining one of the remaining variables with the repeating variables raised to unknown powers.
6. Solve the resulting simultaneous equations for the exponents using dimensional homogeneity.
7. Write the final functional relationship.

---

## 2.8 Distorted Models — When Complete Similarity is Impossible

In many real engineering problems, **simultaneous similarity of all relevant forces is impossible** (e.g., Reynolds–Froude conflict in ship models). The solution is:

- **Undistorted model** — same scale ratio $\lambda_L$ in all directions (ideal but often impractical).
- **Distorted model** — vertical scale $\lambda_v$ and horizontal scale $\lambda_h$ are different.

For a Froude-distorted river model:

$$\lambda_v = \lambda_h^{\,p}, \quad \text{typically } p = 1/2 \text{ to } 2/3$$

**Distortion ratio:** $\delta = \lambda_h / \lambda_v \neq 1$

**Velocity scale in distorted model:**

$$V_m = V_p \, \sqrt{\lambda_v} \quad (\text{Froude-based})$$

**Discharge scale in distorted model:**

$$Q_m = Q_p \, \lambda_h \, \lambda_v^{3/2}$$

> [!WARNING]
> Distorted models give **approximate** results. They are used when the model would otherwise be impractically small (e.g., wide rivers, estuaries).

---

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Typical Model Study | Dominant Similitude |
|---|---|---|
| Dam spillways, stilling basins | Hydraulic model in lab flume | Froude |
| Pipe networks, valves | Water/air flow rig | Reynolds |
| Submarines, torpedoes | Cavitation tunnel | Reynolds + Cauchy |
| Aircraft, rockets | Wind tunnel / shock tube | Mach + Reynolds |
| Estuaries, harbours | Tidal basin model | Froude + Strouhal |
| Hydraulic turbines | Model turbine test rig | Reynolds + Froude (specific speed) |
| Atomizers, inkjet nozzles | Droplet visualisation | Weber |
| Blood flow in arteries | Compliant silicone model | Reynolds + Cauchy (elasticity) |
| Industrial mixers | Bench-scale reactor | Reynolds + Power number |
| Cooling towers | Heat-transfer scaled model | Reynolds + Prandtl (thermal) |
